## α (id: 000001000000000)

α
=

α

noun nom

60\.46 · Number · First, Second, Third, Etc. \[Ordinals]

first first in a series involving time, space, or set Occurring only in titles of NT writings: πρὸς Κορινθίους α ‘First Letter to the Corinthians’; Ἰωάννου α ‘First Epistle of John.’

## ἀββά (id: 000005000000000)

ἀββά
====

ἀββά (G0005) — αββα

noun

12\.12 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

Father (title for God; a Greek transliteration of an Aramaic word meaning ‘father’) one who combines aspects of supernatural authority and care for his people Though there is a widespread tendency to preserve the Aramaic transliteration in the form of either abba or aba, there are frequent dangers in doing so, since the transliterated form may actually correspond to another word in a receptor language and thus provide an obstacle to proper understanding. In general, there is no point in a translation of ἀββά, since the resulting expression would simply be Father Father. Accordingly, in many languages the combination of Abba, Father is simply reduced to Father. In a number of languages, however, a vocative form (that is to say, a form used in direct address) is different from a form used in speaking about God as Father. It is, of course, essential to employ the appropriate grammatical form.\|In a number of languages it is necessary to distinguish clearly between ‘Father’ when referring to the heavenly Father and ‘father’ as a reference to a human father. The use of capitalization is quite satisfactory for the individual who is reading a text but not for one who is hearing it read, and since more people hear a text read than read it for themselves, it is essential that the reference of ‘Father’ be clear. In order to identify the use of ‘Father’ as a title for God, it is possible in many languages to use ‘Father in heaven’ or ‘Father above’ or ‘Father God.’\|In some languages it may even be necessary to identify the ‘Father’ as the creator and therefore employ a phrase such as ‘our Father who created us.’\|A particularly complicating factor involved in the use of ‘Father’ as a title for God is its occurrence without a pronominal reference as to whose father is involved. For example, in a number of languages one cannot speak of ‘father’ without indicating whose father, because a person does not become a father except by some relationship to another individual. Therefore, one must always speak of ‘my father’ or ‘his father’ and never simply ‘the father.’ In passages in which Jesus is speaking of ‘the Father’ in relationship to himself, it is necessary usually to employ a phrase such as ‘my Father.’ When, however, ‘Father’ is used as a title for ‘God’ in his relationship to people generally, then one may speak of ‘our Father’ (normally with an inclusive first person plural pronoun if the language in question makes a distinction between inclusive and exclusive first person plural pronoun referents).

[MRK 14:36](https://ref.ly/Matt14:36) · [ROM 8:15](https://ref.ly/Acts8:15) · [GAL 4:6](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:6)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 14:36; Romans 8:15; Galatians 4:6

2:21](https://ref.ly/Heb2:21) · [JAS 2:23](https://ref.ly/Heb2:23) · [1PE 3:6](https://ref.ly/Jas3:6)

1\.16 · Geographical Objects and Features · Regions Above the Earth

**Collocation:** ὁ κόλπος Ἀβραάμ

Abraham’s bosom, heaven (an idiom, literally: Abraham’s bosom) the heavenly abode, with the implication of close interpersonal relations A literal rendering of κόλπος Ἀβραάμ as ‘the bosom of Abraham’ or ‘the lap of Abraham’ is often misleading. In some languages it may even suggest homosexuality, and in other cases it implies that Lazarus was either a baby or was changed into a baby in heaven. Since κόλπος Ἀβραάμ is generally interpreted as a reference to the eschatological heavenly feast,1 one may be justified in rendering [LUK 16:22](https://ref.ly/Mark16:22) as in TEV “the poor man died and was carried by the angels to sit beside Abraham at the feast in heaven.” In some languages κόλπος Ἀβραάμ in this context is translated as ‘he was carried by the angels to Abraham’s side’ or ‘... to be with Abraham.’

[LUK 16:22](https://ref.ly/Mark16:22)

1 According to ancient Jewish tradition, the end of the age would be characterized by a heavenly feast to be enjoyed by the righteous. This would be presided over by Abraham, and therefore to go to Abraham’s bosom would be equivalent to being an honored guest at this celestial banquet.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 1:1; Matthew 1:2; Matthew 1:17; Matthew 3:9; Matthew 8:11; Matthew 22:32; Mark 12:26; Luke 1:55; Luke 1:73; Luke 3:8; Luke 3:34; Luke 13:16; Luke 13:28; Luke 16:22; Luke 16:23; Luke 16:24; Luke 16:25; Luke 16:29; Luke 16:30; Luke 19:9; Luke 20:37; John 8:33; John 8:37; John 8:39; John 8:40; John 8:52; John 8:53; John 8:56; John 8:57; John 8:58; Acts 3:13; Acts 3:25; Acts 7:2; Acts 7:16; Acts 7:17; Acts 7:32; Acts 13:26; Romans 4:1; Romans 4:2; Romans 4:3; Romans 4:9; Romans 4:12; Romans 4:13; Romans 4:16; Romans 9:7; Romans 11:1; 2 Corinthians 11:22; Galatians 3:6; Galatians 3:7; Galatians 3:8; Galatians 3:9; Galatians 3:14; Galatians 3:16; Galatians 3:18; Galatians 3:29; Galatians 4:22; Hebrews 2:16; Hebrews 6:13; Hebrews 7:1; Hebrews 7:2; Hebrews 7:4; Hebrews 7:5; Hebrews 7:6; Hebrews 7:9; Hebrews 11:8; Hebrews 11:17; James 2:21; James 2:23; 1 Peter 3:6


## ἄβυσσος (id: 000012000000000)

ἄβυσσος
=======

ἄβυσσος (G0012)

noun

1\.20 · Geographical Objects and Features · Regions Below the Surface of the Earth

abyss, abode of evil spirits, very deep place (a figurative extension of meaning of ἄβυσσος ‘pit,’ not occurring in the NT) a location of the dead and a place where the Devil is kept ([REV 20:3](https://ref.ly/Jude20:3)), the abode of the beast as the antichrist ([REV 11:7](https://ref.ly/Jude11:7)), and of Abaddon, as the angel of the underworld ([REV 9:11](https://ref.ly/Jude9:11)) ἄβυσσος is sometimes rendered as ‘a very deep hole’; in other instances, ‘a hole without a bottom’ or ‘the deepest hole in the earth.’

[LUK 8:31](https://ref.ly/Mark8:31) · [ROM 10:7](https://ref.ly/Acts10:7) · [REV 9:1](https://ref.ly/Jude9:1) · [REV 9:2](https://ref.ly/Jude9:2) · [REV 9:11](https://ref.ly/Jude9:11) · [REV 11:7](https://ref.ly/Jude11:7) · [REV 17:8](https://ref.ly/Jude17:8) · [REV 20:1](https://ref.ly/Jude20:1) · [REV 20:3](https://ref.ly/Jude20:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 8:31; Romans 10:7; Revelation 9:1; Revelation 9:2; Revelation 9:11; Revelation 11:7; Revelation 17:8; Revelation 20:1; Revelation 20:3

## ἀγαθός (id: 000018000000000)

ἀγαθός
======

ἀγαθός (G0018)

adj · ἀγαθοεργέω · ἀγαθοποιέω · ἀγαθοποιΐα · ἀγαθοποιός · ἀγαθουργέω · ἀγαθωσύνη · ἀφιλάγαθος · φιλάγαθος

88\.1 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Goodness

good, goodness, good act positive moral qualities of the most general nature In a number of languages there is no abstract term such as ‘goodness.’ Since goodness implies some type of activity involving others, one must frequently use a verb expression, for example, ‘to be good to’ or ‘to benefit.’ The qualification ‘full of’ in [ROM 15:14](https://ref.ly/Acts15:14) must likewise be restructured in a number of languages so as to indicate the fact that one always engages in doing good, for example, ‘that you are always doing good to people.’

57\.33 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Be Rich, Be Wealthy

**Collocation:** τὰ ἀγαθά

goods, possessions (pl) possessions which provide material benefits, usually used with reference to movable or storable possessions rather than real estate It may also be possible to translate [LUK 12:19](https://ref.ly/Mark12:19) as ‘you have all you need to live well for many years.’

[LUK 12:18](https://ref.ly/Mark12:18) · [LUK 12:19](https://ref.ly/Mark12:19)

1 ἀγαθός in [MAT 20:15](https://ref.ly/Matt20:15) may also be understood as meaning good (see [88\.1](#88.1)).

## ἀγαθωσύνη (id: 000019000000000)

ἀγαθωσύνη
=========

ἀγαθωσύνη (G0019)

noun · ἀγαθός

88\.1 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Goodness

good, goodness, good act positive moral qualities of the most general nature In a number of languages there is no abstract term such as ‘goodness.’ Since goodness implies some type of activity involving others, one must frequently use a verb expression, for example, ‘to be good to’ or ‘to benefit.’ The qualification ‘full of’ in [ROM 15:14](https://ref.ly/Acts15:14) must likewise be restructured in a number of languages so as to indicate the fact that one always engages in doing good, for example, ‘that you are always doing good to people.’

[ROM 15:14](https://ref.ly/Acts15:14) · [GAL 5:22](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:22)1 · [EPH 5:9](https://ref.ly/Gal5:9) · [2TH 1:11](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:11)

57\.109 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Give

to be generous, generosity the act of generous giving, with the implication of its relationship to goodness

[GAL 5:22](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:22)1

1 GAL 5:22 ἀγαθωσύνη in this verse may also be understood as goodness (see [88\.1](#88.1)).

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 15:14; Galatians 5:22; Ephesians 5:9; 2 Thessalonians 1:11

ἀγαλλίασις
==========

ἀγαλλίασις (G0020)

noun · ἀγαλλιάω

to be extremely joyful, to rejoice greatly, extreme gladness a state of intensive joy and gladness, often implying verbal expression and body movement (for example, jumping, leaping, dancing)

[LUK 1:14](https://ref.ly/Mark1:14) · [LUK 1:44](https://ref.ly/Mark1:44) · [ACT 2:46](https://ref.ly/John2:46) · [HEB 1:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:9) · [JUD 1:24](https://ref.ly/3John1:24)

## ἀγαλλιάω (id: 000021000000000)

ἀγαλλιάω
========

ἀγαλλιάω (G0021)

ἀγαλλίασις

25\.133 · Attitudes and Emotions · Happy, Glad, Joyful

to be extremely joyful, to be overjoyed, to rejoice greatly to experience a state of great joy and gladness, often involving verbal expression and appropriate body movement In [ACT 2:26](https://ref.ly/John2:26) it may be impossible to say ‘my tongue rejoices.’ One may, however, translate in some languages as ‘I shout because I am so happy.’

## ἀγαπάω (id: 000025000000000)

ἀγαπάω
======

ἀγαπάω (G0025)

ἀγάπη · ἀγαπητός

25\.43 · Attitudes and Emotions · Love, Affection, Compassion

to love, to regard with affection, loving concern, love to have love for someone or something, based on sincere appreciation and high regard Though some persons have tried to assign certain significant differences of meaning between ἀγαπάω\[a], ἀγάπη\[a] and φιλέω\[a], φιλία ([25\.33](#25.33)), it does not seem possible to insist upon a contrast of meaning in any and all contexts. For example, the usage in [JHN 21:15](https://ref.ly/Luke21:15) [JHN 21:16](https://ref.ly/Luke21:16) [JHN 21:17](https://ref.ly/Luke21:17) [JHN 21:17](https://ref.ly/Luke21:17) [JHN 21:17](https://ref.ly/Luke21:17) seems to reflect simply a rhetorical alternation designed to avoid undue repetition. There is, however, one significant clue to possible meaningful differences in at least some contexts, namely, the fact that people are never commanded to love one another with φιλέω or φιλία, but only with ἀγαπάω and ἀγάπη. Though the meanings of these terms overlap considerably in many contexts, there are probably some significant differences in certain contexts; that is to say, φιλέω and φιλία are likely to focus upon love or affection based upon interpersonal association, while ἀγαπάω and ἀγάπη focus upon love and affection based on deep appreciation and high regard. On the basis of this type of distinction, one can understand some of the reasons for the use of ἀγαπάω and ἀγάπη in commands to Christians to love one another. It would, however, be quite wrong to assume that φιλέω and φιλία refer only to human love, while ἀγαπάω and ἀγάπη refer to divine love. Both sets of terms are used for the total range of loving relations between people, between people and God, and between God and Jesus Christ.

to love, to regard with affection, loving concern, love to have love for someone or something, based on sincere appreciation and high regard Though some persons have tried to assign certain significant differences of meaning between ἀγαπάω\[a], ἀγάπη\[a] and φιλέω\[a], φιλία ([25\.33](#25.33)), it does not seem possible to insist upon a contrast of meaning in any and all contexts. 23\.28 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

fellowship meal a special type of communal meal having particular significance for early Christians as an expression of their mutual affection and concern The meaning of ἀγάπη\[b] may be rendered in some languages as meals in which you show your love for one another as you eat together or your eating together as the result of your love for one another.

[JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12)

## ἀγαπητός (id: 000027000000000)

ἀγαπητός
========

ἀγαπητός (G0027)

adj · ἀγαπάω

25\.45 · Attitudes and Emotions · Love, Affection, Compassion

object of one’s affection, one who is loved, beloved, dear (derivative of ἀγαπάω\[a] ‘to love,’ [25\.43](#25.43)) pertaining to one who or that which is loved It is also possible to understand ἀγαπητός in [MAT 12:18](https://ref.ly/Matt12:18) as ‘the only beloved one’ (see [58\.53](#58.53)), and the meaning may, in fact, be simply ‘only’ or ‘unique.’ This meaning has evidently arisen because of the association of ἀγαπητός with μονογενής ‘unique, only’ ([58\.52](#58.52)).

1:5](https://ref.ly/2John1:5) · [3JN 1:11](https://ref.ly/2John1:11) · [JUD 1:3](https://ref.ly/3John1:3) · [JUD 1:17](https://ref.ly/3John1:17) · [JUD 1:20](https://ref.ly/3John1:20)

58\.53 · Nature, Class, Example · Distinctive, Unique

only, only dear pertaining to one who is the only one of his or her class, but at the same time is particularly loved and cherished

[MAT 3:17](https://ref.ly/Matt3:17)1 · [MAT 12:18](https://ref.ly/Matt12:18) · [MAT 17:5](https://ref.ly/Matt17:5) · [MRK 1:11](https://ref.ly/Matt1:11) · [MRK 9:7](https://ref.ly/Matt9:7) · [LUK 3:22](https://ref.ly/Mark3:22) · [LUK 20:13](https://ref.ly/Mark20:13) · [2PE 1:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:17)

1 MAT 3:17 ἀγαπητός in this sense is typical of a term which shares significant components from two different semantic domains. In the context of [MAT 3:17](https://ref.ly/Matt3:17) the focus seems clearly to be upon the distinctive and unique elements of meaning, but it would be entirely wrong to rule out the supplementary component of being loved and cherished (see ἀγαπητός\[a], [25\.45](#25.45)).

## ἀγγέλλω (id: 000032000000000)

ἀγγέλλω
=======

ἀγγέλλω (G0518a)

ἀγγελία · ἄγγελος · ἀναγγέλλω · ἀπαγγέλλω · ἀρχάγγελος · διαγγέλλω · ἐξαγγέλλω · ἐπαγγελία · ἐπάγγελμα · εὐαγγελίζω · εὐαγγέλιον · εὐαγγελιστής · ἰσάγγελος · καταγγελεύς · καταγγέλλω · παραγγελία · παραγγέλλω · προεπαγγέλλομαι · προευαγγελίζομαι · προκαταγγέλλω

33\.189 · Communication · Inform, Announce

to tell, to inform to provide otherwise unknown information1

[JHN 20:18](https://ref.ly/Luke20:18)

1 ἀγγέλλω appears to be more generic in meaning than a term such as ἐμφανίζω\[c] ([33\.208](#33.208)), but it is difficult to indicate in all instances the various aspects of denotation and connotation associated with the meanings in this subdomain.

* **Associated Passages:** John 20:18


## ἄγγελος (id: 000033000000000)

ἄγγελος
=======

ἄγγελος (G0032)

noun · ἀγγέλλω

33\.195 · Communication · Inform, Announce

messenger (derivative of ἀγγέλλω ‘to tell, to inform,’ [33\.189](#33.189)) a person who makes an announcement1

[MAT 11:10](https://ref.ly/Matt11:10) · [MRK 1:2](https://ref.ly/Matt1:2) · [LUK 7:24](https://ref.ly/Mark7:24) · [LUK 7:27](https://ref.ly/Mark7:27) · [LUK 9:52](https://ref.ly/Mark9:52) · [JAS 2:25](https://ref.ly/Heb2:25)

12\.28 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

angel a supernatural being that attends upon or serves as a messenger of a superior supernatural entity In many languages a term for ‘angels’ is borrowed from another dominant language, but in other instances a somewhat descriptive phrase may be employed. The most common expressions for the ‘angels of God’ are ‘messengers’ and ‘messengers from heaven.’ Sometimes these angels are called ‘spirit messengers’ and even ‘flying messengers.’ In some instances they have been called ‘the holy servants of God,’ but an expression such as ‘servants of God’ or even ‘messengers of God’ tends to overlap in meaning with expressions used to characterize the role and function of the prophets who were sent as messengers from God. In some languages a term for ‘angels’ is contrasted with that for ‘prophets’ by calling angels ‘messengers from heaven’ and prophets ‘messengers from God.’ The ‘angels of the Devil’ are often called ‘the Devil’s servants.’\|One cannot avoid problems involved in the rendering of ‘angel’ merely by borrowing a term. In some instances such a borrowing may already exist in the language and with a different meaning. For example, in one instance 'angel' had already been borrowed but with a meaning of babies who died at childbirth or shortly thereafter.

## ἄγγος (id: 000034000000000)

ἄγγος
=====

ἄγγος (G0030a)

noun

6\.120 · Artifacts · Containers

container, vessel a container, primarily for liquids or wet objects The vessels referred to in this context would no doubt have been rather large, containing perhaps some sixteen to twenty liters (or about four to five gallons).

[MAT 13:48](https://ref.ly/Matt13:48)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:48


## ἀγέλη (id: 000035000000000)

ἀγέλη
=====

ἀγέλη (G0034)

noun · ἄγω

4\.8 · Animals · Animals

herd collective for a group of animals In a number of languages a ‘herd’ must be translated merely as ‘many,’ since there is frequently no corresponding collective term which would be applicable only or even primarily to pigs. In some languages, however, there are highly specific terms for various types of groups of animals, so that a distinctive collective expression may be used for pigs, sheep, horses, camels, etc.

[MAT 8:30](https://ref.ly/Matt8:30) · [MAT 8:31](https://ref.ly/Matt8:31) · [MAT 8:32](https://ref.ly/Matt8:32) · [MRK 5:11](https://ref.ly/Matt5:11) · [MRK 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13) · [LUK 8:32](https://ref.ly/Mark8:32) · [LUK 8:33](https://ref.ly/Mark8:33)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 8:30; Matthew 8:31; Matthew 8:32; Mark 5:11; Mark 5:13; Luke 8:32; Luke 8:33

## ἁγιάζω (id: 000038000000000)

ἁγιάζω
======

ἁγιάζω (G0037)

ἁγιασμός · ἅγιος · ἁγιότης · ἁγιωσύνη · ἁγνεία · ἁγνίζω · ἁγνισμός · ἁγνός · ἁγνότης · ἁγνῶς

53\.44 · Religious Activities · Dedicate, Consecrate

to consecrate, consecration, to dedicate to God, dedication to dedicate to the service of and to loyalty to deity Though in certain contexts ἁγιάζω\[a] and ἁγιασμός suggest resulting moral behavior, the emphasis is not upon a manner of life but upon religious activity and observances which reflect one’s dedication or consecration to God. Accordingly, in [1CO 1:2](https://ref.ly/Rom1:2) one may translate ἁγιάζω as who have given themselves to God or who serve God with a whole heart.

[MAT 23:17](https://ref.ly/Matt23:17) · [MAT 23:19](https://ref.ly/Matt23:19) · [JHN 10:36](https://ref.ly/Luke10:36) · [ACT 20:32](https://ref.ly/John20:32) · [1CO 1:2](https://ref.ly/Rom1:2) · [1CO 7:14](https://ref.ly/Rom7:14) · [1CO 7:14](https://ref.ly/Rom7:14) · [EPH 5:26](https://ref.ly/Gal5:26) · [1TI 4:5](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:5) · [2TI 2:21](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:21) · [HEB 2:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:11) · [HEB 2:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:11) · [HEB 9:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:13) · [HEB 10:29](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:29)

88\.26 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Holy, Pure

to make holy to cause someone to have the quality of holiness1

[JHN 10:36](https://ref.ly/Luke10:36) · [JHN 17:17](https://ref.ly/Luke17:17) · [JHN 17:19](https://ref.ly/Luke17:19) · [JHN 17:19](https://ref.ly/Luke17:19) · [ACT 26:18](https://ref.ly/John26:18) · [ROM 15:16](https://ref.ly/Acts15:16) · [1CO 1:2](https://ref.ly/Rom1:2) · [1CO 6:11](https://ref.ly/Rom6:11) · [EPH 5:26](https://ref.ly/Gal5:26) · [1TH 5:23](https://ref.ly/Col5:23) · [2TI 2:21](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:21) · [HEB 2:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:11) · [HEB 2:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:11) · [HEB 10:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:10) · [HEB 10:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:14) · [HEB 10:29](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:29) · [HEB 13:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:12) · [REV 22:11](https://ref.ly/Jude22:11)

88\.27 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Holy, Pure

to hallow, to regard as holy, to honor as holy to feel reverence for or to honor as holy In a number of languages it is impossible to employ an expression such as ‘your name’ as a reference for God himself. Therefore, it may be necessary to translate this expression in [MAT 6:9](https://ref.ly/Matt6:9) as ‘may you be reverenced as holy’ or even ‘may you be acknowledged as God.’

[MAT 6:9](https://ref.ly/Matt6:9) · [LUK 11:2](https://ref.ly/Mark11:2) · [1PE 3:15](https://ref.ly/Jas3:15)

1 Note that ἁγιάζω\[b] denotes the causing of a quality of holiness in contrast with ἁγιάζω\[a] ([53\.44](#53.44)) which means the dedication of someone to the service of or loyalty to deity.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 23:17; Matthew 23:19; John 10:36; Acts 20:32; 1 Corinthians 1:2; 1 Corinthians 7:14; Ephesians 5:26; 1 Timothy 4:5; 2 Timothy 2:21; Hebrews 2:11; Hebrews 9:13; Hebrews 10:29; John 17:17; John 17:19; Acts 26:18; Romans 15:16; 1 Corinthians 6:11; 1 Thessalonians 5:23; Hebrews 10:10; Hebrews 10:14; Hebrews 13:12; Revelation 22:11; Matthew 6:9; Luke 11:2; 1 Peter 3:15

ἁγιασμός
========

ἁγιασμός (G0038)

noun · ἁγιάζω

53\.44 · Religious Activities · Dedicate, Consecrate

consecration, dedication action of being dedicated to the service of and loyalty to deity Though in certain contexts ἁγιάζω\[a] and ἁγιασμός suggest resulting moral behavior, the emphasis is not upon a manner of life but upon religious activity and observances which reflect one’s dedication or consecration to God. Accordingly, in [1CO 1:2](https://ref.ly/Rom1:2) one may translate ἁγιάζω as ‘who have given themselves to God’ or ‘who serve God with a whole heart.’

4:8](https://ref.ly/Jude4:8) · [REV 4:8](https://ref.ly/Jude4:8) · [REV 6:10](https://ref.ly/Jude6:10) · [REV 14:10](https://ref.ly/Jude14:10) · [REV 20:6](https://ref.ly/Jude20:6) · [REV 22:11](https://ref.ly/Jude22:11)

53\.46 · Religious Activities · Dedicate, Consecrate

devout, godly, dedicated pertaining to being dedicated or consecrated to the service of God In some languages it is essential to distinguish between ἅγιος and ὅσιος when these refer to God (see [88\.24](#88.24)) and when they refer to persons or objects which have been consecrated to the service of God. It is both theoretically and practically advantageous to make a significant distinction in meaning on the basis of these contextual differences which occur in the Greek NT. It is not difficult to translate ἅγιος and ὅσιος by expressions meaning ‘dedicated to God,’ ‘devout,’ ‘pious,’ etc., but it may be extremely difficult to translate ἅγιος and ὅσιος when applied to God. In some languages one can only use an expression such as ‘he who is truly God’ or ‘he who is in every respect God.’

[MAT 4:5](https://ref.ly/Matt4:5) · [MAT 7:6](https://ref.ly/Matt7:6) · [MAT 24:15](https://ref.ly/Matt24:15) · [MAT 27:53](https://ref.ly/Matt27:53) · [MRK 6:20](https://ref.ly/Matt6:20) · [LUK 1:49](https://ref.ly/Mark1:49) · [LUK 1:70](https://ref.ly/Mark1:70) · [LUK 1:72](https://ref.ly/Mark1:72) · [LUK 2:23](https://ref.ly/Mark2:23) · [ACT 6:13](https://ref.ly/John6:13) · [ROM 1:7](https://ref.ly/Acts1:7) · [1CO 1:2](https://ref.ly/Rom1:2) · [1CO 3:17](https://ref.ly/Rom3:17) · [1CO 7:14](https://ref.ly/Rom7:14) · [EPH 1:4](https://ref.ly/Gal1:4) · [EPH 2:21](https://ref.ly/Gal2:21) · [EPH 3:5](https://ref.ly/Gal3:5) · [COL 3:12](https://ref.ly/Phil3:12) · [HEB 3:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:1) · [1PE 3:5](https://ref.ly/Jas3:5) · [REV 11:2](https://ref.ly/Jude11:2) · [REV 21:2](https://ref.ly/Jude21:2) · [REV 21:10](https://ref.ly/Jude21:10) · [REV 22:19](https://ref.ly/Jude22:19)

11\.27 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

**Collocation:** οἱ ἅγιοι

God’s people persons who belong to God, and as such constitute a religious entity In rendering οἱ ἅγιοι it is important to avoid an expression which means ‘sanctified,’ for the focus is not upon a particular state of holiness, but upon a special relationship with God. Those who are spoken of as οἱ ἅγιοι may also be admonished to become sanctified.\|In a number of respects, οἱ ἅγιοι is similar in meaning to λαός\[b] 'people of God' ([11\.12](#11.12)) and to ἀδελφός\[b] 'fellow believer' ([11\.23](#11.23)).

20:9](https://ref.ly/Jude20:9)

7\.18 · Constructions · Buildings

**Collocation:** τὸ ἅγιον

sanctuary (derivative of ἅγιος\[a], 'holy', [88\.24](#88.24)) the movable tabernacle or the temple in Jerusalem as a consecrated place ἅγιος\[d] and ἅγιος\[e] should be translated in essentially the same way as ναός\[a] ([7\.15](#7.15)).

[MAT 24:15](https://ref.ly/Matt24:15) · [ACT 6:13](https://ref.ly/John6:13) · [ACT 21:28](https://ref.ly/John21:28) · [HEB 9:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:1)

7\.18 · Constructions · Buildings

**Collocation:** τὰ ἅγια

sanctuary (derivative of ἅγιος\[a], 'holy', [88\.24](#88.24)) the movable tabernacle or the temple in Jerusalem as a consecrated place ἅγιος\[d] and ἅγιος\[e] should be translated in essentially the same way as ναός\[a] ([7\.15](#7.15)).

[HEB 8:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:2) · [HEB 9:24](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:24)

7\.35 · Constructions · Parts and Areas of Buildings

**Collocation:** ἅγια ἁγίων · τὰ ἅγια

the holy place, (most) holy place the interior (either the outer or the inner of the two rooms) of the sanctuary of the Jerusalem Temple or of the earlier tabernacle or of a corresponding ‘spiritual holy place,’ perhaps regarded as being in heaven The inner room was more specifically identified by the phrase ἅγια ἁγίων, literally ‘holy of holies’ [HEB 9:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:3), a Hebrew idiom indicating superlative degree. The inner sanctuary could also be referred to as ‘within the curtain’ (τὸ ἐσώτερον τοῦ καταπετάσματος [HEB 6:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:19)). See ἅγιος\[a] ([88\.24](#88.24)) and [7\.18](#7.18).\|The outer room of the sanctuary may be referred to in some languages as simply ‘the first room of the Holy Temple’ or ‘the first holy room of the Temple.’ The holy of holies may be referred to as ‘the most holy place’ or ‘the second holy room of the Temple’ or ‘the interior holy room of the Temple.’ What is important here is the degree of holiness, not so much the actual location within the Temple. It is for this reason that for the ‘holy of holies’ many translators use ‘the most sacred place’ or ‘the very, very sacred room.’ In this type of context the term ‘sacred’ may be rendered as ‘dedicated especially to God’ or ‘consecrated to God.’

[HEB 9:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:2) · [HEB 9:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:3) · [HEB 9:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:3) · [HEB 9:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:8) · [HEB 9:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:12) · [HEB 9:24](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:24) · [HEB 9:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:25) · [HEB 10:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:19) · [HEB 13:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:11)

## ἄγκιστρον (id: 000044000000000)

ἄγκιστρον
=========

ἄγκιστρον (G0044)

noun · ἄγκυρα

6\.10 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Fishing

fish hook small, curved piece of metal or bone or even a strong thorn, with a sharp point on one end, usually with a barb just behind the point, and a loop or bend on the other end to which a fishing line can be attached; used with some bait attached to it in order to attract a fish A literal rendering of [MAT 17:27](https://ref.ly/Matt17:27) could be quite misleading, since it would imply that only the hook was thrown into the water. What was thrown into the water was a line with a baited hook on the end, and it may be necessary to be explicit in some languages in order to avoid misunderstanding.

[MAT 17:27](https://ref.ly/Matt17:27)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 17:27

anchor a heavy object attached to a boat by a rope or chain and dropped to the bottom of a body of water in order to prevent or restrict the movement of the boat For languages having no term for ‘anchor,’ one may use a descriptive equivalent, for example, ‘heavy weights on ropes to keep a boat from moving.’

## ἀγνωσία (id: 000056000000000)

=======

noun

32\.7 · Understand · Understand

not to understand, to fail to understand to not understand, with the implication of a lack of capacity or ability It is also possible to interpret ἀγνωσία in [1CO 15:34](https://ref.ly/Rom15:34) as meaning to not know about (see [28\.16](#28.16)).

what is not understood that which is not understood or comprehended, implying a lack of capacity For another interpretation of ἀγνωσία in [1PE 2:15](https://ref.ly/Jas2:15), see [28\.16](#28.16).

[1PE 2:15](https://ref.ly/Jas2:15)1

1 1PE 2:15 The semantic structure of φιμοῦν τὴν τῶν ἀφρόνων ἀνθρώπων ἀγνωσίαν is extremely complex, since φιμοῦν ‘to silence’ would normally be related to ‘foolish people,’ and it is clearly these who are the ones talking about things ‘which they do not understand.’

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 15:34; 1 Peter 2:15


## ἄγνωστος (id: 000057000000000)

ἄγνωστος
========

ἄγνωστος (G0057)

adj

28\.27 · Know · Known \[the content of knowledge]

not known, unknown pertaining to not being known In [ACT 17:23](https://ref.ly/John17:23) the phrase ‘Unknown God’ may be simply rendered as ‘the god whom no one knows about,’ but in a number of languages the closest equivalent is ‘the god who has no name.’

[ACT 17:23](https://ref.ly/John17:23)

## ἀγορά (id: 000058000000000)

ἀγορά
=====

ἀγορά (G0058)

noun · ἀγοράζω

57\.207 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Earn, Gain, Do Business

market, marketplace, business center a commercial center with a number of places for doing business ἀγορά differs in meaning from ἐμπόριον ([57\.206](#57.206)) in that the focus is upon a commercial center involving a number of places for doing business. The term ἐμπόριον may be used to refer to the same type of geographical location, but the focus is upon the activity and not the place.

## ἀγοράζω (id: 000059000000000)

ἀγοράζω
=======

ἀγοράζω (G0059)

ἀγορά · ἀγοραῖος · δημηγορέω · ἐξαγοράζω · πανήγυρις

57\.188 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Sell, Buy, Price

to buy, to purchase to acquire possessions or services in exchange for money

[MAT 13:44](https://ref.ly/Matt13:44) · [MAT 13:46](https://ref.ly/Matt13:46) · [MAT 14:15](https://ref.ly/Matt14:15) · [MAT 21:12](https://ref.ly/Matt21:12) · [MAT 25:9](https://ref.ly/Matt25:9) · [MAT 25:10](https://ref.ly/Matt25:10) · [MAT 27:7](https://ref.ly/Matt27:7) · [MRK 6:36](https://ref.ly/Matt6:36) · [MRK 6:37](https://ref.ly/Matt6:37) · [MRK 11:15](https://ref.ly/Matt11:15) · [MRK 15:46](https://ref.ly/Matt15:46) · [MRK 16:1](https://ref.ly/Matt16:1) · [LUK 9:13](https://ref.ly/Mark9:13) · [LUK 14:18](https://ref.ly/Mark14:18) · [LUK 14:19](https://ref.ly/Mark14:19) · [LUK 17:28](https://ref.ly/Mark17:28) · [LUK 22:36](https://ref.ly/Mark22:36) · [JHN 4:8](https://ref.ly/Luke4:8) · [JHN 6:5](https://ref.ly/Luke6:5) · [JHN 13:29](https://ref.ly/Luke13:29) · [1CO 7:30](https://ref.ly/Rom7:30) · [REV 3:18](https://ref.ly/Jude3:18) · [REV 13:17](https://ref.ly/Jude13:17) · [REV 18:11](https://ref.ly/Jude18:11)

37\.131 · Control, Rule · Release, Set Free

to redeem, to set free (figurative extension of meaning of ἀγοράζω\[a] ‘to buy,’ [57\.188](#57.188)) to cause the release or freedom of someone by a means which proves costly to the individual causing the release A literal rendering of ἀγοράζω\[b] or ἐξαγοράζω as ‘to release by means of paying a price’ can be misinterpreted in the sense that Christ actually engaged in some kind of monetary transaction. A literal translation may also lead to the mistaken interpretation, which was widespread in the Middle Ages, that in redeeming the believers God actually paid a price to the Devil. Obviously, ἀγοράζω in [1CO 6:20](https://ref.ly/Rom6:20) and ἐξαγοράζω in [GAL 3:13](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:13) must be understood in a figurative sense.

[1CO 6:20](https://ref.ly/Rom6:20) · [1CO 7:23](https://ref.ly/Rom7:23) · [2PE 2:1](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:1) · [REV 5:9](https://ref.ly/Jude5:9) · [REV 14:3](https://ref.ly/Jude14:3) · [REV 14:4](https://ref.ly/Jude14:4)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:44; Matthew 13:46; Matthew 14:15; Matthew 21:12; Matthew 25:9; Matthew 25:10; Matthew 27:7; Mark 6:36; Mark 6:37; Mark 11:15; Mark 15:46; Mark 16:1; Luke 9:13; Luke 14:18; Luke 14:19; Luke 17:28; Luke 22:36; John 4:8; John 6:5; John 13:29; 1 Corinthians 7:30; Revelation 3:18; Revelation 13:17; Revelation 18:11; 1 Corinthians 6:20; 1 Corinthians 7:23; 2 Peter 2:1; Revelation 5:9; Revelation 14:3; Revelation 14:4


## ἀγοραῖος (id: 000060000000000)

ἀγοραῖος
========

ἀγοραῖος (G0060)

adj · ἀγοράζω

88\.251 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Laziness, Idleness

loafer, bum (derivative of ἀγορά ‘marketplace,’ [57\.207](#57.207)) a person who habitually idles in the marketplace

[ACT 17:5](https://ref.ly/John17:5)

56\.1 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Court of Justice

court, court of justice a court of justice for determining guilt or innocence1 For languages which do not have technical terms for a court, it is usually possible to use some such phrase as ‘to be brought before a judge’ or ‘to have judges decide one’s case.’

[ACT 19:38](https://ref.ly/John19:38)

1 There are no doubt certain subtle differences of meaning in these terms (ἀγοραῖος\[b], κριτήριον\[a], κρίσις\[c], ἡμέρα\[e]), especially in their connotations, but it is not possible to specify the difference on the basis of existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 17:5; Acts 19:38


## ἄγρα (id: 000061000000000)

ἄγρα
====

ἄγρα (G0061)

noun · ἀγρεύω

18\.8 · Attachment · Grasp, Hold

to catch, a catch the act of catching something Though in Greek the term ἄγρα may be used to refer to the catching of a wide variety of objects, in the context of [LUK 5:4](https://ref.ly/Mark5:4) the reference is to fish, and one must make certain that the appropriate term in the receptor language is employed, depending, for example, whether one catches fish, animals, or people.

## ἀγράμματος (id: 000062000000000)

ἀγράμματος
==========

ἀγράμματος (G0062)

adj

27\.23 · Learn · Learn

uneducated, unlearned pertaining to one who has not acquired a formal education (referring primarily to formal training) Some persons have assumed that ἀγράμματος in [ACT 4:13](https://ref.ly/John4:13) means ‘illiterate’ in the sense of not being able to read or write, but this is highly unlikely in view of the almost universal literacy in NT times, and especially as the result of extensive synagogue schools. Evidently, ἀγράμματος in [ACT 4:13](https://ref.ly/John4:13) refers to a lack of formal rabbinic training.

[ACT 4:13](https://ref.ly/John4:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 4:13

to remain outdoors to spend time outdoors, with the possible implication of living outdoors

## ἀγρεύω (id: 000064000000000)

ἀγρεύω
======

ἀγρεύω (G0064)

ἄγρα · ἀγρυπνέω · ἀγρυπνία · ζωγρέω

27\.30 · Learn · Learn Something Against Someone

to trap, to catch off guard, to catch in a mistake (figurative extension of meaning of term meaning literally: ‘to hunt, to trap,’ not occurring in the NT) to acquire information about an error or fault, with the purpose of causing harm or trouble In a number of languages it is possible to retain the figurative meaning of ‘trap’ or ‘catch,’ but in a number of instances other figures are used, for example, ‘to cause to trip,’ ‘to cause to fall,’ ‘to hear wrong words from his mouth,’ or ‘to use his words against him.’

[MRK 12:13](https://ref.ly/Matt12:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 12:13


## ἀγριέλαιος (id: 000065000000000)

ἀγριέλαιος
==========

ἀγριέλαιος (G0065)

noun

3\.11 · Plants · Trees

wild olive tree a tree regarded by some (though probably wrongly) as the ancestor of the domestic olive tree; smaller, produces smaller fruit with less oil than domestic variety; Olea europaea sylvestris In speaking of the wild olive tree as ‘an uncultivated olive tree,’ it is important to make clear that it is not the matter of neglect which produces the wild tree, but the fact that such a tree is essentially different from the cultivated one. A marginal note to this effect may be useful in the context of [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17). The precise identification of the ‘wild olive tree’ cannot be made with certainty. It may be possible in some instances to speak of such a tree as an ‘uncultivated olive tree’ in contrast with a ‘cultivated olive tree.’ It may also be possible to speak of the wild olive tree as ‘the olive tree producing bitter fruit,’ although in reality all olives are bitter before they are cured.\|This reference in [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17) is intentionally strange because the usual course of action was to graft cultivated branches into wild trees.

[ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17) · [ROM 11:24](https://ref.ly/Acts11:24)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 11:17; Romans 11:24

ἄγριος
======

adj · ἀγρός

20\.6 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Violence

violent, stormy (a figurative extension of meaning of ἄγριος ‘wild, savage’) pertaining to what is violent and uncontrolled This expression in [JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13), however, is applied to people, and therefore it may be necessary to make the figurative reference clear, for example, they are like stormy waves of the sea or ... wild waves of the sea.

[JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13)

94\.1

wild pertaining to what is undomesticated and uncultivated in nature

## ἀγρυπνία (id: 000070000000000)

========

noun

23\.73 · Physiological Processes and States · Sleep, Waking

sleeplessness the state of remaining awake because of not being able to go to sleep, whether from anxiety or because of external circumstances In the two occurrences of ἀγρυπνία in the NT ([2CO 6:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:5) [2CO 11:27](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:27)), failure to sleep was evidently the result of external circumstances which prevented normal sleep. It may therefore be possible to translate ἀγρυπνία as it was not possible to sleep or there was no way to sleep.

## ἄγω (id: 000071000000000)

ἄγω
===

ἄγω (G0071)

ἀγωγή · ἀνάγω · ἀνεκδιήγητος · ἀπάγω · ἀποσυνάγωγος · ἀρχηγός · ἀρχισυνάγωγος · διάγω · διηγέομαι · διήγησις · δουλαγωγέω · εἰσάγω · ἐκδιηγέομαι · ἐξάγω · ἐξηγέομαι · ἐπάγω · ἐπανάγω · ἐπεισαγωγή · ἐπισυνάγω · ἐπισυναγωγή · ἡγεμονεύω · ἡγεμονία · ἡγεμών · ἡγέομαι · καθηγητής · κατάγω · μετάγω · ὁδηγέω · παιδαγωγός · παράγω · παρεισάγω · παρείσακτος · περιάγω · προάγω · προηγέομαι · προσάγω · προσαγωγή · συλαγωγέω · συνάγω · συναγωγή · συναπάγομαι · ὑπάγω · χαλιναγωγέω · χειραγωγέω · χειραγωγός · χορηγέω

15\.165 · Linear Movement · Lead, Bring, Take

to lead, to bring to direct or guide the movement of an object, without special regard to point of departure or goal The Spirit of God is also regarded as an agent in leading or directing the movement of a person, for example, ἤγετο ἐν τῷ πνεύματι ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ he was led by the Spirit into the desert ([LUK 4:1](https://ref.ly/Mark4:1)).\|ἄγω\[a] also occurs in contexts in which the leading involves force: ὅταν ἄγωσιν ὑμᾶς παραδιδόντες whenever you are taken into custody and turned over to the courts ([MRK 13:11](https://ref.ly/Matt13:11)); Ἤγοντο δὲ καὶ ἕτεροι κακοῦργοι δύο σὺν αὐτῷ ἀναιρεθῆναι they led two others off also, both of them criminals, to be put to death with him ([LUK 23:32](https://ref.ly/Mark23:32)).

[MAT 10:18](https://ref.ly/Matt10:18) · [MAT 21:2](https://ref.ly/Matt21:2) · [MAT 21:7](https://ref.ly/Matt21:7) · [MRK 13:11](https://ref.ly/Matt13:11) · [LUK 4:9](https://ref.ly/Mark4:9) · [LUK 4:29](https://ref.ly/Mark4:29) · [LUK 18:40](https://ref.ly/Mark18:40) · [LUK 19:27](https://ref.ly/Mark19:27) · [LUK 19:30](https://ref.ly/Mark19:30) · [LUK 19:35](https://ref.ly/Mark19:35) · [LUK 22:54](https://ref.ly/Mark22:54) · [LUK 23:1](https://ref.ly/Mark23:1) · [LUK 23:32](https://ref.ly/Mark23:32) · [JHN 1:42](https://ref.ly/Luke1:42) · [JHN 7:45](https://ref.ly/Luke7:45) · [JHN 8:3](https://ref.ly/Luke8:3) · [JHN 9:13](https://ref.ly/Luke9:13) · [JHN 10:16](https://ref.ly/Luke10:16) · [JHN 18:13](https://ref.ly/Luke18:13) · [JHN 18:28](https://ref.ly/Luke18:28) · [JHN 19:4](https://ref.ly/Luke19:4) · [JHN 19:13](https://ref.ly/Luke19:13) · [ACT 5:21](https://ref.ly/John5:21) · [ACT 5:27](https://ref.ly/John5:27) · [ACT 6:12](https://ref.ly/John6:12) · [ACT 8:32](https://ref.ly/John8:32) · [ACT 9:2](https://ref.ly/John9:2) · [ACT 9:21](https://ref.ly/John9:21) · [ACT 9:27](https://ref.ly/John9:27) · [ACT 11:26](https://ref.ly/John11:26) · [ACT 13:23](https://ref.ly/John13:23) · [ACT 17:15](https://ref.ly/John17:15) · [ACT 17:19](https://ref.ly/John17:19) · [ACT 18:12](https://ref.ly/John18:12) · [ACT 19:37](https://ref.ly/John19:37) · [ACT 20:12](https://ref.ly/John20:12) · [ACT 21:16](https://ref.ly/John21:16) · [ACT 21:34](https://ref.ly/John21:34) · [ACT 22:5](https://ref.ly/John22:5) · [ACT 23:10](https://ref.ly/John23:10) · [ACT 23:18](https://ref.ly/John23:18) · [ACT 23:18](https://ref.ly/John23:18) · [ACT 23:31](https://ref.ly/John23:31) · [ACT 25:6](https://ref.ly/John25:6) · [ACT 25:17](https://ref.ly/John25:17) · [ACT 25:23](https://ref.ly/John25:23) · [1CO 12:2](https://ref.ly/Rom12:2) · [1TH 4:14](https://ref.ly/Col4:14) · [2TI 4:11](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:11) · [HEB 2:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:10)

15\.210 · Linear Movement · Carry, Bear

to carry, to bring to carry or bring, especially animate beings1

[LUK 4:40](https://ref.ly/Mark4:40) · [LUK 10:34](https://ref.ly/Mark10:34) · [2TI 3:6](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:6)

15\.34 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to go away, to leave to move away from a reference point It would also be possible to translate ἄγωμεν in [JHN 11:7](https://ref.ly/Luke11:7) as let us depart for.

[MAT 26:46](https://ref.ly/Matt26:46) · [MRK 1:38](https://ref.ly/Matt1:38) · [MRK 14:42](https://ref.ly/Matt14:42) · [JHN 11:7](https://ref.ly/Luke11:7) · [JHN 11:15](https://ref.ly/Luke11:15) · [JHN 11:16](https://ref.ly/Luke11:16) · [JHN 14:31](https://ref.ly/Luke14:31)

36\.1 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Guide, Lead

to guide, to direct, to lead to so influence others as to cause them to follow a recommended course of action In some languages it is difficult to distinguish readily between expressions for ‘leading’ and those which refer to ‘ruling’ or ‘governing,’ but it is important to try to distinguish clearly between these two different sets of interpersonal relations. In some languages, the concept of ‘leading’ can be expressed by ‘showing how to’ or ‘demonstrating how one ought to.’ In other languages it is possible to speak of ‘leading’ as simply ‘going ahead of,’ but too often such an expression may designate only ‘a scout’ who goes ahead to see whether things are safe, or it may refer only to a person who insists on his prerogative as the most distinguished person in a group.

[LUK 4:1](https://ref.ly/Mark4:1) · [ROM 2:4](https://ref.ly/Acts2:4) · [ROM 8:14](https://ref.ly/Acts8:14) · [GAL 5:18](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:18)

42\.1 · Perform, Do · Function

to carry on, to function, to be operative to be actively performing some function In some languages the equivalent of ‘the courts are functioning’ is ‘the courts are open’ or ‘the judges are on duty’ or ‘the officials are listening to complaints.’

[ACT 19:38](https://ref.ly/John19:38)

67\.64 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

to take place, to be, to occur to occur at a particular or expected time For another interpretation of ἄγω in [LUK 24:21](https://ref.ly/Mark24:21), see [67\.79](#67.79).

[LUK 24:21](https://ref.ly/Mark24:21)

67\.79 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

to spend time to experience a duration of time For another interpretation of ἄγω in [LUK 24:21](https://ref.ly/Mark24:21), see [67\.64](#67.64).

[LUK 24:21](https://ref.ly/Mark24:21)

91\.13 · Discourse Markers · Prompters of Attention

look, listen, pay attention, come now, then prompter of attention, which serves also to emphasize the following statement

[JAS 4:13](https://ref.ly/Heb4:13) · [JAS 5:1](https://ref.ly/Heb5:1)

1 ἄγω\[b], in speaking of the relationship of persons to one another, normally means ‘to bring’ or ‘to lead,’ with the implication that the individual who is brought is able to walk or move on his own power, but in some contexts ἄγω\[b] indicates that the person is actually carried, and thus ἄγω\[b] parallels the use of φέρω\[a] ([15\.187](#15.187)) or φορτίζω ([15\.207](#15.207)) in the sense of ‘to carry’ or ‘to bring’ an object.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 10:18; Matthew 21:2; Matthew 21:7; Mark 13:11; Luke 4:9; Luke 4:29; Luke 18:40; Luke 19:27; Luke 19:30; Luke 19:35; Luke 22:54; Luke 23:1; Luke 23:32; John 1:42; John 7:45; John 8:3; John 9:13; John 10:16; John 18:13; John 18:28; John 19:4; John 19:13; Acts 5:21; Acts 5:27; Acts 6:12; Acts 8:32; Acts 9:2; Acts 9:21; Acts 9:27; Acts 11:26; Acts 13:23; Acts 17:15; Acts 17:19; Acts 18:12; Acts 19:37; Acts 20:12; Acts 21:16; Acts 21:34; Acts 22:5; Acts 23:10; Acts 23:18; Acts 23:31; Acts 25:6; Acts 25:17; Acts 25:23; 1 Corinthians 12:2; 1 Thessalonians 4:14; 2 Timothy 4:11; Hebrews 2:10; Luke 4:40; Luke 10:34; 2 Timothy 3:6; Matthew 26:46; Mark 1:38; Mark 14:42; John 11:7; John 11:15; John 11:16; John 14:31; Luke 4:1; Romans 2:4; Romans 8:14; Galatians 5:18; Acts 19:38; Luke 24:21; James 4:13; James 5:1


## ἀγωγή (id: 000072000000000)

ἀγωγή
=====

ἀγωγή (G0072)

noun · ἄγω

41\.3 · Behavior and Related States · Behavior, Conduct

to live, to conduct oneself, to behave, behavior, conduct to conduct oneself, with apparent focus upon overt daily behavior1

[2TI 3:10](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:10)

1 No doubt there are subtle distinctions in meaning between ἀγωγή, διάγω, ἀναστρέφω\[b], and ἀναστροφή, but it is difficult or impossible to determine this from existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Timothy 3:10


## ἀγών (id: 000073000000000)

ἀγών
====

ἀγών (G0073)

noun · ἀγωνίζομαι

39\.29 · Hostility, Strife · Strife, Struggle

to struggle, to fight to engage in intense struggle, involving physical or nonphysical force against strong opposition In a number of languages a marked distinction is made between physical fighting and nonphysical contending with someone. It is therefore often impossible to carry over any terminology involving war or physical struggling to contexts in which one is speaking of interpersonal hostility or contention.

[PHP 1:30](https://ref.ly/Eph1:30) · [COL 2:1](https://ref.ly/Phil2:1) · [1TH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Col2:2) · [2TI 4:7](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:7)

50\.4 · Contests and Play

race a race involving competition and struggle

[1TH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Col2:2) · [1TI 6:12](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:12) · [2TI 4:7](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:7) · [HEB 12:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:1)

* **Associated Passages:** Philippians 1:30; Colossians 2:1; 1 Thessalonians 2:2; 2 Timothy 4:7; 1 Timothy 6:12; Hebrews 12:1

## ἀδελφή (id: 000079000000000)

ἀδελφή
======

ἀδελφή (G0079)

noun · ἀδελφός

10\.50 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations of the Same Generation

sister a female having the same father and mother as the reference person For a discussion of ἀδελφή\[a] in the possible meaning of cousin, see the discussion under ἀδελφός\[a] ([10\.49](#10.49)).\|A number of languages make a distinction in relative age of sisters, paralleling the usage of distinct terms for ‘brother’ (see [10\.49](#10.49)). The order of names in the NT normally suggests relative age, and therefore in speaking of Martha and Mary in such languages, one may designate Mary as the younger sister, since the order is normally first Martha and then Mary, though in [JHN 11:1](https://ref.ly/Luke11:1) the order is reversed.

[MAT 12:50](https://ref.ly/Matt12:50) · [MAT 13:56](https://ref.ly/Matt13:56) · [MAT 19:29](https://ref.ly/Matt19:29) · [MRK 3:35](https://ref.ly/Matt3:35) · [MRK 6:3](https://ref.ly/Matt6:3) · [MRK 10:29](https://ref.ly/Matt10:29) · [MRK 10:30](https://ref.ly/Matt10:30) · [LUK 10:39](https://ref.ly/Mark10:39) · [LUK 10:40](https://ref.ly/Mark10:40) · [LUK 14:26](https://ref.ly/Mark14:26) · [JHN 11:1](https://ref.ly/Luke11:1) · [JHN 11:3](https://ref.ly/Luke11:3) · [JHN 11:5](https://ref.ly/Luke11:5) · [JHN 11:28](https://ref.ly/Luke11:28) · [JHN 11:39](https://ref.ly/Luke11:39) · [JHN 19:25](https://ref.ly/Luke19:25) · [ACT 23:16](https://ref.ly/John23:16) · [ROM 16:15](https://ref.ly/Acts16:15) · [1TI 5:2](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:2) · [2JN 1:13](https://ref.ly/1John1:13)

11\.24 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

fellow believer, sister in Christ, sister in the faith a close female associate of a group with well\-defined membership (in the NT referring specifically to fellow believers in Christ) Though in a number of languages it is possible to use a term meaning ‘sister’ in the sense of a fellow believer, in some languages this cannot be done. As in the case of ἀδελφός\[b] (see [11\.23](#11.23)), it may be possible to use a term such as ‘relative,’ but more often than not, it may be necessary to use a phrase such as ‘one who also believes’ or ‘one who believes in Christ even as we do.’

[ROM 16:1](https://ref.ly/Acts16:1) · [1CO 7:15](https://ref.ly/Rom7:15) · [1CO 9:5](https://ref.ly/Rom9:5) · [PHM 1:2](https://ref.ly/Titus1:2) · [JAS 2:15](https://ref.ly/Heb2:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 12:50; Matthew 13:56; Matthew 19:29; Mark 3:35; Mark 6:3; Mark 10:29; Mark 10:30; Luke 10:39; Luke 10:40; Luke 14:26; John 11:1; John 11:3; John 11:5; John 11:28; John 11:39; John 19:25; Acts 23:16; Romans 16:15; 1 Timothy 5:2; 2 John 1:13; Romans 16:1; 1 Corinthians 7:15; 1 Corinthians 9:5; Philemon 1:2; James 2:15


## ἀδελφός (id: 000080000000000)

ἀδελφός
=======

ἀδελφός (G0080)

noun · ἀδελφή · ἀδελφότης · φιλαδελφία · φιλάδελφος · ψευδάδελφος

10\.49 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations of the Same Generation

brother a male having the same father (and, usually, mother) as the reference person In a number of languages it is necessary to indicate the difference between older and younger brothers, and this can usually be done on the basis of Semitic usage, since the older brother was normally named first. Therefore, in [MAT 4:18](https://ref.ly/Matt4:18) one may translate ‘Simon and his younger brother Andrew.’\|The interpretation of ἀδελφός\[a] in such passages as [MAT 12:46](https://ref.ly/Matt12:46) [MRK 3:31](https://ref.ly/Matt3:31) [JHN 2:12](https://ref.ly/Luke2:12) as meaning cousins (on the basis of a corresponding Hebrew term, which is used in certain cases to designate masculine relatives of various degrees) is not attested in Greek nor affirmed in the Greek\-English lexicon edited by Arndt, Gingrich, and Danker. Such an interpretation depends primarily on ecclesiastical tradition.\|Though the plural of ἀδελφός\[a] (namely, ἀδελφοί) can mean both brothers and sisters, there is no reason to believe that in [MAT 12:46](https://ref.ly/Matt12:46) [MRK 3:31](https://ref.ly/Matt3:31) [JHN 2:12](https://ref.ly/Luke2:12) [JHN 7:3](https://ref.ly/Luke7:3) [JHN 7:5](https://ref.ly/Luke7:5) [ACT 1:14](https://ref.ly/John1:14) the reference is to both brothers and sisters.\|In [LUK 8:21](https://ref.ly/Mark8:21) (μήτηρ μου καὶ ἀδελφοί μου οὗτοί εἰσιν οἱ τὸν λόγον τοῦ θεοῦ ἀκούοντες καὶ ποιοῦντες my mother and my brothers are those who hear the word of God and act accordingly) ἀδελφοί is used figuratively as a qualification of Jesus followers. In some languages, however, it is necessary to make more evident the qualifying relationship, for example, those who hear the word and act accordingly are my mother and my brothers or ... are just like a mother and brothers to me.

2:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:12) · [1JN 3:12](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:12) · [1JN 3:12](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:12) · [JUD 1:1](https://ref.ly/3John1:1)

11\.23 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

fellow believer, (Christian) brother a close associate of a group of persons having a well\-defined membership (in the NT ἀδελφός\[b] refers specifically to fellow believers in Christ) The masculine form ἀδελφός\[b] may include both men and women, but see also [11\.24](#11.24).\|Though in a number of languages it is possible to use a corresponding term meaning ‘brother’ or ‘brothers’ in the sense of fellow believers, in some languages this cannot be done, and one must employ other types of expressions. In some instances it is possible to generalize a term meaning ‘relative’ and therefore to address or to speak of fellow Christians as ‘relatives’ rather than specifically ‘brothers and sisters.’ In most instances, however, one may only employ a phrase such as ‘those who also believe’ or ‘those who believe in Christ even as we do.’

19:10](https://ref.ly/Jude19:10)

11\.25 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

brother, fellow countryman, fellow Jew, associate a person belonging to the same socio\-religious entity and being of the same age group as the so\-called reference person

[MAT 5:22](https://ref.ly/Matt5:22) · [MAT 5:22](https://ref.ly/Matt5:22) · [MAT 5:23](https://ref.ly/Matt5:23) · [MAT 5:24](https://ref.ly/Matt5:24) · [MAT 5:47](https://ref.ly/Matt5:47) · [ACT 2:37](https://ref.ly/John2:37) · [ACT 7:2](https://ref.ly/John7:2) · [ACT 7:25](https://ref.ly/John7:25) · [ACT 7:26](https://ref.ly/John7:26) · [ACT 7:37](https://ref.ly/John7:37) · [ACT 9:17](https://ref.ly/John9:17) · [ACT 13:15](https://ref.ly/John13:15) · [ACT 13:26](https://ref.ly/John13:26) · [ACT 13:38](https://ref.ly/John13:38) · [ACT 22:1](https://ref.ly/John22:1)1 · [ACT 22:5](https://ref.ly/John22:5) · [ACT 23:1](https://ref.ly/John23:1) · [ACT 23:5](https://ref.ly/John23:5) · [ACT 23:6](https://ref.ly/John23:6) · [ACT 28:17](https://ref.ly/John28:17) · [ACT 28:21](https://ref.ly/John28:21) · [ROM 9:3](https://ref.ly/Acts9:3) · [HEB 7:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:5) · [REV 22:9](https://ref.ly/Jude22:9)

11\.57 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

fellow countryman a person who is a member of the same πατριά\[b] or φυλή\[b] ‘nation’ ([11\.56](#11.56)) The term ἄνδρες is often added to ἀδελφοί\[d], constituting a lexical unit having essentially the same meaning as ἀδελφοί\[d], but with the connotation of greater formality: ἄνδρες ἀδελφοί, ἐξὸν εἰπεῖν μετὰ παρρησίας πρὸς ὑμᾶς ‘fellow countrymen, I can speak to you quite plainly’ [ACT 2:29](https://ref.ly/John2:29).\|συμφυλέτης\[b], and ἀδελφός\[d] may often be translated by a phrase, for example, ‘a person of the same country’ or ‘a man who belongs to the same nation’ or ‘one who is part of the same tribe.’

[MAT 23:8](https://ref.ly/Matt23:8) · [ACT 2:29](https://ref.ly/John2:29) · [ACT 3:17](https://ref.ly/John3:17) · [ACT 3:22](https://ref.ly/John3:22) · [ACT 7:23](https://ref.ly/John7:23) · [HEB 8:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:11)

11\.89 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

neighbor, brother a person who lives close beside others and who thus by implication is a part of a so\-called in\-group, that is, the group with which an individual identifies both ethnically and culturally Though in [MAT 5:22](https://ref.ly/Matt5:22) and [LUK 6:41](https://ref.ly/Mark6:41) one may translate ἀδελφός as ‘brother,’ the evident meaning is not a reference to a sibling, but to a close associate or neighbor, so that the denotation of ἀδελφός is very similar to that of γείτων, περίοικος, and πλησίον\[b].\|It may be that of the three terms γείτων, περίοικος, and πλησίον\[b], the last is somewhat broader in meaning. At least πλησίον is used in [LUK 10:27](https://ref.ly/Mark10:27) in a somewhat extended sense of ‘fellow man,’ while ἀδελφός\[e] probably suggests a more intimate or close relationship.

[MAT 5:22](https://ref.ly/Matt5:22) · [MAT 5:22](https://ref.ly/Matt5:22) · [MAT 7:3](https://ref.ly/Matt7:3) · [MAT 7:4](https://ref.ly/Matt7:4) · [MAT 7:5](https://ref.ly/Matt7:5) · [MAT 18:15](https://ref.ly/Matt18:15) · [MAT 18:15](https://ref.ly/Matt18:15) · [MAT 18:21](https://ref.ly/Matt18:21) · [MAT 18:35](https://ref.ly/Matt18:35) · [LUK 6:41](https://ref.ly/Mark6:41) · [LUK 6:42](https://ref.ly/Mark6:42) · [LUK 6:42](https://ref.ly/Mark6:42) · [LUK 6:42](https://ref.ly/Mark6:42) · [LUK 17:3](https://ref.ly/Mark17:3) · [HEB 8:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:11) · [1JN 3:15](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:15)

1 ACT 22:1 In [ACT 22:1](https://ref.ly/John22:1), the use of ἀδελφοί focuses more upon identification of the speaker with the persons addressed, while πατέρες implies respect accorded those representing an older generation.

## ἀδελφότης (id: 000081000000000)

ἀδελφότης
=========

ἀδελφότης (G0081)

noun · ἀδελφός

11\.22 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

brotherhood, fellow believers an association of persons having a strong sense of unity It is often quite impossible to speak of fellow Christians as ‘the brothers and sisters,’ since a number of receptor languages do not permit this type of extension of meaning from an underlying base meaning ‘brother’ or ‘sister.’ Therefore, it may be necessary to speak of ‘fellow believers’ or ‘those who believe together with others’ or ‘those who likewise believe.’ See also discussion at [11\.23](#11.23).

[1PE 2:17](https://ref.ly/Jas2:17) · [1PE 5:9](https://ref.ly/Jas5:9)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Peter 2:17; 1 Peter 5:9


## ἄδηλος (id: 000082000000000)

ἄδηλος
======

ἄδηλος (G0082)

adj

24\.95 · Sensory Events and States · General Sensory Perception

not evident, not perceived, unseen, unmarked pertaining to not being perceived Though in Greek ἄδηλος may designate anything which is unperceived by any of the senses, in [LUK 11:44](https://ref.ly/Mark11:44) the most evident sense involved would be sight, and therefore one may translate ‘because you are like graves that cannot be seen.’

[LUK 11:44](https://ref.ly/Mark11:44) · [1CO 14:8](https://ref.ly/Rom14:8)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 11:44; 1 Corinthians 14:8

ἀδηλότης

ἀδηλότης (G0083)

noun

uncertainty the uncertainty of some event or state

[1TI 6:17](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:17)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Timothy 6:17

ἀδήλως
======

ἀδήλως (G0084)

adv

30\.65 · Think · To Intend, To Purpose, To Plan

without purpose, unintentionally, aimlessly pertaining to being without a special goal or purpose In a number of languages ‘aimlessly’ may be rendered simply as ‘without having some goal’ or ‘without some reason’ or ‘without trying to accomplish something.’

[1CO 9:26](https://ref.ly/Rom9:26)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 9:26

to be troubled, to be upset, to be distressed to be distressed and troubled, with the probable implication of anguish

[MAT 26:37](https://ref.ly/Matt26:37) · [MRK 14:33](https://ref.ly/Matt14:33) · [PHP 2:26](https://ref.ly/Eph2:26)

## ᾅδης (id: 000086000000000)

ᾅδης
====

ᾅδης (G0086)

noun

1\.19 · Geographical Objects and Features · Regions Below the Surface of the Earth

the world of the dead, Hades a place or abode of the dead, including both the righteous and the unrighteous (in most contexts ᾅδης\[a] is equivalent to the Hebrew term Sheol) There are several problems involved in rendering ᾅδης\[a] as world of the dead, since in some languages this may be interpreted as suggesting that there are two different earths, one for the living and another for the dead. In such cases, ᾅδης\[a] may be more satisfactorily rendered as ‘where the dead are’ or ‘where the dead remain.’\|In [LUK 16:23](https://ref.ly/Mark16:23) ᾅδης\[a] obviously involves torment and punishment. These aspects are important supplementary features of the word ᾅδης\[a] but are not integral elements of the meaning. In [LUK 16:23](https://ref.ly/Mark16:23), however, it may be appropriate to use a term which is equivalent to Greek γέεννα meaning hell (see [1\.21](#1.21)). It is indeed possible that in addressing a GrecoRoman audience Luke would have used ᾅδης in a context implying punishment and torment, since this was a typical Greco\-Roman view of the next world. But since Luke also uses γέεννα, as in [LUK 12:5](https://ref.ly/Mark12:5), it is possible that the choice of ᾅδης in [LUK 16:23](https://ref.ly/Mark16:23) reflects Luke’s intent to emphasize the fact that ᾅδης\[a] includes both the unrighteous and the righteous.

[MAT 11:23](https://ref.ly/Matt11:23) · [LUK 10:15](https://ref.ly/Mark10:15) · [LUK 16:23](https://ref.ly/Mark16:23) · [ACT 2:27](https://ref.ly/John2:27) · [ACT 2:31](https://ref.ly/John2:31) · [REV 1:18](https://ref.ly/Jude1:18) · [REV 20:13](https://ref.ly/Jude20:13) · [REV 20:14](https://ref.ly/Jude20:14)

23\.108 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

death, power of death (a figurative extension of meaning of ᾅδης\[a] Hades, [1\.19](#1.19), as a personification of the power of Hades as the place of the dead) In [1CO 15:55](https://ref.ly/Rom15:55) ᾅδη is a variant reading in the apparatus of θάνατε ‘death’ ([23\.99](#23.99)).\|In some languages it is extremely difficult to personify death except as a reference to some demon which may cause death, but this, of course, is not the reference in this passage. The closest equivalent in some languages is ‘how can death be victorious?’ or ‘death cannot be victorious.’ In some instances it may be necessary to represent the personification as ‘you who cause death,’ and thus this expression in [1CO 15:55](https://ref.ly/Rom15:55) may be translated as ‘you who cause death will not always be victorious’ or ‘... will not in the end be victorious.’

[REV 6:8](https://ref.ly/Jude6:8)

12\.50 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Powers

**Collocation:** πύλαι ᾅδου

death (an idiom, literally: gates of Hades) death as an impersonal supernatural power Some scholars, however, understand πύλαι ᾅδου to mean Satanic powers of evil.

[MAT 16:18](https://ref.ly/Matt16:18)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 11:23; Luke 10:15; Luke 16:23; Acts 2:27; Acts 2:31; Revelation 1:18; Revelation 20:13; Revelation 20:14; Revelation 6:8; Matthew 16:18

## ἀδικέω (id: 000090000000000)

ἀδικέω
======

ἀδικέω (G0091)

to harm, to hurt to hurt or to harm, with the implication of doing something which is wrong and undeserved

## ἀδύνατος (id: 000102000000000)

ἀδύνατος
========

ἀδύνατος (G0102)

adj

74\.22 · Able, Capable

incapable, not being able pertaining to not being able to do or experience something In [ROM 15:1](https://ref.ly/Acts15:1) οἱ δυνατοί refers to capability with respect to faith and τῶν ἀδυνάτων refers, conversely, to those who are incapable with respect to faith. In some languages, therefore, it may be possible to use a similar biblical phrase, weak in the faith.\|In many languages the appropriate equivalent of [ACT 14:8](https://ref.ly/John14:8) would be but in Lystra there was a man who was crippled.

[ACT 14:8](https://ref.ly/John14:8) · [ROM 15:1](https://ref.ly/Acts15:1)

71\.3 · Mode · Possible, Impossible

impossible pertaining to being impossible, presumably because of a lack of power to alter or control circumstances In [HEB 6:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:4) the use of ἀδύνατον seems to be an instance of hyperbole in view of the warnings against apostasy (see [HEB 5:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:11)\-[HEB 6:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:12)). Therefore, one may translate ἀδύνατον in [HEB 6:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:4) as ‘it is extremely difficult to.’ TEV introduces a rhetorical question to express strong doubt, “for how can those who abandon their faith be brought back to repent again?”

[MAT 19:26](https://ref.ly/Matt19:26) · [MRK 10:27](https://ref.ly/Matt10:27) · [LUK 18:27](https://ref.ly/Mark18:27) · [ROM 8:3](https://ref.ly/Acts8:3) · [HEB 6:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:4) · [HEB 6:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:18) · [HEB 10:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:4) · [HEB 11:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:6)

1 This statement in [HEB 6:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:18) may provide certain philosophical problems in view of the doctrine of the absoluteness of God, but no doubt the writer of Hebrews makes this statement in view of the presumed nature of God himself.

## ἀεί (id: 000104000000000)

ἀεί
===

ἀεί (G0104)

adv · ἀΐδιος

67\.86 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

always, constantly, continually duration of time, either continuous or episodic, but without limits In some languages there may be problems involved in rendering ἀεί or διὰ παντός\[b], since there may be a basic distinction between (a) continuous activity and (b) activity which may be defined as episodic in that it regularly recurs in related episodes. For example, in [2CO 6:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:10) the fact of being glad may be related specifically to the occasions of being saddened, while in [MAT 18:10](https://ref.ly/Matt18:10) the focus of meaning is probably upon the continuous nature of the relation.

[ACT 7:51](https://ref.ly/John7:51) · [2CO 4:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:11) · [2CO 6:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:10) · [TIT 1:12](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:12) · [HEB 3:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:10) · [1PE 3:15](https://ref.ly/Jas3:15) · [2PE 1:12](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:12)

## ἀετός (id: 000105000000000)

ἀετός
=====

ἀετός (G0105)

noun

4\.42 · Animals · Birds

eagle, vulture generic word for a large bird of prey; genus Aquila, but may include others, esp. vulture In [REV 12:14](https://ref.ly/Jude12:14) the emphasis is upon strength and speed, and therefore a term meaning ‘eagle’ is probably more satisfactory, but in [MAT 24:28](https://ref.ly/Matt24:28) (and the parallel passages in [LUK 17:37](https://ref.ly/Mark17:37)) the reference is to the eating of dead flesh, and therefore a word meaning ‘vultures’ is more appropriate.\|The basic distinction between eagles and vultures is that the former either capture their prey or feed upon dead carcasses, while vultures only feed upon dead carcasses. Only in the Western Hemisphere are there two distinct families of birds: (1\) birds of prey, which also feed upon dead bodies (eagles) and (2\) vultures, which never take live prey, but only feed upon carcasses.

[MAT 24:28](https://ref.ly/Matt24:28) · [LUK 17:37](https://ref.ly/Mark17:37) · [REV 4:7](https://ref.ly/Jude4:7) · [REV 8:13](https://ref.ly/Jude8:13) · [REV 12:14](https://ref.ly/Jude12:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 24:28; Luke 17:37; Revelation 4:7; Revelation 8:13; Revelation 12:14


## ἄζυμος (id: 000106000000000)

ἄζυμος
======

ἄζυμος (G0106)

adj

5\.13 · Foods and Condiments · Food

without yeast, not having yeast, unleavened pertaining to the absence of yeast The phrase ‘unleavened bread’ may be rendered as ‘bread made without yeast’ or ‘bread which does not rise.’ Perhaps a more difficult problem is involved in the relationship of ‘days’ to ‘unleavened bread,’ and accordingly, the expression ‘the days of Unleavened Bread’ may be rendered as ‘the days when people ate bread that had no yeast.’ Except for [1CO 5:7](https://ref.ly/Rom5:7), in which ἄζυμος occurs in a highly figurative passage referring to pure and true life, this term is used exclusively in reference to the feast or festival of Unleavened Bread.

[MAT 26:17](https://ref.ly/Matt26:17) · [MRK 14:1](https://ref.ly/Matt14:1) · [MRK 14:12](https://ref.ly/Matt14:12) · [LUK 22:1](https://ref.ly/Mark22:1) · [LUK 22:7](https://ref.ly/Mark22:7) · [ACT 12:3](https://ref.ly/John12:3) · [ACT 20:6](https://ref.ly/John20:6) · [1CO 5:7](https://ref.ly/Rom5:7) · [1CO 5:8](https://ref.ly/Rom5:8)

## ἀήρ (id: 000109000000000)

ἀήρ
===

ἀήρ (G0109)

noun

1\.6 · Geographical Objects and Features · Regions Above the Earth

air the space immediately above the earths surface, and not including the dome arching over the earth The expression εἰς ἀέρα λαλοῦντες, literally ‘talking into the air,’ may be regarded as a type of idiom meaning ‘talking to no purpose’ or ‘talking without anyone understanding.’ Accordingly, ἔσεσθε γὰρ εἰς ἀέρα λαλοῦντες may be rendered as ‘you will be talking, but no one will understand’ or ‘you will be talking, but your words will not enter anyone.’ In [ACT 22:23](https://ref.ly/John22:23), κονιορτὸν βαλλόντων εἰς τὸν ἀέρα ‘throwing dust into the air,’ it may be more appropriate in a number of languages to say ‘throwing dust above themselves’ or simply ‘throwing dust up.’ A literal rendering of ‘throwing dust into the air’ might suggest in some languages that the air was some kind of a container in which the dust remained.\|The Greek term ἀήρ in [REV 9:2](https://ref.ly/Jude9:2) (ἐσκοτώθη ὁ ἥλιος καὶ ὁ ἀὴρ ἐκ τοῦ καπνοῦ τοῦ φρέατος ‘the sun and the air were darkened by the smoke from the pit’) may very well be translated as ‘air,’ but since it is a reference primarily to space rather than to substance, it is more natural in a number of languages to say ‘the sky was darkened by the smoke from the pit.’

[ACT 22:23](https://ref.ly/John22:23) · [1CO 14:9](https://ref.ly/Rom14:9) · [1TH 4:17](https://ref.ly/Col4:17) · [REV 9:2](https://ref.ly/Jude9:2) · [REV 16:17](https://ref.ly/Jude16:17)

2\.2 · Natural Substances · Air

air air as an elemental substance οὕτως πυκτεύω ὡς οὐκ ἀέρα δέρων ‘I do not box like someone hitting the air’ [1CO 9:26](https://ref.ly/Rom9:26). In some languages it may be quite impossible to translate literally ‘hitting the air,’ for since the air is not regarded as a kind of material substance, it is impossible to hit it. The closest equivalent may be ‘hitting nothing’ or possibly ‘as though hitting something.’

[1CO 9:26](https://ref.ly/Rom9:26)

1\.7 · Geographical Objects and Features · Regions Above the Earth

air, sky the space above the earth inhabited by and under the control of certain supernatural powers1 In the context of [EPH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Gal2:2) ἐξουσία is best understood as a collective and thus referring to the various supernatural powers regarded as inhabiting the area above the earth and thus controlling in many respects both the behavior and the fate of people. See also [12\.44](#12.44).

[EPH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Gal2:2)

12\.44 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Powers

**Collocation:** ἄρχων τῆς ἐξουσίας τοῦ ἀέρος

power, authority, lordship, ruler, wicked force (title for supernatural forces and powers) a supernatural power having some particular role in controlling the destiny and activities of human beings Some scholars have believed that it is possible to reconstruct at least in part some of the hierarchy represented by these various supernatural forces and powers, on the basis of the neoplatonic system of nine such powers arranged in three orders of three each. NT terminology and usage does not, however, lend itself to such a classification, and it is difficult, if not impossible, to determine what are the significant differences between these supernatural powers and forces.\|For the most part, translators have endeavored to render the various terms in somewhat different ways, for example, ἐξουσία as ‘authority’; δύναμις as ‘power’; κυριότης as ‘ruler’; and phrases such as τὰ πνευματικὰ τῆς πονηρίας ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις as ‘wicked spiritual forces in the heavenly world.’ In a number of languages, however, there are simply not enough terms to designate various kinds of powers, authorities, and forces which could be employed in speaking of such supernatural impersonal powers. Accordingly, some translators have simply used a phrase such as ‘any and all kinds of supernatural powers.’ Where it is possible to make certain lexical distinctions between terms and phrases, this, of course, should be done, but in a number of contexts this may be impossible, and accordingly, some explanation in a marginal note may be required if the reader is to understand satisfactorily the intent of the passages in question.\|In some instances a measure of meaning may be obtained by somewhat paraphrastic expressions. For example, [EPH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Gal2:2) is rendered in some languages as ‘at that time you lived in the way in which the spirit that governs this world dictated you should live. This is the way the one who rules over the power in space commanded you to live. This is the very spirit that now commands the people who disobey God.’

[EPH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Gal2:2)

1 It would be possible to combine the meanings of ἀήρ\[a] ([1\.6](#1.6)) and ἀήρ\[c] ([1\.7](#1.7)) under a single generic definition of space above the earth. One could then leave it to the context to specify the particular area referred to. However, because of the relationship of ἀήρ\[a] to the meaning of οὐρανός\[a] ([1\.5](#1.5)) and the fact that in so many languages a distinct contrast in meaning is preserved for expressions designating the sky in contrast with the area immediately above the surface of the earth, it seems better to make a distinction at this point.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 22:23; 1 Corinthians 14:9; 1 Thessalonians 4:17; Revelation 9:2; Revelation 16:17; 1 Corinthians 9:26; Ephesians 2:2

1 Though ἀθέμιτος may be regarded as meaning literally ‘pertaining to what is not allowed’ ([13\.144](#13.144)), it acquires by association not only the meaning of lawless (a meaning which is possibly applicable to [1PE 4:3](https://ref.ly/Jas4:3)) but also disgusting or wanton in connection with idol worship. Such a semantic development is typical of what often happens when meanings shift from an objective to a subjective orientation.

## αἷμα (id: 000130000000000)

αἷμα
====

αἷμα (G0129)

noun · αἱματεκχυσία · αἱμορροέω

8\.64 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

blood red liquid that circulates in the arteries and veins of humans and animals In [MRK 5:25](https://ref.ly/Matt5:25) it may be important to use a special term for blood, since this passage is a reference to the hemorrhaging of menstruation.\|In [JHN 19:34](https://ref.ly/Luke19:34) (ἐξῆλθεν εὐθὺς αἷμα καὶ ὕδωρ ‘then blood and water came out’) it may be necessary to use a term which can refer to the lymph fluid rather than to water itself; otherwise the reader might understand the water as being urine (with the spear having pierced the bladder) or even possibly water that Jesus had presumably drunk and was still in his stomach. The real reference of ὕδωρ in [JHN 19:34](https://ref.ly/Luke19:34) is to the lymph fluid and not to water as such. It is simply a colorless fluid which was mixed with blood.\|In [REV 8:8](https://ref.ly/Jude8:8) (καὶ ἐγένετο τὸ τρίτον τῆς θαλάσσης αἷμα ‘and a third of the sea became blood’) and [REV 11:6](https://ref.ly/Jude11:6) (καὶ ἐξουσίαν ἔχουσιν ἐπὶ τῶν ὑδάτων στρέφειν αὐτὰ εἰς αἷμα ‘and they had power over the waters to turn them into blood’) the reference may not be to blood as a substance but to the blood\-like color of the objects in question.

12:11](https://ref.ly/Jude12:11) · [REV 14:20](https://ref.ly/Jude14:20) · [REV 16:3](https://ref.ly/Jude16:3) · [REV 16:4](https://ref.ly/Jude16:4) · [REV 16:6](https://ref.ly/Jude16:6) · [REV 16:6](https://ref.ly/Jude16:6) · [REV 17:6](https://ref.ly/Jude17:6) · [REV 17:6](https://ref.ly/Jude17:6) · [REV 18:24](https://ref.ly/Jude18:24) · [REV 19:13](https://ref.ly/Jude19:13)

23\.107 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

death, violent death (a figurative extension of meaning of αἷμα\[a] ‘blood,’ [8\.64](#8.64)) the death of a person, generally as the result of violence or execution It is possible to interpret αἷμα in [ROM 5:9](https://ref.ly/Acts5:9) (δικαιωθέντες νῦν ἐν τῷ αἵματι αὐτοῦ ‘by his death we are put right with God’) as primarily a reference to physiological death. But in such contexts in which αἷμα is used in speaking of the death of Christ, there is no doubt an additional component derived from the occurrence of αἷμα in contexts speaking of atoning sacrifice. Therefore, one may analyze the meaning of αἷμα in such contexts as meaning ‘sacrificial death’ and not merely physiological death. In [ROM 5:9](https://ref.ly/Acts5:9) and similar contexts, a number of translators have preferred to preserve an often recurring phrase such as ‘the shedding of his blood.’ However, in English this can be somewhat ambiguous in that ‘the shedding of blood’ normally refers to the killing of someone else and not to experiencing death. See also [23\.112](#23.112).
