20\.83 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Kill

to kill, killing, to commit murder (a figurative extension of meaning of αἷμα\[a] ‘blood,’ [8\.64](#8.64)) to deprive a person of life by violent means

[MAT 23:30](https://ref.ly/Matt23:30) · [MAT 27:6](https://ref.ly/Matt27:6) · [MAT 27:8](https://ref.ly/Matt27:8) · [MAT 27:24](https://ref.ly/Matt27:24) · [REV 6:10](https://ref.ly/Jude6:10) · [REV 19:2](https://ref.ly/Jude19:2)

20\.84 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Kill

**Collocation:** αἷμα ἐκχέω · αἷμα ἐκχύννω

to murder, to kill (an idiom, literally: to pour out blood) to cause the death of someone by violent means The phrase πᾶν αἷμα δίκαιον ἐκχυννόμενον in [MAT 23:35](https://ref.ly/Matt23:35) may be literally translated as ‘all innocent blood that is poured out.’

[MAT 23:35](https://ref.ly/Matt23:35) · [ROM 3:15](https://ref.ly/Acts3:15)

23\.112 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

**Collocation:** ἐκχύννεται τὸ αἷμα

to die as a sacrifice, sacrificial death (an idiom, literally: the blood pours out) to die, with the implication of a sacrificial purpose The relations between the various elements in this clause in [MAT 26:28](https://ref.ly/Matt26:28) must in some instances be made somewhat more specific, for example, ‘by my dying, I am like a sacrifice for many for the purpose of their sins being forgiven.’ See also [23\.107](#23.107).

[MAT 26:28](https://ref.ly/Matt26:28) · [MRK 14:24](https://ref.ly/Matt14:24) · [LUK 11:50](https://ref.ly/Mark11:50) · [LUK 22:20](https://ref.ly/Mark22:20) · [ACT 22:20](https://ref.ly/John22:20)

23\.182 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

**Collocation:** πηγὴ αἵματος · ῥύσις αἵματος

loss of blood, menstrual flow, bleeding (set phrases, literally: fountain of blood, and: flow of blood, respectively) the loss of blood through menstrual bleeding1

[MRK 5:25](https://ref.ly/Matt5:25) · [MRK 5:29](https://ref.ly/Matt5:29) · [LUK 8:43](https://ref.ly/Mark8:43) · [LUK 8:44](https://ref.ly/Mark8:44)

9\.14 · People · Human Beings

**Collocation:** σὰρξ καὶ αἷμα

person, human being (an idiom, literally: flesh and blood) a human being in contrast with a divine being

[MAT 16:17](https://ref.ly/Matt16:17) · [1CO 15:50](https://ref.ly/Rom15:50) · [GAL 1:16](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:16) · [EPH 6:12](https://ref.ly/Gal6:12)

9\.15 · People · Human Beings

**Collocation:** κοινωνέω αἵματος καὶ σαρκός

to be a person, to be a physical being (an idiom, literally: to share blood and flesh) to have the characteristics and nature of a human being

[HEB 2:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:14)

1 The expressions in [23\.181](#23.181) and [23\.182](#23.182) all refer to a menstrual flow of blood, but the phrases πηγὴ αἵματος and ῥύσις αἵματος seem to be restricted primarily to this meaning, while αἱμορροέω in non\-biblical contexts means simply to suffer a loss of blood or to bleed, but it may, of course, refer to menstrual bleeding. Because of the wider range of reference in αἱμορροέω, this term has been listed separately.

## αἱματεκχυσία (id: 000131000000000)

αἱματεκχυσία
============

αἱματεκχυσία (G0130)

noun

23\.183 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

to cause bleeding, to cause blood to flow, the flow of blood the process of causing blood to flow out It may be important in translating [HEB 9:22](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:22) to introduce some statement with regard to sacrifice, for example, ‘if the blood of the sacrifice did not flow’ or ‘if there was no sacrifice involving blood’ or simply ‘if there was no sacrifice,’ but because of the symbolic value of ‘blood’ in the ritual, some reference to blood should be retained.

[HEB 9:22](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:22)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 9:22


## αἱμορροέω (id: 000132000000000)

αἱμορροέω
=========

αἱμορροέω (G0131)

23\.181 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

to bleed to experience or suffer a loss of blood1

[MAT 9:20](https://ref.ly/Matt9:20)

1 The expressions in [23\.181](#23.181) and [23\.182](#23.182) all refer to a menstrual flow of blood, but the phrases πηγὴ αἵματος and ῥύσις αἵματος seem to be restricted primarily to this meaning, while αἱμορροέω in non\-biblical contexts means simply to suffer a loss of blood or to bleed, but it may, of course, refer to menstrual bleeding. Because of the wider range of reference in αἱμορροέω, this term has been listed separately.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 9:20

## αἴνεσις (id: 000134000000000)

αἴνεσις
=======

αἴνεσις (G0133)

noun · αἰνέω

33\.354 · Communication · Praise

to praise, praise to speak of the excellence of a person, object, or event In a number of languages praise can only be expressed by direct discourse, and this requires some content which provides the basis for praise. For example, in [LUK 2:13](https://ref.ly/Mark2:13) instead of a literal rendering of ‘praising God,’ it may be necessary to have ‘they said, God is wonderful,’ and in [LUK 16:8](https://ref.ly/Mark16:8) it may be necessary to translate ‘the master of this dishonest manager said, You are remarkable for having done such a clever thing.’

[HEB 13:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:15)

1 LUK 18:43 · HEB 13:15 The verbs δίδωμι and ἀναφέρω in [LUK 18:43](https://ref.ly/Mark18:43) and [HEB 13:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:15), respectively, serve as what might be called empty verbs, since the semantic content exists in the nouns αἶνος and αἴνεσις. The primary function of δίδωμι and ἀναφέρω in such contexts is to serve as a vehicle for marking case relations (see Domain 90 Case). The same applies to δίδωμι in [LUK 17:18](https://ref.ly/Mark17:18) (see [33\.357](#33.357)).

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 13:15

=======

noun · αἰνίσσομαι

32\.21 · Understand · Ease or Difficulty in Understanding

that which is puzzling, that which is difficult to understand, puzzle, riddle that which is difficult or impossible to understand In [1CO 13:12](https://ref.ly/Rom13:12) the term αἴνιγμα may be interpreted as lack of clarity in perception, so that the meaning would be ‘that which is difficult to see clearly,’ but the general usage of αἴνιγμα would seem to point to the meaning of difficulty in understanding and comprehension rather than in visual perception. ## αἵρεσις (id: 000139000000000)

αἵρεσις
=======

αἵρεσις (G0139)

noun · αἱρέομαι

11\.50 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

religious party, sect a division or group based upon different doctrinal opinions and/or loyalties and hence by implication in certain contexts an unjustified party or group (applicable in the NT to religious parties) Compare αἵρεσις\[b] in [33\.241](#33.241) where it means the content of false doctrines or teaching.\|It is often difficult to find a term in a receptor language which designates primarily a religious party. Normally one must employ some type of phrase in which the various components of αἵρεσις\[a] are somewhat redistributed. For example, in [ACT 26:5](https://ref.ly/John26:5) the strictest party of our religion may be rendered as ‘those who have the strictest rules about the way they should worship God.’ Similarly, the wrong kind of religious party in [ACT 24:14](https://ref.ly/John24:14) may be rendered as ‘the group of those who worship God in the wrong way.’

[ACT 5:17](https://ref.ly/John5:17) · [ACT 15:5](https://ref.ly/John15:5) · [ACT 24:5](https://ref.ly/John24:5) · [ACT 24:14](https://ref.ly/John24:14) · [ACT 26:5](https://ref.ly/John26:5) · [ACT 28:22](https://ref.ly/John28:22)

false teaching, untrue doctrine, heresy the content of teaching which is not true

division, separate group a division of people into different and opposing sets

**Collocation:** αἴρω τὸν σταυρόν

to be prepared to suffer even unto death, to take up one’s cross (a productive idiomatic expression, literally: to take up one’s cross) to be prepared to endure severe suffering, even to the point of death The idiom ‘to carry one’s cross’ or ‘to take up one’s cross’ involves a number of important features of meaning. Unfortunately, this expression is frequently not understood correctly by many people who hear it or even use it. One reason for this is the actual absence of crucifixion in modern society. If a literal translation is to be employed, then some marginal note will probably be necessary in order to indicate clearly the full implications of such a statement.

=========

αἰσχρότης (G0151)

noun · αἰσχύνομαι

88\.149 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Act Shamefully

to act shamefully, indecent behavior, shameful deed to act in defiance of social and moral standards, with resulting disgrace, embarrassment, and shame Behavior involving disgrace, embarrassment, and shame is often expressed in an idiomatic manner, for example, ‘to bury one’s face,’ ‘to hide one’s eyes,’ or ‘to feel stabbed by people’s eyes.’ Since shame is seemingly a universal type of feeling, there is usually no difficulty involved in obtaining a satisfactory equivalent.

## αἰσχύνη (id: 000152000000000)

αἰσχύνη
=======

αἰσχύνη (G0152)

noun · αἰσχύνομαι

25\.189 · Attitudes and Emotions · Shame, Disgrace, Humiliation

shame, disgrace a painful feeling due to the consciousness of having done or experienced something disgraceful In some languages ‘shame’ may be referred to by a descriptive expression, for example, ‘the feeling of being caught doing something bad’ or ‘the feeling of being seen while sinning.’ In other languages ‘shame’ is expressed idiomatically as ‘to hang the head’ or ‘to turn away the eyes’ or ‘to hide from people’s stares.’

[2CO 4:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:2) · [PHP 3:19](https://ref.ly/Eph3:19) · [REV 3:18](https://ref.ly/Jude3:18)

88\.149 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Act Shamefully

to act shamefully, indecent behavior, shameful deed to act in defiance of social and moral standards, with resulting disgrace, embarrassment, and shame Behavior involving disgrace, embarrassment, and shame is often expressed in an idiomatic manner, for example, ‘to bury one’s face,’ ‘to hide one’s eyes,’ or ‘to feel stabbed by people’s eyes.’ Since shame is seemingly a universal type of feeling, there is usually no difficulty involved in obtaining a satisfactory equivalent.

[JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 14:9; Hebrews 12:2; 2 Corinthians 4:2; Philippians 3:19; Revelation 3:18; Jude 1:13

## αἰτία (id: 000156000000000)

αἰτία
=====

αἰτία (G0156)

noun · αἴτιος

89\.15 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

reason, cause, source, because of reason or cause for an event or state

[MAT 19:3](https://ref.ly/Matt19:3) · [LUK 8:47](https://ref.ly/Mark8:47) · [ACT 10:21](https://ref.ly/John10:21) · [ACT 22:24](https://ref.ly/John22:24) · [ACT 28:20](https://ref.ly/John28:20) · [2TI 1:6](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:6) · [2TI 1:12](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:12) · [TIT 1:13](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:13) · [HEB 2:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:11)

56\.4 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Accusation

cause, case, basis for an accusation (a technical, legal term) the basis of or grounds for an accusation in court In rendering αἰτία\[b] as basis for an accusation, one may sometimes speak of a reason for accusing someone or why someone should be accused of something bad.\|For another interpretation of αἰτία in [JHN 18:38](https://ref.ly/Luke18:38), see [88\.315](#88.315).

[JHN 18:38](https://ref.ly/Luke18:38) · [JHN 19:4](https://ref.ly/Luke19:4) · [JHN 19:6](https://ref.ly/Luke19:6) · [ACT 13:28](https://ref.ly/John13:28) · [ACT 23:28](https://ref.ly/John23:28) · [ACT 28:18](https://ref.ly/John28:18)

56\.5 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Accusation

accusation, charge, complaint the content of legal charges brought against someone

[MAT 27:37](https://ref.ly/Matt27:37) · [MRK 15:26](https://ref.ly/Matt15:26) · [ACT 25:18](https://ref.ly/John25:18) · [ACT 25:27](https://ref.ly/John25:27)

88\.315 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sin, Wrongdoing, Guilt

guilt, wrongdoing, reason for condemning guilt as a basis or reason for condemnation It is also possible to understand αἰτία in [JHN 18:38](https://ref.ly/Luke18:38) as ‘reason for legal accusation,’ as noted in [56\.4](#56.4).

[JHN 18:38](https://ref.ly/Luke18:38) · [JHN 19:6](https://ref.ly/Luke19:6) · [ACT 13:28](https://ref.ly/John13:28)

89\.1 · Relations · Relation

relation a relation existing between two or more objects or events

[MAT 19:10](https://ref.ly/Matt19:10)

## αἰφνίδιος (id: 000159000000000)

αἰφνίδιος
=========

αἰφνίδιος (G0160)

adj · ἄφνω

67\.113 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

suddenly, at once, immediately pertaining to an extremely short period of time between a previous state or event and a subsequent state or event In a number of contexts there is the implication of unexpectedness; but this seems to be a derivative of the context as a whole and not a part of the meaning of the lexical items.

## αἰχμαλωσία (id: 000160000000000)

αἰχμαλωσία
==========

αἰχμαλωσία (G0161)

noun

55\.23 · Military Activities · Prisoners of War

captivity the state of being taken as a prisoner of war and kept a captive In a number of languages it may be necessary to render ‘captivity’ in terms of ‘being a captive,’ and therefore this expression in [REV 13:10](https://ref.ly/Jude13:10) may be rendered as ‘if anyone is meant to become a captive, he will indeed become a captive.’

[EPH 4:8](https://ref.ly/Gal4:8) · [REV 13:10](https://ref.ly/Jude13:10) · [REV 13:10](https://ref.ly/Jude13:10)

## αἰχμαλωτεύω (id: 000161000000000)

αἰχμαλωτεύω
===========

αἰχμαλωτεύω (G0162)

55\.24 · Military Activities · Prisoners of War

to make captive, to take captive, to capture someone in war to cause someone to become a prisoner of war In [EPH 4:8](https://ref.ly/Gal4:8) αἰχμαλωσία ‘captivity’ ([55\.23](#55.23)) is added redundantly to αἰχμαλωτεύω due to Semitic usage. The combination of words simply means ‘to take many captives.’

[EPH 4:8](https://ref.ly/Gal4:8)

* **Associated Passages:** Ephesians 4:8

to make captive, to take captive, to capture someone in war to cause someone to become a prisoner of war

37\.29 · Control, Rule · Control, Restrain

to get control of (a figurative extension of meaning of αἰχμαλωτίζω\[a] ‘to take captive in war,’ [55\.24](#55.24)) to gain complete control over, either by force or deception

## αἰχμάλωτος (id: 000163000000000)

αἰχμάλωτος
==========

αἰχμάλωτος (G0164)

noun

55\.25 · Military Activities · Prisoners of War

captive, prisoner of war one who has been taken captive in war In [LUK 4:18](https://ref.ly/Mark4:18) αἰχμάλωτος occurs on two levels: (1\) in the literal sense of ‘being a captive of war’ and (2\) in the broader sense of referring to all those who are oppressed by foreign domination.

[LUK 4:18](https://ref.ly/Mark4:18)

## αἰών (id: 000164000000000)

αἰών
====

αἰών (G0165)

noun · αἰώνιος

67\.143 · Time · Indefinite Units of Time: Age, Lifetime, Interval, Period

age, era a unit of time as a particular stage or period of history In a number of languages there is no specific term for ‘age’ or ‘era.’ The closest approximation in the context of [MAT 12:32](https://ref.ly/Matt12:32) may be ‘not in these many years and not in the many years which will follow.’\|In [1TI 1:17](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:17) (τῷ δὲ βασιλεῖ τῶν αἰώνων literally ‘to the king of the ages’) the genitive phrase τῶν αἰώνων is better understood as an abstract of unlimited extent of time, and accordingly, one may translate more accurately as ‘the eternal king.’ Compare [67\.95](#67.95).

[MAT 12:32](https://ref.ly/Matt12:32) · [MAT 13:22](https://ref.ly/Matt13:22) · [MAT 13:39](https://ref.ly/Matt13:39) · [MAT 13:40](https://ref.ly/Matt13:40) · [MAT 13:49](https://ref.ly/Matt13:49) · [MAT 24:3](https://ref.ly/Matt24:3) · [MAT 28:20](https://ref.ly/Matt28:20) · [MRK 10:30](https://ref.ly/Matt10:30) · [LUK 18:30](https://ref.ly/Mark18:30) · [LUK 20:35](https://ref.ly/Mark20:35) · [1CO 1:20](https://ref.ly/Rom1:20) · [1CO 2:6](https://ref.ly/Rom2:6) · [1CO 2:6](https://ref.ly/Rom2:6) · [1CO 2:7](https://ref.ly/Rom2:7) · [1CO 2:8](https://ref.ly/Rom2:8) · [1CO 10:11](https://ref.ly/Rom10:11) · [2CO 4:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:4) · [GAL 1:4](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:4) · [EPH 1:21](https://ref.ly/Gal1:21) · [EPH 2:7](https://ref.ly/Gal2:7) · [EPH 3:11](https://ref.ly/Gal3:11) · [1TI 1:17](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:17) · [2TI 4:10](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:10) · [TIT 2:12](https://ref.ly/2Tim2:12) · [HEB 1:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:8) · [HEB 6:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:5) · [HEB 9:26](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:26)

1\.2 · Geographical Objects and Features · Universe, Creation

universe the universe, perhaps with some associated meaning of ‘eon’ or ‘age’ in the sense of the transitory nature of the universe (but this is doubtful in the contexts of [HEB 1:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:2) and [HEB 11:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:3)) In [HEB 1:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:2) it may be essential in a number of languages to translate ‘he is the one through whom God created everything,’ though in some instances a more idiomatic and satisfactory way of rendering the meaning would involve a phrase such as ‘... created both the earth and the sky’ or ‘... the heavens and the earth.’

[HEB 1:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:2) · [HEB 11:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:3)

41\.38 · Behavior and Related States · Particular Patterns of Behavior

world system, worlds standards, world the system of practices and standards associated with secular society (that is, without reference to any demands or requirements of God) αἰών in [1CO 3:18](https://ref.ly/Rom3:18) may also be rendered as ‘by the way in which people in this world think’ or ‘by the things which people in this world think are right.’ In [MRK 4:19](https://ref.ly/Matt4:19) the phrase αἱ μέριμναι τοῦ αἰῶνος may be rendered as ‘the cares which people in this world have’ or ‘the way in which people in this world worry about things.’

[MRK 4:19](https://ref.ly/Matt4:19) · [ROM 12:2](https://ref.ly/Acts12:2) · [1CO 1:20](https://ref.ly/Rom1:20) · [1CO 3:18](https://ref.ly/Rom3:18) · [2CO 4:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:4) · [1TI 6:17](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:17) · [2TI 4:10](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:10)

67\.25 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

**Collocation:** ἀπ᾽ αἰῶνος

long ago, very long ago (an idiom, literally: from an age) a point of time preceding another point of time, with a very long interval between (more emphatic than πάλαι\[a], ἔκπαλαι\[a], [67\.24](#67.24)) Some persons have concluded that ἀπ ̓ αἰῶνος must be understood in the sense of ‘from an earlier age,’ but there seems to be no justification for reading a dispensational implication into this idiomatic expression.

[LUK 1:70](https://ref.ly/Mark1:70) · [ACT 3:21](https://ref.ly/John3:21) · [ACT 15:18](https://ref.ly/John15:18)

67\.133 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Point of Time: Until, Delay, Still, From

**Collocation:** ἐκ τοῦ αἰῶνος · ἀπὸ τῶν αἰώνων · πρὸ παντὸς τοῦ αἰῶνος

since all time, from all ages past, from the beginning of time an exceedingly long period of time from an assumed beginning up to the present

[JHN 9:32](https://ref.ly/Luke9:32) · [EPH 3:9](https://ref.ly/Gal3:9) · [COL 1:26](https://ref.ly/Phil1:26) · [JUD 1:25](https://ref.ly/3John1:25)

67\.95 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** εἰς αἰῶνα · εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα · εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας · εἰς ἡμέραν αἰῶνος · εἰς παντὰς τοὺς αἰῶνας · εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα τοῦ αἰῶνος · εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα τῶν αἰώνων · εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων

always, forever, forever and ever, eternally, (with neg.) never unlimited duration of time, with particular focus upon the future See also [LUK 1:33](https://ref.ly/Mark1:33); [2PE 3:18](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:18); [JUD 1:25](https://ref.ly/3John1:25); [HEB 1:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:8). The more elaborate expressions employing αἰών are somewhat more emphatic in meaning and are to be found especially in the solemn style of doxologies.

22:5](https://ref.ly/Jude22:5)

12\.44 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Powers

**Collocation:** αἰὼν τοῦ κόσμου τούτου

power, authority, lordship, ruler, wicked force title for supernatural forces and powers: a supernatural power having some particular role in controlling the destiny and activities of human beings Some scholars have believed that it is possible to reconstruct at least in part some of the hierarchy represented by these various supernatural forces and powers, on the basis of the neoplatonic system of nine such powers arranged in three orders of three each. NT terminology and usage does not, however, lend itself to such a classification, and it is difficult, if not impossible, to determine what are the significant differences between these supernatural powers and forces.\|For the most part, translators have endeavored to render the various terms in somewhat different ways, for example, ἐξουσία as ‘authority’; δύναμις as ‘power’; κυριότης as ‘ruler’; and phrases such as τὰ πνευματικὰ τῆς πονηρίας ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις as ‘wicked spiritual forces in the heavenly world.’ In a number of languages, however, there are simply not enough terms to designate various kinds of powers, authorities, and forces which could be employed in speaking of such supernatural impersonal powers. Accordingly, some translators have simply used a phrase such as ‘any and all kinds of supernatural powers.’ Where it is possible to make certain lexical distinctions between terms and phrases, this, of course, should be done, but in a number of contexts this may be impossible, and accordingly, some explanation in a marginal note may be required if the reader is to understand satisfactorily the intent of the passages in question.

[EPH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Gal2:2)

12\.24 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

**Collocation:** ὁ θεὸς τοῦ αἰῶνος τούτου

the god of this world, the Devil (a title for the Devil, literally: the god of this world) one who has power or authority over this world (or this age) and is so recognized by people of the world The expression ‘the god of this world’ may be understood in two senses: (1\) the god who rules over this world or (2\) the god whom the people of this world trust or worship. Though many persons have traditionally interpreted ‘the god of this world’ as the one who rules over this world, it is the second meaning which probably fits more accurately in the context of [2CO 4:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:4).

[2CO 4:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:4)

11\.16 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

**Collocation:** υἱοὶ τοῦ αἰῶνος τούτου

non\-religious people, people of this world, people of this age (an idiom, literally: sons of this age) persons who hold to the value system of the world In some instances ‘the people of this world’ may be rendered as ‘the people who live like most people in the world’ or ‘the people who are concerned only about what people say.’ Sometimes the identification with the world is expressed in contrast with people’s attitude concerning God, for example, ‘people who have no use for God.’

[LUK 16:8](https://ref.ly/Mark16:8) · [LUK 20:34](https://ref.ly/Mark20:34)

67\.87 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** ἐχθὲς καὶ σήμερον καὶ εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας

always, eternally, past and present and future (an idiom, literally: yesterday, today, and forever) an unlimited extension of time, from the past into the future

[HEB 13:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:8)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 12:32; Matthew 13:22; Matthew 13:39; Matthew 13:40; Matthew 13:49; Matthew 24:3; Matthew 28:20; Mark 10:30; Luke 18:30; Luke 20:35; 1 Corinthians 1:20; 1 Corinthians 2:6; 1 Corinthians 2:7; 1 Corinthians 2:8; 1 Corinthians 10:11; 2 Corinthians 4:4; Galatians 1:4; Ephesians 1:21; Ephesians 2:7; Ephesians 3:11; 1 Timothy 1:17; 2 Timothy 4:10; Titus 2:12; Hebrews 1:8; Hebrews 6:5; Hebrews 9:26; Hebrews 1:2; Hebrews 11:3; Mark 4:19; Romans 12:2; 1 Corinthians 3:18; 1 Timothy 6:17; Luke 1:70; Acts 3:21; Acts 15:18; John 9:32; Ephesians 3:9; Colossians 1:26; Jude 1:25; Matthew 21:19; Mark 3:29; Mark 11:14; Luke 1:33; Luke 1:55; John 4:14; John 6:51; John 6:58; John 8:35; John 8:51; John 8:52; John 10:28; John 11:26; John 12:34; John 13:8; John 14:16; Romans 1:25; Romans 9:5; Romans 11:36; 1 Corinthians 8:13; 2 Corinthians 9:9; 2 Corinthians 11:31; Galatians 1:5; Ephesians 3:21; Philippians 4:20; 2 Timothy 4:18; Hebrews 5:6; Hebrews 6:20; Hebrews 7:17; Hebrews 7:21; Hebrews 7:24; Hebrews 7:28; Hebrews 13:21; 1 Peter 1:25; 1 Peter 4:11; 1 Peter 5:11; 2 Peter 3:18; 1 John 2:17; 2 John 1:2; Jude 1:13; Revelation 1:6; Revelation 1:18; Revelation 4:9; Revelation 4:10; Revelation 5:13; Revelation 7:12; Revelation 10:6; Revelation 11:15; Revelation 14:11; Revelation 15:7; Revelation 19:3; Revelation 20:10; Revelation 22:5; Ephesians 2:2; Luke 16:8; Luke 20:34; Hebrews 13:8


## αἰώνιος (id: 000165000000000)

αἰώνιος
=======

αἰώνιος (G0166)

adj · αἰών

67\.96 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

eternal pertaining to an unlimited duration of time1 The most frequent use of αἰώνιος in the NT is with ζωή 'life', for example, ἵνα πᾶς ὁ πιστεύων ἐν αὐτῷ ἔχω ζωὴν αἰώνιον ‘so that everyone who believes in him may have eternal life’ [JHN 3:15](https://ref.ly/Luke3:15). In combination with ζωή there is evidently not only a temporal element, but also a qualitative distinction. In such contexts, αἰώνιος evidently carries certain implications associated with αἰώνιος in relationship to divine and supernatural attributes. If one translates ‘eternal life’ as simply ‘never dying,’ there may be serious misunderstandings, since persons may assume that ‘never dying’ refers only to physical existence rather than to ‘spiritual death.’ Accordingly, some translators have rendered ‘eternal life’ as ‘unending real life,’ so as to introduce a qualitative distinction.

## ἀκάθαρτος (id: 000167000000000)

ἀκάθαρτος
=========

ἀκάθαρτος (G0169)

adj

53\.39 · Religious Activities · Defile, Unclean, Common

defiled, ritually unclean pertaining to being ritually unacceptable, either as the result of defilement or because of the very nature of the object itself (for example, ritually unacceptable animals) It is possible that there is some subtle distinction in meaning, particularly on a connotative level, between κοινός\[b] and ἀκάθαρτος in [ACT 10:14](https://ref.ly/John10:14), but it is difficult to determine the precise differences of meaning on the basis of existing contexts. The two terms are probably used in [ACT 10:14](https://ref.ly/John10:14) primarily for the sake of emphasis.

## ἄκακος (id: 000170000000000)

ἄκακος
======

ἄκακος (G0172)

adj

88\.2 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Goodness

without fault, guileless, innocent pertaining to being without fault and hence guileless In [HEB 7:26](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:26) ἄκακος has a far more positive meaning than merely not being bad. In other words, the negation of κακός\[a] bad ([88\.106](#88.106)) does not produce a term which is merely morally neutral, but it designates something of a clearly positive character.1

[HEB 7:26](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:26)

31\.34 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Suppose, Think Possible

unsuspecting, naive pertaining to being unsuspecting or naive with regard to possible deception It may be possible to spell out the implications of ἄκακος in [ROM 16:18](https://ref.ly/Acts16:18) by translating ‘the minds of people who do not suspect lies.’

[ROM 16:18](https://ref.ly/Acts16:18)

1 There is a certain parallel to ἄκακος in the English expression not bad, which in many contexts indicates something which is positively good. In fact, with certain intonation the expression not bad may actually have a stronger positive designation than good.

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 7:26; Romans 16:18


## ἄκανθα (id: 000171000000000)

ἄκανθα
======

ἄκανθα (G0173)

noun · ἀκή · ἀκάνθινος

3\.17 · Plants · Plants That Are Not Trees

thorn plant, thistle, brier any kind of plant with sharp projections on its stem and/or branches; regarded as useless; symbol of hardship Since plants having thorny projections are almost worldwide in distribution, there is no difficulty in obtaining a satisfactory term for such plants. In some instances, however, a descriptive equivalent may be necessary, for example, ‘plants with piercing points’ or ‘plants that stick into one’s flesh.’

ἀκάνθινος (G0174)

adj · ἄκανθα

**Collocation:** ἀκάνθινον στέφανον

crown of thorns (derivative of ἄκανθα ‘thorn plant,’ [3\.17](#3.17)) pertaining to being made or consisting of thorns; in the NT, only in the phrase ἀκάνθινον στέφανον, a crown made from a plant with sharp projections on its stem and/or branches; symbol of hardship It may be necessary to indicate that this crown of thorns is one made of ‘branches of a thorny bush,’ for the crown did not consist merely of thorns but of thorny vines or branches.

without fruit, bearing no fruit, producing no harvest pertaining to not producing seed, fruit, or harvest In [JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12) a literal rendering of ‘even in autumn’ might be misleading in some areas in which autumn as a calendrical term might not be the period for bearing fruit. ============

above criticism, beyond reproach pertaining to what cannot be criticized It may be necessary in some languages to restructure ἀκατάγνωστος and ἀνεπίλημπτος as complete clauses, so that instead of ‘sound words which are above criticism’ in [TIT 2:8](https://ref.ly/2Tim2:8), one may translate ‘sound words which no one can criticize’ or ‘... against which no one can say anything.’ ‘Above reproach’ in [1TI 6:14](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:14) may be rendered as ‘in such a way that no one can criticize it.’

## ἀκατάλυτος (id: 000177000000000)

ἀκατάλυτος
==========

ἀκατάλυτος (G0179)

adj

13\.47 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · State

cannot be brought to an end, cannot be caused to finish pertaining to that which cannot be caused to cease

[HEB 7:16](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:16)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 7:16


## ἀκατάπαυστος (id: 000178000000000)

ἀκατάπαυστος
============

ἀκατάπαυστος (G0180)

adj

68\.56 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

never ceasing, not stopping pertaining to not ceasing from some activity

[2PE 2:14](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:14)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Peter 2:14


## ἀκαταστασία (id: 000179000000000)

ἀκαταστασία
===========

ἀκαταστασία (G0181)

noun

39\.34 · Hostility, Strife · Rebellion

to rebel against, to revolt, to engage in insurrection, rebellion to rise up in open defiance of authority, with the presumed intention to overthrow it or to act in complete opposition to its demands It is possible that ἀκαταστασία in [LUK 21:9](https://ref.ly/Mark21:9) denotes merely unsettled conditions, but it is far more likely to carry the meaning of ‘insurrections’ and ‘revolts.’

[LUK 21:9](https://ref.ly/Mark21:9) · [2CO 12:20](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:20) · [JAS 3:16](https://ref.ly/Heb3:16)

39\.36 · Hostility, Strife · Rebellion

riot, being mobbed violent opposition to someone or something, involving mob action1

[LUK 21:9](https://ref.ly/Mark21:9) · [1CO 14:33](https://ref.ly/Rom14:33) · [2CO 6:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:5) · [JAS 3:16](https://ref.ly/Heb3:16)

1 ἀκαταστασία\[b] differs from ἀκαταστασία\[a] ‘revolt, rebellion’ ([39\.34](#39.34)) in that the violent opposition is directed against some person or local circumstance, rather than against governmental authority.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 21:9; 2 Corinthians 12:20; James 3:16; 1 Corinthians 14:33; 2 Corinthians 6:5


## ἀκατάστατος (id: 000180000000000)

ἀκατάστατος
===========

ἀκατάστατος (G0182)

adj

37\.32 · Control, Rule · Control, Restrain

not controlled by, not subject to, uncontrolled pertaining to being unable to be controlled by something or someone It is also possible to translate ‘it is an evil which cannot be controlled’ as ‘it is an evil which no one can control’ or ‘no one can control this evil.’

[JAS 1:8](https://ref.ly/Heb1:8) · [JAS 3:8](https://ref.ly/Heb3:8)

* **Associated Passages:** James 1:8; James 3:8

noun nom

Akeldama (an Aramaic term meaning ‘field of blood’) a piece of land, probably south of the valley of Hinnom outside Jerusalem, which was bought with Judas money

## ἀκοή (id: 000186000000000)

ἀκοή
====

ἀκοή (G0189)

noun · ἀκούω

24\.52 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

to hear, hearing{N:001}

[MAT 13:14](https://ref.ly/Matt13:14) · [LUK 7:1](https://ref.ly/Mark7:1) · [ACT 17:20](https://ref.ly/John17:20) · [GAL 3:2](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:2) · [GAL 3:5](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:5) · [1TH 2:13](https://ref.ly/Col2:13) · [HEB 4:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:2) · [HEB 5:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:11) · [2PE 2:8](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:8)

24\.53 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

ability to hear, hearing the faculty of hearing In some languages it may be better to speak about the organs involved rather than the related faculties, for example, ‘if the whole body were an eye, what about the ears?’

[MRK 7:35](https://ref.ly/Matt7:35) · [1CO 12:17](https://ref.ly/Rom12:17) · [1CO 12:17](https://ref.ly/Rom12:17) · [HEB 5:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:11)

24\.57 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

what is heard, message (derivative of ἀκούω\[a] ‘to hear,’ [24\.52](#24.52)) that which is heard by someone In a number of languages the closest equivalent of ‘message’ would be ‘that which is said’ rather than ‘that which is heard,’ and accordingly, one may perhaps best translate [ROM 10:16](https://ref.ly/Acts10:16) as ‘who has believed what we said.’

[JHN 12:38](https://ref.ly/Luke12:38) · [ROM 10:16](https://ref.ly/Acts10:16) · [ROM 10:17](https://ref.ly/Acts10:17) · [ROM 10:17](https://ref.ly/Acts10:17) · [GAL 3:2](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:2) · [GAL 3:5](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:5) · [1TH 2:13](https://ref.ly/Col2:13) · [HEB 4:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:2)

33\.213 · Communication · Inform, Announce

news, report, information (derivative of ἀκούω\[c] ‘to receive news,’ [33\.212](#33.212)) the content of the news which is heard In a number of languages, however, it is impossible to speak of ‘news spreading.’ It therefore may be necessary to render [MAT 4:24](https://ref.ly/Matt4:24) as ‘people throughout the whole country of Syria kept hearing from one another about him.’

[MAT 4:24](https://ref.ly/Matt4:24) · [MAT 14:1](https://ref.ly/Matt14:1) · [MAT 24:6](https://ref.ly/Matt24:6) · [MRK 1:28](https://ref.ly/Matt1:28) · [MRK 13:7](https://ref.ly/Matt13:7)

31\.56 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Accept As True

to accept, to listen to, to listen and respond, to pay attention and respond, to heed to believe something and to respond to it on the basis of having heard

[2TI 4:4](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:4)

24\.63 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

**Collocation:** ἀκοῇ ἀκούω

to listen carefully, to listen and listen (a Semitic idiom, literally: to hear with hearing) to listen intently and with presumed continuity

[ACT 28:26](https://ref.ly/John28:26)

25\.11 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire, Want, Wish

**Collocation:** κνήθομαι τὴν ἀκοήν

to have itching ears, to desire to hear what one wants to hear, to be desirous of hearing (an idiom, literally: to itch with respect to hearing) to have one’s ears tickled by what is heard2

[2TI 4:3](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:3)

1 As in the case of other so\-called semantic derivatives, it seems best in this instance likewise to employ only evident glosses. One could, of course define the process of hearing as ‘perceiving by means of the ears,’ but such a definition would be unnecessarily artificial. 2 The idiom κνήθομαι τὴν ἀκοήν involves two quite distinct semantic features. One is the desire to be pleased, and the second is the means of such gratification, namely, what is heard. It would be possible to classify this idiom either under Sensory Events and States (Domain 24\) or here in the domain of Attitudes and Emotions. Since in [2TI 4:3](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:3) the latter seems the more focal feature of meaning, the idiom is included at this point.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:14; Luke 7:1; Acts 17:20; Galatians 3:2; Galatians 3:5; 1 Thessalonians 2:13; Hebrews 4:2; Hebrews 5:11; 2 Peter 2:8; Mark 7:35; 1 Corinthians 12:17; John 12:38; Romans 10:16; Romans 10:17; Matthew 4:24; Matthew 14:1; Matthew 24:6; Mark 1:28; Mark 13:7; 2 Timothy 4:4; Acts 28:26; 2 Timothy 4:3

15\.156 · Linear Movement · Follow, Accompany

to accompany as a follower, to follow, to go along with to follow or accompany someone who takes the lead in determining direction and route of movement ἀκολουθέω\[b] differs from ἀκολουθέω\[a] ([15\.144](#15.144)) in that it specifies a factor of accompaniment rather than merely going behind.

[MAT 4:25](https://ref.ly/Matt4:25) · [MAT 8:19](https://ref.ly/Matt8:19) · [MAT 8:22](https://ref.ly/Matt8:22) · [MAT 8:23](https://ref.ly/Matt8:23) · [MAT 9:9](https://ref.ly/Matt9:9) · [MAT 9:9](https://ref.ly/Matt9:9) · [MAT 10:38](https://ref.ly/Matt10:38) · [MAT 19:2](https://ref.ly/Matt19:2) · [MAT 20:29](https://ref.ly/Matt20:29) · [MAT 20:34](https://ref.ly/Matt20:34) · [MAT 27:55](https://ref.ly/Matt27:55) · [MRK 2:14](https://ref.ly/Matt2:14) · [MRK 2:14](https://ref.ly/Matt2:14) · [MRK 2:15](https://ref.ly/Matt2:15) · [MRK 3:7](https://ref.ly/Matt3:7) · [MRK 6:1](https://ref.ly/Matt6:1) · [MRK 9:38](https://ref.ly/Matt9:38) · [LUK 7:9](https://ref.ly/Mark7:9) · [LUK 9:11](https://ref.ly/Mark9:11) · [LUK 9:49](https://ref.ly/Mark9:49) · [LUK 18:43](https://ref.ly/Mark18:43) · [LUK 22:39](https://ref.ly/Mark22:39) · [LUK 23:27](https://ref.ly/Mark23:27) · [JHN 1:43](https://ref.ly/Luke1:43) · [JHN 6:2](https://ref.ly/Luke6:2) · [JHN 10:5](https://ref.ly/Luke10:5) · [JHN 11:31](https://ref.ly/Luke11:31) · [REV 14:4](https://ref.ly/Jude14:4) · [REV 14:13](https://ref.ly/Jude14:13) · [REV 19:14](https://ref.ly/Jude19:14)

36\.31 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Follow, Be a Disciple

to follow, to be a disciple of to be a follower or a disciple of someone, in the sense of adhering to the teachings or instructions of a leader and in promoting the cause of such a leader Though many translators have attempted to employ the metaphorical significance of ‘to follow’ in the sense of ‘to be a disciple of,’ there are certain dangers in a number of languages. For example, ‘to follow’ may often have the connotation of ‘to pursue after with evil intent.’ In many languages the appropriate equivalent of ‘to follow’ (in the sense of ‘to be a disciple’) is literally ‘to accompany’ or ‘to go along with’ or ‘to be in the group of.’

24\.58 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

to be able to hear, faculty of hearing to have the faculty of hearing in contrast with being deaf2

[MAT 11:5](https://ref.ly/Matt11:5) · [MRK 7:37](https://ref.ly/Matt7:37) · [LUK 7:22](https://ref.ly/Mark7:22) · [LUK 8:8](https://ref.ly/Mark8:8) · [LUK 14:35](https://ref.ly/Mark14:35) · [REV 9:20](https://ref.ly/Jude9:20)

33\.212 · Communication · Inform, Announce

to receive news, to hear to receive information about something, normally by word of mouth In [MRK 6:29](https://ref.ly/Matt6:29) ἀκούω clearly implies more than mere physiological hearing, for the content of the message is included as a significant element in the meaning of ἀκούω\[c]. Compare also ἀκοή\[d] ([33\.213](#33.213)).

36\.14 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

to pay attention to and obey to listen or pay attention to a person, with resulting conformity to what is advised or commanded4

32\.1 · Understand · Understand

to understand, to comprehend to hear and understand a message5

[MAT 13:13](https://ref.ly/Matt13:13) · [MRK 4:9](https://ref.ly/Matt4:9) · [MRK 4:23](https://ref.ly/Matt4:23) · [MRK 4:33](https://ref.ly/Matt4:33) · [JHN 6:60](https://ref.ly/Luke6:60) · [JHN 8:43](https://ref.ly/Luke8:43) · [JHN 12:34](https://ref.ly/Luke12:34) · [ACT 11:18](https://ref.ly/John11:18) · [ACT 14:14](https://ref.ly/John14:14) · [ACT 22:2](https://ref.ly/John22:2) · [ACT 22:9](https://ref.ly/John22:9) · [ACT 26:14](https://ref.ly/John26:14) · [ACT 28:27](https://ref.ly/John28:27) · [ROM 11:8](https://ref.ly/Acts11:8) · [1CO 14:2](https://ref.ly/Rom14:2) · [GAL 4:21](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:21) · [JAS 2:5](https://ref.ly/Heb2:5)

56\.13 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Judicial Hearing, Inquiry

to hear a case, to provide a legal hearing, to hear a case in court to give a judicial hearing in a legal matter6 In some languages the most satisfactory way of speaking about ‘a court hearing’ is to describe the relationship between the principal participants. This may sometimes be done by translating ‘for a judge to listen to an accuser and a defender.’

[JHN 7:51](https://ref.ly/Luke7:51) · [ACT 24:4](https://ref.ly/John24:4) · [ACT 25:22](https://ref.ly/John25:22) · [ACT 25:22](https://ref.ly/John25:22)

24\.67 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

**Collocation:** ἀκούω εἰς τὸ οὖς

to hear in secret (an idiom, literally: to hear into the ear) to hear something in a secret setting In some languages the equivalent of ‘what you hear in secret’ may be ‘what you hear when no one else is around’ or ‘what you hear whispered to you.’

[MAT 10:27](https://ref.ly/Matt10:27)

32\.46 · Understand · Lack of Capacity for Understanding

**Collocation:** τοῖς ὠσὶν βαρέως ἀκούω

to be slow to understand, to be mentally dull (an idiom, literally: to hear heavily with the ears) to be mentally slow or dull in comprehending

[MAT 13:15](https://ref.ly/Matt13:15) · [ACT 28:27](https://ref.ly/John28:27)

24\.63 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

**Collocation:** ἀκοῇ ἀκούω

to listen carefully, to listen and listen (a Semitic idiom, literally: to hear with hearing) to listen intently and with presumed continuity

[MAT 2:9](https://ref.ly/Matt2:9) · [ACT 28:26](https://ref.ly/John28:26)

1 As in the case of other so\-called semantic derivatives, it seems best in this instance likewise to employ only evident glosses. One could, of course define the process of hearing as ‘perceiving by means of the ears,’ but such a definition would be unnecessarily artificial. 2 ἀκούω\[b] in [24\.58](#24.58) differs significantly from ἀκούω\[a] ‘to hear’ ([24\.52](#24.52)) in that ἀκούω\[b] indicates the ability to do something, not merely the activity involved. 3 MAT 18:15 It is obvious that in the use of ἀκούω in [MAT 18:15](https://ref.ly/Matt18:15) there is more than mere sensory perception. There is acceptance of the truth of what is said and the evident willingness to respond appropriately. These two elements are much more in focus than the sensory process of listening. 4 Both ἀκούω\[e] and ἐπιδέχομαι\[b] involve an overlapping of domains, for there is an evident factor of perception in paying attention, but also a clear inference of subsequent obedience. 5 The meaning of ἀκούω\[f] is an excellent example of a meaning which overlaps two domains, but since the focal element is not hearing but comprehending, ἀκούω\[f] is classified in this subdomain. 6 ἀκούω\[g] and διακούω apparently differ somewhat in meaning from ἀνακρίνω\[b] ([56\.12](#56.12)) in that the former two seem to be more neutral in connotation and do not appear to imply later condemnation.

## ἀκρίς (id: 000196000000000)

ἀκρίς
=====

ἀκρίς (G0200)

noun

4\.47 · Animals · Insects

grasshopper, locust six\-legged winged insect characterized by the fact that its third pair of legs is elongated and adapted to hopping; Acrididae family; moves in swarms that migrate to new areas, which they colonize; at other times lives solitary or in small groups; eaten cooked by toasting, grilling, frying, or broiling, though in some places they are also eaten raw; because of their large numbers and swarming characteristics, locusts were a symbol of a vast attacking army against which there was no defense and also a symbol of divine punishment The four NT contexts in which ἀκρίς occurs refer to these insects either as being edible ([MAT 3:4](https://ref.ly/Matt3:4); [MRK 1:6](https://ref.ly/Matt1:6)) or as being very destructive ([REV 9:3](https://ref.ly/Jude9:3)).\|Some persons have assumed that the reference to grasshoppers being eaten by John the Baptist should be understood not as the insects but as carob pods, but there is neither linguistic nor cultural evidence to support such an interpretation.\|In a number of parts of the world there are different kinds of grasshoppers/locusts, some of which are edible, and others which are not edible. It is therefore important in the contexts of [MAT 3:4](https://ref.ly/Matt3:4) and [MRK 1:6](https://ref.ly/Matt1:6) to select a term which designates edible insects.

[MAT 3:4](https://ref.ly/Matt3:4) · [MRK 1:6](https://ref.ly/Matt1:6) · [REV 9:3](https://ref.ly/Jude9:3) · [REV 9:7](https://ref.ly/Jude9:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 3:4; Mark 1:6; Revelation 9:3; Revelation 9:7

ἀκροατήριον
===========

ἀκροατήριον (G0201)

noun · ἀκροατής

audience hall, auditorium a relatively large building normally used for legal hearings, though possibly also employed for more general purposes In a number of languages ἀκροατήριον may be rendered as ‘a large hall’ or ‘a place where many people could gather together’ or ‘a building where many people could sit to listen.’

[ACT 25:23](https://ref.ly/John25:23)

ἀκροατής

24\.56 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

hearer (derivative of ἀκροάομαι ‘to hear,’ not occurring in the NT) one who hears In rendering [ROM 2:13](https://ref.ly/Acts2:13), it may be necessary to specify certain limitations with regard to being ‘hearers,’ for example, ‘for not just those who listen to the Law are righteous in God’s sight’ or ‘for those who merely listen to the Law are not the ones who are righteous in God’s sight.’

[ROM 2:13](https://ref.ly/Acts2:13) · [JAS 1:22](https://ref.ly/Heb1:22) · [JAS 1:23](https://ref.ly/Heb1:23) · [JAS 1:25](https://ref.ly/Heb1:25)

## ἀκροβυστία (id: 000199000000000)

ἀκροβυστία
==========

ἀκροβυστία (G0203)

noun

11\.52 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

being uncircumcised, the foreskin not cut off a state of being uncircumcised by not having the foreskin of the penis cut off1

[ACT 11:3](https://ref.ly/John11:3) · [ROM 2:25](https://ref.ly/Acts2:25) · [ROM 2:26](https://ref.ly/Acts2:26) · [ROM 2:26](https://ref.ly/Acts2:26) · [ROM 2:27](https://ref.ly/Acts2:27) · [ROM 4:10](https://ref.ly/Acts4:10) · [ROM 4:10](https://ref.ly/Acts4:10) · [ROM 4:11](https://ref.ly/Acts4:11) · [ROM 4:11](https://ref.ly/Acts4:11) · [ROM 4:12](https://ref.ly/Acts4:12) · [1CO 7:18](https://ref.ly/Rom7:18) · [1CO 7:19](https://ref.ly/Rom7:19) · [GAL 5:6](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:6) · [GAL 6:15](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:15) · [COL 2:13](https://ref.ly/Phil2:13)

11\.53 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

uncircumcised, Gentiles, the Gentile world a collective for those who are uncircumcised In translating [GAL 2:7](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:7) it is essential in some languages to avoid the impression that there are two gospels, one for the Jews and another for the Gentiles. One may, therefore, render [GAL 2:7](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:7) as ‘I had been entrusted with the good news to the uncircumcised, just as Peter had been for the circumcised’ or even ‘God had given me and Peter the task of preaching the good news; I to the Gentiles and Peter to the Jews.’

[ROM 3:30](https://ref.ly/Acts3:30) · [ROM 4:9](https://ref.ly/Acts4:9) · [GAL 2:7](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:7) · [EPH 2:11](https://ref.ly/Gal2:11) · [COL 3:11](https://ref.ly/Phil3:11)

1 The term ἀκροβυστία as a designation of a state of being uncircumcised belongs technically to a domain of physiological states (Domain 23\). Since, however, its significance for the NT text is only in connection with the socio\-religious character of people, ἀκροβυστία is classified here in Domain 11 Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes (see also [11\.53](#11.53)).

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 11:3; Romans 2:25; Romans 2:26; Romans 2:27; Romans 4:10; Romans 4:11; Romans 4:12; 1 Corinthians 7:18; 1 Corinthians 7:19; Galatians 5:6; Galatians 6:15; Colossians 2:13; Romans 3:30; Romans 4:9; Galatians 2:7; Ephesians 2:11; Colossians 3:11


## ἀκρογωνιαῖος (id: 000200000000000)

ἀκρογωνιαῖος
============

ἀκρογωνιαῖος (G0204)

adj

7\.44 · Constructions · Parts and Areas of Buildings

cornerstone, important stone the cornerstone or capstone of a building, essential to its construction (this term occurs in the NT only figuratively in reference to Christ) Some scholars have assumed that ἀκρογωνιαῖος would refer to the capstone occurring at the high point of a peaked roof, but in the NT ἀκρογωνιαῖος would probably refer to the type of stone which would have been used in the Temple in Jerusalem, and therefore it is far more likely to understand ἀκρογωνιαῖος as a cornerstone rather than a capstone of a peaked roof. It would not have referred to the keystone of an arch. Since, however, in many societies the use of ‘cornerstone’ is not known and in others it may have an entirely different function, it may be more satisfactory to use an expression such as ‘the most important stone’ or ‘the very important stone.’ This serves to describe the function and significance of the cornerstone without trying to indicate precisely its location or form.

[EPH 2:20](https://ref.ly/Gal2:20) · [1PE 2:6](https://ref.ly/Jas2:6)

* **Associated Passages:** Ephesians 2:20; 1 Peter 2:6

noun nom

93\.402 · Names of Persons and Places · Places

an Alexandrian (derivative of Ἀλεξανδρία ‘Alexandria,’ not occurring in the NT) a person who lives in or is a native of Alexandria

[ACT 6:9](https://ref.ly/John6:9) · [ACT 18:24](https://ref.ly/John18:24)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 6:9; Acts 18:24

## Ἀλέξανδρος (id: 000218000000000)

Ἀλέξανδρος
==========

Ἀλέξανδρος (G0223)

noun nom

93\.17a · Names of Persons and Places · Persons

Alexander the son of Simon of Cyrene

[MRK 15:21](https://ref.ly/Matt15:21)

93\.17b · Names of Persons and Places · Persons

Alexander a member of the high\-priestly family

[ACT 4:6](https://ref.ly/John4:6)

93\.17c · Names of Persons and Places · Persons

Alexander a Jew of Ephesus

[ACT 19:33](https://ref.ly/John19:33) · [ACT 19:33](https://ref.ly/John19:33)

93\.17d · Names of Persons and Places · Persons

Alexander a false teacher in the church1

[1TI 1:20](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:20)

93\.17e · Names of Persons and Places · Persons

Alexander a coppersmith and opponent of Paul1

[2TI 4:14](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:14)

1 Some scholars have suggested that Ἀλέξανδρος\[d] and Ἀλέξανδρος\[e] refer to the same person, but this is by no means certain.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 15:21; Acts 4:6; Acts 19:33; 1 Timothy 1:20; 2 Timothy 4:14

## ἀλήθεια (id: 000220000000000)

ἀλήθεια
=======

ἀλήθεια (G0225)

noun

72\.2 · True, False · True, False

truth the content of that which is true and thus in accordance with what actually happened In [JHN 8:32](https://ref.ly/Luke8:32) ἀλήθεια is used to refer to the revelation of God that Jesus brings or, perhaps, to Jesus himself for what he actually is as the revelation of God.

## ἀληθεύω (id: 000221000000000)

ἀληθεύω
=======

ἀληθεύω (G0226)

33\.251 · Communication · Speak Truth, Speak Falsehood

to speak the truth, to tell the truth to communicate what is true In a number of languages it may be difficult to employ a highly abstract term such as ‘truth.’ Accordingly, one may find it necessary to relate ‘truth’ in [GAL 4:16](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:16) to the immediate context, for example, ‘... by telling you what they really want to do’ or ‘... what all this really means.’

[GAL 4:16](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:16) · [EPH 4:15](https://ref.ly/Gal4:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Galatians 4:16; Ephesians 4:15


## ἀληθής (id: 000222000000000)

ἀληθής
======

ἀληθής (G0227)

adj

72\.1 · True, False · True, False

true, truth pertaining to being in accordance with historical fact In a number of languages ‘the truth’ may be rendered simply as ‘what really is’ or ‘what is so’ or ‘what has happened.’

[JHN 4:18](https://ref.ly/Luke4:18) · [JHN 5:31](https://ref.ly/Luke5:31) · [JHN 5:32](https://ref.ly/Luke5:32) · [JHN 8:13](https://ref.ly/Luke8:13) · [JHN 8:14](https://ref.ly/Luke8:14) · [JHN 8:17](https://ref.ly/Luke8:17) · [JHN 10:41](https://ref.ly/Luke10:41) · [JHN 19:35](https://ref.ly/Luke19:35) · [JHN 21:24](https://ref.ly/Luke21:24) · [PHP 4:8](https://ref.ly/Eph4:8) · [TIT 1:13](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:13) · [1JN 2:8](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:8) · [1JN 2:27](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:27) · [3JN 1:12](https://ref.ly/2John1:12)

70\.3 · Real, Unreal

real, really, true, truly pertaining to being real and not imaginary1 The figurative or symbolic sense of this statement does not, of course, take away from its reality.

[JHN 6:55](https://ref.ly/Luke6:55) · [JHN 6:55](https://ref.ly/Luke6:55) · [ACT 12:9](https://ref.ly/John12:9) · [1PE 5:12](https://ref.ly/Jas5:12) · [2PE 2:22](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:22)

88\.39 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Honesty, Sincerity

truthful, honest, a person of integrity pertaining to being truthful and honest In a number of languages one must translate ἀληθής in [MAT 22:16](https://ref.ly/Matt22:16) as ‘you always say what is true.’

[MAT 22:16](https://ref.ly/Matt22:16) · [MRK 12:14](https://ref.ly/Matt12:14) · [JHN 3:33](https://ref.ly/Luke3:33) · [JHN 7:18](https://ref.ly/Luke7:18) · [JHN 8:26](https://ref.ly/Luke8:26) · [ROM 3:4](https://ref.ly/Acts3:4) · [2CO 6:8](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:8)

1 It would seem that the meanings of items in subdomains [70\.1](#70.1)\-[70\.4](#70.4) (ὅλως\[b], ὄντως, ἀληθής\[b], ἀληθινός\[a], ἀληθῶς, and idioms with ἀλήθεια) do reflect certain subtle differences, but it is not at all easy to determine precisely how they differ.

* **Associated Passages:** John 4:18; John 5:31; John 5:32; John 8:13; John 8:14; John 8:17; John 10:41; John 19:35; John 21:24; Philippians 4:8; Titus 1:13; 1 John 2:8; 1 John 2:27; 3 John 1:12; John 6:55; Acts 12:9; 1 Peter 5:12; 2 Peter 2:22; Matthew 22:16; Mark 12:14; John 3:33; John 7:18; John 8:26; Romans 3:4; 2 Corinthians 6:8

## ἀλήθω (id: 000224000000000)

ἀλήθω
=====

ἀλήθω (G0229)

ἄλευρον

46\.16 · Household Activities

to grind grain to grind grain in a mill In [LUK 17:35](https://ref.ly/Mark17:35) and also in [MAT 24:41](https://ref.ly/Matt24:41) the reference is evidently to a hand mill, which was normally operated by two women working together. In societies in which such mills are not known, translators have frequently substituted the process of pounding grain by means of a mortar and pestle.

[MAT 24:41](https://ref.ly/Matt24:41) · [LUK 17:35](https://ref.ly/Mark17:35)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 24:41; Luke 17:35

## ἁλιεύω (id: 000227000000000)

ἁλιεύω
======

ἁλιεύω (G0232)

ἁλίζω

44\.7 · Animal Husbandry, Fishing

to fish to catch fish, whether by means of a line or by a net

[JHN 21:3](https://ref.ly/Luke21:3)1

1 JHN 21:3 In the last part of [JHN 21:3](https://ref.ly/Luke21:3) πιάζω ‘to seize, to catch’ ([18\.3](#18.3)) is used of catching fish, but this usage should be subsumed under the broader meaning of ‘to seize.’

* **Associated Passages:** John 21:3


## ἁλίζω (id: 000228000000000)

ἁλίζω
=====

ἁλίζω (G0233)

αἰγιαλός · ἅλας · ἁλιεύς · ἁλιεύω · ἅλς · ἁλυκός · ἄναλος · διθάλασσος · ἐνάλιον · θάλασσα · παραθαλάσσιος · παράλιος · συναλίζομαι

5\.28 · Foods and Condiments · Condiments

to apply salt to something, to restore the flavor to salt (a causative derivative of ἅλς ‘salt,’ [5\.25](#5.25)) to cause something to taste salty In [MAT 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13) one may employ a causative such as ‘how will the salt be made salty again’ or ‘how can one cause the salt to return.’ In [MRK 9:49](https://ref.ly/Matt9:49) the meaning is particularly obscure. According to OT requirements sacrifices were to be salted ([LEV 2:13](https://ref.ly/Lev2:13)), and it may be that in [MRK 9:49](https://ref.ly/Matt9:49) the fire is regarded as an equivalent of salt and as a symbol of suffering and sacrifice by which the disciple is tested.

[MAT 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13)1 · [MRK 9:49](https://ref.ly/Matt9:49)1

1 MAT 5:13 · MRK 9:49 It would be possible to analyze the occurrence of ἁλίζω in [MAT 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13) and [MRK 9:49](https://ref.ly/Matt9:49) as representing two quite distinct meanings, in the first instance ‘to restore the taste of salt,’ and in the second instance ‘to cause something to taste salty.’ However, this type of distinction does not seem to be necessary, for one may define the meaning of ἁλίζω as ‘to cause something to taste salty.’ This would then apply either to salt itself which has previously lost its salty flavor or to a sacrifice which needs to be salted. However, as already noted, the meaning of ἁλίζω in [MRK 9:49](https://ref.ly/Matt9:49) is extremely obscure and can only be understood in a highly figurative sense.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 5:13; Mark 9:49

## ἀλλά (id: 000230000000000)

ἀλλά
====

ἀλλά (G0235)

cj

89\.125 · Relations · Contrast

but, instead, on the contrary a marker of more emphatic contrast (as compared with δέ\[c], [89\.124](#89.124)) ἀλλά also occurs with μᾶλλον and ἤ in more emphatic phrases marking contrast: ἀλλὰ μᾶλλον δουλευέτωσαν ‘on the contrary, they are to serve (them) even better’ [1TI 6:2](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:2); οὐχί, λέγω ὑμῖν, ἀλλ ̓ ἢ διαμερισμόν ‘not (peace), I tell you, but rather division’ [LUK 12:51](https://ref.ly/Mark12:51).

89\.96 · Relations · Addition

and a marker of a series of coordinate relations which are contrastive with a previously identified event or state

[2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11) · [2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11) · [2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11) · [2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11) · [2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11) · [2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11)

91\.2 · Discourse Markers · Markers of Transition

and, yet a marker of transition, with a slightly adversative implication in some contexts, often best left untranslated In [ROM 15:21](https://ref.ly/Acts15:21) there is certainly no adversative value in the conjunction ἀλλά; if anything, it would only mark some underlying reason, but again it seems best to leave ἀλλά untranslated and to regard it simply as a transitional marker.

91\.11 · Discourse Markers · Markers of Emphasis

certainly, emphatically a marker of contrastive emphasis

89\.136 · Relations · Contrast

**Collocation:** μέν ... ἀλλά

on the one hand ... but on the other hand a marker of sets of items in contrast with one another

## ἅλλομαι (id: 000238000000000)

ἅλλομαι
=======

ἅλλομαι (G0242)

ἐξάλλομαι · ἐφάλλομαι · σαλεύω · σάλος · ἀσαλευτος

15\.238 · Linear Movement · Jump, Leap

to leap, to jump to leap or to jump into the air

[ACT 3:8](https://ref.ly/John3:8) · [ACT 14:10](https://ref.ly/John14:10)

14\.30 · Physical Events and States · Events Involving Liquids and Dry Masses

to bubble up, to well up the action of water forming bubbles and welling up from underneath the ground In the NT ἅλλομαι\[b] occurs only in the figurative context of [JHN 4:14](https://ref.ly/Luke4:14).\|In some languages expressions referring to the flowing of water from a spring depend upon the extent or continuity of such a flow. If the spring is a perennial one, that is to say, if it flows all the time, it may be designated by one term, while if it is intermittent, that is, flows only during the rainy season, it may be referred to by quite a different term. There may also be distinctions as to whether the water flows out and then down or whether it actually comes up from below the surface. In [JHN 4:14](https://ref.ly/Luke4:14) the direction of flow is not important, but it is important to use a term which would indicate continuous flowing.

[JHN 4:14](https://ref.ly/Luke4:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 3:8; Acts 14:10; John 4:14

15:1](https://ref.ly/Jude15:1) · [REV 17:10](https://ref.ly/Jude17:10) · [REV 18:1](https://ref.ly/Jude18:1) · [REV 18:4](https://ref.ly/Jude18:4) · [REV 20:12](https://ref.ly/Jude20:12)

1 Some persons have assumed that the distinction between ἄλλος and ἕτερος is simply a difference between what is another in a series and that which is significantly different in kind, but such a distinction cannot be confirmed from existing contexts, though there may be this type of distinction implied in [GAL 1:6](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:6), [GAL 1:7](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:7) in which another kind of gospel is rejected on the basis that there is no other gospel. ## ἀλλότριος (id: 000241000000000)

ἀλλότριος
=========

ἀλλότριος (G0245)

adj · ἀλλάσσω

92\.20 · Discourse Referentials · Whom or What Spoken or Written About

belonging to another, belonging to someone else a reference to what belongs to someone else

[LUK 16:12](https://ref.ly/Mark16:12) · [ACT 7:6](https://ref.ly/John7:6) · [ROM 14:4](https://ref.ly/Acts14:4) · [ROM 15:20](https://ref.ly/Acts15:20) · [2CO 10:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:15) · [2CO 10:16](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:16) · [1TI 5:22](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:22) · [HEB 9:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:25)

11\.74 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

stranger, foreigner a person from another geographical or cultural region and/or one not known to members of the socio\-political group in question In [JHN 10:5](https://ref.ly/Luke10:5) the most satisfactory rendering of ‘stranger’ is often ‘someone who is not known,’ in other words, ‘they will not follow someone whom they do not know’ or ‘... recognize.’

[MAT 17:25](https://ref.ly/Matt17:25) · [MAT 17:26](https://ref.ly/Matt17:26) · [JHN 10:5](https://ref.ly/Luke10:5) · [JHN 10:5](https://ref.ly/Luke10:5) · [HEB 11:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:9)

39\.12 · Hostility, Strife · Opposition, Hostility

to be strongly opposed to, to be an enemy of pertaining to being opposed as a result of one’s being essentially different Meanings involving enmity, hostility, and opposition are frequently idiomatic in nature and are derived primarily from four major experiential sources: (1\) psychological attitudes, ‘to have one’s heart burn against’; (2\) gestures, ‘to frown against’ or ‘to refuse to snap fingers’ (snapping fingers being a particular form of greeting in various parts of Africa); (3\) body stance, ‘to stand against’; and (4\) movement, ‘to turn one’s back against’ or ‘to turn away from.’

[HEB 11:34](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:34)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 16:12; Acts 7:6; Romans 14:4; Romans 15:20; 2 Corinthians 10:15; 2 Corinthians 10:16; 1 Timothy 5:22; Hebrews 9:25; Matthew 17:25; Matthew 17:26; John 10:5; Hebrews 11:9; Hebrews 11:34

adj

[ACT 10:28](https://ref.ly/John10:28)1

1 ACT 10:28 Though ἀλλόφυλος in its more general meaning belongs to a class of socio\-political terms, its specific usage in the NT justifies the classification of its meaning as socio\-religious.

## ἀλοάω (id: 000245000000000)

ἀλοάω
=====

ἀλοάω (G0248)

ἅλων · μητρολῴας · πατρολῴας

43\.19 · Agriculture

to thresh, to tread out to separate grain from the husks of plants, either by beating or by being tread on by farm animals In a number of societies the idea of having animals tread out grain seems to be extremely wasteful and unsanitary, but there is no way of avoiding a translation of such passages in a more or less literal fashion; otherwise, there is no meaning to the context. It may also be important to provide some type of marginal note to explain more clearly what was involved.

[1CO 9:9](https://ref.ly/Rom9:9) · [1CO 9:10](https://ref.ly/Rom9:10) · [1TI 5:18](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:18)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 9:9; 1 Corinthians 9:10; 1 Timothy 5:18


## ἄλογος (id: 000246000000000)

ἄλογος
======

ἄλογος (G0249)

adj

30\.12 · Think · To Think, Thought

without reason, not able to reason pertaining to a lack of capacity to reason or think properly In rendering [2PE 2:12](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:12) one may translate ‘creatures unable to reason’ as ‘wild animals,’ for this is clearly the reference of the phrase ἄλογα ζῷα.

[2PE 2:12](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:12) · [JUD 1:10](https://ref.ly/3John1:10)

89\.19 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

unreasonable, without basis, absurd pertaining to not providing a reason or cause, in view of something being contrary to reason In some languages the equivalent of being unreasonable is being something which people should not think. Note that ἄλογος\[b] refers to that which is contrary to proper reasoning, while ἄλογος\[a] ([30\.12](#30.12)) pertains to a lack of capacity to reason.

[ACT 25:27](https://ref.ly/John25:27)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Peter 2:12; Jude 1:10; Acts 25:27

ἀλόη
====

ἀλόη (G0250)

noun

6\.209 · Artifacts · Perfumes and Incense

aloes an aromatic resin of a lily\-like plant, often used for embalming a corpse As in the case of σμύρνα ‘myrrh’ ([6\.208](#6.208)), translators normally borrow a term for ‘aloes’ and then employ some kind of marginal note to indicate the nature of the substance.

[JHN 19:39](https://ref.ly/Luke19:39)

* **Associated Passages:** John 19:39


## ἅλς (id: 000248000000000)

ἅλς
===

ἅλς (G0217) — ἅλας

noun · ἁλίζω

5\.25 · Foods and Condiments · Condiments

salt white crystalline substance, mainly consisting of sodium chloride, used for seasoning or preserving food Since salt is a universal condiment, there is no difficulty involved in finding an adequate expression for it, but there is a difficulty in [MAT 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13), since pure salt cannot lose its taste. In the ancient world, however, what was often sold as salt was highly adulterated and the sodium chloride could leach out in humid weather, in which case the residue (normally a form of lime) would be useless. It may, therefore, be important in some languages to provide a marginal note explaining the basis for the biblical statement concerning salt losing its flavor; otherwise, the parable may appear to be either meaningless or misleading.

[MAT 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13) · [MAT 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13) · [MRK 9:50](https://ref.ly/Matt9:50) · [MRK 9:50](https://ref.ly/Matt9:50) · [MRK 9:50](https://ref.ly/Matt9:50) · [LUK 14:34](https://ref.ly/Mark14:34) · [LUK 14:34](https://ref.ly/Mark14:34) · [COL 4:6](https://ref.ly/Phil4:6)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 5:13; Mark 9:50; Luke 14:34; Colossians 4:6

ἁλυκός
======

ἁλυκός (G0252)

adj · ἁλίζω

5\.26 · Foods and Condiments · Condiments

salty (derivative of ἅλς ‘salt,’ [5\.25](#5.25)) consisting of or pertaining to salt It may be difficult in some languages to speak of ‘a salty spring’; rather, it may be necessary to say ‘a spring that produces water that tastes salty.’ In other instances one may have to say ‘a spring that flows from salty ground.’

[JAS 3:12](https://ref.ly/Heb3:12)

* **Associated Passages:** James 3:12

ἅλυσις

ἅλυσις (G0254)

noun · λύω

6\.16 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Binding and Fastening

chain a series of connected metal links; used for binding objects together or restraining prisoners In some languages a distinction is made between terms for a chain used in tying up a person and one employed in agricultural or industrial work.

[MRK 5:3](https://ref.ly/Matt5:3) · [MRK 5:4](https://ref.ly/Matt5:4) · [MRK 5:4](https://ref.ly/Matt5:4) · [LUK 8:29](https://ref.ly/Mark8:29) · [ACT 12:6](https://ref.ly/John12:6) · [ACT 12:7](https://ref.ly/John12:7) · [ACT 21:33](https://ref.ly/John21:33) · [ACT 28:20](https://ref.ly/John28:20) · [EPH 6:20](https://ref.ly/Gal6:20) · [2TI 1:16](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:16) · [REV 20:1](https://ref.ly/Jude20:1)

37\.115 · Control, Rule · Imprison

to be in prison, imprisonment (figurative extension of meaning of ἅλυσις\[a] ‘chain,’ [6\.16](#6.16)) the state of being in prison The state of being a prisoner is often expressed somewhat idiomatically, for example, ‘to be tied hand and foot,’ ‘to be chained like a dog,’ or ‘to look through iron bars.’

[EPH 6:20](https://ref.ly/Gal6:20) · [2TI 1:16](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:16)

## ἄλφα (id: 000253000000000)

ἄλφα
====

ἄλφα (G0001a)

noun nom

61\.7 · Sequence

alpha, the first, the beginning the first in a series, with implications of significance or importance This expression is explained in some manuscripts as ‘the beginning and the end’ and in others as ‘the first and the last’ and in [REV 22:13](https://ref.ly/Jude22:13) as both ‘the first and the last’ and ‘the beginning and the end.’ The combination of alpha and omega in secular literature came to designate the entire universe and all kinds of divine and demonic powers, so that in Revelation this title could refer to Christ’s dominion over the universe. In the use of such an expression as ‘the alpha and the omega,’ there is obviously also an important element of status.

[REV 1:8](https://ref.ly/Jude1:8) · [REV 21:6](https://ref.ly/Jude21:6) · [REV 22:13](https://ref.ly/Jude22:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 1:8; Revelation 21:6; Revelation 22:13

## ἅλων (id: 000255000000000)

ἅλων
====

ἅλων (G0257)

noun · ἀλοάω

7\.65 · Constructions · Miscellaneous Constructions

threshing floor a surface of hard ground or stone where grain was threshed out, either by beating or having animals trample upon it The meaning of ἅλων as ‘threshing floor’ forms the basis for the figurative extension of meaning (a metonymy) referring to the threshed grain still lying on the threshing floor (see [3\.43](#3.43)). The meaning may be made clear by rendering ‘to thresh out completely all of the grain.’ On the other hand, it is possible to interpret ἅλων in [MAT 3:12](https://ref.ly/Matt3:12) in a literal sense and translate ‘he will clean up his threshing floor,’ that is, by gathering up the grain and getting rid of the straw and chaff.

[MAT 3:12](https://ref.ly/Matt3:12) · [LUK 3:17](https://ref.ly/Mark3:17)

3\.43 · Plants · Fruit Parts of Plants

threshed grain (a figurative extension of meaning of ἅλων\[a] ‘threshing floor,’ [7\.65](#7.65)) grain in the process of being threshed ἅλων in [MAT 3:12](https://ref.ly/Matt3:12) may also be interpreted as a threshing floor (see [7\.65](#7.65)).

[MAT 3:12](https://ref.ly/Matt3:12) · [LUK 3:17](https://ref.ly/Mark3:17)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 3:12; Luke 3:17


## ἀλώπηξ (id: 000256000000000)

ἀλώπηξ
======

ἀλώπηξ (G0258)

noun

4\.10 · Animals · Animals

fox, jackal animal looking like a longhaired dog with a pointed nose and a bushy tail; either reddish with white underparts (fox) or yellowish brown with red tips to the fur on the back (jackal); scavenger; associated with destruction and devastation, and also with being insignificant but self\-important; also intelligent and quick\-witted, crafty Though the Greek term ἀλώπηξ and the corresponding Hebrew terms used in the OT may refer to either a fox or a jackal, ἀλώπηξ in the NT seems to refer primarily to a fox (see Fauna and Flora of the Bible, pp. 31–32, for certain significant distinctions between foxes and jackals).\|In areas of the world where the fox is not known, one can sometimes speak of ‘a small wild dog’ or ‘an animal like a small wild dog.’ In other instances translators have borrowed the term ‘fox’ from a dominant language in the area and have then explained the appearance and habits of the animal in a glossary.\|For ἀλώπηξ in a figurative sense as applied by Jesus to Herod Antipas, see [88\.120](#88.120).

[MAT 8:20](https://ref.ly/Matt8:20) · [LUK 9:58](https://ref.ly/Mark9:58)

88\.120 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Bad, Evil, Harmful, Damaging

wicked person, cunning person, fox (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀλώπηξ\[a] ‘fox,’ [4\.10](#4.10)) a wicked person, probably with the implication of being cunning and treacherous Some scholars, however, suggest that ἀλώπηξ in [LUK 13:32](https://ref.ly/Mark13:32) may imply ‘worthlessness.’\|Rendering ἀλώπηξ in [LUK 13:32](https://ref.ly/Mark13:32) by a term meaning ‘fox’ may sometimes be misleading, since in some cultures the fox is regarded as a particularly wise animal, and therefore referring to a person as a fox may be a compliment. A local term for ‘fox’ may have other connotations which are quite undesirable. For example, in some languages a term for fox is used to designate a male or female prostitute, usually of the lowest grade.

[LUK 13:32](https://ref.ly/Mark13:32)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 8:20; Luke 9:58; Luke 13:32

ἁμαρτάνω (G0264)

ἁμάρτημα · ἁμαρτία · ἁμαρτωλός · ἀναμάρτητος · προαμαρτάνω

88\.118 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Bad, Evil, Harmful, Damaging

sinfulness, being evil a state of sinfulness as an integral element of someone’s nature The implication of this statement made in criticism of Jesus in [JHN 9:34](https://ref.ly/Luke9:34) was that he had not adhered rigorously to all the conventional requirements of the OT law as interpreted by the Pharisees.

88\.310 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sin, Wrongdoing, Guilt

guilt, sin the moral consequence of having sinned A number of languages make a clear distinction between the active event of committing sin and the resulting moral effect of guilt, so that one must speak of ‘committing sin’ but ‘forgiving guilt.’ This is often required in some languages since a term meaning ‘to forgive’ is literally ‘to wipe away,’ ‘to erase,’ ‘to blot out,’ or ‘to return to someone.’ The actual event of sinning often does not fit with such verb expressions, since it is not the event itself which is eliminated but the moral consequences of such an event, namely, the guilt.

## ἀμάρτυρος (id: 000265000000000)

ἀμάρτυρος
=========

ἀμάρτυρος (G0267)

adj

33\.267 · Communication · Witness, Testify

without witness pertaining to not having a witness In [ACT 14:17](https://ref.ly/John14:17) it may be rather difficult to introduce what is essentially a double negative, implied by ‘not’ and ‘without.’ Accordingly, [ACT 14:17](https://ref.ly/John14:17) may be rendered as ‘by the good things he does, everyone can know about him from experience.’

[ACT 14:17](https://ref.ly/John14:17)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 14:17


## ἁμαρτωλός (id: 000266000000000)

ἁμαρτωλός
=========

ἁμαρτωλός (G0268)

adj · ἁμαρτάνω

88\.294 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sin, Wrongdoing, Guilt

sinful, sinning (derivative of ἁμαρτάνω ‘to sin,’ [88\.289](#88.289)) pertaining to sinful behavior

[MRK 8:38](https://ref.ly/Matt8:38) · [MRK 14:41](https://ref.ly/Matt14:41) · [JHN 9:16](https://ref.ly/Luke9:16) · [ROM 5:19](https://ref.ly/Acts5:19) · [ROM 7:13](https://ref.ly/Acts7:13)

noun · ἁμαρτάνω

88\.295 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sin, Wrongdoing, Guilt

sinner, outcast (derivative of ἁμαρτάνω ‘to sin,’ [88\.289](#88.289)) a person who customarily sins In contexts such as [MRK 2:17](https://ref.ly/Matt2:17), [MAT 9:10](https://ref.ly/Matt9:10), [LUK 15:2](https://ref.ly/Mark15:2), etc., ἁμαρτωλός may refer to persons who were irreligious in the sense of having no concern for observing the details of the Law. Such people were often treated as social outcasts.

ἀμελέω
======

ἀμελέω (G0272)

## ἀμήτωρ (id: 000280000000000)

ἀμήτωρ
======

ἀμήτωρ (G0282)

adj

10\.17 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

without mother a person for whom there is no record of a female parent or who has never had a female parent or whose mother has died

[HEB 7:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:3)1

1 HEB 7:3 Though it is possible to understand ἀπάτωρ, ἀμήτωρ in [HEB 7:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:3) as referring to some type of supernatural theophany as in the case of the stories of certain Greek gods and goddesses, it is unlikely that this is the meaning. The focus of attention in [HEB 7:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:3) seems to be upon the legitimacy of succession in which the priesthood of Christ is contrasted with that of the descendants of Levi.

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 7:3
