## ἄμμος (id: 000283000000000)

ἄμμος
=====

ἄμμος (G0285)

noun

2\.28 · Natural Substances · Earth, Mud, Sand, Rock

sand ἄμμος\[a] is primarily used in the NT in a figurative sense referring to things which cannot be counted. In such contexts, therefore, what is important is the abundance of sand and not its particular occurrence beside the sea. Therefore, in many contexts it is possible to say ‘as numerous as the grains of sand.’ In this way one need not specify the particular location if the meaning of innumerable quantity is fully evident.\|In some areas of the world there is little or no sand, and therefore it may be necessary to render sand in [MAT 7:26](https://ref.ly/Matt7:26) as ‘loose soil’ or ‘unpacked dirt.’

[MAT 7:26](https://ref.ly/Matt7:26) · [ROM 9:27](https://ref.ly/Acts9:27) · [HEB 11:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:12) · [REV 20:8](https://ref.ly/Jude20:8)

1\.64 · Geographical Objects and Features · Land in Contrast With the Sea

beach a sandy beach or shore ἄμμος\[b] evidently involves a particular type of αἰγιαλός ([1\.63](#1.63)) which is sandy. For languages spoken by persons living along a seacoast, there are usually few if any problems involved in distinguishing various types of land bordering the sea, but for persons living in inland areas, it may be necessary to employ a descriptive phrase such as land along the sea and use such a phrase for various occurrences of different terms.

[REV 12:18](https://ref.ly/Jude12:18)1

1 REV 12:18 It would be possible to regard ἡ ἄμμος τῆς θαλάσσης, literally ‘the sand of the sea,’ as a type of idiom meaning beach. Compare, for example, the meaning of ἄμμος\[a] ([2\.28](#2.28)).

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 7:26; Romans 9:27; Hebrews 11:12; Revelation 20:8; Revelation 12:18


## ἀμνός (id: 000284000000000)

ἀμνός
=====

ἀμνός (G0286)

noun

4\.24 · Animals · Animals

lamb young of sheep πρόβατον; often used for ritual sacrifice In a number of languages a clear distinction is made between male and female lambs. It is therefore essential in any figurative reference to male persons to employ an appropriate term for lamb. In a number of languages, of course, a lamb is simply referred to as a ‘young sheep’ or ‘child sheep.’\|Perhaps the most widespread story about cultural differences in Bible translation is the one which refers to the translation of ‘lamb’ as ‘little seal of God’ in an Eskimo language. However, there is no evidence that this type of rendering was ever used, since in matters of appearance, habitat, and behavior, lambs and little seals have practically nothing in common. Furthermore, Eskimo languages have terms for ‘lamb’ and in some instances, at least, use the expression ‘young, woolly goat,’ since wild goats are well known and wool is well known as an imported product.

[JHN 1:29](https://ref.ly/Luke1:29) · [JHN 1:36](https://ref.ly/Luke1:36) · [ACT 8:32](https://ref.ly/John8:32) · [1PE 1:19](https://ref.ly/Jas1:19)

* **Associated Passages:** John 1:29; John 1:36; Acts 8:32; 1 Peter 1:19


## ἀμοιβή (id: 000285000000000)

ἀμοιβή
======

ἀμοιβή (G0287)

noun

57\.168 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Pay, Price, Cost

recompense, repayment that which is given as a means of recompense or repayment One may also translate ‘repayment to their parents and grandparents’ as ‘to pay back to their parents and grandparents for what these have done for them’ or ‘to help their parents and grandparents because of what these have done to help them.’

[1TI 5:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:4)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Timothy 5:4


## ἄμπελος (id: 000286000000000)

ἄμπελος
=======

ἄμπελος (G0288)

noun · ἀμπελουργός · ἀμπελών

3\.27 · Plants · Plants That Are Not Trees

grapevine creeping plant that develops a woody stem when it matures; Vitis vinifera; grows along the ground until it finds a tree or other object to climb, using tendrils; bears bunches of small round fruit that are sweet and juicy; symbol of blessing, prosperity, and happiness A rendering of ἄμπελος as ‘vine’ rather than as ‘grapevine’ in [JHN 15:1](https://ref.ly/Luke15:1) may cause serious misunderstanding, since it might refer merely to a vine which does not produce fruit. Accordingly, if there is no particular expression for ‘grapevine,’ it may be more satisfactory in [JHN 15:1](https://ref.ly/Luke15:1) to speak of ‘fruit bush’ or ‘fruit plant.’ A term in [JHN 15:1](https://ref.ly/Luke15:1) which would indicate only jungle vines would also result in complete misunderstanding of the function of pruning (see [43\.12](#43.12)), since such jungle vines are never pruned and in fact are only useful when they have been cut down and used for building purposes.\|In some languages a grapevine may be referred to as ‘a bush producing grapes,’ and in certain other languages it is called ‘the wine plant.’

[MAT 26:29](https://ref.ly/Matt26:29) · [MRK 14:25](https://ref.ly/Matt14:25) · [LUK 22:18](https://ref.ly/Mark22:18) · [JHN 15:1](https://ref.ly/Luke15:1) · [JHN 15:4](https://ref.ly/Luke15:4) · [JHN 15:5](https://ref.ly/Luke15:5) · [JAS 3:12](https://ref.ly/Heb3:12) · [REV 14:18](https://ref.ly/Jude14:18) · [REV 14:19](https://ref.ly/Jude14:19)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:29; Mark 14:25; Luke 22:18; John 15:1; John 15:4; John 15:5; James 3:12; Revelation 14:18; Revelation 14:19

## ἀμπελών (id: 000288000000000)

ἀμπελών
=======

ἀμπελών (G0290)

noun · ἄμπελος

3\.28 · Plants · Plants That Are Not Trees

vineyard, orchard of grapevines (derivative of ἄμπελος ‘grapevine’) a field or garden of grapevines, creeping plants that develop a woody stem when they mature; Vitis vinifera; they grow along the ground until they find a tree or other object to climb, using tendrils; they bear bunches of small round fruit that are sweet and juicy; a symbol of blessing, prosperity, and happiness In some instances a vineyard may be described as ‘a farm with grapevines’ or ‘a garden for grapes.’

[MAT 20:1](https://ref.ly/Matt20:1) · [MAT 20:2](https://ref.ly/Matt20:2) · [MAT 20:4](https://ref.ly/Matt20:4) · [MAT 20:7](https://ref.ly/Matt20:7) · [MAT 20:8](https://ref.ly/Matt20:8) · [MAT 21:28](https://ref.ly/Matt21:28) · [MAT 21:33](https://ref.ly/Matt21:33) · [MAT 21:39](https://ref.ly/Matt21:39) · [MAT 21:40](https://ref.ly/Matt21:40) · [MAT 21:41](https://ref.ly/Matt21:41) · [MRK 12:1](https://ref.ly/Matt12:1) · [MRK 12:2](https://ref.ly/Matt12:2) · [MRK 12:8](https://ref.ly/Matt12:8) · [MRK 12:9](https://ref.ly/Matt12:9) · [MRK 12:9](https://ref.ly/Matt12:9) · [LUK 13:6](https://ref.ly/Mark13:6) · [LUK 20:9](https://ref.ly/Mark20:9) · [LUK 20:10](https://ref.ly/Mark20:10) · [LUK 20:13](https://ref.ly/Mark20:13) · [LUK 20:15](https://ref.ly/Mark20:15) · [LUK 20:15](https://ref.ly/Mark20:15) · [LUK 20:16](https://ref.ly/Mark20:16) · [1CO 9:7](https://ref.ly/Rom9:7)

## ἀμύνομαι (id: 000290000000000)

ἀμύνομαι
========

ἀμύνομαι (G0292)

35\.3 · Help, Care For · Help

to help to assist by intervening on behalf of, primarily in terms of defense

[ACT 7:24](https://ref.ly/John7:24)1

1 ACT 7:24 It is possible to interpret ἀμύνομαι in [ACT 7:24](https://ref.ly/John7:24) as meaning to retaliate or even to take revenge, but this would seem to be a redundant meaning, particularly in view of the following statement, καὶ ἐποίησεν ἐκδίκησιν and he took revenge.

## ἀμφιβάλλω (id: 000291000000000)

ἀμφιβάλλω
=========

ἀμφιβάλλω (G0293a)

44\.8 · Animal Husbandry, Fishing

to cast a fishnet to cast a net in order to catch fish The net implied in the use of the verb ἀμφιβάλλω was probably a circular net which had small weights on its edge and which could be thrown by a single person.

[MRK 1:16](https://ref.ly/Matt1:16)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 1:16


## ἀμφίβληστρον (id: 000292000000000)

ἀμφίβληστρον
============

ἀμφίβληστρον (G0293)

noun · ἀμφιβάλλω

6\.12 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Fishing

casting\-net a circular net with weights and a draw rope around its edge; used in fishing An ἀμφίβληστρον had small weights attached to the outer border, and it was normally thrown in such a manner as to encircle fish. In trying to obtain a satisfactory equivalent expression in another language, it is not necessary to try to duplicate all the details of form. What is important is that such a net was thrown rather than being dragged.

[MAT 4:18](https://ref.ly/Matt4:18)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 4:18


## ἀμφιέζω (id: 000293000000000)

ἀμφιέζω
=======

ἀμφιέζω (G0294a) — ἀμφιάζω

49\.3 · Activities Involving Clothing and Adorning

to clothe to put on clothes, implying the clothing being completely around; see also [49\.5](#49.5)

[LUK 12:28](https://ref.ly/Mark12:28)

49\.5 · Activities Involving Clothing and Adorning

to clothe, to adorn to put on clothing to adorn the outward form of something1

[LUK 12:28](https://ref.ly/Mark12:28)

1 As is evident from the contexts cited in [49\.3](#49.3), ἀμφιέζω, ἀμφιέννυμι, and περιβάλλω occur not only in the sense of putting on clothes but in the more generic sense of adorning the outward appearance of something, for these verbs may occur not only with persons who are literally clothed but also with plants which are made attractive.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 12:28


## ἀμφιέννυμι (id: 000294000000000)

ἀμφιέννυμι
==========

ἀμφιέννυμι (G0294)

49\.3 · Activities Involving Clothing and Adorning

to clothe to put on clothes, implying the clothing being completely around; see also [49\.5](#49.5)

[MAT 6:30](https://ref.ly/Matt6:30)1 · [MAT 11:8](https://ref.ly/Matt11:8) · [LUK 7:25](https://ref.ly/Mark7:25)

49\.5 · Activities Involving Clothing and Adorning

to clothe, to adorn to put on clothing to adorn the outward form of something2

[MAT 6:30](https://ref.ly/Matt6:30)1

1 MAT 6:30 Manuscripts alternate frequently between ἀμφιάζω and ἀμφιέννυμι, which appear to be closely related synonyms. 2 As is evident from the contexts cited in [49\.3](#49.3), ἀμφιέζω, ἀμφιέννυμι, and περιβάλλω occur not only in the sense of putting on clothes but in the more generic sense of adorning the outward appearance of something, for these verbs may occur not only with persons who are literally clothed but also with plants which are made attractive.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 6:30; Matthew 11:8; Luke 7:25

both the totality of two In a number of languages ἀμφότεροι may be rendered as ‘the two.’

[MAT 9:17](https://ref.ly/Matt9:17) · [MAT 13:30](https://ref.ly/Matt13:30) · [MAT 15:14](https://ref.ly/Matt15:14) · [LUK 1:6](https://ref.ly/Mark1:6) · [LUK 1:7](https://ref.ly/Mark1:7) · [LUK 5:7](https://ref.ly/Mark5:7) · [LUK 6:39](https://ref.ly/Mark6:39) · [LUK 7:42](https://ref.ly/Mark7:42) · [ACT 8:38](https://ref.ly/John8:38) · [EPH 2:14](https://ref.ly/Gal2:14) · [EPH 2:16](https://ref.ly/Gal2:16) · [EPH 2:18](https://ref.ly/Gal2:18)

59\.26 · Quantity · All, Any, Each, Every \[Totality]

all, everyone all of a few (three or more) It is clear that in [ACT 19:16](https://ref.ly/John19:16) ἀμφότεροι must refer to the seven sons of a Jewish high priest named Sceva.

## ἄμωμον (id: 000299000000000)

ἄμωμον
======

ἄμωμον (G0298a)

noun

5\.23 · Foods and Condiments · Condiments

spice, amomum a generic term for any kind of spice, though often a specific reference to amomum, an Indian type of spice In most translations ἄμωμον is interpreted as spice in general, and though in all languages some types of herb\-derivative condiments are employed, there may be no general term for such spices. One may, however, in some instances use a descriptive phrase, for example, ‘good\-tasting herbs’ or ‘flavoring leaves.’

[REV 18:13](https://ref.ly/Jude18:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 18:13

blameless, without fault, faultless, perfect pertaining to being without fault and hence morally blameless

## ἀνά (id: 000304000000000)

ἀνά
===

ἀνά (G0303)

prep

89\.91 · Relations · Distribution

each, apiece, sets of (in combination with numerable objects) a marker of distributive relations involving numerable objects

[MAT 20:9](https://ref.ly/Matt20:9) · [MAT 20:10](https://ref.ly/Matt20:10) · [LUK 9:3](https://ref.ly/Mark9:3) · [LUK 9:14](https://ref.ly/Mark9:14) · [LUK 10:1](https://ref.ly/Mark10:1) · [JHN 2:6](https://ref.ly/Luke2:6) · [REV 4:8](https://ref.ly/Jude4:8) · [REV 21:21](https://ref.ly/Jude21:21)

61\.4 · Sequence

**Collocation:** ἀνὰ μέρος

in turn, in succession, one after another (an idiom, literally: up to a part, or: according to a part) a series in which each element follows in strict succession

[1CO 14:27](https://ref.ly/Rom14:27)

83\.9 · Spacial Positions · Among, Between, In, Inside

**Collocation:** ἀνὰ μέσον

among, with a position within an area determined by other objects and distributed among such objects1

[MAT 13:25](https://ref.ly/Matt13:25)

83\.10 · Spacial Positions · Among, Between, In, Inside

**Collocation:** ἀνὰ μέσον

in the middle, in the midst a position in the middle of an area (either an object in the midst of other objects or an area in the middle of a larger area)

[MRK 7:31](https://ref.ly/Matt7:31) · [REV 7:17](https://ref.ly/Jude7:17)

89\.143 · Relations · Mediation

**Collocation:** ἀνὰ μέσον

between, among a marker of mediation

[1CO 6:5](https://ref.ly/Rom6:5)

1 There are no doubt certain subtle distinctions in the meanings of the terms in [83\.9](#83.9) (μέσος\[a], ἀνὰ μέσον, ἐντός\[a], ἐν\[b], μετά\[h], εἰς\[e], ἐπί\[c], πρός\[d], παρά\[b]). For example, πρός\[d] may suggest some kind of interaction or response and παρά\[b] may focus upon association, but it is difficult, if not impossible, on the basis of existing contexts to define precisely the differences in meaning.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 20:9; Matthew 20:10; Luke 9:3; Luke 9:14; Luke 10:1; John 2:6; Revelation 4:8; Revelation 21:21; 1 Corinthians 14:27; Matthew 13:25; Mark 7:31; Revelation 7:17; 1 Corinthians 6:5

24\.10 · Sensory Events and States · See

to look up to direct one’s vision upward

[MAT 14:19](https://ref.ly/Matt14:19) · [MRK 6:41](https://ref.ly/Matt6:41) · [MRK 7:34](https://ref.ly/Matt7:34) · [MRK 8:24](https://ref.ly/Matt8:24) · [MRK 16:4](https://ref.ly/Matt16:4) · [LUK 9:16](https://ref.ly/Mark9:16) · [LUK 19:5](https://ref.ly/Mark19:5) · [LUK 21:1](https://ref.ly/Mark21:1) · [ACT 22:13](https://ref.ly/John22:13)

24\.42 · Sensory Events and States · See

to gain sight, to be able to see, to regain one’s sight, gaining of sight to become able to see, whether for the first time or again There is nothing in the statement of [LUK 7:22](https://ref.ly/Mark7:22) to indicate whether this is a matter of being able to see for the first time or being able to regain one’s lost sight. It is, however, better to assume that it is a matter of regaining sight unless the context specifies ‘being blind from birth’ or ‘always blind.’

## ἀναγεννάω (id: 000315000000000)

ἀναγεννάω
=========

ἀναγεννάω (G0313)

γεννάω

13\.55 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Change of State

to cause to be born again, to be given new birth (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀναγεννάω ‘to be physically born again,’ not occurring in the NT) to cause to be changed as a form of spiritual rebirth In a number of languages the use of ἀναγεννάω in the sense of ‘to be born again spiritually’ is extremely difficult for people to understand, even as it was in biblical times. However, the concept is such an integral element in so much NT thought and teaching that the expression should be retained, though it may very well require some brief marginal explanation or at least significant cross\-referencing.\|In its NT usage ἀναγεννάω, of course, has nothing to do with birth as such, but refers to a radical change in personality, with the attendant change in state, and it is for that reason that ἀναγεννάω is here classified in Domain 13 Be, Become, Exist, Happen. See also [41\.53](#41.53).

[1PE 1:3](https://ref.ly/Jas1:3) · [1PE 1:23](https://ref.ly/Jas1:23)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Peter 1:3; 1 Peter 1:23

37\.33 · Control, Rule · Compel, Force

to compel, to force to compel someone to act in a particular manner In some instances ἀναγκάζω and δέω\[c] may be rendered as ‘to cause it to be necessary for.’ In other instances a simple causative seems to be the closest equivalent. For example, the last clause of [GAL 2:14](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:14) may be expressed as ‘how can you make Gentiles live like Jews,’ and in [ACT 20:22](https://ref.ly/John20:22) one may translate ‘and now the Spirit makes me go to Jerusalem.’

to bring up, to lead up to bring or lead up

[MAT 4:1](https://ref.ly/Matt4:1) · [LUK 2:22](https://ref.ly/Mark2:22) · [LUK 4:5](https://ref.ly/Mark4:5) · [ACT 9:39](https://ref.ly/John9:39) · [ACT 12:4](https://ref.ly/John12:4)1 · [ACT 16:34](https://ref.ly/John16:34) · [ROM 10:7](https://ref.ly/Acts10:7) · [HEB 13:20](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:20)

15\.211 · Linear Movement · Carry, Bear

to offer to, to present to to bring an offering to2

[ACT 7:41](https://ref.ly/John7:41)

54\.4 · Maritime Activities

to set sail, to put out to sea to begin to go by boat In some languages a distinction is made in putting out to sea based upon whether the boat goes out from a beach and thus passes through waves or whether it goes out to sea from a harbor. In the various contexts of the book of Acts it is preferable to use a term which implies a harbor, while in contexts which speak of the Sea of Galilee, movement would normally be from a beach (see [54\.5](#54.5)).

[LUK 8:22](https://ref.ly/Mark8:22) · [ACT 13:13](https://ref.ly/John13:13) · [ACT 16:11](https://ref.ly/John16:11) · [ACT 18:21](https://ref.ly/John18:21) · [ACT 20:3](https://ref.ly/John20:3) · [ACT 20:13](https://ref.ly/John20:13) · [ACT 21:1](https://ref.ly/John21:1) · [ACT 21:2](https://ref.ly/John21:2) · [ACT 27:2](https://ref.ly/John27:2) · [ACT 27:4](https://ref.ly/John27:4) · [ACT 27:12](https://ref.ly/John27:12) · [ACT 27:21](https://ref.ly/John27:21) · [ACT 28:10](https://ref.ly/John28:10) · [ACT 28:11](https://ref.ly/John28:11)

1 ACT 12:4 One may assume from the use of ἀνάγω in [ACT 12:4](https://ref.ly/John12:4) that the prison was probably an underground prison, and Peter would then be led up to a courtroom which would be higher than the prison. 2 Though the Greek term ἀνάγω means literally ‘to lead up’ (see ἀνάγω\[a] [15\.176](#15.176)), it is not necessary in translating to always introduce the element of height or to translate it by some expression meaning ‘up.’ In ancient temples the idol or god was often on an elevated platform, and so one can readily understand how a term meaning ‘to bring up’ or ‘to carry up’ could readily acquire the meaning of ‘to carry up to’ or ‘to present to.’

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 4:1; Luke 2:22; Luke 4:5; Acts 9:39; Acts 12:4; Acts 16:34; Romans 10:7; Hebrews 13:20; Acts 7:41; Luke 8:22; Acts 13:13; Acts 16:11; Acts 18:21; Acts 20:3; Acts 20:13; Acts 21:1; Acts 21:2; Acts 27:2; Acts 27:4; Acts 27:12; Acts 27:21; Acts 28:10; Acts 28:11

## ἀναδέχομαι (id: 000326000000000)

ἀναδέχομαι
==========

ἀναδέχομαι (G0324)

δέχομαι

34\.53 · Association · Welcome, Receive

to welcome, to receive, to accept, to have as a guest to accept the presence of a person with friendliness1

[ACT 28:7](https://ref.ly/John28:7)

90\.75 · Case · Experiencer

to experience, to receive to experience something as the recipient of an event

[HEB 11:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:17)2

1 There are no doubt certain contextual constraints involved in the choice of ἀναδέχομαι\[a], ἀποδέχομαι\[a], ἐπιδέχομαι\[a], εἰσδέχομαι, ὑποδέχομαι, προσδέχομαι\[b], and παραδέχομαι\[b] in the sense that the spacial relationships involved in the welcoming of a guest can be reflected in the prepositional prefixes. For example, in [LUK 8:40](https://ref.ly/Mark8:40) the use of ἀποδέχομαι may focus upon the particular source, while ἐπιδέχομαι in [3JN 1:10](https://ref.ly/2John1:10) may be relevant in view of the fact that the believers are coming to a particular point. προσδέχομαι in [LUK 15:2](https://ref.ly/Mark15:2) may suggest a kind of reciprocal relationship which is established with outcasts, and παραδέχομαι in [ACT 15:4](https://ref.ly/John15:4) may simply point to spacial proximity. However, it would be wrong to insist upon clear distinctions in meaning based on the very limited number of contexts involved. Some of these same distinctions in spacial relations may exist for the set προσλαμβάνω\[e], παραλαμβάνω\[d], ἀπολαμβάνω\[d]. 2 HEB 11:17 Though one may render ἀναδέχομαι in [HEB 11:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:17) as ‘to receive,’ in reality the receiver is simply the individual who experiences either the event of promising or the content of the promises as expressed through subsequent events.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 28:7; Hebrews 11:17

## ἀναζάω (id: 000328000000000)

ἀναζάω
======

ἀναζάω (G0326)

ζάω

23\.93 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to come back to life, to live again, to be resurrected, resurrection to come back to life after having once died This word is also found as a variant reading: Χριστὸς καὶ ἀπέθανεν καὶ ἀνέστη καὶ ἀνέζησεν ‘Christ also died, rose, and lives again’ [ROM 14:9](https://ref.ly/Acts14:9) (apparatus).\|In a number of languages there is a difficulty involved in formulating some expression for ‘resurrection’ or ‘living again,’ since such a phrase may refer to what is technically known as metempsychosis, that is to say, the rebirth of the soul in another existence, a belief which is widely held in a number of areas of south Asia. This problem may be avoided in some languages by speaking of ‘his body will live again’ or ‘his body will come back to life’ or ‘he will be the same person when he lives again.’\|In [LUK 15:24](https://ref.ly/Mark15:24), ὅτι οὗτος ὁ υἱός μου νεκρὸς ἦν καὶ ἀνέζησεν ‘because this son of mine was dead and he has come back to life,’ the figurative hyperbole may reflect the practice of referring to a person as dead and then coming back to life if he has been completely separated for a time from all family relations, but then has later been discovered alive and well. It is possible, of course, that in [LUK 15:24](https://ref.ly/Mark15:24) the expression is an idiom, but it is more likely to be simply a figurative usage.

[LUK 15:24](https://ref.ly/Mark15:24) · [ROM 14:9](https://ref.ly/Acts14:9)

42\.6 · Perform, Do · Function

to begin to function, to spring to life, to suddenly be active (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀναζάω\[a] ‘to come back to life,’ [23\.93](#23.93)) to begin to function, with a possible implication of the suddenness of the action1

[ROM 7:9](https://ref.ly/Acts7:9)

1 It is possible that the prefix ἀνα in ἀναζάω\[b] could mean to come to life again, but in this particular context the force of repeated action seems to have been lost.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 15:24; Romans 14:9; Romans 7:9

to reactivate, to cause to begin to be active again (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀναζωπυρέω ‘to rekindle a fire,’ not occurring in the NT) to cause something to begin again It may also be possible to translate ἀναζωπυρέω in [2TI 1:6](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:6) as ‘to cause to take on new life.’ A few translators have used an expression such as ‘to keep alive the gift,’ primarily in order to avoid the impression that Timothy had in some measure departed from his earlier dedication to the gospel.

to adopt, adoption to formally and legally declare that someone who is not one’s own child is henceforth to be treated and cared for as one’s own child, including complete rights of inheritance In a number of languages adoption is spoken of as ‘making a child a son’ or ‘causing a child to become a daughter.’ In some instances the equivalent expression is merely ‘to treat someone as a son’ or ‘... daughter.’

## ἀνακάμπτω (id: 000346000000000)

ἀνακάμπτω
=========

ἀνακάμπτω (G0344)

κάμπτω

15\.89 · Linear Movement · Return

to move back, to return to move back to a point or area from which one has previously departed, but with more explicit emphasis upon the return (compare ὑποστρέφω\[a], [15\.88](#15.88)) In [LUK 10:6](https://ref.ly/Mark10:6) ἀνακάμπτω\[a] occurs in a somewhat figurative context, but it is difficult to determine precisely to what extent the use of this term suggests actual movement.1

[MAT 2:12](https://ref.ly/Matt2:12) · [LUK 10:6](https://ref.ly/Mark10:6) · [ACT 18:21](https://ref.ly/John18:21) · [HEB 11:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:15)

31\.64 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Change an Opinion Concerning Truth

to turn back again to, to change to a former belief, to turn back from (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀνακάμπτω\[a] to return [15\.89](#15.89)); to turn back to a previous belief

1 2PE 2:21 In [2PE 2:21](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:21) ἀνακάμπτω occurs in an apparatus variant in a figurative sense of returning to a previous state with regard to divine revelation. 2 2PE 2:21 The substitution of ἀνακάμπτω for ὑποστρέφω in certain manuscripts of [2PE 2:21](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:21) would indicate clearly how closely these terms seem to be related in meaning. Any distinctions of meaning which they may have had would evidently be connotative.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:12; Luke 10:6; Acts 18:21; Hebrews 11:15


## ἀνάκειμαι (id: 000347000000000)

ἀνάκειμαι
=========

ἀνάκειμαι (G0345)

κεῖμαι

17\.23 · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Recline \[to eat]

to recline, to eat, to be at table, to eat, to dine, to sit down to eat to be in a reclining position as one eats (with the focus either upon the position or the act of eating)

[MAT 22:10](https://ref.ly/Matt22:10) · [MAT 22:11](https://ref.ly/Matt22:11) · [MAT 26:7](https://ref.ly/Matt26:7) · [MAT 26:20](https://ref.ly/Matt26:20) · [MRK 14:18](https://ref.ly/Matt14:18) · [LUK 22:27](https://ref.ly/Mark22:27) · [LUK 22:27](https://ref.ly/Mark22:27) · [JHN 6:11](https://ref.ly/Luke6:11) · [JHN 12:2](https://ref.ly/Luke12:2) · [JHN 13:28](https://ref.ly/Luke13:28)

23\.21 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

to eat a meal to eat a meal, with possible reference to the fact of the people reclining to eat1 In biblical times there were apparently three different positions involved in the eating of a meal. People might, for example, (1\) be seated on cushions around a low table or (2\) be seated on chairs around a table or (3\) recline on couches facing a table. Meals which involved reclining on couches would normally have been more elaborate, but the verbs ἀνάκειμαι\[b] and κατάκειμαι\[c] have been generalized in meaning to denote the eating of a meal without necessarily specifying the particular reclining position of those who participated in the meal.\|For the translator, it may be extremely awkward to speak of ‘reclining to eat,’ since this may have connotations of people who are too lazy to sit up at the table or who are too sick to partake of an ordinary meal. It is for that reason that instead of rendering ἀνάκειμαι\[b] or κατάκειμαι\[c] as ‘to recline to eat,’ translators use expressions such as ‘to sit down to eat’ or ‘to eat a meal.’

[MAT 9:10](https://ref.ly/Matt9:10) · [MRK 6:26](https://ref.ly/Matt6:26) · [MRK 16:14](https://ref.ly/Matt16:14)

17\.25 · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Recline \[to eat]

**Collocation:** ἀνάκειμαι ἐν τῷ κόλπῳ

to dine in the place of honor (an idiom, literally: to recline on the bosom) to take the place of honor at a meal These expressions in [JHN 13:23](https://ref.ly/Luke13:23) and [JHN 13:25](https://ref.ly/Luke13:25) would mean that the so\-called ‘beloved disciple’ would be at the right of Jesus, since guests at a meal would be reclining on the left side so as to permit freedom of the right hand in dining. It may, of course, be that the same expression could be used of persons sitting at a table rather than reclining on couches.\|Literal translations of ἀνάκειμαι ἐν τῷ κόλπῳ and ἀναπίπτω ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος can result in serious misunderstanding, since they might imply overt homosexuality. There is no justification for reading into such idioms this type of implication.

[JHN 13:23](https://ref.ly/Luke13:23)

1 One would assume that ἀνάκειμαι\[b] and κατάκειμαι\[c] would have almost opposite meanings in view of the prefixes ἀνα and κατα. It is, of course, possible that ἀνάκειμαι\[b] refers to being propped up on a couch where one was eating and κατάκειμαι\[c] refers specifically to reclining, that is to say, lying down, but the contexts in which these two expressions occur do not provide a clear basis for such a distinction in meaning. In fact, the two are largely generalized to simply mean to eat a meal, though their etymological history may be significantly different.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 22:10; Matthew 22:11; Matthew 26:7; Matthew 26:20; Mark 14:18; Luke 22:27; John 6:11; John 12:2; John 13:28; Matthew 9:10; Mark 6:26; Mark 16:14; John 13:23

to bring together to bring everything together in terms of some unifying principle or person

## ἀνακλίνω (id: 000349000000000)

ἀνακλίνω
========

ἀνακλίνω (G0347) — ἀνακλίνομαι

κλίνω

17\.28 · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Lie

**Collocation:** ἀνακλίνομαι

to recline, to eat, to be at table, to eat, to dine, to sit down to eat to be in a reclining position as one eats (with the focus either upon the position or the act of eating)

## ἀνακρίνω (id: 000351000000000)

ἀνακρίνω
========

ἀνακρίνω (G0350)

κρίνω

27\.44 · Learn · Try To Learn

to examine carefully, to investigate, to study thoroughly to try to learn the nature or truth of something by the process of careful study, evaluation and judgment

[ACT 17:11](https://ref.ly/John17:11) · [1CO 2:14](https://ref.ly/Rom2:14) · [1CO 10:25](https://ref.ly/Rom10:25) · [1CO 10:27](https://ref.ly/Rom10:27)

56\.12 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Judicial Hearing, Inquiry

to investigate in court, to hear a case, to interrogate, to question to conduct a judicial inquiry The use of ἀνακρίνω in [1CO 9:3](https://ref.ly/Rom9:3) does not imply necessarily the strictly legal procedures of a courtroom. It is also possible to interpret ἀνακρίνω in [1CO 9:3](https://ref.ly/Rom9:3) as meaning ‘to criticize’ or ‘to judge’ (see [33\.412](#33.412)).

[LUK 23:14](https://ref.ly/Mark23:14) · [ACT 4:9](https://ref.ly/John4:9) · [ACT 12:19](https://ref.ly/John12:19) · [ACT 24:8](https://ref.ly/John24:8) · [ACT 28:18](https://ref.ly/John28:18) · [1CO 4:3](https://ref.ly/Rom4:3) · [1CO 4:4](https://ref.ly/Rom4:4) · [1CO 9:3](https://ref.ly/Rom9:3)

33\.412 · Communication · Criticize

to criticize to express disapproval of what someone has done For another interpretation of ἀνακρίνω in [1CO 9:3](https://ref.ly/Rom9:3), see [56\.12](#56.12).

[1CO 4:3](https://ref.ly/Rom4:3) · [1CO 4:4](https://ref.ly/Rom4:4) · [1CO 9:3](https://ref.ly/Rom9:3)

30\.109 · Think · To Distinguish, To Evaluate, To Judge

to judge carefully, to evaluate carefully to make a judgment on the basis of careful and detailed information1

[1CO 2:14](https://ref.ly/Rom2:14) · [1CO 2:15](https://ref.ly/Rom2:15) · [1CO 2:15](https://ref.ly/Rom2:15) · [1CO 4:3](https://ref.ly/Rom4:3) · [1CO 10:25](https://ref.ly/Rom10:25) · [1CO 10:27](https://ref.ly/Rom10:27) · [1CO 14:24](https://ref.ly/Rom14:24)

1 The meanings of κρίνω\[c] ([30\.108](#30.108)), ἀνακρίνω\[d] and διακρίνω\[a] ([30\.109](#30.109)) are all closely related to the process of learning and of understanding, and in some contexts the meanings shade one into the other.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 17:11; 1 Corinthians 2:14; 1 Corinthians 10:25; 1 Corinthians 10:27; Luke 23:14; Acts 4:9; Acts 12:19; Acts 24:8; Acts 28:18; 1 Corinthians 4:3; 1 Corinthians 4:4; 1 Corinthians 9:3; 1 Corinthians 2:15; 1 Corinthians 14:24

## ἀνάπαυσις (id: 000373000000000)

ἀνάπαυσις
=========

ἀνάπαυσις (G0372)

noun · παύω

68\.34 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to cease, to stop to cease from an activity in which one is engaged It is possible that ἀνάπαυσις in [REV 4:8](https://ref.ly/Jude4:8) should be interpreted as a period of cessation, but such a meaning would only be derivative of this particular context.

[REV 4:8](https://ref.ly/Jude4:8)

23\.80 · Physiological Processes and States · Tire, Rest

rest, to rest to become physically refreshed after ceasing activity or work In the use of ἀναπαύομαι\[a] and ἀνάπαυσις\[b], the focus of meaning seems to be upon the restorative character of rest rather than mere cessation of activity (see [68\.34](#68.34)).

[MAT 11:29](https://ref.ly/Matt11:29)

23\.87 · Physiological Processes and States · Tire, Rest

a place to rest (derivative of ἀναπαύομαι\[a] ‘to rest,’ [23\.80](#23.80)) a location for resting It is possible, however, to treat ἀνάπαυσις in [MAT 12:43](https://ref.ly/Matt12:43) as not being a place of rest, but simply a state of rest as in ἀνάπαυσις\[b] ([23\.80](#23.80)). Compare [MAT 11:29](https://ref.ly/Matt11:29), in which ἀνάπαυσις appears to refer to a state rather than to a location.

[MAT 12:43](https://ref.ly/Matt12:43) · [LUK 11:24](https://ref.ly/Mark11:24)

22\.37 · Trouble, Hardship, Relief, Favorable Circumstances · Relief from Trouble

relief relief from trouble and related anxiety

[MAT 11:29](https://ref.ly/Matt11:29) · [REV 14:11](https://ref.ly/Jude14:11)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 4:8; Matthew 11:29; Matthew 12:43; Luke 11:24; Revelation 14:11


## ἀναπαύω (id: 000374000000000)

ἀναπαύω
=======

ἀναπαύω (G0373) — ἀναπαύομαι

παύω

23\.84 · Physiological Processes and States · Tire, Rest

to cause to rest, to give rest to cause someone to become physically refreshed as the result of resting from work In some languages it may be difficult to speak of ‘causing someone to become refreshed by resting.’ Normally this would be accomplished simply by causing a person not to have to work. Accordingly, [MAT 11:28](https://ref.ly/Matt11:28) may be expressed in some languages as ‘I will make it possible for you no longer to have to work’ or ‘... to toil hard.’ This, however, must not be understood merely in the sense of ‘to give a person a vacation’ or ‘to make it possible for someone to live without working.’

[MAT 11:28](https://ref.ly/Matt11:28) · [1CO 16:18](https://ref.ly/Rom16:18) · [PHM 1:20](https://ref.ly/Titus1:20)

23\.80 · Physiological Processes and States · Tire, Rest

**Collocation:** ἀναπαύομαι

rest, to rest to become physically refreshed after ceasing activity or work In the use of ἀναπαύομαι\[a] and ἀνάπαυσις\[b], the focus of meaning seems to be upon the restorative character of rest rather than mere cessation of activity (see [68\.34](#68.34)).

[MAT 26:45](https://ref.ly/Matt26:45) · [MRK 6:31](https://ref.ly/Matt6:31) · [MRK 14:41](https://ref.ly/Matt14:41) · [LUK 12:19](https://ref.ly/Mark12:19) · [2CO 7:13](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:13) · [PHM 1:7](https://ref.ly/Titus1:7) · [1PE 4:14](https://ref.ly/Jas4:14) · [REV 14:13](https://ref.ly/Jude14:13)

85\.58 · Existence in Space · Remain, Stay

**Collocation:** ἀναπαύομαι

to remain, to abide to remain in a place, with the implication of continuing to rest

[REV 6:11](https://ref.ly/Jude6:11)

13\.25 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · State

**Collocation:** ἀναπαύομαι

to remain on, to continue to be on to continue to be in a state in or on someone, with the implication of beneficent result

[1PE 4:14](https://ref.ly/Jas4:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 11:28; 1 Corinthians 16:18; Philemon 1:20; Matthew 26:45; Mark 6:31; Mark 14:41; Luke 12:19; 2 Corinthians 7:13; Philemon 1:7; 1 Peter 4:14; Revelation 14:13; Revelation 6:11

πίπτω

17\.23 · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Recline \[to eat]

to recline, to eat, to be at table, to eat, to dine, to sit down to eat to be in a reclining position as one eats (with the focus either upon the position or the act of eating)

[MAT 15:35](https://ref.ly/Matt15:35) · [MRK 6:40](https://ref.ly/Matt6:40) · [MRK 8:6](https://ref.ly/Matt8:6) · [LUK 11:37](https://ref.ly/Mark11:37) · [LUK 14:10](https://ref.ly/Mark14:10) · [LUK 17:7](https://ref.ly/Mark17:7) · [LUK 22:14](https://ref.ly/Mark22:14) · [JHN 6:10](https://ref.ly/Luke6:10) · [JHN 6:10](https://ref.ly/Luke6:10) · [JHN 13:12](https://ref.ly/Luke13:12) · [JHN 21:20](https://ref.ly/Luke21:20)

17\.25 · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Recline \[to eat]

**Collocation:** ἀναπίπτω ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος

to dine in the place of honor (an idiom, literally: to recline on the bosom) to take the place of honor at a meal These expressions in [JHN 13:23](https://ref.ly/Luke13:23) and [JHN 13:25](https://ref.ly/Luke13:25) would mean that the so\-called beloved disciple would be at the right of Jesus, since guests at a meal would be reclining on the left side so as to permit freedom of the right hand in dining. It may, of course, be that the same expression could be used of persons sitting at a table rather than reclining on couches.\|Literal translations of ἀνάκειμαι ἐν τῷ κόλπῳ and ἀναπίπτω ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος can result in serious misunderstanding, since they might imply overt homosexuality. There is no justification for reading into such idioms this type of implication.\|This expression (ἀναπεσὼν ... ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος) in [JHN 13:25](https://ref.ly/Luke13:25) (following on ἀνακείμενος ... ἐν τῷ κόλπῳ in [JHN 13:23](https://ref.ly/Luke13:23)) seems to be typical of the tendency of the Gospel of John to employ relatively close synonyms with essentially the same meaning. It is possible, however, in view of the aorist participle in [JHN 13:25](https://ref.ly/Luke13:25) that the phrase ἀναπεσὼν ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος could also be understood in a more literal sense to mean ‘to move closer to the side (of Jesus).’

[JHN 13:25](https://ref.ly/Luke13:25)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 15:35; Mark 6:40; Mark 8:6; Luke 11:37; Luke 14:10; Luke 17:7; Luke 22:14; John 6:10; John 13:12; John 21:20; John 13:25

πληρόω

[1TH 2:16](https://ref.ly/Col2:16)

## ἀνάπτω (id: 000383000000000)

======

ἅπτω

14\.65 · Physical Events and States · Burning

to ignite, to kindle, to set ablaze, to start a fire, to light a lamp to cause the process of burning to begin As already noted in the series of terms listed in [14\.63](#14.63), languages differ appreciably in terms employed for different phases and results of burning. For example, to set something on fire for the sake of providing warmth may be expressed by quite a different term from that which is used in speaking of the process of setting something on fire in order to destroy it.\|It is possible that ἀνάπτω differs from ἅπτω either in the intensity of the activity or with special emphasis upon the initial phase of setting something on fire.

## ἀνάστασις (id: 000388000000000)

ἀνάστασις
=========

ἀνάστασις (G0386)

noun · ἵστημι

23\.93 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to come back to life, to live again, to be resurrected, resurrection to come back to life after having once died In a number of languages there is a difficulty involved in formulating some expression for ‘resurrection’ or ‘living again,’ since such a phrase may refer to what is technically known as metempsychosis, that is to say, the rebirth of the soul in another existence, a belief which is widely held in a number of areas of south Asia. This problem may be avoided in some languages by speaking of ‘his body will live again’ or ‘his body will come back to life’ or ‘he will be the same person when he lives again.’\|One may also translate ‘there is no resurrection’ as ‘people will not live again.’

[MAT 22:23](https://ref.ly/Matt22:23) · [MAT 22:28](https://ref.ly/Matt22:28) · [MAT 22:30](https://ref.ly/Matt22:30) · [MAT 22:31](https://ref.ly/Matt22:31) · [MRK 12:18](https://ref.ly/Matt12:18) · [MRK 12:23](https://ref.ly/Matt12:23) · [LUK 14:14](https://ref.ly/Mark14:14) · [LUK 20:27](https://ref.ly/Mark20:27) · [LUK 20:33](https://ref.ly/Mark20:33) · [LUK 20:35](https://ref.ly/Mark20:35) · [LUK 20:36](https://ref.ly/Mark20:36) · [JHN 5:29](https://ref.ly/Luke5:29) · [JHN 5:29](https://ref.ly/Luke5:29) · [JHN 11:24](https://ref.ly/Luke11:24) · [JHN 11:25](https://ref.ly/Luke11:25) · [ACT 1:22](https://ref.ly/John1:22) · [ACT 2:31](https://ref.ly/John2:31) · [ACT 4:2](https://ref.ly/John4:2) · [ACT 4:33](https://ref.ly/John4:33) · [ACT 17:18](https://ref.ly/John17:18) · [ACT 17:32](https://ref.ly/John17:32) · [ACT 23:6](https://ref.ly/John23:6) · [ACT 23:8](https://ref.ly/John23:8) · [ACT 24:15](https://ref.ly/John24:15) · [ACT 24:21](https://ref.ly/John24:21) · [ACT 26:23](https://ref.ly/John26:23) · [ROM 1:4](https://ref.ly/Acts1:4) · [ROM 6:5](https://ref.ly/Acts6:5) · [1CO 15:12](https://ref.ly/Rom15:12) · [1CO 15:13](https://ref.ly/Rom15:13) · [1CO 15:21](https://ref.ly/Rom15:21) · [1CO 15:42](https://ref.ly/Rom15:42) · [PHP 3:10](https://ref.ly/Eph3:10) · [2TI 2:18](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:18) · [HEB 6:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:2) · [HEB 11:35](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:35) · [HEB 11:35](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:35) · [1PE 1:3](https://ref.ly/Jas1:3) · [1PE 3:21](https://ref.ly/Jas3:21) · [REV 20:5](https://ref.ly/Jude20:5) · [REV 20:6](https://ref.ly/Jude20:6)

87\.39 · Status · High Status or Rank \[including persons of high status]

to rise, to rise up, rising up a process of change from a lower to a higher status (note the contrast with πτῶσις\[b] ‘a falling,’ [87\.75](#87.75)) ἀνάστασις in [LUK 2:34](https://ref.ly/Mark2:34) may also be interpreted merely as a change for the better (see [13\.60](#13.60)).\|Reversals of rank and status are a frequent theme in the declaration of the prophets, and this becomes particularly significant in the Magnificat (see [LUK 1:46](https://ref.ly/Mark1:46)).

[LUK 2:34](https://ref.ly/Mark2:34)

13\.60 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Change of State

rising up a change for the better For another interpretation of ἀνάστασις in [LUK 2:34](https://ref.ly/Mark2:34), see [87\.39](#87.39).

[LUK 2:34](https://ref.ly/Mark2:34)

## ἀνασταυρόω (id: 000390000000000)

ἀνασταυρόω
==========

ἀνασταυρόω (G0388)

σταυρόω

20\.77 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Kill

to crucify again to cause to die on a cross a second time In extrabiblical Greek ἀνασταυρόω is essentially equivalent in meaning to σταυρόω ([20\.76](#20.76)), but in [HEB 6:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:6) ἀνασταυρόω is used figuratively of believers whose sin causes Christ to be crucified again in the sense of exposing Christ to public shame by virtue of the misdeeds of his professed followers. Some scholars, however, interpret ἀνασταυρόω in [HEB 6:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:6) as referring simply to the original crucifixion and thus interpret ἀνασταυρόω as being essentially synonymous with σταυρόω.

## ἀνατέλλω (id: 000395000000000)

ἀνατέλλω
========

ἀνατέλλω (G0393)

τέλλω · ἀνατολή · ἐξανατέλλω

15\.104 · Linear Movement · Come/Go Up, Ascend

to come up, to move upward, to rise to move up, especially of the upward movement of the sun, stars, or clouds

[MAT 5:45](https://ref.ly/Matt5:45) · [MAT 13:6](https://ref.ly/Matt13:6) · [MRK 4:6](https://ref.ly/Matt4:6) · [MRK 16:2](https://ref.ly/Matt16:2) · [LUK 12:54](https://ref.ly/Mark12:54) · [JAS 1:11](https://ref.ly/Heb1:11)

14\.41 · Physical Events and States · Light

to dawn, to become light to change from darkness to light in the early morning hours In some languages dawning is divided into more than one aspect or period of time. The first may be only a glimmering of light in the east; the second period makes visibility quite easy; and the third period involves shafts of light or colored clouds visible in the sky immediately before sunrise. Usually an expression for one of these periods of time is more generalized in usage, and therefore it is probably satisfactory to adopt such an expression for any transition from darkness to light.

[MAT 4:16](https://ref.ly/Matt4:16) · [2PE 1:19](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:19)

10\.35 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

be a descendant, offspring (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀνατέλλω\[a] ‘to rise,’ as of the sun, [15\.104](#15.104)) to be an important descendant, presumably of a distinguished ancestor, normally with a number of intervening generations

[HEB 7:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 5:45; Matthew 13:6; Mark 4:6; Mark 16:2; Luke 12:54; James 1:11; Matthew 4:16; 2 Peter 1:19; Hebrews 7:14

## ἀνατολή (id: 000397000000000)

ἀνατολή
=======

ἀνατολή (G0395)

noun · ἀνατέλλω

15\.104 · Linear Movement · Come/Go Up, Ascend

to come up, to move upward, to rise to move up, especially of the upward movement of the sun, stars, or clouds It is also possible to understand ἀνατολή in [MAT 2:2](https://ref.ly/Matt2:2) as meaning ‘the east’ (see ἀνατολή\[b], [82\.1](#82.1)).

[MAT 2:2](https://ref.ly/Matt2:2)

82\.1 · Spacial Orientations · North, South, East, West

east east (as the direction of the rising sun) In most languages east and west are related to the rising or setting sun, although in some languages there may be some local geographical feature which serves as a marker of direction, for example, the location of a particular mountain, the ocean, or a river. For north, some languages employ ‘to the left of the rising sun,’ that is to say, the direction is oriented in terms of the left hand of a person facing toward the rising sun. Similarly, south would be ‘to the right of the rising sun.’

[MAT 2:1](https://ref.ly/Matt2:1) · [MAT 2:2](https://ref.ly/Matt2:2) · [MAT 2:9](https://ref.ly/Matt2:9) · [MAT 8:11](https://ref.ly/Matt8:11) · [MAT 24:27](https://ref.ly/Matt24:27) · [MRK 16:8](https://ref.ly/Matt16:8) · [LUK 13:29](https://ref.ly/Mark13:29) · [REV 7:2](https://ref.ly/Jude7:2) · [REV 16:12](https://ref.ly/Jude16:12) · [REV 21:13](https://ref.ly/Jude21:13)

14\.42 · Physical Events and States · Light

**Collocation:** ἀνατολὴ ἐξ ὕψους

the dawn from on high (an idiom, literally: dawning from on high) the bright dawn of salvation (or possibly to be understood as a title for the Messiah) The expression ἀνατολὴ ἐξ ὕψους occurs in a highly poetic liturgical passage in Luke 1, and many translators have preferred to retain the more or less literal rendering. Others, however, have interpreted this as the dawning of salvation and have therefore translated ἐπισκέψεται ἡμᾶς ἀνατολὴ ἐξ ὕψους as ‘salvation will dawn upon us from heaven’ or ‘from heaven salvation will come to us as the dawn does.’ If one wishes to employ ἀνατολὴ ἐξ ὕψους as simply a title for the Messiah, one can translate ‘the Messiah will come to us,’ but it is normally better to preserve the figurative expression and relate it to the coming of the Messiah as ‘the Messiah will come to us even as the dawn does from on high.’ One may also treat the problem of interpretation in a footnote.

[LUK 1:78](https://ref.ly/Mark1:78)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:2; Matthew 2:1; Matthew 2:9; Matthew 8:11; Matthew 24:27; Mark 16:8; Luke 13:29; Revelation 7:2; Revelation 16:12; Revelation 21:13; Luke 1:78

ἀνατρέφω
========

ἀνατρέφω (G0397)

τρέφω

35\.51 · Help, Care For · Rear, Bring Up

to raise, to rear, to bring up to raise a child to maturity by providing for physical and psychological needs1 To raise or bring up children may be expressed in a number of different ways, for example, ‘to give food to children,’ ‘to take care of children,’ ‘to be a parent to children,’ ‘to cause a child to become an adult,’ or ‘to make a child become like oneself.’

[ACT 7:20](https://ref.ly/John7:20) · [ACT 7:21](https://ref.ly/John7:21)

33\.232 · Communication · Teach

**Collocation:** ἀνατρέφω παρὰ τοὺς πόδας

to be taught by, to be educated under the direction of (an idiom, literally: to be trained at the feet of) to be given extensive and formal instruction by someone In a number of languages the closest equivalent of this part of [ACT 22:3](https://ref.ly/John22:3) is simply ‘Gamaliel was his teacher.’

[ACT 22:3](https://ref.ly/John22:3)

1 There may very well be subtle differences of meaning in the series in [35\.51](#35.51), but there is no direct contextual evidence to support significant distinctions in meaning.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 7:20; Acts 7:21; Acts 22:3


## ἀναφαίνω (id: 000400000000000)

ἀναφαίνω
========

ἀναφαίνω (G0398)

φαίνω

24\.23 · Sensory Events and States · See

to come to be seen, to appear, to come into view to come to a point of being visible, with focus upon the process of becoming seen In a number of languages there may be difficulties involved in speaking of ‘the kingdom of God appearing.’ It may not be difficult to speak of a ‘person appearing,’ because an individual may ‘show himself’ or ‘cause himself to be seen.’ It may therefore be necessary to render this expression in [LUK 19:11](https://ref.ly/Mark19:11) as ‘that soon people will be seeing the kingdom of God’ or ‘... experiencing the kingdom of God’ or ‘... become aware of the kingdom of God.’

[LUK 19:11](https://ref.ly/Mark19:11) · [ACT 21:3](https://ref.ly/John21:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 19:11; Acts 21:3

excessive, extreme (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀνάχυσις ‘flood,’ not occurring in the NT) an extremely high point on a scale of extent and implying an excess of something with negative value In some languages the equivalent of this phrase in [1PE 4:4](https://ref.ly/Jas4:4) is ‘they lived very bad lives’ or ‘there was nothing bad that they didn’t do.’

========

χωρέω

15\.53 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to withdraw, to retire, to go off, to go away to move away from a location, implying a considerable distance

[MAT 2:14](https://ref.ly/Matt2:14) · [MAT 2:22](https://ref.ly/Matt2:22) · [MAT 9:24](https://ref.ly/Matt9:24) · [MAT 12:15](https://ref.ly/Matt12:15) · [MAT 14:13](https://ref.ly/Matt14:13) · [MAT 15:21](https://ref.ly/Matt15:21) · [MAT 27:5](https://ref.ly/Matt27:5) · [MRK 3:7](https://ref.ly/Matt3:7) · [JHN 6:15](https://ref.ly/Luke6:15) · [ACT 23:19](https://ref.ly/John23:19) · [ACT 26:31](https://ref.ly/John26:31)

15\.89 · Linear Movement · Return

to move back, to return to move back to a point or area from which one has previously departed, but with more explicit emphasis upon the return (compare ὑποστρέφω\[a], [15\.88](#15.88))

[MAT 2:12](https://ref.ly/Matt2:12)1 · [MAT 2:13](https://ref.ly/Matt2:13) · [MAT 4:12](https://ref.ly/Matt4:12)

1 MAT 2:12 It is possible that ἀναχωρέω in [MAT 2:12](https://ref.ly/Matt2:12) means only ‘to depart,’ ‘to withdraw,’ and is thus equivalent to ἀναχωρέω\[a] ([15\.53](#15.53)), but it is also possible that ἀναχωρέω\[b] differs from the other verbs in this series ([15\.89](#15.89)) by focusing attention upon the distance involved.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:14; Matthew 2:22; Matthew 9:24; Matthew 12:15; Matthew 14:13; Matthew 15:21; Matthew 27:5; Mark 3:7; John 6:15; Acts 23:19; Acts 26:31; Matthew 2:12; Matthew 2:13; Matthew 4:12


## ἀνάψυξις (id: 000405000000000)

ἀνάψυξις
========

ἀνάψυξις (G0403)

noun · ψύχομαι

22\.35 · Trouble, Hardship, Relief, Favorable Circumstances · Relief from Trouble

relief, breathing space relief from distressful, burdensome circumstances In [ACT 3:20](https://ref.ly/John3:20) the phrase καιροὶ ἀναψύξεως is generally regarded as a reference to the Messianic age.\|In a number of languages it may be extremely difficult to speak of ‘relief coming from the Lord.’ It is easy enough to speak of animate beings ‘coming,’ but to have a state of relief ‘coming’ may be semantically impossible. However, the entire expression in [ACT 3:20](https://ref.ly/John3:20) may be restructured as ‘so that the Lord may cause you to have relief from trouble’ or ‘... cause you to no longer be troubled.’ For another interpretation of ἀνάψυξις in [ACT 3:20](https://ref.ly/John3:20), see [25\.148](#25.148).

[ACT 3:20](https://ref.ly/John3:20)

25\.148 · Attitudes and Emotions · Encouragement, Consolation

refreshing, encouragement, recovery of happiness a state of cheer and encouragement after a period of having been troubled or upset For another interpretation of ἀνάψυξις in [ACT 3:20](https://ref.ly/John3:20), see [22\.35](#22.35).

[ACT 3:20](https://ref.ly/John3:20)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 3:20

to encourage, to cheer up to cause someone to recover a state of cheer or encouragement after a time of anxiety and trouble ἀναψύχω may be rendered idiomatically in a number of instances, for example, ‘to give one’s heart back to a person’ or ‘to make one’s heart feel strong again.’

## ἄνεμος (id: 000420000000000)

ἄνεμος
======

ἄνεμος (G0417)

noun · ἀνεμίζομαι

14\.4 · Physical Events and States · Wind

wind, blowing, to blow air in relatively rapid movement, but without specification as to the force of the movement As in the case of English (which has, for example, such terms as breeze, zephyr, gust, storm, whirlwind, cyclone, tornado, etc.), other languages likewise normally have a number of different terms depending upon the strength of the wind and whether the wind may be carrying substances such as sand or dust. In some languages there may also be a problem involved in the use of terms for ‘wind,’ since some of these may have meanings of malicious or malevolent spirits, or they may imply a disease\-bringing event.

[MAT 7:25](https://ref.ly/Matt7:25) · [MAT 7:27](https://ref.ly/Matt7:27) · [MAT 8:26](https://ref.ly/Matt8:26) · [MAT 8:27](https://ref.ly/Matt8:27) · [MAT 11:7](https://ref.ly/Matt11:7) · [MAT 14:24](https://ref.ly/Matt14:24) · [MAT 14:30](https://ref.ly/Matt14:30) · [MAT 14:32](https://ref.ly/Matt14:32) · [MAT 24:31](https://ref.ly/Matt24:31) · [MRK 4:37](https://ref.ly/Matt4:37) · [MRK 4:39](https://ref.ly/Matt4:39) · [MRK 4:39](https://ref.ly/Matt4:39) · [MRK 4:41](https://ref.ly/Matt4:41) · [MRK 6:48](https://ref.ly/Matt6:48) · [MRK 6:51](https://ref.ly/Matt6:51) · [MRK 13:27](https://ref.ly/Matt13:27) · [LUK 7:24](https://ref.ly/Mark7:24) · [LUK 8:23](https://ref.ly/Mark8:23) · [LUK 8:24](https://ref.ly/Mark8:24) · [LUK 8:25](https://ref.ly/Mark8:25) · [JHN 6:18](https://ref.ly/Luke6:18) · [ACT 27:4](https://ref.ly/John27:4) · [ACT 27:7](https://ref.ly/John27:7) · [ACT 27:14](https://ref.ly/John27:14) · [ACT 27:15](https://ref.ly/John27:15) · [EPH 4:14](https://ref.ly/Gal4:14) · [JAS 3:4](https://ref.ly/Heb3:4) · [JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12) · [REV 6:13](https://ref.ly/Jude6:13) · [REV 7:1](https://ref.ly/Jude7:1) · [REV 7:1](https://ref.ly/Jude7:1)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 7:25; Matthew 7:27; Matthew 8:26; Matthew 8:27; Matthew 11:7; Matthew 14:24; Matthew 14:30; Matthew 14:32; Matthew 24:31; Mark 4:37; Mark 4:39; Mark 4:41; Mark 6:48; Mark 6:51; Mark 13:27; Luke 7:24; Luke 8:23; Luke 8:24; Luke 8:25; John 6:18; Acts 27:4; Acts 27:7; Acts 27:14; Acts 27:15; Ephesians 4:14; James 3:4; Jude 1:12; Revelation 6:13; Revelation 7:1

==========

adj · δέχομαι

71\.5 · Mode · Possible, Impossible

impossible pertaining to not being possible, in the sense of not being in accord with human experience In view of the double negation in [LUK 17:1](https://ref.ly/Mark17:1), it may be better to translate ‘things that make people fall into sin are bound to happen.’ The derivative meaning of ἀνένδεκτος in the context of [LUK 17:1](https://ref.ly/Mark17:1) is thus a matter of inevitability as based upon human experience (see [71\.38](#71.38)).

## ἀνεξεραύνητος (id: 000422000000000)

=============

adj · ἐραυνάω

32\.23 · Understand · Ease or Difficulty in Understanding

impossible to understand, unfathomable, impossible to comprehend pertaining to being impossible to understand on the basis of careful examination or investigation

[ROM 11:33](https://ref.ly/Acts11:33)1

1 ROM 11:33 It is possible that ἀνεξεραύνητος and ἀνεξιχνίαστος are somewhat different in meaning, but they appear to be used in [ROM 11:33](https://ref.ly/Acts11:33) as very close synonyms. From a semotactic standpoint, however, it is easy to see why ἀνεξεραύνητα would be combined with κρίματα and ἀνεξιχνίαστοι would be combined with ὁδοί.

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 11:33

=======

ἀνετάζω (G0426)

ἐτάζω

56\.16 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Judicial Hearing, Inquiry

to interrogate, to examine to interrogate a defendant during a judicial hearing, often by means of torture or lashing In the Roman legal system it was customary to use various forms of physical torture in order to extract confessions from persons who were not Romans and who had been charged with serious crimes, particularly crimes against the state.

## ἄνευ (id: 000430000000000)

ἄνευ
====

ἄνευ (G0427)

prep

89\.120 · Relations · Dissociation

without, not with, no relationship to, apart from, independent of a marker of negatively linked elements1 The phrase ἄνευ τοῦ πατρὸς ὑμῶν in [MAT 10:29](https://ref.ly/Matt10:29) is elliptical, for it presumes some type of involvement by God in such an event. Some have interpreted the phrase ἄνευ τοῦ πατρὸς ὑμῶν as meaning ‘without your Father’s consent,’ while others interpret it as meaning ‘without your Father’s knowledge.’ The particular manner or mode of involvement by God must depend upon the broader context and not upon the meaning of ἄνευ.\|The phrase ἄνευ λόγου in [1PE 3:1](https://ref.ly/Jas3:1) is semantically equivalent to an event, for it means essentially ‘without their saying a word’ or ‘without them saying anything.’

[MAT 10:29](https://ref.ly/Matt10:29) · [1PE 3:1](https://ref.ly/Jas3:1) · [1PE 4:9](https://ref.ly/Jas4:9)

1 It appears impossible to find a significant distinction between ἄνευ and ἄτερ. It may be that χωρίς\[a] differs from ἄνευ and ἄτερ in focusing upon a greater degree of separation or lack of involvement.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 10:29; 1 Peter 3:1; 1 Peter 4:9

## ἀνεψιός (id: 000434000000000)

ἀνεψιός
=======

ἀνεψιός (G0431)

noun

10\.52 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations of the Same Generation

cousin the child of one’s uncle and aunt, either on the fathers side or the mothers side Some languages make a clear distinction between so\-called ‘parallel cousins’ and ‘cross cousins.’ Parallel cousins would be the offspring of one’s father’s brother or of one’s mother’s sister, while cross cousins would be the offspring of one’s father’s sister or of one’s mother’s brother. There is no way of knowing from the context of [COL 4:10](https://ref.ly/Phil4:10) what the specific relationship is between Mark and Barnabas, but for languages which have no general term for cousins, but only specific terms for parallel cousins or cross cousins, it is necessary to select one or another of these specific terms in speaking of Mark. By means of a footnote one can indicate that the Greek text does not specify which type of cousin is involved.

[COL 4:10](https://ref.ly/Phil4:10)

* **Associated Passages:** Colossians 4:10

noun

3\.24 · Plants · Plants That Are Not Trees

dill annual plant that can reach 50 cm in height; has fine leaves; yellow flowers form a shape like a covered cup; leaves and seeds have a pleasant, spicy smell; Anethum graveolens; garden herb, used in cooking In designating ‘dill’ as well as ἡδύοσμον ‘mint,’ πήγανον ‘rue,’ and κύμινον ‘cumin,’ it is probably wise to borrow a term and use a classifier, for example, ‘a seasoning herb called dill.’

[MAT 23:23](https://ref.ly/Matt23:23)

11:14](https://ref.ly/Rom11:14) · [1CO 13:11](https://ref.ly/Rom13:11) · [1TI 2:8](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:8) · [1TI 2:12](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:12) · [JAS 1:8](https://ref.ly/Heb1:8) · [JAS 2:2](https://ref.ly/Heb2:2)

9\.1 · People · Human Beings

(in the singular) person, human being, individual, (in the plural) people, persons, mankind a human being (normally an adult) In [MAT 14:35](https://ref.ly/Matt14:35) (ἐπιγνόντες αὐτὸν οἱ ἄνδρες τοῦ τόπου ἐκείνου ‘when the people of that place recognized him’) one may argue that οἱ ἄνδρες refers specifically to males, but the context would seem to indicate that the reference is to people in general. In translation a pronominal expression may frequently be employed when ἀνήρ\[b] occurs in the text, for example, ‘when those who lived in that place recognized him.’\|It is not uncommon in languages for a term which is often used to refer to an adult male to be employed also in a generic sense of ‘person.’ This is especially true when such terms are used in the plural form.\|In a number of instances, generic meanings of receptor languages have not been recognized as such, since translators have assumed that such receptor\-language terms refer only to members of a particular tribe or group (because they have been found only in specific contexts), while in reality such terms often designate people in general. One must, however, be on the alert for seemingly generic terms which refer only to the so\-called ‘in\-group,’ that is to say, members of a particular tribe, society, or community.\|The parallelism in the [ROM 4:8](https://ref.ly/Acts4:8) quotation from [PSA 32:1](https://ref.ly/Ps32:1)\-[PSA 32:2](https://ref.ly/Ps32:2) indicates clearly that the reference of ἀνήρ is not a particular male but any person.

10\.53 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Based upon Marriage

husband a man who is married to a woman ἀνήρ in the meaning of ‘husband’ is normally clearly marked by context, usually involving a so\- called ‘possessive marker,’ though in some contexts such as [JHN 4:17](https://ref.ly/Luke4:17) and [MAT 19:10](https://ref.ly/Matt19:10), the distinctions in meaning are quite clear, but it is only from each individual context that one can determine whether ἀνήρ means simply ‘man’ (see [9\.24](#9.24)) or ‘husband.’ See also discussion at [10\.54](#10.54).

## ἀνθίστημι (id: 000439000000000)

ἀνθίστημι
=========

ἀνθίστημι (G0436)

ἵστημι

39\.1 · Hostility, Strife · Opposition, Hostility

to oppose, to be hostile toward, to show hostility to oppose someone, involving not only a psychological attitude but also a corresponding behavior1 In translating this phrase in [GAL 2:11](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:11) it may be appropriate in some languages to say ‘I spoke against him with other people present.’

[ACT 13:8](https://ref.ly/John13:8) · [GAL 2:11](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:11) · [2TI 3:8](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:8) · [2TI 3:8](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:8) · [2TI 4:15](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:15)

39\.18 · Hostility, Strife · Resistance

to resist to resist by actively opposing pressure or power In a number of languages one may appropriately translate ‘to resist’ in a number of contexts as ‘to fight back against’ or ‘to oppose in return.’ In some contexts, however, such as [ACT 7:51](https://ref.ly/John7:51), the meaning may perhaps be best expressed by a negation, for example, ‘you have never been willing to yield to the Holy Spirit.’

[MAT 5:39](https://ref.ly/Matt5:39) · [LUK 21:15](https://ref.ly/Mark21:15) · [ACT 6:10](https://ref.ly/John6:10) · [ROM 9:19](https://ref.ly/Acts9:19) · [ROM 13:2](https://ref.ly/Acts13:2) · [ROM 13:2](https://ref.ly/Acts13:2) · [EPH 6:13](https://ref.ly/Gal6:13) · [JAS 4:7](https://ref.ly/Heb4:7) · [1PE 5:9](https://ref.ly/Jas5:9)

1 There are probably certain significant distinctions in meaning in the various verbs listed in [39\.1](#39.1), but on the basis of NT usage it is not possible to determine precisely what these distinctions may be.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 13:8; Galatians 2:11; 2 Timothy 3:8; 2 Timothy 4:15; Matthew 5:39; Luke 21:15; Acts 6:10; Romans 9:19; Romans 13:2; Ephesians 6:13; James 4:7; 1 Peter 5:9


## ἀνθομολογέομαι (id: 000440000000000)

ἀνθομολογέομαι
==============

ἀνθομολογέομαι (G0437)

33\.351 · Communication · Thanks

to thank, to give thanks to to acknowledge one’s thankfulness, restricted in NT usage to contexts in which God is the one being thanked

[LUK 2:38](https://ref.ly/Mark2:38)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 2:38


## ἄνθος (id: 000441000000000)

ἄνθος
=====

ἄνθος (G0438)

noun

3\.56 · Plants · Non\-Fruit Parts of Plants

flower the blossom of a plant In some languages it may be difficult to use generic expressions such as ‘flower’ or ‘plant.’ One may therefore be obliged to use a more specific designation of a type of flower which lasts for only a day or so. In [JAS 1:10](https://ref.ly/Heb1:10) it is the fragile and temporary nature of the blossom which is important in this figurative context.

[JAS 1:10](https://ref.ly/Heb1:10) · [JAS 1:11](https://ref.ly/Heb1:11) · [1PE 1:24](https://ref.ly/Jas1:24) · [1PE 1:24](https://ref.ly/Jas1:24)

* **Associated Passages:** James 1:10; James 1:11; 1 Peter 1:24


## ἀνθρακιά (id: 000442000000000)

ἀνθρακιά
========

ἀνθρακιά (G0439)

noun · ἄνθραξ

2\.6 · Natural Substances · Fire

charcoal fire The occurrence of ἀνθρακιά ‘charcoal fire’ in [JHN 18:18](https://ref.ly/Luke18:18) fits the context well, since a charcoal fire provides a maximum of heat with a minimum of smoke. The making and use of charcoal is widespread throughout the world, and there is generally no difficulty in obtaining a satisfactory term for ‘charcoal.’ However, where such a term does not exist, it is possible to speak of ‘a wood fire’ or simply ‘a fire.’

[JHN 18:18](https://ref.ly/Luke18:18) · [JHN 21:9](https://ref.ly/Luke21:9)

* **Associated Passages:** John 18:18; John 21:9


## ἄνθραξ (id: 000443000000000)

ἄνθραξ
======

ἄνθραξ (G0440)

noun · ἀνθρακιά

3\.67 · Plants · Wood and Wood Products

charcoal ἄνθραξ occurs in the NT only in [ROM 12:20](https://ref.ly/Acts12:20) in the adage ἄνθρακας πυρὸς σωρεύσεις ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν αὐτοῦ ‘heap coals of fire on his head.’ This idiomatic expression probably refers to making someone blush with shame and remorse (see [25\.199](#25.199)).\|In rendering ἄνθρακας πυρός it is not necessary to use a word which specifically refers to ‘flaming charcoal.’ What is important is that the embers be understood as ‘fiery embers’ or ‘hot ashes’ or ‘burning ashes.’

[ROM 12:20](https://ref.ly/Acts12:20)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 12:20


## ἀνθρωπάρεσκος (id: 000444000000000)

ἀνθρωπάρεσκος
=============

ἀνθρωπάρεσκος (G0441)

adj

25\.98 · Attitudes and Emotions · Acceptable To, To Be Pleased With

pleasing people, men\-pleaser pertaining to causing people to be pleased, with the implication of being in contrast to God or at the sacrifice of some principle The expression ‘men\-pleasers’ may often be rendered as ‘those who are just trying to make people like them.’

[EPH 6:6](https://ref.ly/Gal6:6) · [COL 3:22](https://ref.ly/Phil3:22)

* **Associated Passages:** Ephesians 6:6; Colossians 3:22


## ἀνθρώπινος (id: 000445000000000)

ἀνθρώπινος
==========

ἀνθρώπινος (G0442)

adj · ἄνθρωπος

9\.6 · People · Human Beings

human, of people (derivative of ἄνθρωπος\[a] ‘people,’ [9\.1](#9.1)) pertaining to being a person Many languages, however, lack an adjectival form equivalent to ‘human,’ and therefore one may speak of ‘human wisdom’ as being ‘wisdom that people have.’ At the same time, languages may actually lack an abstract term such as ‘wisdom,’ and therefore it may be necessary to translate ‘taught by human wisdom’ as ‘taught by those people who are wise.’

[ROM 6:19](https://ref.ly/Acts6:19) · [1CO 2:13](https://ref.ly/Rom2:13) · [1CO 4:3](https://ref.ly/Rom4:3) · [1CO 10:13](https://ref.ly/Rom10:13) · [JAS 3:7](https://ref.ly/Heb3:7) · [1PE 2:13](https://ref.ly/Jas2:13)

9\.5 · People · Human Beings

**Collocation:** χεὶρ ἀνθρωπίνη

person, people (an idiom, literally: human hand) a human being as a means of accomplishing something

[ACT 17:25](https://ref.ly/John17:25)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 6:19; 1 Corinthians 2:13; 1 Corinthians 4:3; 1 Corinthians 10:13; James 3:7; 1 Peter 2:13; Acts 17:25


## ἀνθρωποκτόνος (id: 000446000000000)

ἀνθρωποκτόνος
=============

ἀνθρωποκτόνος (G0443)

noun

20\.85 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Kill

murderer a person who murders another person

[JHN 8:44](https://ref.ly/Luke8:44) · [1JN 3:15](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:15) · [1JN 3:15](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:15)

* **Associated Passages:** John 8:44; 1 John 3:15


## ἄνθρωπος (id: 000447000000000)

ἄνθρωπος
========

ἄνθρωπος (G0444)

noun · ἀνθρωπάρεσκος · ἀνθρώπινος · ἀνθρωποκτόνος · φιλανθρωπία · φιλανθρώπως

9\.1 · People · Human Beings

(in the singular) person, human being, individual, (in the plural) people, persons,
 mankind a human being (normally an adult) In [MAT 14:35](https://ref.ly/Matt14:35) (ἐπιγνόντες αὐτὸν οἱ ἄνδρες τοῦ τόπου ἐκείνου ‘when the people of that place recognized him’) one may argue that οἱ ἄνδρες refers specifically to males, but the context would seem to indicate that the reference is to people in general. In translation a pronominal expression may frequently be employed when ἀνήρ\[b] occurs in the text, for example, ‘when those who lived in that place recognized him’.\|It is not uncommon in languages for a term which is often used to refer to an adult male to be employed also in a generic sense of ‘person.’ This is especially true when such terms are used in the plural form.\|In a number of instances, generic meanings of receptor languages have not been recognized as such, since translators have assumed that such receptor\-language terms refer only to members of a particular tribe or group (because they have been found only in specific contexts), while in reality such terms often designate people in general. One must, however, be on the alert for seemingly generic terms which refer only to the so\-called ‘in\-group,’ that is to say, members of a particular tribe, society, or community.

9\.24 · People · Males

man an adult male person of marriageable age1

10\.53 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Based upon Marriage

husband a man who is married to a woman ἀνήρ in the meaning of ‘husband’ is normally clearly marked by context, usually involving a so\- called ‘possessive marker,’ though in some contexts such as [JHN 4:17](https://ref.ly/Luke4:17) and [MAT 19:10](https://ref.ly/Matt19:10), the distinctions in meaning are quite clear, but it is only from each individual context that one can determine whether ἀνήρ means simply ‘man’ (see [9\.24](#9.24)) or ‘husband.’ See also discussion at [10\.54](#10.54).

[MAT 19:3](https://ref.ly/Matt19:3) · [MAT 19:5](https://ref.ly/Matt19:5) · [MAT 19:10](https://ref.ly/Matt19:10)

8\.3 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Body

**Collocation:** ὁ ἔξω ἄνθρωπος

body, physical form (an idiom, literally: the outside person) the physical nature or aspect of a person The phrase ὁ ἔξω ἄνθρωπος is to be understood in direct contrast with ὁ ἔσω (ἄνθρωπος) in the same verse, which refers to the psychological or spiritual nature of human personality (see [26\.1](#26.1)).

[2CO 4:16](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:16)

26\.1 · Psychological Faculties

**Collocation:** ὁ ἔσω (ἄνθρωπος) · ὁ ἐν τῷ κρυπτῷ (ἄνθρωπος)

the inner being, the inmost being, inwardly (idioms, literally: the inner (person), and: the hidden (person)) the psychological faculty, including intellectual, emotional, and spiritual aspects, in contrast with the purely physical aspects of human existence In a number of languages it is quite impossible to speak of the psychological faculty of a person as being merely ‘that which is within.’ It is frequently necessary to refer to a particular organ of the body, for example, ‘the heart’ or ‘the liver’ or ‘the spleen.’ In some languages, however, one may refer to this faculty as being ‘the person who stands inside’ or ‘the real person’ or even ‘the other person.’\|In [LUK 17:21](https://ref.ly/Mark17:21) the phrase ἐντὸς ὑμῶν in the statement ἰδοὺ γὰρ ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ ἐντὸς ὑμῶν ἐστιν look, God’s reign is within you may constitute a reference to the same inner being designated by the phrases ὁ ἔσω and ὁ ἐν τῷ κρυπτῷ. On this basis some scholars have suggested that the phrase ἐντὸς ὑμῶν can be interpreted as a potentiality for participation and hence be translated within your grasp, but it is more likely that one should understand the phrase ἐντὸς ὑμῶν in [LUK 17:21](https://ref.ly/Mark17:21) as a spacial relationship, for example, ‘in your midst’ or ‘among you’ (see [83\.9](#83.9)).

[ROM 7:22](https://ref.ly/Acts7:22) · [EPH 3:16](https://ref.ly/Gal3:16) · [1PE 3:4](https://ref.ly/Jas3:4)

41\.43 · Behavior and Related States · Particular Patterns of Behavior

**Collocation:** παλαιὸς ἄνθρωπος

old self, old pattern of life (an idiom, literally: old person, or: former person) the old or former pattern of behavior, in contrast with a new pattern of behavior which people should conform to In a number of languages one can best render this expression in [EPH 4:22](https://ref.ly/Gal4:22) as ‘don’t live the way you used to.’

[ROM 6:6](https://ref.ly/Acts6:6) · [EPH 4:22](https://ref.ly/Gal4:22) · [COL 3:9](https://ref.ly/Phil3:9)

9\.2 · People · Human Beings

**Collocation:** υἱοὶ τῶν ἀνθρώπων

people, mankind (a Semitic idiom, literally: sons of men) human beings (equivalent in denotative meaning to ἄνθρωπος\[a], [9\.1](#9.1)) It is rare indeed that one can reproduce literally the Semitic phrase ‘sons of men,’ since the expression is likely to be interpreted as relating only to the male offspring of fathers.

[MRK 3:28](https://ref.ly/Matt3:28) · [EPH 3:5](https://ref.ly/Gal3:5)

9\.3 · People · Human Beings

**Collocation:** υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου

Son of Man a title with Messianic implications used by Jesus concerning himself This title ‘Son of Man’ served not only to affirm but also to hide Christ’s Messianic role.\|In a number of languages there are serious complications involved in a literal translation of υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ‘Son of Man.’ In the first place, this is likely to be understood in a more or less literal sense of ‘son of a man’ and thus a denial of the virgin birth. Such is particularly true in languages which have two words for ‘son,’ one meaning ‘son of a man’ and the other, ‘son of a woman.’ Under such circumstances, a literal translation would be a clear denial of the virgin birth.
