[ACT 5:6](https://ref.ly/John5:6) · [ACT 5:17](https://ref.ly/John5:17) · [ACT 7:37](https://ref.ly/John7:37) · [ACT 9:41](https://ref.ly/John9:41)

23\.94 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to raise to life, to make live again to cause someone to live again after having once died

[JHN 6:39](https://ref.ly/Luke6:39) · [JHN 6:40](https://ref.ly/Luke6:40) · [JHN 6:44](https://ref.ly/Luke6:44) · [JHN 6:54](https://ref.ly/Luke6:54) · [ACT 2:24](https://ref.ly/John2:24) · [ACT 2:32](https://ref.ly/John2:32) · [ACT 3:26](https://ref.ly/John3:26) · [ACT 13:33](https://ref.ly/John13:33) · [ACT 13:34](https://ref.ly/John13:34) · [ACT 17:31](https://ref.ly/John17:31) · [EPH 5:14](https://ref.ly/Gal5:14) · [1TH 4:14](https://ref.ly/Col4:14) · [1TH 4:16](https://ref.ly/Col4:16)

23\.59 · Physiological Processes and States · Birth, Procreation

**Collocation:** ἀνίστημι σπέρμα

to beget, to procreate, to become the father of (an idiom, literally: to raise up seed) the male role in begetting children In contexts such as [MAT 22:24](https://ref.ly/Matt22:24), [MRK 12:19](https://ref.ly/Matt12:19), and [LUK 20:28](https://ref.ly/Mark20:28) it may be important to add a note concerning levirate responsibilities, that is to say, the requirement for a younger brother to beget children by a deceased older brother’s wife and the reckoning of such an offspring as being the descendant of the older deceased brother.

[MAT 22:24](https://ref.ly/Matt22:24)

17\.6 · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Stand

to stand up to assume a standing position

[MAT 12:41](https://ref.ly/Matt12:41) · [MAT 26:62](https://ref.ly/Matt26:62) · [MRK 14:57](https://ref.ly/Matt14:57) · [MRK 14:60](https://ref.ly/Matt14:60) · [LUK 4:16](https://ref.ly/Mark4:16) · [LUK 4:39](https://ref.ly/Mark4:39) · [LUK 5:25](https://ref.ly/Mark5:25) · [LUK 8:55](https://ref.ly/Mark8:55) · [LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25) · [LUK 11:7](https://ref.ly/Mark11:7) · [LUK 11:8](https://ref.ly/Mark11:8) · [LUK 11:32](https://ref.ly/Mark11:32) · [LUK 22:46](https://ref.ly/Mark22:46) · [JHN 11:31](https://ref.ly/Luke11:31) · [ACT 1:15](https://ref.ly/John1:15) · [ACT 5:34](https://ref.ly/John5:34) · [ACT 9:6](https://ref.ly/John9:6) · [ACT 9:11](https://ref.ly/John9:11) · [ACT 9:18](https://ref.ly/John9:18) · [ACT 9:34](https://ref.ly/John9:34) · [ACT 9:34](https://ref.ly/John9:34) · [ACT 9:39](https://ref.ly/John9:39) · [ACT 9:40](https://ref.ly/John9:40) · [ACT 10:13](https://ref.ly/John10:13) · [ACT 10:20](https://ref.ly/John10:20) · [ACT 10:23](https://ref.ly/John10:23) · [ACT 10:26](https://ref.ly/John10:26) · [ACT 11:7](https://ref.ly/John11:7) · [ACT 11:28](https://ref.ly/John11:28) · [ACT 12:7](https://ref.ly/John12:7) · [ACT 13:16](https://ref.ly/John13:16) · [ACT 14:10](https://ref.ly/John14:10) · [ACT 14:20](https://ref.ly/John14:20) · [ACT 15:7](https://ref.ly/John15:7) · [ACT 22:10](https://ref.ly/John22:10) · [ACT 22:16](https://ref.ly/John22:16) · [ACT 23:9](https://ref.ly/John23:9) · [ACT 26:16](https://ref.ly/John26:16) · [ACT 26:30](https://ref.ly/John26:30) · [1CO 10:7](https://ref.ly/Rom10:7)

15\.36 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to depart, to go away from, to leave to move away from a reference point, with the possible implication of ‘getting up and leaving’

[MAT 9:9](https://ref.ly/Matt9:9) · [MRK 1:35](https://ref.ly/Matt1:35) · [MRK 2:14](https://ref.ly/Matt2:14) · [MRK 7:24](https://ref.ly/Matt7:24) · [MRK 9:27](https://ref.ly/Matt9:27) · [MRK 10:1](https://ref.ly/Matt10:1) · [LUK 1:39](https://ref.ly/Mark1:39) · [LUK 4:29](https://ref.ly/Mark4:29) · [LUK 4:38](https://ref.ly/Mark4:38) · [LUK 5:28](https://ref.ly/Mark5:28) · [LUK 6:8](https://ref.ly/Mark6:8) · [LUK 15:18](https://ref.ly/Mark15:18) · [LUK 15:20](https://ref.ly/Mark15:20) · [LUK 17:19](https://ref.ly/Mark17:19) · [LUK 22:45](https://ref.ly/Mark22:45) · [LUK 23:1](https://ref.ly/Mark23:1) · [LUK 24:12](https://ref.ly/Mark24:12) · [LUK 24:33](https://ref.ly/Mark24:33) · [ACT 8:26](https://ref.ly/John8:26) · [ACT 8:27](https://ref.ly/John8:27)

13\.81 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Exist

to come into existence, to appear, to arise to come into existence, with the implication of assuming a place or position

[ACT 3:22](https://ref.ly/John3:22) · [ACT 5:37](https://ref.ly/John5:37) · [ACT 6:9](https://ref.ly/John6:9) · [ACT 7:18](https://ref.ly/John7:18) · [ACT 20:30](https://ref.ly/John20:30) · [ROM 15:12](https://ref.ly/Acts15:12) · [HEB 7:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:11) · [HEB 7:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:15)

23\.93 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to come back to life, to live again, to be resurrected, resurrection to come back to life after having once died In a number of languages there is a difficulty involved in formulating some expression for ‘resurrection’ or ‘living again,’ since such a phrase may refer to what is technically known as metempsychosis, that is to say, the rebirth of the soul in another existence, a belief which is widely held in a number of areas of south Asia. This problem may be avoided in some languages by speaking of ‘his body will live again’ or ‘his body will come back to life’ or ‘he will be the same person when he lives again.’

[MRK 5:42](https://ref.ly/Matt5:42) · [MRK 8:31](https://ref.ly/Matt8:31) · [MRK 9:9](https://ref.ly/Matt9:9) · [MRK 9:10](https://ref.ly/Matt9:10) · [MRK 9:31](https://ref.ly/Matt9:31) · [MRK 10:34](https://ref.ly/Matt10:34) · [MRK 12:23](https://ref.ly/Matt12:23) · [MRK 12:25](https://ref.ly/Matt12:25) · [MRK 16:9](https://ref.ly/Matt16:9) · [LUK 9:8](https://ref.ly/Mark9:8) · [LUK 9:19](https://ref.ly/Mark9:19) · [LUK 16:31](https://ref.ly/Mark16:31) · [LUK 18:33](https://ref.ly/Mark18:33) · [LUK 24:7](https://ref.ly/Mark24:7) · [LUK 24:46](https://ref.ly/Mark24:46) · [JHN 11:23](https://ref.ly/Luke11:23) · [JHN 11:24](https://ref.ly/Luke11:24) · [JHN 20:9](https://ref.ly/Luke20:9) · [ACT 10:41](https://ref.ly/John10:41) · [ACT 17:3](https://ref.ly/John17:3)

39\.34 · Hostility, Strife · Rebellion

to rebel against, to revolt, to engage in insurrection, rebellion to rise up in open defiance of authority, with the presumed intention to overthrow it or to act in complete opposition to its demands1

[MRK 3:26](https://ref.ly/Matt3:26) · [ACT 5:36](https://ref.ly/John5:36)

1 It is possible that ἐπανίστημι and ἐπίστασις\[b], with the prefix ἐπι, differ in some measure from ἀνίστημι\[h] and στάσις\[a] by virtue of the prefixal element which may suggest action directed specifically toward some object, but there is insufficient contextual evidence to support such distinct meanings.

## ἀνόητος (id: 000454000000000)

ἀνόητος
=======

ἀνόητος (G0453)

adj

32\.50 · Understand · Lack of Capacity for Understanding

foolish, stupid, without understanding pertaining to unwillingness to use one’s mental faculties in order to understand As in the case of ἀσύνετος ([32\.49](#32.49)), the meaning of ἀνόητος is that people presumably would not use their capacity for understanding and as a result, thought and behaved foolishly. ἀνόητος does not imply the mental state of being an idiot or imbecile.

[LUK 24:25](https://ref.ly/Mark24:25) · [ROM 1:14](https://ref.ly/Acts1:14) · [GAL 3:1](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:1) · [GAL 3:3](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:3) · [1TI 6:9](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:9) · [TIT 3:3](https://ref.ly/2Tim3:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 24:25; Romans 1:14; Galatians 3:1; Galatians 3:3; 1 Timothy 6:9; Titus 3:3


## ἄνοια (id: 000455000000000)

ἄνοια
=====

ἄνοια (G0454)

noun

32\.51 · Understand · Lack of Capacity for Understanding

to lack understanding, absence of understanding the state of being devoid of understanding ἄνοια does not imply in [2TI 3:9](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:9) that people are incapable of understanding, but that they evidently are unwilling to understand.

[2TI 3:9](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:9)

88\.183 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Anger, Be Indignant With

extreme fury, great rage a state of such extreme anger as to suggest an incapacity to use one’s mind

[LUK 6:11](https://ref.ly/Mark6:11)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Timothy 3:9; Luke 6:11

ἀνοίγω (G0455)

οἴγνυμι · ἄνοιξις · διανοίγω

79\.110 · Features of Objects · Open, Closed

to open, to make open to cause something to be open (see also discussion at [79\.111](#79.111))1

[MAT 2:11](https://ref.ly/Matt2:11) · [MAT 3:16](https://ref.ly/Matt3:16) · [MAT 7:7](https://ref.ly/Matt7:7) · [MAT 7:8](https://ref.ly/Matt7:8) · [MAT 17:27](https://ref.ly/Matt17:27) · [MAT 25:11](https://ref.ly/Matt25:11) · [MAT 27:52](https://ref.ly/Matt27:52) · [LUK 3:21](https://ref.ly/Mark3:21) · [LUK 11:9](https://ref.ly/Mark11:9) · [LUK 11:10](https://ref.ly/Mark11:10) · [LUK 12:36](https://ref.ly/Mark12:36) · [LUK 13:25](https://ref.ly/Mark13:25) · [JHN 1:51](https://ref.ly/Luke1:51) · [JHN 10:3](https://ref.ly/Luke10:3) · [ACT 5:23](https://ref.ly/John5:23) · [ACT 9:8](https://ref.ly/John9:8) · [ACT 9:40](https://ref.ly/John9:40) · [ACT 10:11](https://ref.ly/John10:11) · [ACT 12:10](https://ref.ly/John12:10) · [ACT 12:14](https://ref.ly/John12:14) · [ACT 12:16](https://ref.ly/John12:16) · [ACT 16:26](https://ref.ly/John16:26) · [ACT 16:27](https://ref.ly/John16:27) · [ROM 3:13](https://ref.ly/Acts3:13) · [REV 3:7](https://ref.ly/Jude3:7) · [REV 3:7](https://ref.ly/Jude3:7) · [REV 3:20](https://ref.ly/Jude3:20) · [REV 4:1](https://ref.ly/Jude4:1) · [REV 5:2](https://ref.ly/Jude5:2) · [REV 5:3](https://ref.ly/Jude5:3) · [REV 5:4](https://ref.ly/Jude5:4) · [REV 5:5](https://ref.ly/Jude5:5) · [REV 5:9](https://ref.ly/Jude5:9) · [REV 6:1](https://ref.ly/Jude6:1) · [REV 6:3](https://ref.ly/Jude6:3) · [REV 6:5](https://ref.ly/Jude6:5) · [REV 6:7](https://ref.ly/Jude6:7) · [REV 6:9](https://ref.ly/Jude6:9) · [REV 6:12](https://ref.ly/Jude6:12) · [REV 8:1](https://ref.ly/Jude8:1) · [REV 9:2](https://ref.ly/Jude9:2) · [REV 10:2](https://ref.ly/Jude10:2) · [REV 10:8](https://ref.ly/Jude10:8) · [REV 11:19](https://ref.ly/Jude11:19) · [REV 12:16](https://ref.ly/Jude12:16) · [REV 15:5](https://ref.ly/Jude15:5) · [REV 19:11](https://ref.ly/Jude19:11) · [REV 20:12](https://ref.ly/Jude20:12) · [REV 20:12](https://ref.ly/Jude20:12)

24\.69 · Sensory Events and States · Hear

**Collocation:** ἀνοίγουσιν αἱ ἀκοαί

to regain one’s hearing, to have one’s hearing restored (an idiom, literally: ears open, or possibly: hearing opens) to become able to hear2 In some languages it may be relatively meaningless to translate literally ‘his ears were opened,’ since this might mean nothing more than the removal of some wax from the ear channel. It may therefore be necessary to translate ‘his ears were made so that he could hear again’ or ‘his lack of hearing was healed.’

[MRK 7:35](https://ref.ly/Matt7:35)

71\.9 · Mode · Possible, Impossible

**Collocation:** ἀνοίγω θύραν

to make it possible (an idiom, literally: to open a door) to make possible some opportunity

[ACT 14:27](https://ref.ly/John14:27) · [1CO 16:9](https://ref.ly/Rom16:9) · [2CO 2:12](https://ref.ly/1Cor2:12) · [COL 4:3](https://ref.ly/Phil4:3) · [REV 3:8](https://ref.ly/Jude3:8)

33\.29 · Communication · Language Levels

**Collocation:** ἀνοίγω τὸ στόμα

to address, to start speaking, to begin to speak, to utter (Semitic idiom, literally: to open the mouth) to begin to speak in a somewhat formal and systematic manner

[MAT 5:2](https://ref.ly/Matt5:2) · [MAT 13:35](https://ref.ly/Matt13:35) · [LUK 1:64](https://ref.ly/Mark1:64) · [ACT 8:32](https://ref.ly/John8:32) · [ACT 8:35](https://ref.ly/John8:35) · [ACT 10:34](https://ref.ly/John10:34) · [ACT 18:14](https://ref.ly/John18:14) · [REV 13:6](https://ref.ly/Jude13:6)

24\.43 · Sensory Events and States · See

**Collocation:** ἀνοίγω τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς

to open the eyes, to cause a blind person to see (an idiom, literally: to open the eyes) to cause someone to be able to see A strictly literal translation of ἀνοίγω τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς ‘to open the eyes’ may be entirely misleading since it would imply simply opening the eyelids. It is accordingly much better to translate as ‘to cause to see.’

[MAT 9:30](https://ref.ly/Matt9:30) · [MAT 20:33](https://ref.ly/Matt20:33) · [JHN 9:10](https://ref.ly/Luke9:10) · [JHN 9:14](https://ref.ly/Luke9:14) · [JHN 9:17](https://ref.ly/Luke9:17) · [JHN 9:21](https://ref.ly/Luke9:21) · [JHN 9:26](https://ref.ly/Luke9:26) · [JHN 9:30](https://ref.ly/Luke9:30) · [JHN 9:32](https://ref.ly/Luke9:32) · [JHN 10:21](https://ref.ly/Luke10:21) · [JHN 11:37](https://ref.ly/Luke11:37) · [ACT 26:18](https://ref.ly/John26:18)

33\.252 · Communication · Speak Truth, Speak Falsehood

**Collocation:** τὸ στόμα ἀνοίγω πρός

to be completely open with, to conceal nothing from, to speak the whole truth to (an idiom, literally: to open the mouth to) to speak the complete truth to someone

[2CO 6:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:11)

1 It is possible that διανοίγω\[a] is somewhat more emphatic than ἀνοίγω. 2 The plural form αἱ ἀκοαί probably refers primarily to the inner ear or the faculty of hearing rather than to the outer ears as body parts.

ἀνομία
======

noun

88\.139 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Act Lawlessly

[MAT 7:23](https://ref.ly/Matt7:23) · [MAT 13:41](https://ref.ly/Matt13:41) · [MAT 23:28](https://ref.ly/Matt23:28) · [MAT 24:12](https://ref.ly/Matt24:12) · [ROM 4:7](https://ref.ly/Acts4:7) · [ROM 6:19](https://ref.ly/Acts6:19) · [ROM 6:19](https://ref.ly/Acts6:19) · [2CO 6:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:14) · [2TH 2:3](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:3) · [2TH 2:7](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:7) · [TIT 2:14](https://ref.ly/2Tim2:14) · [HEB 1:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:9) · [HEB 10:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:17) · [1JN 3:4](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:4) · [1JN 3:4](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:4)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 7:23; Matthew 13:41; Matthew 23:28; Matthew 24:12; Romans 4:7; Romans 6:19; 2 Corinthians 6:14; 2 Thessalonians 2:3; 2 Thessalonians 2:7; Titus 2:14; Hebrews 1:9; Hebrews 10:17; 1 John 3:4


## ἄνομος (id: 000460000000000)

ἄνομος
======

ἄνομος (G0459)

adj

88\.140 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Act Lawlessly

lawless (derivative of ἀνομία ‘to live lawlessly,’ [88\.139](#88.139)) pertaining to living without regard to law, in the sense of refusing to obey laws

[LUK 22:37](https://ref.ly/Mark22:37) · [ACT 2:23](https://ref.ly/John2:23) · [2TH 2:8](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:8) · [1TI 1:9](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:9) · [2PE 2:8](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:8)

33\.57 · Communication · Written Language

without the Law pertaining to being without the Law (specifically the first five books of the OT) Even from the two contexts of [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) and [ROM 2:12](https://ref.ly/Acts2:12), it should be clear that ἄνομος\[b] and ἀνόμως carry quite different implications in different contexts. In [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) a phrase such as ‘without the Law’ must be understood in the sense of ‘not being under obligation to the Law’ or ‘not being bound by the Law,’ and so [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) must be translated in a number of languages as ‘with those who are not bound by the Law, I live as one who is not bound by the Law,’ referring in these instances to the Law of Moses. It is important, however, in rendering the expression ‘not bound by the Law’ to avoid a phrase which would mean ‘lawless’ or ‘heedless of the Law.’ In the case of [ROM 2:12](https://ref.ly/Acts2:12), the phrase ‘without the Law’ must refer to those who are ignorant of the Law and thus are not bound by it, and so [ROM 2:12](https://ref.ly/Acts2:12) must be rendered in some languages as ‘those who sin without knowing anything about the Law are lost, but quite apart from any relationship to the Law.’\|For another interpretation of ἄνομος in [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21), see [11\.42](#11.42).

[1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) · [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) · [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) · [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21)

adj

11\.42 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

Gentile, heathen, pagan a non\-Jew who lives completely without reference to the Jewish Law For another interpretation of ἄνομος in [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21), see [33\.57](#33.57).\|In a number of contexts, it may be best to speak of ‘Gentiles’ as ‘those who are not Jews.’ In some languages the equivalent of ‘Gentiles’ is simply ‘foreigners,’ but one must be very certain that the use of a term meaning ‘foreigners’ will actually designate non\-Jews rather than those people who do not speak the particular receptor language.\|It would be a serious mistake to render ἄνομος as ‘lawless’ on the basis of its etymology. To speak of a ‘lawless person’ is to imply that he transgresses existing laws rather than being one who is not subject to the Mosaic Law.

[1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) · [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) · [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) · [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 22:37; Acts 2:23; 2 Thessalonians 2:8; 1 Timothy 1:9; 2 Peter 2:8; 1 Corinthians 9:21


## ἀνόμως (id: 000461000000000)

ἀνόμως
======

ἀνόμως (G0460)

adv

33\.57 · Communication · Written Language

without the Law pertaining to being without the Law (specifically the first five books of the OT) Even from the two contexts of [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) and [ROM 2:12](https://ref.ly/Acts2:12), it should be clear that ἄνομος\[b] and ἀνόμως carry quite different implications in different contexts. In [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) a phrase such as ‘without the Law’ must be understood in the sense of ‘not being under obligation to the Law’ or ‘not being bound by the Law,’ and so [1CO 9:21](https://ref.ly/Rom9:21) must be translated in a number of languages as ‘with those who are not bound by the Law, I live as one who is not bound by the Law,’ referring in these instances to the Law of Moses. It is important, however, in rendering the expression ‘not bound by the Law’ to avoid a phrase which would mean ‘lawless’ or ‘heedless of the Law.’ In the case of [ROM 2:12](https://ref.ly/Acts2:12), the phrase ‘without the Law’ must refer to those who are ignorant of the Law and thus are not bound by it, and so [ROM 2:12](https://ref.ly/Acts2:12) must be rendered in some languages as ‘those who sin without knowing anything about the Law are lost, but quite apart from any relationship to the Law.’

[ROM 2:12](https://ref.ly/Acts2:12) · [ROM 2:12](https://ref.ly/Acts2:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 2:12

1 It is possible that ἀνακύπτω differs to some extent in meaning from ἀνορθόω\[b] in that the focus of ἀνακύπτω is upon the reversal of a process. This is linguistically possible since ἀνα may mean not only up but also may refer to the reversal of an event, for example, ἀνάγω to restore and ἀνακαλύπτω to uncover.

[1TI 6:2](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:2)

1 The various terms noted in [35\.1](#35.1) (ἀντιλαμβάνω\[a], ἐπιλαμβάνομαι\[d], ὑπολαμβάνω\[c], συμβάλλω\[e], παρίστημι\[l], ἀντέχω\[c], ἐπαρκέω\[a], βοηθέω, βοήθεια\[a]) with a generic meaning of help no doubt possess certain contrastive features of meaning, at least of a connotative type, but there is no specific evidence of this in NT usage.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 1:54; Acts 20:35; 1 Timothy 6:2

## ἀντιτάσσω (id: 000498000000000)

ἀντιτάσσω
=========

τάσσω

39\.1 · Hostility, Strife · Opposition, Hostility

to oppose, to be hostile toward, to show hostility to oppose someone, involving not only a psychological attitude but also a corresponding behavior1 It may be that in some languages one can best render ἀντιτάσσομαι in [ACT 18:6](https://ref.ly/John18:6) as ‘to speak against,’ though in some languages opposition is often expressed idiomatically as ‘to show a sour face toward,’ ‘to have a mean heart toward,’ or ‘to turn one’s back on.’

[ACT 18:6](https://ref.ly/John18:6) · [ROM 13:2](https://ref.ly/Acts13:2) · [JAS 4:6](https://ref.ly/Heb4:6) · [JAS 5:6](https://ref.ly/Heb5:6) · [1PE 5:5](https://ref.ly/Jas5:5)

1 There are probably certain significant distinctions in meaning in the various verbs listed in [39\.1](#39.1), but on the basis of NT usage it is not possible to determine precisely what these distinctions may be.

## ἄνυδρος (id: 000504000000000)

ἄνυδρος
=======

ἄνυδρος (G0504)

adj · ὕδωρ

2\.8 · Natural Substances · Water

waterless, dry pertaining to the absence of water or moisture See also [1\.86](#1.86) for ἄνυδρος τόπος ‘lonely place.’\|In the NT ἄνυδρος occurs in the figurative expressions ‘waterless springs’ ([2PE 2:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:17)) and ‘waterless clouds.’ A phrase such as ‘waterless springs’ may be rendered simply as ‘springs in which there is no water,’ but in some languages such a phrase would seem to be a complete contradiction, since a term for ‘spring’ would imply the existence of water. It may therefore be necessary to translate ‘waterless springs’ as ‘springs which have ceased to produce water.’ ‘Waterless clouds’ are often best rendered as ‘clouds which will not produce rain’ or ‘clouds from which no rain will fall.’ In [JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12) the phrase ‘waterless clouds’ is a description of persons whose teaching and behavior are of no value in building up the faith of believers. Such a metaphor must often be changed into a simile, for example, ‘they are like clouds which do not produce rain.’

[MAT 12:43](https://ref.ly/Matt12:43) · [LUK 11:24](https://ref.ly/Mark11:24) · [2PE 2:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:17) · [JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 12:43; Luke 11:24; 2 Peter 2:17; Jude 1:12


## ἀνυπόκριτος (id: 000505000000000)

ἀνυπόκριτος
===========

ἀνυπόκριτος (G0505)

adj · κρίνω

73\.8 · Genuine, Phony

genuine, sincere pertaining to being genuine and sincere, and hence lacking in pretense or show

[ROM 12:9](https://ref.ly/Acts12:9) · [2CO 6:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:6) · [1TI 1:5](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:5) · [2TI 1:5](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:5) · [JAS 3:17](https://ref.ly/Heb3:17) · [1PE 1:22](https://ref.ly/Jas1:22)1

1 1PE 1:22 It is possible to interpret ἀνυπόκριτος in [1PE 1:22](https://ref.ly/Jas1:22) as being simply the negative state of hypocrisy and thus semantically neutral, but there seems to be an important positive element and therefore ἀνυπόκριτος is classified here under Genuine, rather than in Domain 88 Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior, Subdomain C' Hypocrisy, Pretense.

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 12:9; 2 Corinthians 6:6; 1 Timothy 1:5; 2 Timothy 1:5; James 3:17; 1 Peter 1:22

not controlled by, not subject to, uncontrolled pertaining to being unable to be controlled by something or someone It is possible to restructure this statement in [HEB 2:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:8) so as to read ‘God left nothing that man could not be in control of.’ It is also possible to translate this expression as ‘and God put him in control over all things.’

up, above a position above, often with the point of orientation left implicit In [JHN 2:7](https://ref.ly/Luke2:7) ἄνω could be defined as that position above the point where liquid would normally be when an object was full.

up, upwards, toward above extension toward a goal which is up or above

## ἄνωθεν (id: 000508000000000)

ἄνωθεν
======

ἄνωθεν (G0509)

adv · ἀνά

84\.13 · Spacial Extensions · Extension From a Source

from above, from the top of extension from a source which is above In [JHN 3:31](https://ref.ly/Luke3:31) the reference of ἄνωθεν is obviously heaven, and in many languages it is essential to translate ‘he who comes from heaven is far above all.’\|In [JHN 3:3](https://ref.ly/Luke3:3) ἄνωθεν may also mean ‘again’ (see [67\.55](#67.55)). For ἄνωθεν as part of an idiom, see [41\.53](#41.53).

[MAT 27:51](https://ref.ly/Matt27:51) · [MRK 15:38](https://ref.ly/Matt15:38) · [JHN 3:3](https://ref.ly/Luke3:3) · [JHN 3:7](https://ref.ly/Luke3:7) · [JHN 3:31](https://ref.ly/Luke3:31) · [JHN 19:11](https://ref.ly/Luke19:11) · [JHN 19:23](https://ref.ly/Luke19:23) · [JAS 1:17](https://ref.ly/Heb1:17) · [JAS 3:15](https://ref.ly/Heb3:15) · [JAS 3:17](https://ref.ly/Heb3:17)

67\.55 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

again a subsequent point of time involving repetition In [JHN 3:3](https://ref.ly/Luke3:3) ἄνωθεν involves a play on the two distinct meanings of the word, namely, ἄνωθεν\[b] 'again' and ἄνωθεν\[a] 'from above' (see [84\.13](#84.13)). For the idiom γεννάω ἄνωθεν, see [41\.53](#41.53).

[JHN 3:3](https://ref.ly/Luke3:3) · [JHN 3:7](https://ref.ly/Luke3:7) · [GAL 4:9](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:9)

67\.90 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

for a long time duration of time for a relatively long period in the past

[LUK 1:3](https://ref.ly/Mark1:3) · [ACT 26:5](https://ref.ly/John26:5)

41\.53 · Behavior and Related States · Change Behavior

**Collocation:** γεννάω ἄνωθεν

to be born again, to experience new birth, rebirth (an idiom, literally: to be born again) to experience a complete change in one’s way of life to what it should be, with the implication of return to a former state or relation It is also possible to understand ἄνωθεν in [JHN 3:3](https://ref.ly/Luke3:3) as meaning ‘from above’ or ‘from God’ (see [84\.13](#84.13)), a literary parallel to the phrase ἐκ θεοῦ ἐγεννήθησαν in [JHN 1:13](https://ref.ly/Luke1:13). In [JHN 3:3](https://ref.ly/Luke3:3), however, Nicodemus understood ἄνωθεν as meaning ‘again’ (see [67\.55](#67.55)) and γεννάω as ‘physical birth’ (see [23\.52](#23.52)).

[JHN 3:3](https://ref.ly/Luke3:3) · [JHN 3:7](https://ref.ly/Luke3:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 27:51; Mark 15:38; John 3:3; John 3:7; John 3:31; John 19:11; John 19:23; James 1:17; James 3:15; James 3:17; Galatians 4:9; Luke 1:3; Acts 26:5

## ἀξιόω (id: 000514000000000)

ἀξιόω
=====

ἀξιόω (G0515)

ἀνάξιος · ἄξιος · ἀξίως · καταξιόω

65\.18 · Value · Worthy, Not Worthy

to regard as worthy of, to consider as meriting, to regard as being valuable for to consider something of a comparable merit or worth1 It is possible that in [LUK 7:7](https://ref.ly/Mark7:7) the focus is upon social status, and in many languages the equivalent would be ‘I do not think of myself as being important enough to come to you.’ In some languages the idea of ‘not being important enough’ may be rendered figuratively as ‘being too small a person.’

[LUK 7:7](https://ref.ly/Mark7:7) · [2TH 1:11](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:11) · [1TI 5:17](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:17) · [HEB 3:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:3) · [HEB 10:29](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:29)

30\.95 · Think · To Choose, To Select, To Prefer

to choose, to decide in favor of, to prefer to make a choice on the basis of greater worth

[ACT 15:38](https://ref.ly/John15:38)

25\.5 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire, Want, Wish

to desire, to want, to like to desire something on the basis of its evident worth or value

[ACT 28:22](https://ref.ly/John28:22)

1 ἀξιόω\[a] and καταξιόω are semantically complex in that they involve an intellectual activity as well as an abstract of merit or worth. Accordingly, these meanings may be justifiably classified under Domain 30 Think, Subdomain G To Distinguish, To Evaluate, To Judge. These meanings are typical instances of semantic overlapping.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 7:7; 2 Thessalonians 1:11; 1 Timothy 5:17; Hebrews 3:3; Hebrews 10:29; Acts 15:38; Acts 28:22

56\.38 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Lead Off to Punishment

to lead off (to punishment or to death) to lead a person away for punishment after being sentenced, frequently employed in a context of putting a condemned person to death For another interpretation of ἀπάγω in [ACT 12:19](https://ref.ly/John12:19), see [20\.65](#20.65).

20\.65 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Kill

to kill, to execute to deprive a person of life, with the implication of this being the result of condemnation by legal or quasi\-legal procedures For another interpretation of ἀπάγω in [ACT 12:19](https://ref.ly/John12:19), see [56\.38](#56.38).

[ACT 12:19](https://ref.ly/John12:19)

88\.152 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Mislead, Lead Astray, Deceive

to lead astray, to mislead, to deceive to cause someone to depart from correct behavior

84\.33 · Spacial Extensions · Extension Along a Path

to extend to, to lead to, to lead from, to go to mark an extension along a route Though the context of [MAT 7:13](https://ref.ly/Matt7:13) is figurative, ἀπάγω should be interpreted in the specific sense of marking a route from one place to another.

[MAT 7:13](https://ref.ly/Matt7:13) · [MAT 7:14](https://ref.ly/Matt7:14)

1 MAT 27:31 ἀπάγω\[a] is frequently used in contexts referring to persons being led off to trial or to execution, but there seems to be no reason for setting up a separate meaning.

## ἀπαρνέομαι (id: 000533000000000)

ἀπαρνέομαι
==========

ἀπαρνέομαι (G0533)

ἀρνέομαι

33\.277 · Communication · Admit, Confess, Deny

to deny to say that one does not know about or is in any way related to a person or event1 In translating ἀρνέομαι\[a] and ἀπαρνέομαι\[a], one can often use an equivalent phrase such as ‘to say that one does not know’ or ‘to say that one has nothing to do with.’\|In denying an event, as in [LUK 8:45](https://ref.ly/Mark8:45), it may be possible to translate ‘but they all said they knew nothing about it.’\|It is also possible to deny having done something, and therefore one may translate the response in [LUK 8:45](https://ref.ly/Mark8:45) as ‘but they all said that they did not do it’ or ‘... that they did not touch him.’

[MAT 26:34](https://ref.ly/Matt26:34) · [MAT 26:35](https://ref.ly/Matt26:35) · [MAT 26:75](https://ref.ly/Matt26:75) · [MRK 14:30](https://ref.ly/Matt14:30) · [MRK 14:31](https://ref.ly/Matt14:31) · [MRK 14:72](https://ref.ly/Matt14:72) · [LUK 22:34](https://ref.ly/Mark22:34) · [LUK 22:61](https://ref.ly/Mark22:61)

30\.52 · Think · To Think About, with the Implied Purpose of Responding Appropriately

to disregard, to pay no attention to, to say no to to refuse to give thought to or express concern for2 There are a number of problems involved in rendering appropriately ἀρνέομαι\[d] and ἀπαρνέομαι\[b] as in [LUK 9:23](https://ref.ly/Mark9:23) and [MRK 8:34](https://ref.ly/Matt8:34).\|In a number of languages it simply makes no sense to translate ‘to say No to oneself,’ nor is it possible in many instances to use an expression such as ‘to deny oneself,’ since it almost always implies to deny oneself something. Sometimes the meaning may be expressed in a figurative or idiomatic manner, for example, ‘to refuse to pay attention to what one’s own desires are saying’ or ‘to refuse to think about what one just wants for oneself.’ In certain instances other kinds of idioms may be employed, for example, ‘to put oneself at the end of the line’ or even ‘to say to one’s heart, Keep quiet.’

[MAT 16:24](https://ref.ly/Matt16:24) · [MRK 8:34](https://ref.ly/Matt8:34)

34\.49 · Association · Establish or Confirm a Relation

to deny, to reject to deny strongly, with the implication of rejection

[LUK 12:9](https://ref.ly/Mark12:9)

1 It is quite possible that ἀπαρνέομαι\[a] is somewhat more forceful in meaning than ἀρνέομαι\[a], but this cannot be demonstrated from NT usage. 2 ἀπαρνέομαι\[b] appears to be somewhat more forceful in meaning than ἀρνέομαι\[d].

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:34; Matthew 26:35; Matthew 26:75; Mark 14:30; Mark 14:31; Mark 14:72; Luke 22:34; Luke 22:61; Matthew 16:24; Mark 8:34; Luke 12:9

[ROM 11:16](https://ref.ly/Acts11:16) · [2TH 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:13) · [JAS 1:18](https://ref.ly/Heb1:18)

61\.8 · Sequence

first the first of a set, often in relation to something being given

[ROM 16:5](https://ref.ly/Acts16:5) · [1CO 15:20](https://ref.ly/Rom15:20) · [1CO 15:23](https://ref.ly/Rom15:23) · [1CO 16:15](https://ref.ly/Rom16:15) · [2TH 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:13) · [REV 14:4](https://ref.ly/Jude14:4)

57\.171 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Pay, Price, Cost

foretaste, pledge, foretaste and pledge (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀπαρχή\[a] ‘first portion,’ [53\.23](#53.23)) a foretaste and pledge of blessings to come In many respects ἀπαρχή\[c] parallels closely the meaning of ἀρραβών ([57\.170](#57.170)).

[ROM 8:23](https://ref.ly/Acts8:23)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 11:16; 2 Thessalonians 2:13; James 1:18; Romans 16:5; 1 Corinthians 15:20; 1 Corinthians 15:23; 1 Corinthians 16:15; Revelation 14:4; Romans 8:23

1 In Attic Greek ἅπας occurs after consonants and πᾶς after vowels, but this distinction is not maintained in the NT, and there is insufficient evidence to indicate any significant distinction in meaning, though some persons have believed that ἅπας is somewhat more emphatic.

## ἀπατάω (id: 000538000000000)

ἀπατάω
======

ἀπατάω (G0538)

πατέω · ἀπάτη · ἐξαπατάω · φρεναπάτης

31\.12 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Hold a Wrong View, Be Mistaken

to mislead, to deceive, deception to cause someone to have misleading or erroneous views concerning the truth1

[EPH 5:6](https://ref.ly/Gal5:6) · [1TI 2:14](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:14) · [JAS 1:26](https://ref.ly/Heb1:26)

1 This series of meanings containing the stem ἀπατ overlaps considerably in meaning with the previous series containing the stem πλαν ([31\.8](#31.8)\-[31\.11](#31.11)). Both sets of terms involve deception and erroneous views. It is difficult to determine the precise implications of differences in meaning, but it may be that terms with the stem πλαν are somewhat more related to general deceptive behavior rather than primarily to misconceptions.

* **Associated Passages:** Ephesians 5:6; 1 Timothy 2:14; James 1:26

## ἀπάτωρ (id: 000540000000000)

ἀπάτωρ
======

ἀπάτωρ (G0540)

adj

10\.15 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

without father a person for whom there is no record of a male parent or who has never had a male parent or whose father has died

[HEB 7:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:3)1

1 HEB 7:3 Though it is possible to understand ἀπάτωρ, ἀμήτωρ in [HEB 7:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:3) as referring to some type of supernatural theophany as in the case of the stories of certain Greek gods and goddesses, it is unlikely that this is the meaning. The focus of attention in [HEB 7:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:3) seems to be upon the legitimacy of succession in which the priesthood of Christ is contrasted with that of the descendants of Levi.

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 7:3


## ἀπαύγασμα (id: 000541000000000)

ἀπαύγασμα
=========

ἀπαύγασμα (G0541)

noun · αὐγάζω

14\.48 · Physical Events and States · Light

radiance, reflection shining, either in the sense of radiance from a source or the reflection of a source of light It is impossible to determine whether ἀπαύγασμα should be interpreted in an active sense and therefore ‘radiance,’ or in a passive sence and therefore as ‘reflection.’ If the meaning is ‘radiance,’ then one may translate this phrase in [HEB 1:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:3) as ‘he shines with God’s glory’ or even ‘God’s glory shines through him.’ If one understands the meaning of ἀπαύγασμα as being ‘reflection,’ then one may often say ‘God’s glory shines back from him.’

[HEB 1:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 1:3

85\.27 · Existence in Space · Be in a Place

[1CO 5:3](https://ref.ly/Rom5:3) · [2CO 10:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:1) · [2CO 10:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:11) · [2CO 13:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor13:2) · [2CO 13:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor13:10) · [PHP 1:27](https://ref.ly/Eph1:27) · [COL 2:5](https://ref.ly/Phil2:5)

1 ACT 17:10 Despite the fact that ἄπειμι\[a] is morphologically derived from ἀπό ‘from’ and εἶμι ‘to come’ or ‘to go,’ there are certain contexts, such as in [ACT 17:10](https://ref.ly/John17:10), in which there appears to be no meaning of separation or departure, but merely generalized movement. ἄπειμι\[a], however, seems to be not as generalized in meaning of movement as in the case of κινέω\[a], γίνομαι\[e], and μεταβαίνω\[a] ([15\.1](#15.1), 2\).

unable to be tempted, one who cannot be tempted pertaining to not being able to be tempted There may be some difficulties involved in rendering the passive expression in [JAS 1:13](https://ref.ly/Heb1:13), but it is often possible to restructure this as an active expression with an indefinite person made the subject, for example, ‘no one can tempt God to do what is bad.’ It is also possible to shift the meaning slightly in terms of desire or will and thus say ‘God would never want to do what is evil.’

========

ἐλαύνω

15\.56 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to drive away, to force to leave to cause to move away from a point by threat or by force A strictly literal translation of [ACT 18:16](https://ref.ly/John18:16) as ‘he drove them out of court’ might imply that Gallio personally and physically forced them out of the court; probably, he simply caused them to be driven out of the court by court officials. Therefore, it may be necessary in some languages to indicate this causative relationship by ‘he ordered them to leave the court’ or ‘he ordered the officials to force them out of the court.’

36\.36 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Follow, Be a Disciple

**Collocation:** ἀπέρχομαι εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω

to no longer be a disciple, to no longer follow (an idiom, literally: to go back to what lies behind) to cease being a follower or disciple of

[JHN 6:66](https://ref.ly/Luke6:66)

88\.279 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sexual Misbehavior

**Collocation:** ἀπέρχομαι ὀπίσω σαρκὸς ἑτέρας

ἀπέχω

ἀπέχω (G0568) — ἀπέχομαι

ἔχω

to receive in full, to be paid in full to receive something in full, with the implication that all that is due has been paid It may also be possible to translate the final part of [MAT 6:2](https://ref.ly/Matt6:2) as ‘they have received all that is coming to them’ or ‘... all that they deserve.’

to experience all one deserves to experience an event to the limit of what one could expect

## ἀπιστέω (id: 000565000000000)

ἀπιστέω
=======

ἀπιστέω (G0569)

31\.39 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

to not believe, to disbelieve, to not think to be true to believe that something is not true

[MRK 16:11](https://ref.ly/Matt16:11) · [LUK 24:11](https://ref.ly/Mark24:11) · [LUK 24:41](https://ref.ly/Mark24:41)

31\.97 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

to not believe (in), to refuse to believe, to not trust in, unbelief to refuse to put one’s trust or reliance in something or someone1 One of the serious problems involved in rendering ‘to believe’ or ‘to refuse to believe’ is that so frequently the NT text does not indicate precisely the goal or object of such belief.\|In a number of languages, however, the syntactic requirements are such that some clear goal must be in the immediate context. For example, in [MRK 16:16](https://ref.ly/Matt16:16) it may be possible to translate ‘whoever refuses to believe the good news shall be condemned.’

[MRK 16:16](https://ref.ly/Matt16:16) · [2TI 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:13)

31\.105 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Be a Believer, Christian Faith

to not be a believer, to be a non\-Christian, to be an infidel, not believing, to be a pagan to not believe in the good news about Jesus Christ and hence not become a follower2

[ACT 28:24](https://ref.ly/John28:24) · [ROM 3:3](https://ref.ly/Acts3:3) · [2TI 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:13) · [1PE 2:7](https://ref.ly/Jas2:7)

1 In contrast with ἀπιστέω\[a] ([31\.39](#31.39)) meaning to not believe that something is true, ἀπιστέω\[b] (and ἀπιστία\[b] ) involve a refusal to put one’s trust, reliance, or belief in something or someone. ἀπιστέω\[b] thus has a number of significant added components of meaning. 2 The same factors apply to the classification of ἀπιστέω\[c] and ἀπιστία\[c] as apply to the classification of πιστεύω\[c] and πίστις\[d] ([31\.102](#31.102)), in view of the fact that a set of specific beliefs or relationships is clearly implied.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 16:11; Luke 24:11; Luke 24:41; Mark 16:16; 2 Timothy 2:13; Acts 28:24; Romans 3:3; 1 Peter 2:7


## ἀπιστία (id: 000566000000000)

ἀπιστία
=======

ἀπιστία (G0570)

noun

31\.89 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

lacking in trustworthiness, unfaithfulness the state of being someone in whom confidence cannot be placed The occurrence of both ἀπιστία and πίστις ([31\.88](#31.88)) in this expression in [ROM 3:3](https://ref.ly/Acts3:3) may cause certain difficulties in appropriately rendering the meaning in other languages. It may therefore be necessary to spell out in some detail the fuller implications of ἀπιστία and πίστις. Moreover, the question implies a negative response, but the question (since it is rhetorical) can easily be rendered as a strong negative statement, for example, ‘the fact that they were persons who could not be trusted does not mean that God cannot be trusted.’

[ROM 3:3](https://ref.ly/Acts3:3)

31\.97 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

to not believe (in), to refuse to believe, to not trust in, unbelief to refuse to put one’s trust or reliance in something or someone1 One of the serious problems involved in rendering ‘to believe’ or ‘to refuse to believe’ is that so frequently the NT text does not indicate precisely the goal or object of such belief.\|In a number of languages, however, the syntactic requirements are such that some clear goal must be in the immediate context. For example, in [MRK 16:16](https://ref.ly/Matt16:16) it may be possible to translate ‘whoever refuses to believe the good news shall be condemned.’

[MRK 6:6](https://ref.ly/Matt6:6) · [MRK 9:24](https://ref.ly/Matt9:24) · [ROM 4:20](https://ref.ly/Acts4:20) · [ROM 11:20](https://ref.ly/Acts11:20) · [ROM 11:23](https://ref.ly/Acts11:23) · [HEB 3:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:19)

31\.105 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Be a Believer, Christian Faith

to not be a believer, to be a non\-Christian, to be an infidel, not believing, to be a pagan to not believe in the good news about Jesus Christ and hence not become a follower2

[MAT 13:58](https://ref.ly/Matt13:58) · [MRK 16:14](https://ref.ly/Matt16:14) · [ROM 11:23](https://ref.ly/Acts11:23) · [1TI 1:13](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:13) · [HEB 3:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:12)

1 In contrast with ἀπιστέω\[a] ([31\.39](#31.39)) meaning to not believe that something is true, ἀπιστέω\[b] (and ἀπιστία\[b] ) involve a refusal to put one’s trust, reliance, or belief in something or someone. ἀπιστέω\[b] thus has a number of significant added components of meaning. 2 The same factors apply to the classification of ἀπιστέω\[c] and ἀπιστία\[c] as apply to the classification of πιστεύω\[c] and πίστις\[d] ([31\.102](#31.102)), in view of the fact that a set of specific beliefs or relationships is clearly implied.

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 3:3; Mark 6:6; Mark 9:24; Romans 4:20; Romans 11:20; Romans 11:23; Hebrews 3:19; Matthew 13:58; Mark 16:14; 1 Timothy 1:13; Hebrews 3:12


## ἄπιστος (id: 000567000000000)

ἄπιστος
=======

ἄπιστος (G0571)

adj

31\.40 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

unbelievable, impossible to be believed (derivative of ἀπιστέω\[a] ‘to not believe,’ [31\.39](#31.39)) pertaining to not being believable

[ACT 26:8](https://ref.ly/John26:8)

31\.98 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

to be unbelieving, to be lacking in trust (derivative of ἀπιστέω\[b] ‘to not believe (in),’ [31\.97](#31.97)) pertaining to not believing, with the implication of refusing to believe

[MAT 17:17](https://ref.ly/Matt17:17) · [MRK 9:19](https://ref.ly/Matt9:19) · [LUK 9:41](https://ref.ly/Mark9:41) · [LUK 12:46](https://ref.ly/Mark12:46) · [JHN 20:27](https://ref.ly/Luke20:27) · [TIT 1:15](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:15)

adj

31\.106 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Be a Believer, Christian Faith

one who is not a believer, unbeliever one who does not believe the good news about Jesus Christ It is clearly not enough in most languages to render ἄπιστος\[b] in [MRK 9:19](https://ref.ly/Matt9:19) (see [31\.98](#31.98)).

[MRK 9:19](https://ref.ly/Matt9:19) · [1CO 6:6](https://ref.ly/Rom6:6) · [1CO 10:27](https://ref.ly/Rom10:27) · [1CO 14:22](https://ref.ly/Rom14:22) · [1CO 14:22](https://ref.ly/Rom14:22) · [2CO 4:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:4) · [2CO 6:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:15) · [1TI 5:8](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:8) · [REV 21:8](https://ref.ly/Jude21:8)

11\.19 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

non\-Christian a person who does not belong to the group of believers in Christ1 In [1CO 7:15](https://ref.ly/Rom7:15) it may be necessary to indicate the so\-called goal of belief or trust, for example, ‘if one who is not a believer in Christ’ or ‘if the one who is not a follower of Christ.’

[1CO 7:12](https://ref.ly/Rom7:12) · [1CO 7:13](https://ref.ly/Rom7:13) · [1CO 7:14](https://ref.ly/Rom7:14) · [1CO 7:14](https://ref.ly/Rom7:14) · [1CO 7:15](https://ref.ly/Rom7:15) · [1CO 10:27](https://ref.ly/Rom10:27) · [1CO 14:22](https://ref.ly/Rom14:22) · [1CO 14:22](https://ref.ly/Rom14:22) · [1CO 14:23](https://ref.ly/Rom14:23) · [1CO 14:24](https://ref.ly/Rom14:24) · [2CO 6:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:14)

1 The problems of classifying ἄπιστος are essentially similar to those involving πιστή\[b] (\= πιστός\[e]; [11\.17](#11.17) and footnote).

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 26:8; Matthew 17:17; Mark 9:19; Luke 9:41; Luke 12:46; John 20:27; Titus 1:15; 1 Corinthians 6:6; 1 Corinthians 10:27; 1 Corinthians 14:22; 2 Corinthians 4:4; 2 Corinthians 6:15; 1 Timothy 5:8; Revelation 21:8; 1 Corinthians 7:12; 1 Corinthians 7:13; 1 Corinthians 7:14; 1 Corinthians 7:15; 1 Corinthians 14:23; 1 Corinthians 14:24; 2 Corinthians 6:14

ἁπλοῦς
======

ἁπλοῦς (G0573)

adj · ἁπλότης

23\.132 · Physiological Processes and States · Health, Vigor, Strength

to be healthy, to be sound pertaining to being healthy, with the implication of sound, proper functioning (in the NT with particular reference to the eyes) For another interpretation of ἁπλοῦς in [LUK 11:34](https://ref.ly/Mark11:34), see [57\.107](#57.107). See also [23\.149](#23.149).

89\.142 · Relations · Substance

of, consisting of, out of, made of a marker of the substance of which something consists or out of which it is made

[MAT 3:4](https://ref.ly/Matt3:4) · [LUK 22:18](https://ref.ly/Mark22:18) · [ACT 2:18](https://ref.ly/John2:18)

90\.7 · Case · Agent, Personal or Nonpersonal, Causative or Immediate, Direct or Indirect

by, from a marker of agent which may also be regarded as a source3 The expression ζητοῦντες ... δόξαν in [1TH 2:6](https://ref.ly/Col2:6) clearly indicates an event of ‘seeking to be honored’ or ‘seeking to be given glory.’ In such an instance, ἀπό serves to mark not only the agent but also a source.

90\.11 · Case · Instrument

by, from a marker of instrument which serves as a source of information or reason

[MAT 7:16](https://ref.ly/Matt7:16) · [MAT 7:20](https://ref.ly/Matt7:20) · [MAT 11:19](https://ref.ly/Matt11:19) · [MAT 24:32](https://ref.ly/Matt24:32) · [MRK 13:28](https://ref.ly/Matt13:28) · [ACT 28:23](https://ref.ly/John28:23)

since, from a marker of the extent of time from a point in the past

89\.25 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

because of a marker of cause or reason, with focus upon the source5

[MAT 7:15](https://ref.ly/Matt7:15) · [MAT 10:17](https://ref.ly/Matt10:17) · [MAT 10:28](https://ref.ly/Matt10:28) · [MAT 13:44](https://ref.ly/Matt13:44) · [MAT 14:26](https://ref.ly/Matt14:26) · [MAT 16:6](https://ref.ly/Matt16:6) · [MAT 16:11](https://ref.ly/Matt16:11) · [MAT 16:12](https://ref.ly/Matt16:12) · [MAT 16:12](https://ref.ly/Matt16:12) · [MAT 18:7](https://ref.ly/Matt18:7) · [MAT 28:4](https://ref.ly/Matt28:4) · [MRK 8:15](https://ref.ly/Matt8:15) · [MRK 12:38](https://ref.ly/Matt12:38) · [LUK 12:1](https://ref.ly/Mark12:1) · [LUK 12:4](https://ref.ly/Mark12:4) · [LUK 18:3](https://ref.ly/Mark18:3) · [LUK 19:3](https://ref.ly/Mark19:3) · [LUK 20:46](https://ref.ly/Mark20:46) · [LUK 21:26](https://ref.ly/Mark21:26) · [LUK 22:45](https://ref.ly/Mark22:45) · [LUK 24:41](https://ref.ly/Mark24:41) · [JHN 21:6](https://ref.ly/Luke21:6) · [ACT 11:19](https://ref.ly/John11:19) · [ACT 12:14](https://ref.ly/John12:14) · [ACT 16:33](https://ref.ly/John16:33) · [ACT 20:9](https://ref.ly/John20:9) · [ACT 22:11](https://ref.ly/John22:11) · [ACT 28:3](https://ref.ly/John28:3) · [HEB 5:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:7) · [HEB 5:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:8) · [JAS 1:27](https://ref.ly/Heb1:27) · [REV 18:15](https://ref.ly/Jude18:15)

90\.19 · Case · Responsibility

by, on (the basis of), of, upon a marker of one who is responsible for an event or state

[LUK 12:57](https://ref.ly/Mark12:57) · [LUK 21:30](https://ref.ly/Mark21:30) · [JHN 7:28](https://ref.ly/Luke7:28) · [JHN 8:28](https://ref.ly/Luke8:28) · [JHN 8:42](https://ref.ly/Luke8:42) · [JHN 10:18](https://ref.ly/Luke10:18) · [JHN 14:10](https://ref.ly/Luke14:10) · [JHN 15:4](https://ref.ly/Luke15:4)

63\.15 · Whole, Unite, Part, Divide · Part

**Collocation:** ἀπὸ μέρους

being part of, as a part of, in part, partially (an idiom, literally: from a part) the state of being part of something

[ROM 11:25](https://ref.ly/Acts11:25) · [ROM 15:15](https://ref.ly/Acts15:15) · [2CO 1:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:14) · [2CO 2:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor2:5)

67\.109 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** ἀπὸ μέρους

not long, temporary, for a little while, for a while (an idiom, literally: from a part) pertaining to a relatively short period of time, with emphasis upon the temporary nature of the event or state

[ROM 11:25](https://ref.ly/Acts11:25) · [ROM 15:24](https://ref.ly/Acts15:24)

61\.2 · Sequence

**Collocation:** ἀπὸ μιᾶς

one after another, one by one (an idiom, literally: from one) a sequence of single units, one after another

[LUK 14:18](https://ref.ly/Mark14:18)

67\.133 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Point of Time: Until, Delay, Still, From

**Collocation:** ἀπὸ τῶν αἰώνων

since all time, from all ages past, from the beginning of time an exceedingly long period of time from an assumed beginning up to the present

[EPH 3:9](https://ref.ly/Gal3:9) · [COL 1:26](https://ref.ly/Phil1:26)

13\.62 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Change of State

**Collocation:** ἀπό … εἰς

from ... to, to, for a marker of a change of state

[ROM 8:21](https://ref.ly/Acts8:21) · [2CO 3:18](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:18) · [GAL 1:6](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:6)

67\.26 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

**Collocation:** ἀφ᾽ ἡμερῶν ἀρχαίων

long ago, some time ago (an idiom, literally: from ancient days) a point of time preceding another point of time, with a considerable interval This reference to Peter having been chosen by God sometime before to bring the gospel to the Gentiles can hardly be regarded as a reference to ancient times, though some persons understand this to mean that God’s decision was made at the beginning of time. The usage of ἀφ ̓ ἡμερῶν ἀρχαίων is probably designed to emphasize the established nature of God’s decision for Peter to take the gospel to the Gentiles beginning with the centurion Cornelius. The fact that this was relatively early in the development of the church may also serve to explain the use of the idiom.

[ACT 15:7](https://ref.ly/John15:7)

1 MRK 8:11 In [MRK 8:11](https://ref.ly/Matt8:11) (see [84\.3](#84.3)) and [MAT 16:1](https://ref.ly/Matt16:1) (see [84\.4](#84.4)) there is a clear overlap of reference between ἀπό\[c] and ἐκ\[a]. 2 JHN 11:18 In [JHN 11:18](https://ref.ly/Luke11:18) ἀπό with a statement of measurement may be analyzed as marking the extent of the measurement from a particular point. 3 In most instances ἀπό as a marker of agent involves communication, but an expression such as occurs in [MAT 20:20](https://ref.ly/Matt20:20) may very well give rise to the use of ἀπό in the double sense of both agent and source of communication. In the phrase αἰτοῦσά τι ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ seeking something from him, the immediate response would have been a communication of a promise, so that the ἀπό would clearly reflect not only the immediate agent of the promise but the ultimate source for the requested benefit. 4 LUK 24:21 The phrase ἀφ᾽ οὗ ‘since’ is best interpreted as an elliptical expression in which the relative pronoun οὗ refers to a specific temporal element or to an elliptical one, as occurs in a number of instances. 5 No doubt the meanings in this series differ somewhat, but it is not possible on the basis of the contexts now available to determine precisely what designative or associative features of meaning may be involved.

## ἀποβαίνω (id: 000572000000000)

ἀποβαίνω
========

ἀποβαίνω (G0576)

βαίνω

15\.39 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to disembark, to get off to get off or to depart, as from a ship

[LUK 5:2](https://ref.ly/Mark5:2) · [JHN 21:9](https://ref.ly/Luke21:9)

89\.41 · Relations · Result

**Collocation:** ἀποβαίνω εἰς

to result in, to lead to (an idiom, literally: to go away into) to result in a state

[LUK 21:13](https://ref.ly/Mark21:13) · [PHP 1:19](https://ref.ly/Eph1:19)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 5:2; John 21:9; Luke 21:13; Philippians 1:19


## ἀποβάλλω (id: 000573000000000)

ἀποβάλλω
========

ἀποβάλλω (G0577)

βάλλω

49\.19 · Activities Involving Clothing and Adorning

to throw off, to remove and throw aside to remove a piece of clothing quickly and cast it aside

[MRK 10:50](https://ref.ly/Matt10:50)

13\.45 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · State

to remove, to drive out, to do away with to cause a state to cease by force and with the implication of elimination

[HEB 10:35](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:35)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 10:50; Hebrews 10:35


## ἀποβλέπω (id: 000574000000000)

ἀποβλέπω
========

ἀποβλέπω (G0578)

βλέπω

30\.31 · Think · To Think, Thought

to think about, to fix one’s attention on to keep thinking about, without having one’s attention distracted

[HEB 11:26](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:26)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 11:26


## ἀπόβλητος (id: 000575000000000)

ἀπόβλητος
=========

ἀπόβλητος (G0579)

adj · βάλλω

57\.141 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Receive

something to be rejected (derivative of ἀποβάλλω ‘to reject,’ not occurring in the NT) that which is to be rejected The closest equivalent of ἀπόβλητος in a number of languages is ‘something to be thrown away’ or ‘something to refuse.’

[1TI 4:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:4)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Timothy 4:4


## ἀποβολή (id: 000576000000000)

ἀποβολή
=======

ἀποβολή (G0580)

noun · βάλλω

34\.38 · Association · Belong To, Be Included in the Membership of, Be Excluded From

rejection, elimination the removal of someone from a particular association In rendering this clause in [ROM 11:15](https://ref.ly/Acts11:15), it may be necessary to indicate more specifically the participants and the implied relationships, for example, ‘for if their being rejected meant that people in general were reconciled to God.’

[ROM 11:15](https://ref.ly/Acts11:15)

13\.103 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Exist

loss, destruction the event of ceasing to exist

[ACT 27:22](https://ref.ly/John27:22)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 11:15; Acts 27:22


## ἀπογίνομαι (id: 000577000000000)

ἀπογίνομαι
==========

ἀπογίνομαι (G0581)

γίνομαι

68\.40 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to cease, to stop (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀπογίνομαι ‘to die,’ not occurring in the NT) to cease, with a complete and abrupt change It is possible that in the translation of this phrase in [1PE 2:24](https://ref.ly/Jas2:24) one might wish to preserve the figurative form and translate as ‘dying to sin.’ For another interpretation of ἀπογίνομαι in [1PE 2:24](https://ref.ly/Jas2:24), see [74\.27](#74.27).

[1PE 2:24](https://ref.ly/Jas2:24)

74\.27 · Able, Capable

to be dead to, to not respond to, to have no part in (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀπογίνομαι ‘to die,’ not occurring in the NT) to be unable to respond or react to any impulse or desire In a number of languages it is extremely difficult to speak of ‘dying to sin.’ In some instances one can preserve the figurative meaning by an expression such as ‘to be like dead as far as desiring to sin’ or ‘to be like a corpse as far as temptations to sin are concerned.’\|For another interpretation of ἀπογίνομαι in [1PE 2:24](https://ref.ly/Jas2:24), see [68\.40](#68.40).

[1PE 2:24](https://ref.ly/Jas2:24)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Peter 2:24


## ἀπογραφή (id: 000578000000000)

ἀπογραφή
========

ἀπογραφή (G0582)

noun · γράφω

33\.43 · Communication · Written Language

census the event of registering persons in connection with taking a census One may construct a descriptive equivalent of ‘census’ by speaking of ‘when everyone’s name is taken down and he is counted’ or ‘a time when the government writes down everyone’s name on a list’ or ‘... in books.’

[LUK 2:2](https://ref.ly/Mark2:2) · [ACT 5:37](https://ref.ly/John5:37)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 2:2; Acts 5:37


## ἀπογράφω (id: 000579000000000)

ἀπογράφω
========

ἀπογράφω (G0583)

γράφω

33\.42 · Communication · Written Language

to register, to put on a census list to register someone, often in connection with taking a census In a number of languages the equivalent of ἀπογράφω is simply ‘to have one’s name put in a book’ or ‘to have one’s name put in a list of those living in a particular place.’\|In [HEB 12:23](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:23) (καὶ ἐκκλησίᾳ πρωτοτόκων ἀπογεγραμμένων ἐν οὐρανοῖς ‘and at the gathering of the firstborn, whose names are written in heaven’) ἀπογράφω is used in a figurative sense, but it is usually translated more or less literally.

[LUK 2:1](https://ref.ly/Mark2:1) · [LUK 2:3](https://ref.ly/Mark2:3) · [LUK 2:5](https://ref.ly/Mark2:5) · [HEB 12:23](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:23)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 2:1; Luke 2:3; Luke 2:5; Hebrews 12:23

===========

ἀποδείκνυμι (G0584)

δείκνυμι

to demonstrate, to show, to make clearly known to cause something to be known as genuine, with possible focus upon the source of such knowledge

[ACT 2:22](https://ref.ly/John2:22)

28\.65 · Know · Able To Be Known

to show publicly, to demonstrate publicly to cause something to be known publicly

[1CO 4:9](https://ref.ly/Rom4:9) · [2TH 2:4](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:4)

72\.5 · True, False · True, False

to show to be true, to prove to demonstrate that something is true

[ACT 25:7](https://ref.ly/John25:7)

## ἀπόδειξις (id: 000581000000000)

ἀπόδειξις
=========

ἀπόδειξις (G0585)

noun · δείκνυμι

28\.52 · Know · Well Known, Clearly Shown, Revealed

proof, evidence, verification, indication (derivative of ἀποδείκνυμι\[a] to demonstrate, to show, [28\.50](#28.50)) the means by which one knows that something is a fact.12

[1CO 2:4](https://ref.ly/Rom2:4)

1 It is possible that ἀπόδειξις should be classified as having a meaning somewhat distinct from ἔνδειξις and ἔνδειγμα and thus be more closely related to ἀποδείκνυμι\[a] ([28\.50](#28.50)), but there is no clear evidence of this in existing contexts. 2 As in the case of βέβαιος\[c] ([28\.43](#28.43)), βέβαιοω\[b] ([28\.43](#28.43)), and βεβαίωσις ([28\.44](#28.44)), there are two distinct sets of semantic features in the series ἔνδειξις, ἔνδειγμα, and ἀπόδειξις. The element of showing or demonstrating implies clearly making something known, but that which is made known is done in a clear, convincing, and confirming manner and therefore shown to be certain or true. This double set of semantic features is contained in such terms as proof, evidence and confirmation.

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 2:4


## ἀποδεκατόω (id: 000582000000000)

ἀποδεκατόω
==========

ἀποδεκατόω (G0586)

δεκατόω

57\.114 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Give

to give a tenth, to tithe to give a tenth of one’s income

[MAT 23:23](https://ref.ly/Matt23:23) · [LUK 11:42](https://ref.ly/Mark11:42) · [LUK 18:12](https://ref.ly/Mark18:12)

57\.115 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Give

to collect tithes, to exact tithes from to cause someone to pay a tenth In a number of languages there are problems involved in speaking of ‘a tenth,’ and accordingly there are difficulties in formulating expressions about ‘tithing.’ In some languages one may speak of ‘a tenth’ as ‘one part of ten parts’ or ‘one part together with nine parts.’ ‘To tithe’ may be rendered as ‘from ten parts to pay one part’ or ‘to pay one part and to keep nine parts.’

[HEB 7:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:5)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 23:23; Luke 11:42; Luke 18:12; Hebrews 7:5


## ἀπόδεκτος (id: 000583000000000)

ἀπόδεκτος
=========

ἀπόδεκτος (G0587)

adj · δέχομαι

25\.85 · Attitudes and Emotions · Acceptable To, To Be Pleased With

pleasing, acceptable pertaining to that which is pleasing in view of its being acceptable In a number of languages it is necessary to restructure expressions containing ‘acceptable to’ or ‘pleasing to.’ For example, in [ACT 10:35](https://ref.ly/John10:35) it may be necessary to translate ‘God accepts whoever worships him and does what is right’ or ‘God is pleased with whoever worships him and does what is right.’ Similarly, in [1TI 2:3](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:3) one may translate ‘this is good, and God our Savior likes it’ or ‘... is happy because of it.’

[1TI 2:3](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:3) · [1TI 5:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:4)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Timothy 2:3; 1 Timothy 5:4


## ἀποδέχομαι (id: 000584000000000)

ἀποδέχομαι
==========

ἀποδέχομαι (G0588)

δέχομαι

34\.53 · Association · Welcome, Receive

to welcome, to receive, to accept, to have as a guest to accept the presence of a person with friendliness1

[LUK 8:40](https://ref.ly/Mark8:40) · [LUK 9:11](https://ref.ly/Mark9:11) · [ACT 18:27](https://ref.ly/John18:27) · [ACT 21:17](https://ref.ly/John21:17) · [ACT 28:30](https://ref.ly/John28:30)

31\.52 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Accept As True

to accept, to receive, acceptance, reception to come to believe something to be true and to respond accordingly, with some emphasis upon the source

[ACT 2:41](https://ref.ly/John2:41)

31\.26 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Acknowledge

to acknowledge, to recognize, to think about favorably to recognize or acknowledge the truth of something, normally implying something good See discussion of [ACT 24:3](https://ref.ly/John24:3) at [83\.8](#83.8).

[ACT 24:3](https://ref.ly/John24:3)

1 There are no doubt certain contextual constraints involved in the choice of ἀναδέχομαι\[a], ἀποδέχομαι\[a], ἐπιδέχομαι\[a], εἰσδέχομαι, ὑποδέχομαι, προσδέχομαι\[b], and παραδέχομαι\[b] in the sense that the spacial relationships involved in the welcoming of a guest can be reflected in the prepositional prefixes. For example, in [LUK 8:40](https://ref.ly/Mark8:40) the use of ἀποδέχομαι may focus upon the particular source, while ἐπιδέχομαι in [3JN 1:10](https://ref.ly/2John1:10) may be relevant in view of the fact that the believers are coming to a particular point. προσδέχομαι in [LUK 15:2](https://ref.ly/Mark15:2) may suggest a kind of reciprocal relationship which is established with outcasts, and παραδέχομαι in [ACT 15:4](https://ref.ly/John15:4) may simply point to spacial proximity. However, it would be wrong to insist upon clear distinctions in meaning based on the very limited number of contexts involved. Some of these same distinctions in spacial relations may exist for the set προσλαμβάνω\[e], παραλαμβάνω\[d], ἀπολαμβάνω\[d].

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 8:40; Luke 9:11; Acts 18:27; Acts 21:17; Acts 28:30; Acts 2:41; Acts 24:3

38\.16 · Punish, Reward · Reward, Recompense

to reward, to recompense to recompense someone, whether positively or negatively, depending upon what the individual deserves

[MAT 6:4](https://ref.ly/Matt6:4) · [MAT 6:6](https://ref.ly/Matt6:6) · [MAT 6:18](https://ref.ly/Matt6:18) · [MAT 16:27](https://ref.ly/Matt16:27) · [ROM 2:6](https://ref.ly/Acts2:6) · [ROM 12:17](https://ref.ly/Acts12:17) · [1TH 5:15](https://ref.ly/Col5:15) · [2TI 4:8](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:8) · [2TI 4:14](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:14) · [HEB 12:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:11) · [1PE 3:9](https://ref.ly/Jas3:9) · [REV 18:6](https://ref.ly/Jude18:6) · [REV 18:6](https://ref.ly/Jude18:6) · [REV 22:12](https://ref.ly/Jude22:12)

13\.136 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Happen

to cause to happen, to do, to pay back to cause to happen what has been promised, often in relation to vows or oaths

[MAT 5:33](https://ref.ly/Matt5:33) · [1CO 7:3](https://ref.ly/Rom7:3)

90\.46 · Case · Agent of a Numerable Event

to make, to perform, to do, to give a marker of an agent relation with a numerable event, with the probable implication of some transfer involved

[MAT 12:36](https://ref.ly/Matt12:36) · [LUK 16:2](https://ref.ly/Mark16:2) · [ACT 4:33](https://ref.ly/John4:33) · [HEB 13:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:17) · [1PE 4:5](https://ref.ly/Jas4:5) · [REV 22:2](https://ref.ly/Jude22:2)

57\.186 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Sell, Buy, Price

to sell to dispose of property or provide services in exchange for money or other valuable considerations πιπράσκω and ἀποδίδομαι (ἀποδίδωμι\[e]) may also be used to refer to selling a person into slavery. Note, for example, πιπράσκω in [MAT 18:25](https://ref.ly/Matt18:25) (ἐκέλευσεν αὐτὸν ὁ κύριος πραθῆναι ‘his master ordered him to be sold as a slave’) and ἀποδίδομαι in [ACT 7:9](https://ref.ly/John7:9) (οἱ πατριάρχαι ζηλώσαντες τὸν Ἰωσὴφ ἀπέδοντο εἰς Αἴγυπτον ‘the patriarchs were jealous of Joseph and sold him to be a slave in Egypt’).\|In a number of languages, however, it may be important to use a distinctive term for selling a person into slavery. In fact, it may be necessary to describe the event rather explicitly, for example, ‘to receive money for handing a person over to someone else to be a slave.’\|In [ROM 7:14](https://ref.ly/Acts7:14) πιπράσκω is used figuratively in the phrase πεπραμένος ὑπὸ τὴν ἁμαρτίαν ‘sold as a slave to sin,’ in the sense that sin is personified as the master.

[ACT 5:8](https://ref.ly/John5:8) · [ACT 7:9](https://ref.ly/John7:9) · [HEB 12:16](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:16)

1 ἀποδίδωμι\[a] occurs with a very wide range of types of transfer, for example, wages, proceeds, taxes, debts, and rewards.

## ἀποθνῄσκω (id: 000595000000000)

ἀποθνῄσκω
=========

ἀποθνῄσκω (G0599)

θνῄσκω

23\.99 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to die, death the process of dying In a number of languages clear distinctions are made in the use of terms for dying on the basis of the manner or circumstances of death. For example, one term may refer to death primarily as the result of old age, while another term may refer to death caused by sickness, and still other terms may be used for death caused by violence. Careful distinctions must be used in determining the choice of terms based on particular contexts.\|In [JHN 6:50](https://ref.ly/Luke6:50) ἀποθνῄσκω must be understood in a spiritual rather than in a strictly literal sense. This bold figurative language is characteristic of the Gospel of John and should not be eliminated, though in some languages a strictly literal translation may be understood only in a literal sense and thus lead to misinterpretation. In some instances, therefore, it may be necessary to suggest the metaphorical significance by translating ‘he will not, as it were, die.’

to face death, to be likely to die to be in imminent danger of dying It would also be possible to render καθ ̓ ἡμέραν ἀποθνῄσκω in [1CO 15:31](https://ref.ly/Rom15:31) as ‘I am in danger of being killed each day.’

74\.27 · Able, Capable

to be dead to, to not respond to, to have no part in (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀποθνῄσκω\[a] ‘to die,’ [23\.99](#23.99)) to be unable to respond or react to any impulse or desire In a number of languages it is extremely difficult to speak of ‘dying to sin.’ In some instances one can preserve the figurative meaning by an expression such as ‘to be like dead as far as desiring to sin’ or ‘to be like a corpse as far as temptations to sin are concerned.’

## ἀποκαθιστάνω (id: 000596000000000)

ἀποκαθιστάνω
============

ἀποκαθιστάνω (G0600) — ἀποκαθίστημι

ἵστημι

13\.65 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Change of State

to restore, to cause again to be, restoration to change to a previous good state A rendering of ἀποκαθίστημι in [MRK 3:5](https://ref.ly/Matt3:5) as was healed is justified on the basis that at a previous time the hand was crippled, but ἀποκαθίστημι in and of itself does not mean to be healed. Note, however, a contrasting situation in ἰάομαι\[b] ([13\.66](#13.66)).

[MAT 12:13](https://ref.ly/Matt12:13) · [MAT 17:11](https://ref.ly/Matt17:11) · [MRK 3:5](https://ref.ly/Matt3:5) · [MRK 8:25](https://ref.ly/Matt8:25) · [MRK 9:12](https://ref.ly/Matt9:12) · [LUK 6:10](https://ref.ly/Mark6:10) · [ACT 1:6](https://ref.ly/John1:6)

15\.74 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to send back, to cause to go back to send someone back to a place where one has been before

[HEB 13:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:19)

## ἀποκαραδοκία (id: 000599000000000)

ἀποκαραδοκία
============

ἀποκαραδοκία (G0603)

noun

25\.64 · Attitudes and Emotions · Hope, Look Forward To

what one eagerly expects, eager expectancy, eager desire that which one looks forward to with eagerness and desire In [ROM 8:19](https://ref.ly/Acts8:19) (see [25\.63](#25.63)) ἀποκαραδοκία serves in some respects to reinforce the meaning of ἀπεκδέχομαι, but it appears to add a significant component of desire.

[ROM 8:19](https://ref.ly/Acts8:19) · [PHP 1:20](https://ref.ly/Eph1:20)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 8:19; Philippians 1:20


## ἀποκαταλλάσσω (id: 000600000000000)

ἀποκαταλλάσσω
=============

ἀποκαταλλάσσω (G0604)

ἀλλάσσω

40\.1 · Reconciliation, Forgiveness · Reconciliation

to reconcile, to make things right with one another, reconciliation to reestablish proper friendly interpersonal relations after these have been disrupted or broken (the componential features of this series of meanings involve (1\) disruption of friendly relations because of (2\) presumed or real provocation (3\) overt behavior designed to remove hostility, and (4\) restoration of original friendly relations)1 Because of the variety and complexity of the components involved in reconciliation, it is often necessary to use an entire phrase in order to communicate satisfactorily the meanings of the terms in this subdomain. In some languages, however, reconciliation is often spoken of in idiomatic terms, for example, ‘to cause to become friends again,’ ‘to cause to snap fingers again’ (a symbol of friendly interpersonal relations in many parts of Africa), ‘to cause to be one again,’ or ‘to take away the separation.’ A particularly crucial element in terms for reconciliation is the assigning of responsibility for original guilt in causing the estrangement. Some terms, for example, imply that the individual who initiates reconciliation is by doing so admitting his guilt in causing the estrangement. This, of course, provides a completely untenable meaning for reconciliation in speaking of God reconciling people to himself through Christ.\|In a number of languages the contextual basis for an expression for reconciliation is often found in terms relating to the reconciliation of husbands and wives. Such expressions fit in well with many contexts in the Scriptures, especially in speaking of reconciliation of people to God, since God is frequently referred to as the husband and the believers as the wife.

[EPH 2:16](https://ref.ly/Gal2:16) · [COL 1:20](https://ref.ly/Phil1:20)

1 It may be that there are certain subtle distinctions of meaning in the series in [40\.1](#40.1), but there appears to be no contextual indication to justify setting up completely separate meanings.

* **Associated Passages:** Ephesians 2:16; Colossians 1:20

## ἀπόκριμα (id: 000606000000000)

ἀπόκριμα
========

ἀπόκριμα (G0610)

noun · κρίνω

56\.26 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Judge, Condemn, Acquit

decision, verdict an official decision, frequently involving a legal verdict In [2CO 1:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:9) ἀπόκριμα is used figuratively; no actual official decision had been made, but in view of all of the difficulties which Paul and his colleagues had suffered, it seemed as though such an official decision had been rendered.

## ἀπόκρισις (id: 000608000000000)

ἀπόκρισις
=========

ἀπόκρισις (G0612)

noun · κρίνω

33\.185 · Communication · Question, Answer

answer, reply (derivative of ἀποκρίνομαι\[a] ‘to reply,’ [33\.184](#33.184)) that which is said in response to a question

[LUK 2:47](https://ref.ly/Mark2:47) · [LUK 20:26](https://ref.ly/Mark20:26) · [JHN 1:22](https://ref.ly/Luke1:22) · [JHN 19:9](https://ref.ly/Luke19:9)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 2:47; Luke 20:26; John 1:22; John 19:9


## ἀποκρύπτω (id: 000609000000000)

ἀποκρύπτω
=========

ἀποκρύπτω (G0613)

κρύπτω

28\.80 · Know · Not Able To Be Known, Secret

to keep secret, to conceal to cause something to remain unknown, with the implication of concealment and inaccessibility1 The passive expression ‘concealed’ may be rendered in an active form as ‘God has kept people from knowing about this wisdom.’

[LUK 10:21](https://ref.ly/Mark10:21) · [1CO 2:7](https://ref.ly/Rom2:7) · [EPH 3:9](https://ref.ly/Gal3:9) · [COL 1:26](https://ref.ly/Phil1:26)

1 It is not easy to distinguish clearly between ἀποκρύπτω ([28\.80](#28.80)) and συγκαλύπτω ([28\.81](#28.81)), but ἀποκρύπτω, particularly in passive forms, may imply concealment on the basis of the nature of the object itself, while συγκαλύπτω would appear, at least in certain contexts, to imply a greater measure of intent or purpose.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 10:21; 1 Corinthians 2:7; Ephesians 3:9; Colossians 1:26


## ἀπόκρυφος (id: 000610000000000)

ἀπόκρυφος
=========

ἀπόκρυφος (G0614)

adj · κρύπτω

28\.70 · Know · Not Able To Be Known, Secret

secret, not able to be known pertaining to not being able to be known and thus secret, possibly in view of something being separate The triple negation in [LUK 8:17](https://ref.ly/Mark8:17) may lead to some shift in a number of languages, for example, ‘everything which is secret now will be known.’

[MRK 4:22](https://ref.ly/Matt4:22) · [LUK 8:17](https://ref.ly/Mark8:17) · [COL 2:3](https://ref.ly/Phil2:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 4:22; Luke 8:17; Colossians 2:3

13:10](https://ref.ly/Jude13:10) · [REV 13:15](https://ref.ly/Jude13:15) · [REV 19:21](https://ref.ly/Jude19:21)

13\.44 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · State

to do away with, to eliminate (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀποκτείνω\[a] ‘to kill,’ [20\.61](#20.61)) to cause a state to cease, with the implication of strong emphasis and forceful action In [EPH 2:16](https://ref.ly/Gal2:16) there is a subtle rhetorical device employed in ascribing to Christ the activity of ‘killing,’ when in reality he was the one who was killed on the cross in order to make life possible.

[EPH 2:16](https://ref.ly/Gal2:16)

1 Though some semanticists have analyzed terms meaning to kill as being simply a causative relating to death, that is, to cause to die or to cause to no longer live, this does not seem to be a satisfactory manner in which to treat the essential semantic components of expressions meaning to kill, to murder, to execute, etc. Though from the standpoint of propositional logic the results of the activities are essentially the same, nevertheless, the manner in which the death is produced is certainly different. The meaning of to kill involves unnatural death, normally a degree of violence, and often evil intent.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 10:28; Matthew 14:5; Matthew 16:21; Matthew 17:23; Matthew 21:35; Matthew 21:38; Matthew 21:39; Matthew 22:6; Matthew 23:34; Matthew 23:37; Matthew 24:9; Matthew 26:4; Mark 3:4; Mark 6:19; Mark 8:31; Mark 9:31; Mark 10:34; Mark 12:5; Mark 12:7; Mark 12:8; Mark 14:1; Luke 9:22; Luke 11:47; Luke 11:48; Luke 11:49; Luke 12:4; Luke 12:5; Luke 13:4; Luke 13:31; Luke 13:34; Luke 18:33; Luke 20:14; Luke 20:15; John 5:18; John 7:1; John 7:19; John 7:20; John 7:25; John 8:22; John 8:37; John 8:40; John 11:53; John 12:10; John 16:2; John 18:31; Acts 3:15; Acts 7:52; Acts 21:31; Acts 23:12; Acts 23:14; Acts 27:42; Romans 7:11; Romans 11:3; 2 Corinthians 3:6; 1 Thessalonians 2:15; Revelation 2:13; Revelation 2:23; Revelation 6:8; Revelation 6:11; Revelation 9:5; Revelation 9:15; Revelation 9:18; Revelation 9:20; Revelation 11:5; Revelation 11:7; Revelation 11:13; Revelation 13:10; Revelation 13:15; Revelation 19:21; Ephesians 2:16

=======

κυέω

to cause, to give rise to, to give birth to to cause a state, with focus upon the process

[JAS 1:15](https://ref.ly/Heb1:15)

ἀποκυλίω
========

ἀποκυλίω (G0617)

κυλίομαι

to cause to roll away, to roll away to cause to roll away from a particular point

[MAT 28:2](https://ref.ly/Matt28:2) · [MRK 16:3](https://ref.ly/Matt16:3) · [MRK 16:4](https://ref.ly/Matt16:4) · [LUK 24:2](https://ref.ly/Mark24:2)
