## ἀπολαμβάνω (id: 000614000000000)

ἀπολαμβάνω
==========

ἀπολαμβάνω (G0618)

λαμβάνω

57\.128 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Receive

to receive from, to obtain, to receive to receive, with probable focus upon the source

[LUK 16:25](https://ref.ly/Mark16:25) · [GAL 4:5](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:5) · [COL 3:24](https://ref.ly/Phil3:24) · [2JN 1:8](https://ref.ly/1John1:8)

57\.136 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Receive

to get back, to receive back, to be paid back to receive back something which one has previously possessed or its equivalent

[LUK 6:34](https://ref.ly/Mark6:34) · [LUK 15:27](https://ref.ly/Mark15:27)

15\.177 · Linear Movement · Lead, Bring, Take

to lead off, to lead away, to take away, to take aside to lead or take away from a particular point

[MRK 7:33](https://ref.ly/Matt7:33)

34\.53 · Association · Welcome, Receive

to welcome, to receive, to accept, to have as a guest to accept the presence of a person with friendliness1

[LUK 15:27](https://ref.ly/Mark15:27)

90\.63 · Case · Experiencer

to undergo, to experience to experience some event or state, often with the implication of something negatively valued2

[LUK 23:41](https://ref.ly/Mark23:41) · [ROM 1:27](https://ref.ly/Acts1:27)

1 There are no doubt certain contextual constraints involved in the choice of ἀναδέχομαι\[a], ἀποδέχομαι\[a], ἐπιδέχομαι\[a], εἰσδέχομαι, ὑποδέχομαι, προσδέχομαι\[b], and παραδέχομαι\[b] in the sense that the spacial relationships involved in the welcoming of a guest can be reflected in the prepositional prefixes. For example, in [LUK 8:40](https://ref.ly/Mark8:40) the use of ἀποδέχομαι may focus upon the particular source, while ἐπιδέχομαι in [3JN 1:10](https://ref.ly/2John1:10) may be relevant in view of the fact that the believers are coming to a particular point. προσδέχομαι in [LUK 15:2](https://ref.ly/Mark15:2) may suggest a kind of reciprocal relationship which is established with outcasts, and παραδέχομαι in [ACT 15:4](https://ref.ly/John15:4) may simply point to spacial proximity. However, it would be wrong to insist upon clear distinctions in meaning based on the very limited number of contexts involved. Some of these same distinctions in spacial relations may exist for the set προσλαμβάνω\[e], παραλαμβάνω\[d], ἀπολαμβάνω\[d]. 2 There are no doubt certain subtle distinctions of meaning in this series of related expressions in [90\.63](#90.63) (ἀπολαμβάνω\[e], ἐπιλαμβάνομαι\[f], λαμβάνω\[h], μεταλαμβάνω\[b]), but it is difficult to determine the precise distinctions on the basis of existing contexts. As in the case of a number of verbs which mark an experiencer relation, an equivalent translation consists simply of a passive form of a verb related to the noun complement. For example, in the case of [JHN 7:23](https://ref.ly/Luke7:23), one might very well translate ‘if a person is circumcised on the Sabbath.’

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 16:25; Galatians 4:5; Colossians 3:24; 2 John 1:8; Luke 6:34; Luke 15:27; Mark 7:33; Luke 23:41; Romans 1:27

## ἀπόλλυμι (id: 000617000000000)

ἀπόλλυμι
========

ἀπόλλυμι (G0622) — ἀπόλλυμαι

ὄλλυμι · ὄλεθρος

20\.31 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Destroy

to ruin, to destroy, destruction to destroy or to cause the destruction of persons, objects, or institutions In a number of languages it is difficult to find a general term for ‘destruction.’ What has often happened is the extension of meaning from a more specific type of reference, for example, ‘to smash,’ to a more generic meaning of ‘to destroy,’ and whether one understands the specific or more generic meaning depends largely upon the context. Accordingly, in [ACT 8:3](https://ref.ly/John8:3) one may find a figurative usage of a term meaning ‘to smash’ (though understood in a more generic sense), for example, ‘Saul then worked in order to smash the church.’

to lose to lose something which one already possesses ἀπόλλυμι in the sense of losing what one already possesses may be analyzed basically as ‘not to have that which one has previously had.’ There is, however, no suggestion in ἀπόλλυμι\[c] as to the particular type of circumstances involved in the loss, whether, for example, from neglect, by accident, or as the result of some external force.\|It is also possible to interpret ἀπόλλυμι in [LUK 15:8](https://ref.ly/Mark15:8) as ‘to not know where something is’ (see [27\.29](#27.29)).

23\.114 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

**Collocation:** ἀπόλλυμι τὴν ψυχήν

to experience the loss of life, to die an idiom, literally: to suffer the destruction of one’s life, or: to have one’s life destroyed In [MAT 10:39](https://ref.ly/Matt10:39) some scholars see in the use of ψυχή a reference not to physical life but to a particular quality of life. This is difficult to justify except in terms of the total context, and it may be that in [MAT 10:39](https://ref.ly/Matt10:39) there is a degree of intentional ambiguity with respect to the meaning of ψυχή.

## ἀπολύτρωσις (id: 000624000000000)

ἀπολύτρωσις
===========

ἀπολύτρωσις (G0629)

noun · λύω

37\.128 · Control, Rule · Release, Set Free

to set free, to liberate, to deliver, liberation, deliverance to release or set free, with the implied analogy to the process of freeing a slave1 In a number of languages one cannot speak of ‘being set free’ without specifying the particular manner or circumstances involved. In the case of [LUK 24:21](https://ref.ly/Mark24:21), for example, it may be necessary to translate ‘to liberate Israel’ as ‘to cause Israel to be free from foreign control’ or ‘... from the power of Rome.’ A similar type of translation may be required in [LUK 2:38](https://ref.ly/Mark2:38), but in the case of [EPH 1:7](https://ref.ly/Gal1:7) it may be necessary to specify ‘we are set free from sin’ or ‘we are set free from our bad desires.’

[LUK 21:28](https://ref.ly/Mark21:28) · [ROM 3:24](https://ref.ly/Acts3:24) · [ROM 8:23](https://ref.ly/Acts8:23) · [1CO 1:30](https://ref.ly/Rom1:30) · [EPH 1:7](https://ref.ly/Gal1:7) · [EPH 1:14](https://ref.ly/Gal1:14) · [EPH 4:30](https://ref.ly/Gal4:30) · [COL 1:14](https://ref.ly/Phil1:14) · [HEB 9:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:15) · [HEB 11:35](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:35)

1 It is possible that ἀπολύω\[c] is somewhat more emphatic in meaning than λύω\[b] in view of the prepositional prefix ἀπο, but NT contexts are not sufficient to indicate any precise degree of distinction. The same possible distinction also applies to the meanings in [37\.128](#37.128).

34\.78 · Association · Marriage, Divorce

to divorce, to separate to dissolve the marriage bond Expressions for divorce are often based on terms meaning literally ‘to send away,’ ‘to separate from,’ or ‘to leave one another.’ However, in a number of languages idiomatic expressions are employed, for example ‘to send him off with his clothes,’ ‘to untie the knot between them,’ or ‘to throw away her hearthstones.’\|Some persons have attempted to make an important distinction between ἀφίημι in [1CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/Rom7:11) and χωρίζω in [1CO 7:15](https://ref.ly/Rom7:15) on the assumption that ἀφίημι implies legal divorce, while χωρίζω only relates to separation. Such a distinction, however, seems to be quite artificial.

[MAT 1:19](https://ref.ly/Matt1:19) · [MAT 5:31](https://ref.ly/Matt5:31) · [MAT 5:32](https://ref.ly/Matt5:32) · [MAT 5:32](https://ref.ly/Matt5:32) · [MAT 19:3](https://ref.ly/Matt19:3) · [MAT 19:7](https://ref.ly/Matt19:7) · [MAT 19:8](https://ref.ly/Matt19:8) · [MAT 19:9](https://ref.ly/Matt19:9) · [MRK 10:2](https://ref.ly/Matt10:2) · [MRK 10:4](https://ref.ly/Matt10:4) · [MRK 10:11](https://ref.ly/Matt10:11) · [MRK 10:12](https://ref.ly/Matt10:12) · [LUK 16:18](https://ref.ly/Mark16:18) · [LUK 16:18](https://ref.ly/Mark16:18)

40\.8 · Reconciliation, Forgiveness · Forgiveness

to pardon, to forgive, forgiveness to remove the guilt resulting from wrongdoing It is extremely important to note that the focus in the meanings of ἀφίημι\[f], ἄφεσις\[a], and ἀπολύω\[e] is upon the guilt of the wrongdoer and not upon the wrongdoing itself. The event of wrongdoing is not undone, but the guilt resulting from such an event is pardoned. To forgive, therefore, means essentially to remove the guilt resulting from wrongdoing.\|Some languages make a clear distinction between guilt and sin, and terms for forgiveness are therefore related to guilt and not to the wrongdoing. Therefore, ‘to forgive sins’ is literally ‘to forgive guilt.’ Though terms for ‘forgiveness’ are often literally ‘to wipe out,’ ‘to blot out,’ or ‘to do away with,’ it is obviously not possible to blot out or to wipe out an event, but it is possible to remove or obliterate the guilt.

[MAT 18:27](https://ref.ly/Matt18:27) · [LUK 6:37](https://ref.ly/Mark6:37) · [LUK 6:37](https://ref.ly/Mark6:37)

15\.38 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to go away, to depart, to leave to depart from a place or set of circumstances, with perhaps an implication of finality or significant separation or rupture being involved

[ACT 28:25](https://ref.ly/John28:25)

1 It is possible that ἀπολύω\[a] and ἀφίημι\[a] differ somewhat in meaning in that ἀπολύω\[a] may suggest more permission and ἀφίημι\[a] more dismissal, but this cannot be determined from NT usage. In [ACT 15:30](https://ref.ly/John15:30), however, οἱ … ἀπολυθέντες implies clearly being sent (see ἀπολύω\[b], [15\.66](#15.66)). 2 It is possible that ἀπολύω\[c] is somewhat more emphatic in meaning than λύω\[b] in view of the prepositional prefix ἀπο, but NT contexts are not sufficient to indicate any precise degree of distinction. The same possible distinction also applies to the meanings in [37\.128](#37.128).

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 14:15; Matthew 14:22; Matthew 14:23; Matthew 15:23; Matthew 15:32; Matthew 15:39; Mark 6:36; Mark 6:45; Mark 8:3; Mark 8:9; Luke 2:29; Luke 8:38; Luke 9:12; Luke 14:4; Acts 15:30; Acts 15:33; Acts 19:40; Acts 23:22; Acts 13:3; Matthew 27:15; Matthew 27:17; Matthew 27:21; Matthew 27:26; Mark 15:6; Mark 15:9; Mark 15:11; Mark 15:15; Luke 13:12; Luke 23:16; Luke 23:18; Luke 23:20; Luke 23:22; Luke 23:25; John 18:39; John 19:10; John 19:12; Acts 3:13; Acts 4:21; Acts 4:23; Acts 5:40; Acts 16:35; Acts 16:36; Acts 17:9; Acts 26:32; Acts 28:18; Hebrews 13:23; Matthew 1:19; Matthew 5:31; Matthew 5:32; Matthew 19:3; Matthew 19:7; Matthew 19:8; Matthew 19:9; Mark 10:2; Mark 10:4; Mark 10:11; Mark 10:12; Luke 16:18; Matthew 18:27; Luke 6:37; Acts 28:25

to wipe off, to wipe away to wipe off, as a means of getting rid of some adhering substance Since the action of wiping off the dust from one’s feet was a symbolic act of denunciation, it may be necessary in some languages to translate this expression in [LUK 10:11](https://ref.ly/Mark10:11) as ‘we wipe off as a way of denouncing you’ or ‘we wipe off as a way of showing how bad you are.’

## ἀποπλανάω (id: 000630000000000)

ἀποπλανάω
=========

ἀποπλανάω (G0635) — ἀποπλανάομαι

πλανάω

31\.11 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Hold a Wrong View, Be Mistaken

to deceive, to mislead, to cause to have completely wrong views to cause someone to definitely go astray in one’s beliefs or views1

[MRK 13:22](https://ref.ly/Matt13:22)

31\.67 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Change an Opinion Concerning Truth

to stray from the truth, to wander from the truth, to go astray from to no longer believe what is true, but to start believing what is false2 Though in some languages one can preserve the figurative meaning of ‘to wander’ or ‘to go astray’ in connection with truth, it is not possible to do so in many languages, and therefore it may be necessary to spell out in some detail precisely what is involved, for example, ‘to stop believing what is true and to start believing what is false.’

[1TI 6:10](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:10)

1 The meanings of πλανάω and πλάνη\[a] ([31\.8](#31.8)), πλάνος ([31\.9](#31.9)), πλάνη\[b] ([31\.10](#31.10)), and ἀποπλανάω ([31\.11](#31.11)) focus primarily upon the misleading or deceptive views which people are caused to have, but these same terms may refer to more than mere mistaken ideas or opinions. They may involve deception of a behavioral nature, and therefore these same meanings could be in Domain 88 Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior, Subdomain U Mislead, Lead Astray, Deceive. 2 It is possible that ἀποπλανάομαι differs slightly from πλανάομαι\[b] in emphasizing the departure from truth.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 13:22; 1 Timothy 6:10

## ἀποσκίασμα (id: 000638000000000)

ἀποσκίασμα
==========

ἀποσκίασμα (G0644)

noun · σκιά

14\.61 · Physical Events and States · Darkness

shadow the shape or shade cast by an object which blocks rays of light In a number of languages a term for ‘shadow’ has a number of very important meanings. In some instances it is regarded as a part of personality which goes on to the next world, since a dead person no longer ‘casts a shadow.’ The shadow may also be regarded as a kind of counterpart of the personality, a type of alter ego. In cases of severe illness one may say ‘his shadow has walked away,’ and in many parts of the world the shadow of a person is an important element in the practice of black magic, for to damage a shadow may be regarded as a means of causing harm to a person.\|In [JAS 1:17](https://ref.ly/Heb1:17) one may translate ‘shifting shadow’ as ‘a shadow which constantly changes.’

[JAS 1:17](https://ref.ly/Heb1:17)

* **Associated Passages:** James 1:17


## ἀποσπάω (id: 000639000000000)

ἀποσπάω
=======

ἀποσπάω (G0645)

σπάομαι

15\.214 · Linear Movement · Pull, Draw, Drag

to pull out, to draw out, to drag to pull or draw away from or out of

[MAT 26:51](https://ref.ly/Matt26:51)

31\.74 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Change an Opinion Concerning Truth

to draw away, to lead away, to lure away to (figurative extension of meaning of ἀποσπάω\[a] ‘to pull out, to drag,’ [15\.214](#15.214)) to cause a change of belief so as to correspond more with the beliefs of the person or factor causing the change

[ACT 20:30](https://ref.ly/John20:30)1

15\.54 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

**Collocation:** ἀποσπάομαι

to leave, to go off, to withdraw to draw oneself away from, suggesting that the movement was not sudden

[LUK 22:41](https://ref.ly/Mark22:41) · [ACT 21:1](https://ref.ly/John21:1)2

1 ACT 20:30 In view of the interpersonal relationship implied in the occurrence of ἀποσπάω in [ACT 20:30](https://ref.ly/John20:30), it would be possible to classify this meaning under the domain of Association (34\). 2 ACT 21:1 It is possible to interpret ἀποσπάομαι in [ACT 21:1](https://ref.ly/John21:1) as referring to the act of saying goodbye, and thus being equivalent to ἀποτάσσομαι\[b] ([15\.55](#15.55)).

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:51; Acts 20:30; Luke 22:41; Acts 21:1


## ἀποστασία (id: 000640000000000)

ἀποστασία
=========

ἀποστασία (G0646)

noun · ἵστημι

39\.34 · Hostility, Strife · Rebellion

to rebel against, to revolt, to engage in insurrection, rebellion to rise up in open defiance of authority, with the presumed intention to overthrow it or to act in complete opposition to its demands1 It is also possible that ἀποστασία differs slightly in meaning from ἐπίστασις\[b] by virtue of the differences in prefixal formation, but there is insufficient contextual evidence to support such a view.

[ACT 21:21](https://ref.ly/John21:21) · [2TH 2:3](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:3)

1 It is possible that ἐπανίστημι and ἐπίστασις\[b], with the prefix ἐπι, differ in some measure from ἀνίστημι\[h] and στάσις\[a] by virtue of the prefixal element which may suggest action directed specifically toward some object, but there is insufficient contextual evidence to support such distinct meanings.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 21:21; 2 Thessalonians 2:3

==========

noun · ἵστημι

written notice of divorce a written statement prepared by a husband and given to a wife as evidence of a legal divorce1

[MAT 5:31](https://ref.ly/Matt5:31) · [MAT 19:7](https://ref.ly/Matt19:7) · [MRK 10:4](https://ref.ly/Matt10:4)

1 In [MAT 19:7](https://ref.ly/Matt19:7) and [MRK 10:4](https://ref.ly/Matt10:4) ἀποστάσιον occurs together with βιβλίον\[b] notice ([33\.38](#33.38)), but in [MAT 5:31](https://ref.ly/Matt5:31) ἀποστάσιον occurs in the absolute sense and denotes both the document and its particular contents.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 5:31; Matthew 19:7; Mark 10:4


## ἀποστεγάζω (id: 000642000000000)

ἀποστεγάζω
==========

ἀποστεγάζω (G0648)

στέγω

45\.11 · Building, Constructing

to remove the roof, to unroof to take the roof off of a house In [MRK 2:4](https://ref.ly/Matt2:4) ἀποστεγάζω refers to only a part of the roof, and therefore in a number of languages it may be necessary to translate as ‘they made a hole in the roof.’ In view of the type of houses built in Palestine in NT times, the roof no doubt would have been flat, held up by heavy beams over which were laid planks or sticks and then covered with sun\-baked clay.

[MRK 2:4](https://ref.ly/Matt2:4)

## ἀποστερέω (id: 000644000000000)

ἀποστερέω
=========

ἀποστερέω (G0650)

στερέω

57\.248 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Steal, Rob

to defraud, to deprive by deception to take something from someone by means of deception or trickery

[MRK 10:19](https://ref.ly/Matt10:19) · [1CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/Rom6:7) · [1CO 6:8](https://ref.ly/Rom6:8)

57\.47 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Need, Lack

to deprive of to cause someone not to possess something Though the resulting state of ἀποστερέω\[b] may be the mere absence of something, nevertheless the implication is that some activity has taken place to cause a person no longer to possess something. This meaning may then be expressed in some languages as to have something taken away from someone.

[1CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/Rom6:7) · [1CO 6:8](https://ref.ly/Rom6:8) · [1CO 7:5](https://ref.ly/Rom7:5) · [1TI 6:5](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:5)

57\.159 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Pay, Price, Cost

to withhold payment, to not pay to not pay someone for what is owed

========

ἀποστολή (G0651)

noun · στέλλομαι

53\.73 · Religious Activities · Roles and Functions

[ACT 1:25](https://ref.ly/John1:25) · [ROM 1:5](https://ref.ly/Acts1:5) · [1CO 9:2](https://ref.ly/Rom9:2) · [GAL 2:8](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:8)

## ἀπόστολος (id: 000646000000000)

ἀπόστολος
=========

ἀπόστολος (G0652)

noun · στέλλομαι

53\.74 · Religious Activities · Roles and Functions

apostle, special messenger one who fulfills the role of being a special messenger (generally restricted to the immediate followers of Jesus Christ, but also extended, as in the case of Paul, to other early Christians active in proclaiming the message of the gospel) The relationship of an apostle to Jesus Christ is sometimes expressed as ‘being Christ’s messenger’ or ‘being a special messenger of Jesus Christ.’ In such a phrase, the term ‘special’ refers to having been commissioned by Jesus Christ for a particular task or role.

[MAT 10:2](https://ref.ly/Matt10:2) · [MRK 6:30](https://ref.ly/Matt6:30) · [LUK 6:13](https://ref.ly/Mark6:13) · [LUK 9:10](https://ref.ly/Mark9:10) · [LUK 17:5](https://ref.ly/Mark17:5) · [LUK 1:11](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:11) · [TIT 1:1](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:1) · [HEB 3:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:1) · [1PE 1:1](https://ref.ly/Jas1:1) · [2PE 1:1](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:1) · [2PE 3:2](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:2) · [JUD 1:17](https://ref.ly/3John1:17) · [REV 2:2](https://ref.ly/Jude2:2) · [REV 18:20](https://ref.ly/Jude18:20) · [REV 21:14](https://ref.ly/Jude21:14)

33\.194 · Communication · Inform, Announce

messenger (derivative of ἀποστέλλω\[b] ‘to send a message,’ [15\.67](#15.67)) one who is sent with a message1

[LUK 11:49](https://ref.ly/Mark11:49) · [JHN 13:16](https://ref.ly/Luke13:16) · [2CO 8:23](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:23) · [PHP 2:25](https://ref.ly/Eph2:25)

1 ἀπόστολος\[b] ([33\.194](#33.194)) and ἄγγελος\[a] ([33\.195](#33.195)) appear to differ in meaning in that in the case of ἀπόστολος\[b] the focus is upon one who is sent, while in the case of ἄγγελος\[a] the focus appears to be upon the message which is communicated.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 10:2; Mark 6:30; Luke 6:13; Luke 9:10; Luke 17:5; Luke 22:14; Luke 24:10; Acts 1:2; Acts 1:26; Acts 2:37; Acts 2:42; Acts 2:43; Acts 4:33; Acts 4:35; Acts 4:36; Acts 4:37; Acts 5:2; Acts 5:12; Acts 5:18; Acts 5:29; Acts 5:40; Acts 6:6; Acts 8:1; Acts 8:14; Acts 8:18; Acts 9:27; Acts 11:1; Acts 14:4; Acts 14:14; Acts 15:2; Acts 15:4; Acts 15:6; Acts 15:22; Acts 15:23; Acts 16:4; Romans 1:1; Romans 11:13; Romans 16:7; 1 Corinthians 1:1; 1 Corinthians 4:9; 1 Corinthians 9:1; 1 Corinthians 9:2; 1 Corinthians 9:5; 1 Corinthians 12:28; 1 Corinthians 12:29; 1 Corinthians 15:7; 1 Corinthians 15:9; 2 Corinthians 1:1; 2 Corinthians 11:5; 2 Corinthians 11:13; 2 Corinthians 12:11; 2 Corinthians 12:12; Galatians 1:1; Galatians 1:17; Galatians 1:19; Ephesians 1:1; Ephesians 2:20; Ephesians 3:5; Ephesians 4:11; Colossians 1:1; 1 Thessalonians 2:7; 1 Timothy 1:1; 1 Timothy 2:7; 2 Timothy 1:1; 2 Timothy 1:11; Titus 1:1; Hebrews 3:1; 1 Peter 1:1; 2 Peter 1:1; 2 Peter 3:2; Jude 1:17; Revelation 2:2; Revelation 18:20; Revelation 21:14; Luke 11:49; John 13:16; 2 Corinthians 8:23; Philippians 2:25

## ἀποστρέφω (id: 000648000000000)

ἀποστρέφω
=========

ἀποστρέφω (G0654) — ἀποστρέφομαι

στρέφω

31\.70 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Change an Opinion Concerning Truth

to cause to turn away from, to cause to change one’s belief, to mislead to cause someone to turn away from a previous belief For another interpretation of ἀποστρέφω in [ACT 3:26](https://ref.ly/John3:26), see [68\.44](#68.44). For another interpretation of ἀποστρέφω in [LUK 23:14](https://ref.ly/Mark23:14), see [88\.264](#88.264).

[LUK 23:14](https://ref.ly/Mark23:14) · [ACT 3:26](https://ref.ly/John3:26)

88\.264 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Licentiousness, Perversion

to lead astray, to pervert, to mislead to cause someone to depart from correct behavior and thus engage in serious wrongdoing For another interpretation of ἀποστρέφω in [LUK 23:14](https://ref.ly/Mark23:14), see [31\.70](#31.70).

[LUK 23:14](https://ref.ly/Mark23:14)

85\.52 · Existence in Space · Put, Place

to return, to put back to cause something to be in a place where it was formerly

[MAT 26:52](https://ref.ly/Matt26:52)

13\.63 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Change of State

to change, to turn into, to remove from to cause something to turn into or to become something else or to become removed from something or someone

[ROM 11:26](https://ref.ly/Acts11:26)

68\.44 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to stop, to cease, to reject (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀποστρέφω ‘to cause to turn back from,’ not occurring in the NT) to cease doing something, with the implication of rejection For another interpretation of ἀποστρέφω in [ACT 3:26](https://ref.ly/John3:26), see [31\.70](#31.70).

[MAT 5:42](https://ref.ly/Matt5:42) · [ACT 3:26](https://ref.ly/John3:26) · [2TI 4:4](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:4) · [TIT 1:14](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:14) · [HEB 12:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:25)

31\.62 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Change an Opinion Concerning Truth

to turn away from, to reject to no longer continue to believe as one has and hence to turn away from or to reject a previous set of beliefs

[TIT 1:14](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:14)

34\.26 · Association · Join, Begin To Associate

to fall away, to forsake, to turn away to abandon a former relationship or association, or to dissociate (a type of reversal of beginning to associate)1

[2TI 1:15](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:15) · [HEB 12:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:25)

35\.18 · Help, Care For · Help

to refuse to help to refuse to provide help to someone

[MAT 5:42](https://ref.ly/Matt5:42) · [2TI 1:15](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:15)

1 The meanings of παραπίπτω, ἐκπίπτω\[c], ἀποστρέφομαι\[b], and ἀφίσταμαι\[b] appear to focus upon the initial aspects of dissociation. As such, they constitute a reversal of the process of joining or beginning to associate.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 23:14; Acts 3:26; Matthew 26:52; Romans 11:26; Matthew 5:42; 2 Timothy 4:4; Titus 1:14; Hebrews 12:25; 2 Timothy 1:15

## ἀποσυνάγωγος (id: 000650000000000)

ἀποσυνάγωγος
============

ἀποσυνάγωγος (G0656)

adj · ἄγω

11\.46 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

expelled from the synagogue a state of having been excommunicated from membership in a synagogue In rendering [JHN 9:22](https://ref.ly/Luke9:22) it is important to indicate clearly that this is not a matter of an individual merely being forced outside of a synagogue building, but excluded from membership and thus from worship in the synagogue. In some languages this is made explicit by saying ‘his name will be erased from the list of those belonging to the synagogue’ or ‘he will no longer be able to enter a synagogue’ or ‘he will be rejected as a member of the synagogue.’

[JHN 9:22](https://ref.ly/Luke9:22) · [JHN 12:42](https://ref.ly/Luke12:42) · [JHN 16:2](https://ref.ly/Luke16:2)

* **Associated Passages:** John 9:22; John 12:42; John 16:2


## ἀποτάσσω (id: 000651000000000)

ἀποτάσσω
========

ἀποτάσσω (G0657) — ἀποτάσσομαι

τάσσω

33\.23 · Communication · Discourse Types

to say goodbye to employ formalized expressions appropriate to leaving or saying farewell to someone, possibly involving the communication of final arrangements for leaving

[MRK 6:46](https://ref.ly/Matt6:46) · [LUK 9:61](https://ref.ly/Mark9:61)

15\.55 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to take leave of, to say goodbye to say goodbye and to leave In a number of languages the equivalent of ‘to take leave of’ or ‘saying goodbye’ is highly idiomatic, for example, ‘to snap fingers as one leaves,’ ‘to raise one’s hands together,’ ‘to clasp one’s hands before leaving,’ or ‘to bow to one another as one leaves.’

[ACT 18:18](https://ref.ly/John18:18) · [ACT 18:21](https://ref.ly/John18:21) · [2CO 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Cor2:13)

57\.70 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Take, Obtain, Gain, Lose

to give up, to part with one’s possessions to willingly give up or set aside what one possesses

[LUK 14:33](https://ref.ly/Mark14:33)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 6:46; Luke 9:61; Acts 18:18; Acts 18:21; 2 Corinthians 2:13; Luke 14:33


## ἀποτελέω (id: 000652000000000)

ἀποτελέω
========

ἀποτελέω (G0658)

τελέω

68\.22 · Aspect · Complete, Finish, Succeed

to complete, to finish, to end, to accomplish to bring an activity to a successful finish1 It is also possible to translate ἀποτελέω in [LUK 13:32](https://ref.ly/Mark13:32) as ‘am performing,’ but the focus evidently is upon the completion of a series of such healings. The final phrase of [LUK 13:32](https://ref.ly/Mark13:32), καὶ τῇ τρίτω τελειοῦμαι ‘and on the third day I will be finished,’ points to a possible distinction between ἀποτελέω and τελειόω, in which the first apparently serves to mark the end of a particular series, while the second points to the completion of anything and everything involved.

[LUK 13:32](https://ref.ly/Mark13:32) · [JAS 1:15](https://ref.ly/Heb1:15)

1 There are no doubt certain subtle distinctions, particularly of emphasis, in the series τελέω, ἐκτελέω\[a], ἀποτελέω, ἐπιτελέω\[a], συντελέω\[a], and τελειόω\[c], but it is not possible to determine with precision the particular distinctions on the basis of existing contexts, though possibly ἐκτελέω and ἀποτελέω emphasize more finality, while ἐπιτελέω\[a] may focus upon the resulting purpose.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 13:32; James 1:15


## ἀποτίθημι (id: 000653000000000)

ἀποτίθημι
=========

ἀποτίθημι (G0659) — ἀποτίθεμαι

τίθημι

85\.44 · Existence in Space · Put, Place

to put away, to put out of the way, to remove to put or take something away from its normal location1 In [MAT 14:3](https://ref.ly/Matt14:3) the semantic element of ‘out of the way’ is implied in the activity of ‘putting in prison.’

[MAT 14:3](https://ref.ly/Matt14:3) · [ACT 7:58](https://ref.ly/John7:58) · [HEB 12:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:1) · [JAS 1:21](https://ref.ly/Heb1:21) · [1PE 2:1](https://ref.ly/Jas2:1)

68\.37 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to stop, to cease (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀποτίθημι\[a] ‘to put away,’ [85\.44](#85.44)) to cease doing what one is accustomed to doing

[EPH 4:22](https://ref.ly/Gal4:22) · [EPH 4:25](https://ref.ly/Gal4:25) · [COL 3:8](https://ref.ly/Phil3:8) · [JAS 1:21](https://ref.ly/Heb1:21)

1 It would also be possible to classify the meanings of ἀποτίθημι\[a] and ἀφαιρέω\[a] in Domain 15 Linear Movement, but in a number of contexts the focus seems not to be primarily upon the movement but upon the removal from a particular location. In other words, the focus is upon the absence of an object from a place rather than the movement involved in removal.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 14:3; Acts 7:58; Hebrews 12:1; James 1:21; 1 Peter 2:1; Ephesians 4:22; Ephesians 4:25; Colossians 3:8


## ἀποτινάσσω (id: 000654000000000)

ἀποτινάσσω
==========

ἀποτινάσσω (G0660)

τινάσσω

16\.8 · Non\-Linear Movement

to shake off, to shake out, to shake from to shake something out or off, in order to get rid of an object or a substance In a number of languages quite different terms for shaking will be required for the contexts of [ACT 28:5](https://ref.ly/John28:5) and [LUK 9:5](https://ref.ly/Mark9:5), since in the first instance it is an animate being which clings, and in the second instance it is simply a substance that adheres.\|In a number of languages, however, the symbolic meaning of ‘shaking dust off one’s feet’ is not at all clear, so that it may be necessary to add a type of classifier to indicate that such an action symbolizes the breaking off of association. It is for this reason that the expression ἐκτιναξάμενος τὰ ἱμάτια in [ACT 18:6](https://ref.ly/John18:6) is often translated as ‘he protested by shaking the dust from his clothes.’

[LUK 9:5](https://ref.ly/Mark9:5) · [ACT 28:5](https://ref.ly/John28:5)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 9:5; Acts 28:5


## ἀποτίνω (id: 000655000000000)

ἀποτίνω
=======

ἀποτίνω (G0661)

τίνω

57\.156 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Pay, Price, Cost

to pay back, to remunerate (a technical, legal term) to pay compensation for damages or costs It is also possible to translate this expression in [PHM 1:19](https://ref.ly/Titus1:19) as ‘I will pay you for any loss which you have had.’

[PHM 1:19](https://ref.ly/Titus1:19)

* **Associated Passages:** Philemon 1:19

ἀποτολμάω
=========

τολμάω

to be very bold, to be very daring to be particularly bold or daring in what one does

noun · τομός

to show harshness, to act harshly, to be harsh, harshness to act harshly toward someone

## ἀποτόμως (id: 000658000000000)

ἀποτόμως
========

ἀποτόμως (G0664)

adv · τομός

88\.74 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Kindness, Harshness

harshly (derivative of ἀποτομία ‘to show harshness,’ [88\.73](#88.73)) pertaining to acting or dealing harshly with someone In some languages the equivalent of ἀποτομία ([88\.73](#88.73)) or ἀποτόμως is a negative expression combined with showing kindness, and therefore one may translate ‘to refuse to show kindness.’ In a number of circumstances, however, the equivalent of ἀποτομία or ἀποτόμως is a highly idiomatic expression, for example, ‘to refuse to look at,’ ‘to frown upon,’ or ‘to push aside.’

[2CO 13:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor13:10) · [TIT 1:13](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:13)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Corinthians 13:10; Titus 1:13

to escape, to avoid to become safe from danger by avoiding or escaping1

1 It is possible that ἐκφεύγω\[b] and ἀποφεύγω differ somewhat in meaning from φεύγω\[b] and from each other, but it is not possible to determine from existing contexts what the distinctive features might be, except perhaps to indicate that ἐκφεύγω\[b] and ἀποφεύγω may be connotatively somewhat more emphatic than φεύγω\[b]. Note, also, that in this series of terms meaning to escape, there is no special indication of movement, but simply the fact of not having to experience some particular difficulty or danger.

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Peter 1:4; 2 Peter 2:18; 2 Peter 2:20

χωρίζω

15\.14 · Linear Movement · Move, Come/Go

to move away, to disappear to move away from a normal location, with the implication of disappearing It would be difficult to translate ἀποχωρίζομαι in [REV 6:14](https://ref.ly/Jude6:14) as meaning literally ‘to go away,’ for this would seem strange in a reference to the sky. If, however, one employs an expression meaning to disappear, it may be necessary to say literally ‘could no longer be seen.’ For another interpretation of ἀποχωρίζομαι in [REV 6:14](https://ref.ly/Jude6:14), see [63\.30](#63.30).

[REV 6:14](https://ref.ly/Jude6:14)

63\.30 · Whole, Unite, Part, Divide · Separate

to separate definitely, to go one’s own way, to split up to separate more or less definitively one from another (evidently somewhat more emphatic in meaning than χωρίζω\[a] ‘to separate,’ [63\.29](#63.29)) For another interpretation of ἀποχωρίζομαι in [REV 6:14](https://ref.ly/Jude6:14), see ἀποχωρίζομαι\[a] [15\.14](#15.14).

[ACT 15:39](https://ref.ly/John15:39) · [REV 6:14](https://ref.ly/Jude6:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 6:14; Acts 15:39


## ἀποψύχω (id: 000668000000000)

ἀποψύχω
=======

ἀποψύχω (G0674)

ψύχομαι

23\.184 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

to faint to lose one’s consciousness temporarily It is possible that ἀποψύχω in [LUK 21:26](https://ref.ly/Mark21:26) refers primarily to a psychological experience rather than a physiological one. Some scholars, however, have interpreted ἀποψύχω in this context as meaning ‘to die.’ For another interpretation of ἀποψύχω in [LUK 21:26](https://ref.ly/Mark21:26), see ἀποψύχω\[b] [25\.293](#25.293).

[LUK 21:26](https://ref.ly/Mark21:26)

25\.293 · Attitudes and Emotions · Discouragement

to be totally disheartened, to be completely discouraged, to lose heart (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀποψύχω\[a] ‘to faint,’ [23\.184](#23.184)) to become totally disheartened and thus ready to give up For another interpretation of ἀποψύχω in [LUK 21:26](https://ref.ly/Mark21:26), see ἀποψύχω\[a] [23\.184](#23.184).

[LUK 21:26](https://ref.ly/Mark21:26)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 21:26

## ἀπρόσιτος (id: 000670000000000)

ἀπρόσιτος
=========

ἀπρόσιτος (G0676)

adj · εἶμι

15\.80 · Linear Movement · Come Near, Approach

unapproachable, that which cannot be approached pertaining to not being capable of being approached It may be necessary to indicate, either in the text or in a marginal note, the reason why the light cannot be approached, for one could interpret this passage to mean that the light was either so small as not to be generally visible or so far away that it could not be reached. If, however, one uses a term for ‘light’ which implies extreme intensity of light, then the context should indicate the basis for its being unapproachable.

## ἀπρόσκοπος (id: 000671000000000)

ἀπρόσκοπος
==========

ἀπρόσκοπος (G0677)

adj

88\.318 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sin, Wrongdoing, Guilt

blameless, without blame pertaining to being blameless in view of not having given offense In a number of languages the concept of ‘being blameless’ as in [88\.317](#88.317) and [88\.318](#88.318) can only be expressed by a verbal expression involving some measure of complaint. For example, the expression in [ACT 24:16](https://ref.ly/John24:16) may be rendered as ‘I do my best to live in such a way that neither God nor people can blame me’ or ‘... can complain against me.’

without causing offense, without causing trouble pertaining to not causing offense

[1CO 10:32](https://ref.ly/Rom10:32)

====

ἅπτω (G0680) — ἅπτομαι

ἀνάπτω · ἁφή · καθάπτω · περιάπτω

14\.65 · Physical Events and States · Burning

to ignite, to kindle, to set ablaze, to start a fire, to light a lamp to cause the process of burning to begin As already noted in the series of terms listed in [14\.63](#14.63), languages differ appreciably in terms employed for different phases and results of burning. ## ἀπώλεια (id: 000677000000000)

ἀπώλεια
=======

ἀπώλεια (G0684)

noun · ὀλοθρεύω

20\.31 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Destroy

destruction act of destroying or causing the destruction of persons, objects, or institutions In a number of languages it is difficult to find a general term for ‘destruction.’ What has often happened is the extension of meaning from a more specific type of reference, for example, ‘to smash,’ to a more generic meaning of ‘to destroy,’ and whether one understands the specific or more generic meaning depends largely upon the context. Accordingly, in [ACT 8:3](https://ref.ly/John8:3) one may find a figurative usage of a term meaning ‘to smash’ (though understood in a more generic sense), for example, ‘Saul then worked in order to smash the church.’

[MAT 7:13](https://ref.ly/Matt7:13) · [JHN 17:12](https://ref.ly/Luke17:12) · [ACT 8:20](https://ref.ly/John8:20) · [ROM 9:22](https://ref.ly/Acts9:22) · [PHP 1:28](https://ref.ly/Eph1:28) · [PHP 3:19](https://ref.ly/Eph3:19) · [2TH 2:3](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:3) · [1TI 6:9](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:9) · [HEB 10:39](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:39) · [2PE 2:1](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:1) · [2PE 2:1](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:1) · [2PE 2:3](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:3) · [2PE 3:7](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:7) · [2PE 3:16](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:16) · [REV 17:8](https://ref.ly/Jude17:8) · [REV 17:11](https://ref.ly/Jude17:11)

65\.14 · Value · Valuable, Lacking in Value

waste, ruin an action demonstrating complete disregard for the value of something In the context of [MRK 14:4](https://ref.ly/Matt14:4) the understanding of ἀπώλεια in the literal sense of ‘destruction’ (see [20\.31](#20.31)) does not seem to be adequate. The following verse concerning the three hundred denarii would seem to indicate that it was disregard for the value of something rather than destroying the perfume. In fact, the use of the perfume would not have been a matter of destroying. Accordingly, an equivalent of ‘waste’ seems to be far more satisfactory. In some languages the equivalent of this question in [MRK 14:4](https://ref.ly/Matt14:4) is ‘why did she not think about the value of the perfume?’

[MAT 26:8](https://ref.ly/Matt26:8) · [MRK 14:4](https://ref.ly/Matt14:4)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 7:13; John 17:12; Acts 8:20; Romans 9:22; Philippians 1:28; Philippians 3:19; 2 Thessalonians 2:3; 1 Timothy 6:9; Hebrews 10:39; 2 Peter 2:1; 2 Peter 2:3; 2 Peter 3:7; 2 Peter 3:16; Revelation 17:8; Revelation 17:11; Matthew 26:8; Mark 14:4

## ἆρα (id: 000680000000000)

ἆρα
===

ἆρα (G0687)

ptcl

69\.14 · Affirmation, Negation · Markers for a Negative Response to Questions

indeed, then, ever a marker of a negative response to questions, usually implying anxiety or impatience1 In order to attempt to do some justice to the occurrence of the particle ἆρα, one may introduce a variety of terms which make a question somewhat more emphatically negative. In the case of [LUK 18:8](https://ref.ly/Mark18:8) one might translate ‘will he indeed find faith?’ In the case of [GAL 2:17](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:17) one might translate ‘how, then, could Christ ever be a servant of sin?’

[LUK 18:8](https://ref.ly/Mark18:8) · [ACT 8:30](https://ref.ly/John8:30) · [GAL 2:17](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:17)

1 In English it is difficult to provide a direct translation of ἆρα in this sense, though other languages may have a regularly corresponding particle with essentially the same significance.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 18:8; Acts 8:30; Galatians 2:17

## ἀργύριον (id: 000687000000000)

ἀργύριον
========

ἀργύριον (G0694)

noun · ἄργυρος

2\.51 · Natural Substances · Metals

silver the metal silver; after gold the next most highly valued metal in the ancient world As in the case of ‘gold’ ([2\.49](#2.49)), there is usually no difficulty involved in obtaining a satisfactory term for ‘silver’ in most languages. In [1PE 1:18](https://ref.ly/Jas1:18), however, there is a problem in speaking of silver and gold that ‘perish,’ or even as something that can be ‘destroyed,’ since in reality one does not destroy such elements, and silver and gold do not really ‘perish.’ On the other hand, silver and gold can become oxidized and lose their value, especially if they are impure (see [2\.60](#2.60)). Therefore, it seems far better to emphasize the lack of value in translating [1PE 1:18](https://ref.ly/Jas1:18). A suitable rendering may then be ‘not with things that can lose their value as silver and gold.’

[1PE 1:18](https://ref.ly/Jas1:18)

6\.73 · Artifacts · Money and Monetary Units

silver money, silver coin silver in the form of stamped coins used as money1

[MAT 25:18](https://ref.ly/Matt25:18) · [MAT 25:27](https://ref.ly/Matt25:27) · [MAT 26:15](https://ref.ly/Matt26:15) · [MAT 27:3](https://ref.ly/Matt27:3) · [MAT 27:5](https://ref.ly/Matt27:5) · [MAT 27:6](https://ref.ly/Matt27:6) · [MAT 27:9](https://ref.ly/Matt27:9) · [MAT 28:12](https://ref.ly/Matt28:12) · [MAT 28:15](https://ref.ly/Matt28:15) · [MRK 14:11](https://ref.ly/Matt14:11) · [LUK 9:3](https://ref.ly/Mark9:3) · [LUK 19:15](https://ref.ly/Mark19:15) · [LUK 19:23](https://ref.ly/Mark19:23) · [LUK 22:5](https://ref.ly/Mark22:5) · [ACT 7:16](https://ref.ly/John7:16) · [ACT 8:20](https://ref.ly/John8:20) · [ACT 19:19](https://ref.ly/John19:19)

6\.69 · Artifacts · Money and Monetary Units

**Collocation:** ἀργύριον καὶ χρυσίον

money (an idiom, literally: silver and gold) a generic expression for currency

[ACT 3:6](https://ref.ly/John3:6) · [ACT 20:33](https://ref.ly/John20:33) · [1PE 1:18](https://ref.ly/Jas1:18)

1 Except for the occurrence of ἀργύριον in the plural ([MAT 26:15](https://ref.ly/Matt26:15)), it would be possible to derive all of these references to copper (bronze), silver, and gold coins as direct and predictable metonymic correspondences of the respective terms for substances (see [2\.54](#2.54), 51, 49\). Since, however, some languages employ clear distinctions between metals as substances and metals when manufactured into the form of coins, the present classification would seem to be more useful.

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Peter 1:18; Matthew 25:18; Matthew 25:27; Matthew 26:15; Matthew 27:3; Matthew 27:5; Matthew 27:6; Matthew 27:9; Matthew 28:12; Matthew 28:15; Mark 14:11; Luke 9:3; Luke 19:15; Luke 19:23; Luke 22:5; Acts 7:16; Acts 8:20; Acts 19:19; Acts 3:6; Acts 20:33


## ἀργυροκόπος (id: 000688000000000)

===========

noun

## ἄργυρος (id: 000689000000000)

ἄργυρος
=======

ἄργυρος (G0696)

noun · ἀργύριον · ἀργυροκόπος · ἀργυροῦς · ἀφιλάργυρος · φιλαργυρία · φιλάργυρος

2\.51 · Natural Substances · Metals

silver the metal silver; after gold the next most highly valued metal in the ancient world As in the case of ‘gold’ ([2\.49](#2.49)), there is usually no difficulty involved in obtaining a satisfactory term for ‘silver’ in most languages. In [1PE 1:18](https://ref.ly/Jas1:18), however, there is a problem in speaking of silver and gold that ‘perish,’ or even as something that can be ‘destroyed,’ since in reality one does not destroy such elements, and silver and gold do not really ‘perish.’ On the other hand, silver and gold can become oxidized and lose their value, especially if they are impure (see [2\.60](#2.60)). Therefore, it seems far better to emphasize the lack of value in translating [1PE 1:18](https://ref.ly/Jas1:18). A suitable rendering may then be ‘not with things that can lose their value as silver and gold.’

[ACT 17:29](https://ref.ly/John17:29) · [1CO 3:12](https://ref.ly/Rom3:12) · [JAS 5:3](https://ref.ly/Heb5:3) · [REV 18:12](https://ref.ly/Jude18:12)

6\.73 · Artifacts · Money and Monetary Units

silver money, silver coin silver in the form of stamped coins used as money1

[MAT 10:9](https://ref.ly/Matt10:9)

1 Except for the occurrence of ἀργύριον in the plural ([MAT 26:15](https://ref.ly/Matt26:15)), it would be possible to derive all of these references to copper (bronze), silver, and gold coins as direct and predictable metonymic correspondences of the respective terms for substances (see [2\.54](#2.54), 51, 49\). Since, however, some languages employ clear distinctions between metals as substances and metals when manufactured into the form of coins, the present classification would seem to be more useful.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 17:29; 1 Corinthians 3:12; James 5:3; Revelation 18:12; Matthew 10:9

## Ἄρειος Πάγος (id: 000691000000000)

Ἄρειος Πάγος
============

Ἄρειος Πάγος (G0697)

noun nom

93\.412 · Names of Persons and Places · Places

Areopagus (literally: hill of Mars) the location of an Athenian court, traditionally associated with a rocky hill close to the Acropolis, though probably located in the marketplace at the foot of the hill For another interpretation of Ἄρειος Πάγος in [ACT 17:19](https://ref.ly/John17:19) [ACT 17:22](https://ref.ly/John17:22), see Ἄρειος Πάγος\[b] [11\.81](#11.81).

[ACT 17:19](https://ref.ly/John17:19) · [ACT 17:19](https://ref.ly/John17:19) · [ACT 17:22](https://ref.ly/John17:22) · [ACT 17:22](https://ref.ly/John17:22)

11\.81 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

council of the Areopagus an advisory council of Athens dealing with ethical, cultural, and religious matters It is also possible to understand τοῦ Αρείου Πάγου as referring to the location rather than to the council (see \[a]).\|It is probably wise in translating Αρειος Πάγος\[b] to simply transliterate the expression, since it has the status of a proper name. On the other hand, it is extremely important that some footnote be employed so as to indicate something of the significance of this advisory council of Athens.

[ACT 17:19](https://ref.ly/John17:19) · [ACT 17:19](https://ref.ly/John17:19) · [ACT 17:22](https://ref.ly/John17:22) · [ACT 17:22](https://ref.ly/John17:22)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 17:19; Acts 17:22

## ἀρήν (id: 000699000000000)

ἀρήν
====

ἀρήν (G0704)

noun · ἀρνίον

4\.24 · Animals · Animals

lamb young of sheep (πρόβατον); often used for ritual sacrifice In a number of languages a clear distinction is made between male and female lambs. It is therefore essential in any figurative reference to male persons to employ an appropriate term for lamb. In a number of languages, of course, a lamb is simply referred to as a ‘young sheep’ or ‘child sheep.’\|Perhaps the most widespread story about cultural differences in Bible translation is the one which refers to the translation of ‘lamb’ as ‘little seal of God’ in an Eskimo language. However, there is no evidence that this type of rendering was ever used, since in matters of appearance, habitat, and behavior, lambs and little seals have practically nothing in common. Furthermore, Eskimo languages have terms for ‘lamb’ and in some instances, at least, use the expression ‘young, woolly goat,’ since wild goats are well known and wool is well known as an imported product.

[LUK 10:3](https://ref.ly/Mark10:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 10:3

## ἀριστάω (id: 000704000000000)

ἀριστάω
=======

ἀριστάω (G0709)

ἄριστον

23\.20 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

to eat a meal, to have a meal to eat a meal, without reference to any particular time of the day or to the type of food involved In some languages it is impossible to speak of ‘having a meal’ without indicating the time of day or the nature of the food consumed.

[LUK 11:37](https://ref.ly/Mark11:37)

23\.24 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

to have breakfast to eat the earlier meal of the day Some scholars, however, interpret ἀριστάω in [JHN 21:12](https://ref.ly/Luke21:12) as meaning any kind of meal (see ἀριστάω\[a], [23\.20](#23.20)), and therefore one would translate as ‘Jesus said to them, Come have something to eat’ or ‘... have a meal.’\|In a number of languages the earlier meal of the day may be spoken of as ‘the morning meal’ or ‘the first meal.’ In some languages it is actually called ‘the leftovers meal,’ but see the discussion on meals at different times of the day at [23\.25](#23.25).

[JHN 21:12](https://ref.ly/Luke21:12) · [JHN 21:15](https://ref.ly/Luke21:15)

## ἀριστερός (id: 000705000000000)

ἀριστερός
=========

ἀριστερός (G0710) — ἀριστερά

adj

82\.7 · Spacial Orientations · Left, Right, Straight Ahead, Opposite

left, left side pertaining to being to the left of some point of reference In [2CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:7) the phrase ὅπλον ἀριστερόν, literally ‘left\-hand weapon,’ refers to a weapon used by the left hand and therefore a defensive weapon, while ὅπλον δεξιόν, literally ‘weapon for the right hand,’ refers to an offensive weapon.\|For an interpretation of ἀριστερός as part of an idiom in [MRK 10:37](https://ref.ly/Matt10:37), see [87\.35](#87.35).

[MAT 6:3](https://ref.ly/Matt6:3) · [MRK 10:37](https://ref.ly/Matt10:37) · [LUK 23:33](https://ref.ly/Mark23:33) · [2CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:7)

8\.33 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

left hand The left hand is designated in a number of languages by an idiomatic phrase which normally contrasts with expressions for the right hand. For example, one may often speak of the left hand as ‘the bad hand’ or ‘the weak hand,’ and in some instances as ‘the anus hand,’ since this is the hand used in cleansing the body after defecation. See also [8\.32](#8.32).

[MAT 6:3](https://ref.ly/Matt6:3) · [2CO 6:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 6:3; Mark 10:37; Luke 23:33; 2 Corinthians 6:7

## ἄριστον (id: 000707000000000)

ἄριστον
=======

ἄριστον (G0712)

noun · ἀριστάω

23\.22 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

a meal, a banquet, a feast a meal whether simple or elaborate (this generic meaning of ἄριστον is in contrast with the more specific meaning of ἄριστον\[b], [23\.23](#23.23)) Though ἄριστον\[a], δεῖπνον\[a], and βρῶσις\[b] are highly generic in this usage, it is not necessary to translate these terms by a corresponding generic term in another language, since a more specific term may fit the context more satisfactorily. In fact, a generic term might be quite inappropriate in that it would suggest in the case of [MRK 6:21](https://ref.ly/Matt6:21) that Herod was quite stingy in simply providing an ordinary meal for his officials.

[MAT 22:4](https://ref.ly/Matt22:4) · [LUK 11:38](https://ref.ly/Mark11:38)

25\.81 · Attitudes and Emotions · Content, Satisfied

to be content, to be satisfied to be happy or content as the result of having what one desires or needs to be In a number of languages ‘to be content’ is expressed negatively, for example, ‘to not complain.’ In other instances, one may be forced to employ a type of direct or indirect discourse, for example, ‘to acknowledge that it is enough’ or ‘to say, It is all that is needed.’

## ἄρκος (id: 000710000000000)

ἄρκος
=====

ἄρκος (G0715a)

noun

4\.12 · Animals · Animals

bear large wild four\-footed mammal with thick fur and doglike face; walks on all fours but may stand up on its back legs to get a better view; may be 2 m. tall when standing up and weigh over 200 kilos; feeds on roots, berries, wild fruit, mice, and lizards, and, occasionally, small livestock; Ursus arctos syriacus; dangerous when provoked or robbed of cubs; associated with violence and danger The term ἄρκος ‘bear’ occurs in the NT only in [REV 13:2](https://ref.ly/Jude13:2) as a description of the apocalyptic beast. In languages where a term for bear is not known, it is probably best to borrow a word and describe the referent in a footnote or glossary. In [REV 13:2](https://ref.ly/Jude13:2) a somewhat descriptive phrase is sometimes employed, for example, ‘with large feet having claws like those of an animal called bear.’ By this means the information about the bear’s feet has been incorporated into the text, but usually it is better to leave such information to a marginal note.

[REV 13:2](https://ref.ly/Jude13:2)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 13:2


## ἅρμα (id: 000711000000000)

ἅρμα
====

ἅρμα (G0716)

noun

6\.52 · Artifacts · Vehicles

chariot open vehicle with two or four wheels; harnessed to and pulled by horses; used in war or for travelling In [REV 9:9](https://ref.ly/Jude9:9) the reference is to war chariots drawn by horses, while in [ACT 8:28](https://ref.ly/John8:28) [ACT 8:38](https://ref.ly/John8:38) the reference is to a travelling chariot in which the Ethiopian official was riding. In languages which have no technical term for chariot, it is possible to speak of a war chariot as ‘a war carriage’ or ‘a horse\-drawn cart for fighting.’ A travelling chariot may be described as ‘a travelling carriage’ or ‘a horse\-drawn vehicle.’ A further description of such chariots may be given in a glossary.

[ACT 8:28](https://ref.ly/John8:28) · [ACT 8:29](https://ref.ly/John8:29) · [ACT 8:38](https://ref.ly/John8:38) · [REV 9:9](https://ref.ly/Jude9:9)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 8:28; Acts 8:29; Acts 8:38; Revelation 9:9

## ἁρμός (id: 000715000000000)

ἁρμός
=====

ἁρμός (G0719)

noun · ἁρμόζομαι

8\.59 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

joint a part of the body which joins two parts together In the use of ἁρμός the emphasis is not upon the joints as a moving part of the body but as a means by which different parts of the body are effectively joined together. Accordingly, ἁρμός overlaps in meaning with ἁφή\[a] ([18\.17](#18.17)). For problems in translating [HEB 4:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:12), see [8\.62](#8.62).

[HEB 4:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 4:12


## ἀρνέομαι (id: 000716000000000)

ἀρνέομαι
========

ἀρνέομαι (G0720)

ἀπαρνέομαι

33\.277 · Communication · Admit, Confess, Deny

to deny to say that one does not know about or is in any way related to a person or event1 In translating ἀρνέομαι\[a] and ἀπαρνέομαι\[a], one can often use an equivalent phrase such as ‘to say that one does not know’ or ‘to say that one has nothing to do with.’\|In denying an event, as in [LUK 8:45](https://ref.ly/Mark8:45), it may be possible to translate ‘but they all said they knew nothing about it.’ It is also possible to deny having done something, and therefore one may translate the response in [LUK 8:45](https://ref.ly/Mark8:45) as ‘but they all said that they did not do it’ or ‘... that they did not touch him.’

[MAT 10:33](https://ref.ly/Matt10:33) · [MAT 10:33](https://ref.ly/Matt10:33) · [MAT 26:70](https://ref.ly/Matt26:70) · [MAT 26:72](https://ref.ly/Matt26:72) · [MRK 14:68](https://ref.ly/Matt14:68) · [MRK 14:70](https://ref.ly/Matt14:70) · [LUK 8:45](https://ref.ly/Mark8:45) · [LUK 22:57](https://ref.ly/Mark22:57) · [JHN 1:20](https://ref.ly/Luke1:20) · [JHN 13:38](https://ref.ly/Luke13:38) · [JHN 18:25](https://ref.ly/Luke18:25) · [JHN 18:27](https://ref.ly/Luke18:27) · [ACT 3:13](https://ref.ly/John3:13) · [ACT 3:14](https://ref.ly/John3:14) · [ACT 4:16](https://ref.ly/John4:16) · [2TI 2:12](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:12) · [1JN 2:22](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:22) · [1JN 2:22](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:22) · [1JN 2:23](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:23)

34\.48 · Association · Establish or Confirm a Relation

to deny to deny any relationship of association with someone In a number of languages one may best translate ‘deny’ as ‘to make manifest not to know.’ Accordingly, one may translate the first part of [LUK 12:9](https://ref.ly/Mark12:9) as ‘whoever shows people that he does not know me’ or ‘whoever refuses to admit that he does know me.’ Denial need not involve some verbal formulation, though in some languages this is the most common practice. See also [33\.277](#33.277).

[LUK 12:9](https://ref.ly/Mark12:9) · [1TI 5:8](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:8) · [2TI 2:12](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:12) · [2TI 2:12](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:12) · [2TI 3:5](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:5) · [TIT 1:16](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:16) · [2PE 2:1](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:1) · [1JN 2:22](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:22) · [1JN 2:23](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:23) · [JUD 1:4](https://ref.ly/3John1:4) · [REV 2:13](https://ref.ly/Jude2:13) · [REV 3:8](https://ref.ly/Jude3:8)

31\.25 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Agree, Consent

to refuse to agree to, to not consent to to refuse to agree or consent to something

[HEB 11:24](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:24)

30\.52 · Think · To Think About, with the Implied Purpose of Responding Appropriately

to disregard, to pay no attention to, to say no to to refuse to give thought to or express concern for2 There are a number of problems involved in rendering appropriately ἀρνέομαι\[d] and ἀπαρνέομαι\[b] as in [LUK 9:23](https://ref.ly/Mark9:23) and [MRK 8:34](https://ref.ly/Matt8:34).\|In a number of languages it simply makes no sense to translate ‘to say No to oneself,’ nor is it possible in many instances to use an expression such as ‘to deny oneself,’ since it almost always implies to deny oneself something. Sometimes the meaning may be expressed in a figurative or idiomatic manner, for example, ‘to refuse to pay attention to what one’s own desires are saying’ or ‘to refuse to think about what one just wants for oneself.’ In certain instances other kinds of idioms may be employed, for example, ‘to put oneself at the end of the line’ or even ‘to say to one’s heart, Keep quiet.’

[LUK 9:23](https://ref.ly/Mark9:23) · [TIT 2:12](https://ref.ly/2Tim2:12)

36\.43 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Follow, Be a Disciple

to refuse to follow, to refuse to obey, to reject to refuse to follow someone as a leader

[ACT 7:35](https://ref.ly/John7:35) · [TIT 1:16](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:16) · [1JN 2:22](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:22) · [1JN 2:23](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:23)

88\.231 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Hypocrisy, Pretense

to be false to oneself, to be untrue to oneself to behave in a way which is untrue to one’s real self, in a sense of denying certain valid aspects of one’s personality It is possible in some languages to translate ‘he cannot be false to himself’ in [2TI 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:13) as ‘he cannot be different from what he really is’ or ‘he cannot be otherwise than good.’

[2TI 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:13)

1 It is quite possible that ἀπαρνέομαι\[a] is somewhat more forceful in meaning than ἀρνέομαι\[a], but this cannot be demonstrated from NT usage. 2 ἀπαρνέομαι\[b] appears to be somewhat more forceful in meaning than ἀρνέομαι\[d].

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 10:33; Matthew 26:70; Matthew 26:72; Mark 14:68; Mark 14:70; Luke 8:45; Luke 22:57; John 1:20; John 13:38; John 18:25; John 18:27; Acts 3:13; Acts 3:14; Acts 4:16; 2 Timothy 2:12; 1 John 2:22; 1 John 2:23; Luke 12:9; 1 Timothy 5:8; 2 Timothy 3:5; Titus 1:16; 2 Peter 2:1; Jude 1:4; Revelation 2:13; Revelation 3:8; Hebrews 11:24; Luke 9:23; Titus 2:12; Acts 7:35; 2 Timothy 2:13

## ἀρνίον (id: 000718000000000)

ἀρνίον
======

ἀρνίον (G0721)

noun · ἀρήν

4\.25 · Animals · Animals

lamb, sheep, ram (derivative of ἀρήν ‘lamb,’ [4\.24](#4.24)) young of sheep (πρόβατον); often used for ritual sacrifice; or a sheep of any age In the one context in the NT, namely, [REV 13:11](https://ref.ly/Jude13:11), in which ἀρνίον refers literally to a sheep, it is used in a phrase referring to the horns of an ἀρνίον. In such a context the reference is undoubtedly to a ‘ram,’ that is to say, the adult male of sheep.

[REV 13:11](https://ref.ly/Jude13:11)

11\.29 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

a person who is like a lamb (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀρνίον\[a] ‘lamb,’ [4\.25](#4.25)) a follower of Christ, with the implication of helplessness and dependence Compare [11\.30](#11.30) and [11\.31](#11.31).

[JHN 21:15](https://ref.ly/Luke21:15)

4\.26 · Animals · Animals

Lamb a title for Christ ἀρνίον\[c] as a title of Jesus Christ occurs only in the book of Revelation, but it is used there more than twenty times. In contrast with ἀρνίον\[b] in βόσκε τὰ ἀρνία μου ‘feed my lambs’ ([JHN 21:15](https://ref.ly/Luke21:15); see [11\.29](#11.29)), designating people who need care and help, the supplementary components of meaning in ἀρνίον\[c] involve the atoning sacrifice of Jesus Christ on the cross. Some persons have wanted to eliminate the notion of ‘lamb’ in this title, especially in the case of languages in which sheep are looked down on as relatively worthless scavengers (as is the case in certain tropical areas of the world), but in view of the significance of the symbolism of ‘lamb’ in terms of OT sacrificial practices, it is wrong to avoid a term meaning ‘lamb.’ A marginal note, however, may be necessary to explain the significance of this title.

[REV 5:6](https://ref.ly/Jude5:6) · [REV 5:8](https://ref.ly/Jude5:8) · [REV 5:12](https://ref.ly/Jude5:12) · [REV 5:13](https://ref.ly/Jude5:13) · [REV 6:1](https://ref.ly/Jude6:1) · [REV 6:16](https://ref.ly/Jude6:16) · [REV 7:9](https://ref.ly/Jude7:9) · [REV 7:10](https://ref.ly/Jude7:10) · [REV 7:14](https://ref.ly/Jude7:14) · [REV 7:17](https://ref.ly/Jude7:17) · [REV 12:11](https://ref.ly/Jude12:11) · [REV 13:8](https://ref.ly/Jude13:8) · [REV 14:1](https://ref.ly/Jude14:1) · [REV 14:4](https://ref.ly/Jude14:4) · [REV 14:4](https://ref.ly/Jude14:4) · [REV 14:10](https://ref.ly/Jude14:10) · [REV 15:3](https://ref.ly/Jude15:3) · [REV 17:14](https://ref.ly/Jude17:14) · [REV 17:14](https://ref.ly/Jude17:14) · [REV 19:7](https://ref.ly/Jude19:7) · [REV 19:9](https://ref.ly/Jude19:9) · [REV 21:9](https://ref.ly/Jude21:9) · [REV 21:14](https://ref.ly/Jude21:14) · [REV 21:22](https://ref.ly/Jude21:22) · [REV 21:23](https://ref.ly/Jude21:23) · [REV 21:27](https://ref.ly/Jude21:27) · [REV 22:1](https://ref.ly/Jude22:1) · [REV 22:3](https://ref.ly/Jude22:3)

## ἀροτριάω (id: 000719000000000)

ἀροτριάω
========

ἀροτριάω (G0722)

ἄροτρον

43\.4 · Agriculture

to plow, to use a plow to plow, as a means of preparing land for sowing In biblical times plows did not turn over the soil but were used simply to break the surface, seldom to a depth of more than about ten inches (or twenty five centimeters).

[LUK 17:7](https://ref.ly/Mark17:7) · [1CO 9:10](https://ref.ly/Rom9:10) · [1CO 9:10](https://ref.ly/Rom9:10)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 17:7; 1 Corinthians 9:10


## ἄροτρον (id: 000720000000000)

ἄροτρον
=======

ἄροτρον (G0723)

noun · ἀροτριάω

6\.4 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Agriculture and Husbandry

plow a flat triangular blade (the “plowshare”) made of hard wood or metal, connected to a handle; used to cut the soil in preparation for the sowing of seed or the planting of seedlings In most parts of the world at least some type of plow is known, and in ancient Palestine the plow was not a turning plow, but one which simply dug a relatively shallow furrow, but in doing so, loosened the soil. Where plows are not known or used, it is possible to employ a descriptive phrase such as ‘a tool for preparing the ground for sowing.’ For ἄροτρον as part of a Semitic idiom in [LUK 9:62](https://ref.ly/Mark9:62), see [68\.6](#68.6).

[LUK 9:62](https://ref.ly/Mark9:62)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 9:62

to rob, to carry off, to plunder, to forcefully seize to forcefully take something away from someone else, often with the implication of a sudden attack

[HEB 10:34](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:34)

57\.237 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Steal, Rob

[MAT 23:25](https://ref.ly/Matt23:25)

grasping, violent greed a state of strong desire to gain things and, if necessary, by violent means

[LUK 11:39](https://ref.ly/Mark11:39)

## ἁρπαγμός (id: 000722000000000)

ἁρπαγμός
========

ἁρπαγμός (G0725)

noun · ἁρπάζω

57\.235 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Steal, Rob

to rob, to carry off, to plunder, to forcefully seize to forcefully take something away from someone else, often with the implication of a sudden attack This interpretation of [PHP 2:6](https://ref.ly/Eph2:6) reflects the position of Jesus after the incarnation and the fact that he had no thought of trying to become equal with God because he already possessed all of the qualities of deity. Some traditional translations have rendered the latter part of [PHP 2:6](https://ref.ly/Eph2:6) as ‘did not think it robbery to be equal with God,’ but see [57\.236](#57.236).

[PHP 2:6](https://ref.ly/Eph2:6)

57\.236 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Steal, Rob

something to hold by force, something to be forcibly retained that which is to be held on to forcibly Compare the rendering of ἁρπαγμός\[a] in [57\.235](#57.235). Since ἁρπαγμός may mean not only ‘to grasp something forcefully which one does not have’ ([57\.235](#57.235)) but also ‘to retain by force what one possesses,’ it is possible to translate [PHP 2:6](https://ref.ly/Eph2:6) in two quite different ways. This second interpretation of ἁρπαγμός presumes the position of Jesus prior to the incarnation and hence his willingness to experience the kenosis or ‘emptying’ of his divine prerogatives. In any translation of [PHP 2:6](https://ref.ly/Eph2:6) it is important that both possible renderings be clearly indicated, one in the text and the other in the margin.

[PHP 2:6](https://ref.ly/Eph2:6)

* **Associated Passages:** Philippians 2:6

ἁρπάζω
======

ἁρπάζω (G0726)

ἁρπαγή · ἁρπαγμός · ἅρπαξ · διαρπάζω · συναρπάζω

18\.4 · Attachment · Grasp, Hold

to seize, to snatch away, to take away to grab or seize by force, with the purpose of removing and/or controlling In [MAT 13:19](https://ref.ly/Matt13:19) the context as a whole is figurative, but a more or less literal rendering of ‘snatches’ or ‘seizes’ is probably satisfactory.

[MAT 13:19](https://ref.ly/Matt13:19) · [JHN 6:15](https://ref.ly/Luke6:15) · [ACT 8:39](https://ref.ly/John8:39) · [ACT 23:10](https://ref.ly/John23:10) · [2CO 12:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:2) · [2CO 12:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:4) · [1TH 4:17](https://ref.ly/Col4:17) · [JUD 1:23](https://ref.ly/3John1:23) · [REV 12:5](https://ref.ly/Jude12:5)

39\.49 · Hostility, Strife · Attack

to attack, to seize to attack, with the implication of seizing It is also possible that in [MAT 11:12](https://ref.ly/Matt11:12) ἁρπάζω is to be understood in the sense of ‘attack’: βιασταὶ ἁρπάζουσιν αὐτήν ‘violent men attack it.’

[MAT 11:12](https://ref.ly/Matt11:12) · [JHN 10:12](https://ref.ly/Luke10:12)

57\.235 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Steal, Rob

to rob, to carry off, to plunder, to forcefully seize to forcefully take something away from someone else, often with the implication of a sudden attack

[MAT 12:29](https://ref.ly/Matt12:29)

37\.28 · Control, Rule · Control, Restrain

to gain control over, to seize, to snatch away to gain control over by force Though in [JHN 10:28](https://ref.ly/Luke10:28) ἁρπάζω would appear to be in a literal context in view of the expression ἐκ τῆς χειρός μου ‘out of my hand,’ nevertheless ἁρπάζω is certainly figurative in meaning and so is χείρ ‘hand.’

[JHN 10:28](https://ref.ly/Luke10:28) · [JHN 10:29](https://ref.ly/Luke10:29)

9:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:10)

5\.1 · Foods and Condiments · Food

food any kind of food or nourishment1 Almost all languages have at least a few general terms for food, though these may be derived from verbal expressions meaning essentially ‘that which is eaten.’ In some instances a distinction may be made between ‘that which is chewed’ and ‘that which is drunk,’ and it may therefore be important in certain contexts to speak of food in general by a phrase meaning ‘that which is either chewed or drunk.’ If one fails to include both types of food, the reader might interpret a passage such as [MAT 6:11](https://ref.ly/Matt6:11) as requesting only solid foods.\|In the place of a general term or phrase such as ‘that which is eaten’ or ‘that which is consumed,’ some languages generalize the meaning of a particular term. For example, in certain parts of the Orient the term meaning specifically ‘rice’ also refers to ‘food’ in general. In fact, some persons will declare, ‘I have not eaten!’ if they have not had rice as part of a meal. A similar type of meaning has developed in English with respect to ‘bread.’ For example, in rendering [LUK 4:4](https://ref.ly/Mark4:4) (οὐκ ἐπ’ ἄρτῳ μόνῳ ζήσεται ὁ ἄνθρωπος ‘man shall not live by bread alone’) it would be wrong to employ a strictly literal rendering, since this might mean simply that people should not live on a diet consisting solely of bread. The meaning of [LUK 4:4](https://ref.ly/Mark4:4) is, of course, ‘to live means more than merely eating.’

[MAT 6:11](https://ref.ly/Matt6:11) · [MAT 15:2](https://ref.ly/Matt15:2) · [MAT 15:26](https://ref.ly/Matt15:26) · [MRK 3:20](https://ref.ly/Matt3:20) · [MRK 6:8](https://ref.ly/Matt6:8) · [MRK 7:2](https://ref.ly/Matt7:2) · [MRK 7:5](https://ref.ly/Matt7:5) · [LUK 7:33](https://ref.ly/Mark7:33) · [LUK 11:3](https://ref.ly/Mark11:3) · [LUK 14:1](https://ref.ly/Mark14:1) · [LUK 14:15](https://ref.ly/Mark14:15) · [LUK 15:17](https://ref.ly/Mark15:17) · [JHN 6:31](https://ref.ly/Luke6:31) · [JHN 6:32](https://ref.ly/Luke6:32) · [JHN 6:32](https://ref.ly/Luke6:32) · [JHN 6:33](https://ref.ly/Luke6:33) · [JHN 6:34](https://ref.ly/Luke6:34) · [JHN 6:35](https://ref.ly/Luke6:35) · [JHN 6:41](https://ref.ly/Luke6:41) · [JHN 6:48](https://ref.ly/Luke6:48) · [JHN 6:50](https://ref.ly/Luke6:50) · [JHN 6:51](https://ref.ly/Luke6:51) · [JHN 6:51](https://ref.ly/Luke6:51) · [JHN 6:51](https://ref.ly/Luke6:51) · [JHN 6:58](https://ref.ly/Luke6:58) · [JHN 6:58](https://ref.ly/Luke6:58) · [JHN 13:18](https://ref.ly/Luke13:18) · [2TH 3:8](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:8) · [2TH 3:12](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:12)

53\.26 · Religious Activities · Offering, Sacrifice

**Collocation:** ἄρτοι τῆς προθέσεως

bread offered to God, consecrated bread (an idiom, literally: bread of the placing forth) bread which was set out as an offering in the presence of God in the Tabernacle and later in the Temple In some languages ἄρτοι τῆς προθέσεως is rendered as ‘bread placed before God’ or ‘bread placed in the presence of God.’

[MAT 12:4](https://ref.ly/Matt12:4) · [MRK 2:26](https://ref.ly/Matt2:26) · [LUK 6:4](https://ref.ly/Mark6:4) · [HEB 9:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:2)

23\.20 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

**Collocation:** ἄρτον κλάω

to eat a meal, to have a meal (an idiom, literally: to break bread) to eat a meal, without reference to any particular time of the day or to the type of food involved In some languages it is impossible to speak of ‘having a meal’ without indicating the time of day or the nature of the food consumed.\|The implication of ‘eating together’ comes from the total context. ἐν δὲ τῇ μιᾷ τῶν σαββάτων συνηγμένων ἡμῶν κλάσαι ἄρτον ‘on the first day of the week we gathered together for a meal’ [ACT 20:7](https://ref.ly/John20:7). No doubt the reference in [ACT 20:7](https://ref.ly/John20:7) is to the ‘fellowship meal,’ called ‘agape,’ which constituted the early Christian form of the ‘Lord’s Supper.’ See [23\.28](#23.28).

[ACT 2:46](https://ref.ly/John2:46) · [ACT 20:7](https://ref.ly/John20:7) · [ACT 20:11](https://ref.ly/John20:11)

57\.190 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Earn, Gain, Do Business

**Collocation:** τὸν ἑαυτοῦ ἄρτον ἐσθίω

to earn a living, to earn a livelihood (an idiom, literally: to eat one’s own bread) to earn a living by one’s own efforts

[2TH 3:12](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:12)

1 There are no doubt certain subtle differences of meaning in the various terms listed here as meaning food, but it is impossible to determine such distinctions in meaning from NT contexts. Perhaps τροφή and διατροφή are somewhat more generic in meaning and may suggest a greater emphasis upon nourishment. At the same time, βρῶσις\[c] and βρῶμα\[a] may imply solid substantive food, but it is difficult, if not impossible, to argue for such subtle connotative distinctions in meanings which are essentially generic.

## ἀρχή (id: 000742000000000)

ἀρχή
====

ἀρχή (G0746)

noun · ἄρχω

68\.1 · Aspect · Begin, Start

beginning initiation of an action, process, or state of being It would also be possible to treat ἀρχή in [MRK 1:1](https://ref.ly/Matt1:1) as indicating sequence (Domain 61\), so that the contents of verse 1 could be regarded as a type of title for the following section. It seems far better, however, to regard ἀρχή in [MRK 1:1](https://ref.ly/Matt1:1) as being closely related to what immediately follows in verse 2, so that one may translate ‘the good news about Jesus Christ the Son of God began as the prophet Isaiah had written ...’

[MAT 24:8](https://ref.ly/Matt24:8) · [MRK 1:1](https://ref.ly/Matt1:1) · [MRK 13:8](https://ref.ly/Matt13:8) · [JHN 2:11](https://ref.ly/Luke2:11) · [JHN 8:25](https://ref.ly/Luke8:25) · [COL 1:18](https://ref.ly/Phil1:18) · [HEB 3:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:14) · [HEB 6:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:1) · [1JN 2:24](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:24)

beginning, to begin a point of time at the beginning of a duration

[MAT 19:4](https://ref.ly/Matt19:4) · [MAT 19:8](https://ref.ly/Matt19:8) · [MAT 24:21](https://ref.ly/Matt24:21) · [MRK 10:6](https://ref.ly/Matt10:6) · [MRK 13:19](https://ref.ly/Matt13:19) · [JHN 1:1](https://ref.ly/Luke1:1) · [JHN 1:2](https://ref.ly/Luke1:2) · [JHN 6:64](https://ref.ly/Luke6:64) · [JHN 8:44](https://ref.ly/Luke8:44) · [JHN 15:27](https://ref.ly/Luke15:27) · [JHN 16:4](https://ref.ly/Luke16:4) · [ACT 11:15](https://ref.ly/John11:15) · [ACT 26:4](https://ref.ly/John26:4) · [PHP 4:15](https://ref.ly/Eph4:15) · [HEB 1:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:10) · [HEB 2:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:3) · [HEB 3:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:14) · [HEB 7:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:3) · [2PE 3:4](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:4) · [1JN 1:1](https://ref.ly/2Pet1:1) · [1JN 2:7](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:7) · [1JN 2:13](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:13) · [1JN 2:14](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:14) · [1JN 2:24](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:24) · [1JN 3:8](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:8) · [1JN 3:11](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:11) · [2JN 1:5](https://ref.ly/1John1:5) · [2JN 1:6](https://ref.ly/1John1:6) · [REV 21:6](https://ref.ly/Jude21:6) · [REV 22:13](https://ref.ly/Jude22:13)

89\.16 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

first cause, origin one who or that which constitutes an initial cause It is also possible to understand ἀρχή in [REV 3:14](https://ref.ly/Jude3:14) as meaning ‘ruler’ (see [37\.56](#37.56)).

[REV 3:14](https://ref.ly/Jude3:14)

37\.55 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

sphere of authority, limit of one’s rule the sphere of one’s authority or rule

[JUD 1:6](https://ref.ly/3John1:6)

37\.56 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

ruler, governor (derivative of ἄρχω ‘to rule,’ [37\.54](#37.54)) one who rules or governs

[LUK 12:11](https://ref.ly/Mark12:11) · [LUK 20:20](https://ref.ly/Mark20:20) · [ROM 8:38](https://ref.ly/Acts8:38) · [1CO 15:24](https://ref.ly/Rom15:24) · [EPH 1:21](https://ref.ly/Gal1:21) · [TIT 3:1](https://ref.ly/2Tim3:1)

12\.44 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Powers

power, authority, lordship, ruler, wicked force (title for supernatural forces and powers) a supernatural power having some particular role in controlling the destiny and activities of human beings Some scholars have believed that it is possible to reconstruct at least in part some of the hierarchy represented by these various supernatural forces and powers, on the basis of the neoplatonic system of nine such powers arranged in three orders of three each. NT terminology and usage does not, however, lend itself to such a classification, and it is difficult, if not impossible, to determine what are the significant differences between these supernatural powers and forces.\|In the case of [EPH 1:21](https://ref.ly/Gal1:21) some scholars apply the terms in question both to the supernatural and to the human sphere, thus emphasizing the totality of Christ’s rule. This has a considerable measure of justification in view of the concepts held by persons in the ancient world, since various earthly powers were supposed to reflect corresponding supernatural powers.\|For the most part, translators have endeavored to render the various terms in somewhat different ways, for example, ἐξουσία as ‘authority’; δύναμις as ‘power’; κυριότης as ‘ruler’; and phrases such as τὰ πνευματικὰ τῆς πονηρίας ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις as ‘wicked spiritual forces in the heavenly world.’ In a number of languages, however, there are simply not enough terms to designate various kinds of powers, authorities, and forces which could be employed in speaking of such supernatural impersonal powers. Accordingly, some translators have simply used a phrase such as ‘any and all kinds of supernatural powers.’ Where it is possible to make certain lexical distinctions between terms and phrases, this, of course, should be done, but in a number of contexts this may be impossible, and accordingly, some explanation in a marginal note may be required if the reader is to understand satisfactorily the intent of the passages in question.\|In some instances a measure of meaning may be obtained by somewhat paraphrastic expressions. For example, Ephesians 2:2 is rendered in some languages as ‘at that time you lived in the way in which the spirit that governs this world dictated you should live. This is the way the one who rules over the power in space commanded you to live. This is the very spirit that now commands the people who disobey God.’

[ROM 8:38](https://ref.ly/Acts8:38) · [EPH 1:21](https://ref.ly/Gal1:21) · [EPH 3:10](https://ref.ly/Gal3:10) · [EPH 6:12](https://ref.ly/Gal6:12) · [COL 1:16](https://ref.ly/Phil1:16) · [COL 2:10](https://ref.ly/Phil2:10) · [COL 2:15](https://ref.ly/Phil2:15)

58\.20 · Nature, Class, Example · Basic Principles or Features Defining the Nature of Something

elementary aspect, simple truth elementary and preliminary aspects defining the nature of something Compare also the occurrence of ἀρχή\[g] in [HEB 5:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:12) in which it serves to reinforce the meaning of στοιχεῖα (see [58\.19](#58.19)).

[LUK 1:2](https://ref.ly/Mark1:2) · [HEB 5:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:12) · [HEB 6:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:1)

79\.106 · Features of Objects · Shapes

corner the corner of a two\-dimensional object, such as a sheet of cloth

[ACT 10:11](https://ref.ly/John10:11) · [ACT 11:5](https://ref.ly/John11:5)

## ἀρχηγός (id: 000743000000000)

ἀρχηγός
=======

ἀρχηγός (G0747)

noun

68\.2 · Aspect · Begin, Start

initiator, founder, originator one who causes something to begin For another interpretation of ἀρχηγός in [HEB 2:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:10), see ἀρχηγός\[b] [36\.6](#36.6).

[ACT 3:15](https://ref.ly/John3:15) · [HEB 2:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:10) · [HEB 12:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:2)

36\.6 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Guide, Lead

pioneer leader, founding leader a person who as originator or founder of a movement continues as the leader In order to indicate clearly the significance of ἀρχηγός in [HEB 2:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:10), it may be important to employ a translation such as ‘who established a way of salvation and leads people to it.’ But it is also possible to understand ἀρχηγός in [HEB 2:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:10) as meaning only the ‘initiator’ or ‘founder’ (see ἀρχηγός\[a] [68\.2](#68.2)).

[ACT 5:31](https://ref.ly/John5:31) · [HEB 2:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:10) · [HEB 12:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:2)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 3:15; Hebrews 2:10; Hebrews 12:2; Acts 5:31
