## ἀρχιποίμην (id: 000746000000000)

ἀρχιποίμην
==========

ἀρχιποίμην (G0750)

noun

44\.5 · Animal Husbandry, Fishing

chief shepherd, head shepherd the head shepherd who directs the activities of other shepherds (in its only NT occurrence; a figurative reference to Christ)

[1PE 5:4](https://ref.ly/Jas5:4)1

1 1PE 5:4 The occurrence of ἀρχιποίμην ‘chief shepherd’ in [1PE 5:4](https://ref.ly/Jas5:4) is an excellent example of figurative usage. There is no indication that by the time 1 Peter was written ἀρχιποίμην had acquired the conventional meaning of a title designating Christ as one who had principal responsibility for believers. The meaning of ἀρχιποίμην, therefore, is simply ‘chief shepherd,’ but the reference is to Christ in a function which does not involve the literal care of sheep.

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Peter 5:4

## ἄρχω (id: 000752000000000)

ἄρχω
====

ἄρχω (G0756) — ἄρχομαι

ἀπαρχή · ἀρχηγός · ἀρχιερατικός · ἀρχιερεύς · ἀρχιποίμην · ἀρχισυνάγωγος · ἀρχιτέκτων · ἀρχιτελώνης · ἀρχιτρίκλινος · ἀρχοστασία · ἄρχων · ἐθνάρχης · ἑκατοντάρχης · ἐνάρχομαι · ἐπαρκέω · πατριάρχης · πειθαρχέω · πολιτάρχης · προενάρχομαι · προϋπάρχω · στρατοπεδάρχης · τετρααρχέω · τετραάρχης · ὕπαρξις · ὑπάρχω · χιλίαρχος

37\.54 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

to rule, to govern to rule or govern, with the implication of preeminent position and status

[MRK 10:42](https://ref.ly/Matt10:42) · [ROM 15:12](https://ref.ly/Acts15:12)

68\.1 · Aspect · Begin, Start

to begin, to commence, beginning to initiate an action, process, or state of being ἄρχω\[b] may also be used in an absolute sense in which the related activity is merely implied: αὐτὸς ἦν Ἰησοῦς ἀρχόμενος ὡσεὶ ἐτῶν τριάκοντα ‘when Jesus began, he was about thirty years old’ [LUK 3:23](https://ref.ly/Mark3:23). In rendering [LUK 3:23](https://ref.ly/Mark3:23), it may be useful to translate as ‘when Jesus began his ministry, he was about thirty years old.’

37\.56 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

ruler, governor (derivative of ἄρχω ‘to rule,’ [37\.54](#37.54)) one who rules or governs

[MAT 9:18](https://ref.ly/Matt9:18) · [MAT 9:23](https://ref.ly/Matt9:23) · [MAT 9:34](https://ref.ly/Matt9:34) · [MAT 12:24](https://ref.ly/Matt12:24) · [MAT 20:25](https://ref.ly/Matt20:25) · [MRK 3:22](https://ref.ly/Matt3:22) · [LUK 8:41](https://ref.ly/Mark8:41) · [LUK 11:15](https://ref.ly/Mark11:15) · [LUK 14:1](https://ref.ly/Mark14:1) · [LUK 18:18](https://ref.ly/Mark18:18) · [LUK 23:13](https://ref.ly/Mark23:13) · [LUK 23:35](https://ref.ly/Mark23:35) · [LUK 24:20](https://ref.ly/Mark24:20) · [JHN 7:26](https://ref.ly/Luke7:26) · [JHN 7:48](https://ref.ly/Luke7:48) · [JHN 12:31](https://ref.ly/Luke12:31) · [JHN 12:42](https://ref.ly/Luke12:42) · [JHN 14:30](https://ref.ly/Luke14:30) · [JHN 16:11](https://ref.ly/Luke16:11) · [ACT 3:17](https://ref.ly/John3:17) · [ACT 4:5](https://ref.ly/John4:5) · [ACT 4:8](https://ref.ly/John4:8) · [ACT 4:26](https://ref.ly/John4:26) · [ACT 7:27](https://ref.ly/John7:27) · [ACT 7:35](https://ref.ly/John7:35) · [ACT 7:35](https://ref.ly/John7:35) · [ACT 13:27](https://ref.ly/John13:27) · [ACT 14:5](https://ref.ly/John14:5) · [ACT 23:5](https://ref.ly/John23:5) · [ROM 13:3](https://ref.ly/Acts13:3) · [1CO 2:6](https://ref.ly/Rom2:6) · [1CO 2:8](https://ref.ly/Rom2:8)

56\.29 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Judge, Condemn, Acquit

official, judge a minor government official serving as a judge Though in [LUK 12:58](https://ref.ly/Mark12:58) ἄρχων has essentially the same reference as κριτής and δικαστής ([56\.28](#56.28)), the meaning differs in that ἄρχων represents government authority functioning in judgment on cases.

[LUK 12:58](https://ref.ly/Mark12:58) · [ACT 16:19](https://ref.ly/John16:19)

12\.44 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Powers

**Collocation:** ἄρχων τῆς ἐξουσίας τοῦ ἀέρος

power, authority, lordship, ruler, wicked force (ἄρχων τῆς ἐξουσίας τοῦ ἀέρος \- title for supernatural forces and powers) a supernatural power having some particular role in controlling the destiny and activities of human beings Some scholars have believed that it is possible to reconstruct at least in part some of the hierarchy represented by these various supernatural forces and powers, on the basis of the neoplatonic system of nine such powers arranged in three orders of three each. NT terminology and usage does not, however, lend itself to such a classification, and it is difficult, if not impossible, to determine what are the significant differences between these supernatural powers and forces.\|In the case of [EPH 1:21](https://ref.ly/Gal1:21) some scholars apply the terms in question both to the supernatural and to the human sphere, thus emphasizing the totality of Christ’s rule. This has a considerable measure of justification in view of the concepts held by persons in the ancient world, since various earthly powers were supposed to reflect corresponding supernatural powers.\|For the most part, translators have endeavored to render the various terms in somewhat different ways, for example, ἐξουσία as ‘authority’; δύναμις as ‘power’; κυριότης as ‘ruler’; and phrases such as τὰ πνευματικὰ τῆς πονηρίας ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις as ‘wicked spiritual forces in the heavenly world.’ In a number of languages, however, there are simply not enough terms to designate various kinds of powers, authorities, and forces which could be employed in speaking of such supernatural impersonal powers. Accordingly, some translators have simply used a phrase such as ‘any and all kinds of supernatural powers.’ Where it is possible to make certain lexical distinctions between terms and phrases, this, of course, should be done, but in a number of contexts this may be impossible, and accordingly, some explanation in a marginal note may be required if the reader is to understand satisfactorily the intent of the passages in question.

[EPH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Gal2:2) · [REV 1:5](https://ref.ly/Jude1:5)

37\.95 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

**Collocation:** ἄρχων τῶν Ἰουδαίων

a member of the Council (a title, literally: ruler of the Jews) a member of the highest legal, legislative, and judicial body among the Jews1

[JHN 3:1](https://ref.ly/Luke3:1)

1 One may classify ἄρχων τῶν Ἰουδαίων in Domain 11 Groups and Classes of Persons, but the phrase ἄρχων τῶν Ἰουδαίων seems to focus primarily upon the authority and status which such a person has, rather than merely upon his being a member of a deliberative body, as in the case of terms such as συνέδριον\[a] city council ([11\.79](#11.79)), γερουσία and πρεσβυτέριον Sanhedrin ([11\.83](#11.83)).

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 9:18; Matthew 9:23; Matthew 9:34; Matthew 12:24; Matthew 20:25; Mark 3:22; Luke 8:41; Luke 11:15; Luke 14:1; Luke 18:18; Luke 23:13; Luke 23:35; Luke 24:20; John 7:26; John 7:48; John 12:31; John 12:42; John 14:30; John 16:11; Acts 3:17; Acts 4:5; Acts 4:8; Acts 4:26; Acts 7:27; Acts 7:35; Acts 13:27; Acts 14:5; Acts 23:5; Romans 13:3; 1 Corinthians 2:6; 1 Corinthians 2:8; Luke 12:58; Acts 16:19; Ephesians 2:2; Revelation 1:5; John 3:1

## ἀσθένεια (id: 000765000000000)

ἀσθένεια
========

ἀσθένεια (G0769)

noun

74\.23 · Able, Capable

incapacity, weakness, limitation a state of incapacity to do or experience something A literal rendering of this expression in [2CO 12:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:9) may not be meaningful, and in fact can be quite misleading. δύναμις is best interpreted as ‘God’s power’ and ἀσθένεια as the weakness which Paul experienced. Therefore, one may be able to translate as ‘my power is greatest when you are weak’ (see Today’s English Version).

[ROM 6:19](https://ref.ly/Acts6:19) · [ROM 8:26](https://ref.ly/Acts8:26) · [1CO 15:43](https://ref.ly/Rom15:43) · [2CO 11:30](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:30) · [2CO 12:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:5) · [2CO 12:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:9) · [2CO 12:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:9) · [2CO 12:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:10) · [2CO 13:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor13:4) · [GAL 4:13](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:13) · [HEB 4:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:15) · [HEB 5:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:2) · [HEB 7:28](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:28)

23\.143 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

illness, disability, weakness the state of being ill and thus incapacitated in some manner It may be extremely difficult in some languages to speak of ‘taking illness’ or ‘carrying away diseases.’ A strictly literal rendering of such an expression might suggest immediately that Jesus was a type of medicine man who healed others by taking upon himself precisely the same kinds of diseases (a widespread shamanistic practice). It may therefore be necessary in some languages to translate [MAT 8:17](https://ref.ly/Matt8:17) as ‘he caused us to no longer have illness and disease.’ In this way, one may avoid a specific type of shamanistic practice which would be inappropriate in this context.

[MAT 8:17](https://ref.ly/Matt8:17) · [LUK 5:15](https://ref.ly/Mark5:15) · [LUK 8:2](https://ref.ly/Mark8:2) · [LUK 13:11](https://ref.ly/Mark13:11) · [LUK 13:12](https://ref.ly/Mark13:12) · [JHN 5:5](https://ref.ly/Luke5:5) · [JHN 11:4](https://ref.ly/Luke11:4) · [ACT 28:9](https://ref.ly/John28:9) · [1TI 5:23](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:23) · [HEB 11:34](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:34)

25\.269 · Attitudes and Emotions · Fear, Terror, Alarm

timidity, being fearful (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀσθένεια\[a] ‘weakness,’ [74\.23](#74.23)) a state of timidity resulting from a lack of confidence

[1CO 2:3](https://ref.ly/Rom2:3)

22\.3 · Trouble, Hardship, Relief, Favorable Circumstances · Trouble, Hardship, Distress

helpless, helpless condition pertaining to a state of helplessness in view of circumstances The meaning of ‘helpless’ may be expressed in some languages as ‘when we could not do anything about it.’ For another interpretation of ἀσθενής in [ROM 5:6](https://ref.ly/Acts5:6), see ἀσθενής\[b] [88\.117](#88.117).

[ROM 5:6](https://ref.ly/Acts5:6)

79\.69 · Features of Objects · Strong, Weak

weak pertaining to being physically weak Such a literal translation of this expression in [1PE 3:7](https://ref.ly/Jas3:7), however, may be misleading, for in general τῷ γυναικείῳ is interpreted as a reference to one’s wife. Therefore, one may translate as ‘treating with respect one’s wife as the physically weaker partner’ or ‘... as of the weaker sex.’

## ἀσκός (id: 000775000000000)

ἀσκός
=====

noun

6\.132 · Artifacts · Containers

wineskin a bag made of skin or leather; used for temporarily storing and carrying wine A number of translators have attempted to substitute ‘bottles’ for ‘wineskins,’ but this has not been satisfactory, since fermenting wine does not normally break glass bottles, while it would break old wineskins. In circumstances in which the use of wineskins is not known, it may be necessary to employ some type of descriptive phrase (for example, ‘a container made of skin’ or ‘a closed bag of skin’ or ‘a goat skin like a bottle’) and a fuller explanation in the margin.

to welcome, to accept gladly to welcome something or someone, with focus upon the initial greeting In some languages, however, it may be difficult to speak of ‘welcoming promises,’ but one can often render this relationship as ‘they were happy to know about what had been promised.’ For another interpretation of ἀσπάζομαι in [HEB 11:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:13), see ἀσπάζομαι\[b] [25\.130](#25.130).

## ἀστήρ (id: 000789000000000)

ἀστήρ
=====

ἀστήρ (G0792)

noun · ἄστρον

1\.30 · Geographical Objects and Features · Heavenly Bodies

star, planet a star or a planet Since in [JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13) godless people are likened to ‘wandering stars’ or ‘planets,’ it may be best to introduce this expression by a simile, for example, ‘they are like wandering stars.’ In some languages there is a serious difficulty involved in translating ἀστὴρ πλανήτης as ‘planet,’ since the planets or their courses through the heavens are regarded as beneficial. In [JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13) the focal meaning is upon the heretical nature of these godless persons who do not keep on the right course. It may therefore be necessary to translate ἀστέρες πλανῆται in [JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13) as ‘stars that do not stay where they should stay’ or ‘... do not remain in their right places.’ See also [15\.26](#15.26).

[MAT 2:2](https://ref.ly/Matt2:2) · [MAT 2:7](https://ref.ly/Matt2:7) · [MAT 2:9](https://ref.ly/Matt2:9) · [MAT 2:10](https://ref.ly/Matt2:10) · [MAT 24:29](https://ref.ly/Matt24:29) · [MRK 13:25](https://ref.ly/Matt13:25) · [1CO 15:41](https://ref.ly/Rom15:41) · [1CO 15:41](https://ref.ly/Rom15:41) · [1CO 15:41](https://ref.ly/Rom15:41) · [JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13) · [REV 1:16](https://ref.ly/Jude1:16) · [REV 1:20](https://ref.ly/Jude1:20) · [REV 1:20](https://ref.ly/Jude1:20) · [REV 2:1](https://ref.ly/Jude2:1) · [REV 2:28](https://ref.ly/Jude2:28) · [REV 3:1](https://ref.ly/Jude3:1) · [REV 6:13](https://ref.ly/Jude6:13) · [REV 8:10](https://ref.ly/Jude8:10) · [REV 8:11](https://ref.ly/Jude8:11) · [REV 8:12](https://ref.ly/Jude8:12) · [REV 9:1](https://ref.ly/Jude9:1) · [REV 12:1](https://ref.ly/Jude12:1) · [REV 12:4](https://ref.ly/Jude12:4) · [REV 22:16](https://ref.ly/Jude22:16)

1\.33 · Geographical Objects and Features · Heavenly Bodies

**Collocation:** ἀστὴρ πρωϊνός

morning star, bright star in the morning sky (an idiom, literally: morning star) a planet which is conspicuous in the morning sky (usually either Venus or Mars) In [REV 2:28](https://ref.ly/Jude2:28) the singular pronoun αὐτῷ refers to any and all of the persons in Thyatira, and therefore it is usually best rendered as plural. A literal rendering of [REV 2:28](https://ref.ly/Jude2:28) may be confusing, since it would suggest that God would be handing over the morning star to persons in Thyatira. A more satisfactory rendering may be ‘I will cause the morning star to shine upon them’ or possibly ‘I will bring light into their hearts.’

[REV 2:28](https://ref.ly/Jude2:28) · [REV 22:16](https://ref.ly/Jude22:16)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:2; Matthew 2:7; Matthew 2:9; Matthew 2:10; Matthew 24:29; Mark 13:25; 1 Corinthians 15:41; Jude 1:13; Revelation 1:16; Revelation 1:20; Revelation 2:1; Revelation 2:28; Revelation 3:1; Revelation 6:13; Revelation 8:10; Revelation 8:11; Revelation 8:12; Revelation 9:1; Revelation 12:1; Revelation 12:4; Revelation 22:16

## ἀστραπή (id: 000793000000000)

ἀστραπή
=======

ἀστραπή (G0796)

noun · ἀστράπτω

14\.16 · Physical Events and States · Thunder and Lightning

lightning the flash of lightning Terms referring to thunder and lightning often have important taboo connotations. The specific terms for thunder and lightning may be avoided and figurative expressions employed in their place in order to avoid danger which might occur from being struck by lightning or frightened by a thunderstorm. One can, therefore, expect in some languages a number of different terms, and it is important to avoid expressions which might have wrong connotations.

[MAT 24:27](https://ref.ly/Matt24:27) · [MAT 28:3](https://ref.ly/Matt28:3) · [LUK 10:18](https://ref.ly/Mark10:18) · [LUK 17:24](https://ref.ly/Mark17:24) · [REV 4:5](https://ref.ly/Jude4:5) · [REV 8:5](https://ref.ly/Jude8:5) · [REV 11:19](https://ref.ly/Jude11:19) · [REV 16:18](https://ref.ly/Jude16:18)

14\.46 · Physical Events and States · Light

bright beam shafts or beams of light radiating from a source It is often better to render ἀστραπή\[b] as a type of qualifying adverb, for example, brightly.

[LUK 11:36](https://ref.ly/Mark11:36)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 24:27; Matthew 28:3; Luke 10:18; Luke 17:24; Revelation 4:5; Revelation 8:5; Revelation 11:19; Revelation 16:18; Luke 11:36

14\.47 · Physical Events and States · Light

to glisten, to dazzle, to gleam, to flash to give off or to reflect a very bright light1

[LUK 17:24](https://ref.ly/Mark17:24) · [LUK 24:4](https://ref.ly/Mark24:4)

## ἄστρον (id: 000795000000000)

ἄστρον
======

ἄστρον (G0798)

noun · ἀστήρ

1\.30 · Geographical Objects and Features · Heavenly Bodies

star, planet a star or a planet In selecting a term for ‘star,’ it is important to avoid an expression which may relate to only one set of stars, that is to say, a particular constellation, since there may be connotations associated with such a constellation which can radically alter the intent of a particular passage. Some constellations, for example, may be regarded as kindly disposed toward mankind, while others may be regarded as hostile.

[LUK 21:25](https://ref.ly/Mark21:25) · [ACT 7:43](https://ref.ly/John7:43) · [ACT 27:20](https://ref.ly/John27:20) · [HEB 11:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:12)

1\.31 · Geographical Objects and Features · Heavenly Bodies

constellation a group of stars Normally a constellation may be described as ‘a group of stars’ or ‘a family of stars’ or ‘stars that travel together’ or even ‘stars that live together.’ It is possible, however, to interpret the occurrence of ἄστρον in [ACT 7:43](https://ref.ly/John7:43) as being a reference to a particular planet (namely, Saturn) rather than a constellation.

[ACT 7:43](https://ref.ly/John7:43)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 21:25; Acts 7:43; Acts 27:20; Hebrews 11:12

## ἀσύνετος (id: 000798000000000)

ἀσύνετος
========

ἀσύνετος (G0801)

adj · συνετός

32\.49 · Understand · Lack of Capacity for Understanding

without understanding, senseless, foolish pertaining to a lack of capacity for insight and understanding Care must be exercised in the rendering of ἀσύνετος in [MAT 15:16](https://ref.ly/Matt15:16) and [ROM 1:21](https://ref.ly/Acts1:21), since a lack of capacity for understanding may result from mental deficiencies or from a lack of the proper use of mental capacity. It is, of course, this latter meaning which is involved in the use of ἀσύνετος in these contexts.

[MAT 15:16](https://ref.ly/Matt15:16) · [MRK 7:18](https://ref.ly/Matt7:18) · [ROM 1:21](https://ref.ly/Acts1:21) · [ROM 1:31](https://ref.ly/Acts1:31) · [ROM 10:19](https://ref.ly/Acts10:19)

adj

34\.45 · Association · Establish or Confirm a Relation

not keeping a promise, not abiding by an agreement pertaining to not being bound or not regarding oneself as bound by any covenant or agreement It is also possible to translate ἀσύνθετος in [ROM 1:31](https://ref.ly/Acts1:31) as ‘they feel no obligation to keep their agreements’ or ‘they do not feel bound to do what they have promised to do.’

[ROM 1:31](https://ref.ly/Acts1:31)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 1:31


## ἀσφάλεια (id: 000800000000000)

ἀσφάλεια
========

ἀσφάλεια (G0803)

noun

21\.9 · Danger, Risk, Safe, Save · Safe, Free from Danger

safety, security a state of safety and security, implying a complete lack of danger In some languages safety or security is expressed in a negative way, for example, ‘without any danger’ or ‘without anything that might possibly harm.’

[ACT 5:23](https://ref.ly/John5:23) · [1TH 5:3](https://ref.ly/Col5:3)

31\.41 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

certainty, being without doubt a state of certainty with regard to a belief It is also possible to render [LUK 1:4](https://ref.ly/Mark1:4) as ‘so that you may know that those things that you have been taught are certainly true.’

[LUK 1:4](https://ref.ly/Mark1:4)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 5:23; 1 Thessalonians 5:3; Luke 1:4

=======

adj

safe, safely, secure, securely pertaining to a state of safety and security, and hence free from danger

[PHP 3:1](https://ref.ly/Eph3:1) · [HEB 6:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:19)

31\.42 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

worthy of being believed, certainly true, completely believable pertaining to being certain and thus completely believable

to make secure, to be guarded to cause something to be secure in the sense of something which could not be tampered with or opened

[MAT 27:64](https://ref.ly/Matt27:64) · [MAT 27:65](https://ref.ly/Matt27:65) · [MAT 27:66](https://ref.ly/Matt27:66)

to fasten, to tie to fasten something securely It is possible that in [ACT 16:24](https://ref.ly/John16:24) τὸ ξύλον designates ‘stocks,’ namely, two pieces of wood through which the feet could be placed so as to make a prisoner immobile (see [6\.21](#6.21)).

## ἀσφαλῶς (id: 000803000000000)

=======

adv

21\.10 · Danger, Risk, Safe, Save · Safe, Free from Danger

safe, safely, secure, securely pertaining to a state of safety and security, and hence free from danger In [ACT 16:23](https://ref.ly/John16:23) ἀσφαλῶς does not refer to the safety of the prisoners but to the security with which they were guarded, hence eliminating any danger of their escaping.

worthy of being believed, certainly true, completely believable pertaining to being certain and thus completely believable

## ἀσχημονέω (id: 000804000000000)

ἀσχημονέω
=========

ἀσχημονέω (G0807)

σχῆμα

88\.149 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Act Shamefully

to act shamefully, indecent behavior, shameful deed to act in defiance of social and moral standards, with resulting disgrace, embarrassment, and shame Behavior involving disgrace, embarrassment, and shame is often expressed in an idiomatic manner, for example, ‘to bury one’s face,’ ‘to hide one’s eyes,’ or ‘to feel stabbed by people’s eyes.’ Since shame is seemingly a universal type of feeling, there is usually no difficulty involved in obtaining a satisfactory equivalent.

[1CO 7:36](https://ref.ly/Rom7:36) · [1CO 13:5](https://ref.ly/Rom13:5)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 7:36; 1 Corinthians 13:5

## αὐξάνω (id: 000833000000000)

αὐξάνω
======

αὐξάνω (G0837) — αὐξάνομαι

αὔξησις · συναυξάνομαι · ὑπεραυξάνω

59\.62 · Quantity · Increase, Decrease

to increase, to grow, to spread, to extend to increase in the extent of or in the instances of an activity or state If one assumes that ἐπίγνωσις in [COL 1:10](https://ref.ly/Phil1:10) is to be understood in the sense of ‘knowledge about’ (see [28\.18](#28.18)), then one may translate this expression as ‘you will know more and more about God,’ but if ἐπίγνωσις is to be understood in the sense of ‘to experience knowledge’ (see [28\.2](#28.2)), one may ‘you will know God more and more’ or ‘you will come to know more and more what God does.’

[ACT 6:7](https://ref.ly/John6:7) · [ACT 7:17](https://ref.ly/John7:17) · [ACT 12:24](https://ref.ly/John12:24) · [ACT 19:20](https://ref.ly/John19:20) · [2CO 10:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:15) · [EPH 2:21](https://ref.ly/Gal2:21) · [EPH 4:15](https://ref.ly/Gal4:15) · [COL 1:6](https://ref.ly/Phil1:6) · [COL 1:10](https://ref.ly/Phil1:10) · [1PE 2:2](https://ref.ly/Jas2:2) · [2PE 3:18](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:18)

59\.63 · Quantity · Increase, Decrease

to cause to increase, to increase to cause something to increase

[1CO 3:6](https://ref.ly/Rom3:6) · [1CO 3:7](https://ref.ly/Rom3:7) · [2CO 9:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:10)

23\.188 · Physiological Processes and States · Grow, Growth

to grow, growth to grow, to increase in size, whether of animate beings or of plants In a number of languages one must make a clear distinction in the choice of terms referring to the growth of an animate being (animal or human) and the growth of plants.

[MAT 6:28](https://ref.ly/Matt6:28) · [MAT 13:32](https://ref.ly/Matt13:32) · [MRK 4:8](https://ref.ly/Matt4:8) · [LUK 1:80](https://ref.ly/Mark1:80) · [LUK 2:40](https://ref.ly/Mark2:40) · [LUK 12:27](https://ref.ly/Mark12:27) · [LUK 13:19](https://ref.ly/Mark13:19) · [COL 2:19](https://ref.ly/Phil2:19)

87\.37 · Status · High Status or Rank \[including persons of high status]

to become more important, to enjoy greater respect or honor to increase in status In some languages the increase or decrease of respect or honor must be expressed idiomatically, for example, ‘he must become more of a chief while I become more of a follower’ or ‘he must become a big man while I become a small man.’

[JHN 3:30](https://ref.ly/Luke3:30)

78\.5 · Degree · Much, Little \[Positive\-Negative Degree]

to increase, to grow to increase in the degree of a state It would be possible to interpret αὐξάνομαι in [2CO 10:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:15) as being quantitative, but with an expression of state such as πίστις, intensity of degree seems to be more appropriate. In a number of languages, however, it may be necessary to render this expression in [2CO 10:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:15) as ‘hoping that you may trust more and more.’

[2CO 10:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 6:7; Acts 7:17; Acts 12:24; Acts 19:20; 2 Corinthians 10:15; Ephesians 2:21; Ephesians 4:15; Colossians 1:6; Colossians 1:10; 1 Peter 2:2; 2 Peter 3:18; 1 Corinthians 3:6; 1 Corinthians 3:7; 2 Corinthians 9:10; Matthew 6:28; Matthew 13:32; Mark 4:8; Luke 1:80; Luke 2:40; Luke 12:27; Luke 13:19; Colossians 2:19; John 3:30


## αὔξησις (id: 000834000000000)

αὔξησις
=======

αὔξησις (G0838)

noun · αὐξάνω

23\.188 · Physiological Processes and States · Grow, Growth

to grow, growth to grow, to increase in size, whether of animate beings or of plants In a number of languages one must make a clear distinction in the choice of terms referring to the growth of an animate being (animal or human) and the growth of plants\|In [COL 2:19](https://ref.ly/Phil2:19) αὔξησις is used figuratively in the sense that the reference of ‘body’ is the church rather than a physical body. Normally a term for ‘growth’ would imply not only size but also number, that is to say, the growth of the church would be in terms of the increase of members of the church, though it might refer to spiritual maturity. In some instances, however, it may be necessary to specify ‘growth in numbers’ so as to avoid the implication which might be derived from the context, namely, that the church is simply a building which gets bigger and bigger.

[EPH 4:16](https://ref.ly/Gal4:16) · [COL 2:19](https://ref.ly/Phil2:19)

* **Associated Passages:** Ephesians 4:16; Colossians 2:19


## αὔριον (id: 000835000000000)

αὔριον
======

αὔριον (G0839)

adv · ἐπαύριον

67\.207 · Time · Units of Time with Reference to Other Units or Points of Time: Yesterday, Today, Next Day

tomorrow a day following the day of a discourse

[MAT 6:30](https://ref.ly/Matt6:30) · [MAT 6:34](https://ref.ly/Matt6:34) · [MAT 6:34](https://ref.ly/Matt6:34) · [LUK 10:35](https://ref.ly/Mark10:35) · [LUK 12:28](https://ref.ly/Mark12:28) · [LUK 13:32](https://ref.ly/Mark13:32) · [LUK 13:33](https://ref.ly/Mark13:33) · [ACT 4:3](https://ref.ly/John4:3) · [ACT 4:5](https://ref.ly/John4:5) · [ACT 23:20](https://ref.ly/John23:20) · [ACT 25:22](https://ref.ly/John25:22) · [1CO 15:32](https://ref.ly/Rom15:32) · [JAS 4:13](https://ref.ly/Heb4:13) · [JAS 4:14](https://ref.ly/Heb4:14)

67\.58 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

soon, very soon a point of time subsequent to another point of time and indicating imminence, that is to say, the subsequent event is regarded as almost begun

[1CO 15:32](https://ref.ly/Rom15:32)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 6:30; Matthew 6:34; Luke 10:35; Luke 12:28; Luke 13:32; Luke 13:33; Acts 4:3; Acts 4:5; Acts 23:20; Acts 25:22; 1 Corinthians 15:32; James 4:13; James 4:14

αὐστηρός
========

αὐστηρός (G0840)

adj · ἄζω · αὐχμηρός

88\.138 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Act Harshly

exacting, severe pertaining to being exacting, with a possible implication of being severe in either a favorable or unfavorable sense

[LUK 19:21](https://ref.ly/Mark19:21) · [LUK 19:22](https://ref.ly/Mark19:22)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 19:21; Luke 19:22


## αὐτάρκεια (id: 000837000000000)

αὐτάρκεια
=========

αὐτάρκεια (G0841)

noun

25\.83 · Attitudes and Emotions · Content, Satisfied

self\-content, contentment, contentment with what one has the state of being content with one’s circumstances or lot in life

[1TI 6:6](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:6)

75\.6 · Adequate, Qualified

what is adequate, what is sufficient, what is needed, adequacy a state of adequacy or sufficiency In a number of languages the equivalent of this expression in [2CO 9:8](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:8) may be ‘always having all that you need’ or, stated negatively, ‘not lacking in anything.’

[2CO 9:8](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:8)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Timothy 6:6; 2 Corinthians 9:8

adj

condemned by one’s own actions pertaining to one who is condemned as the result of his own actions

## αὐτόματος (id: 000840000000000)

αὐτόματος
=========

αὐτόματος (G0844)

adj · αὐτός

89\.21 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

without any cause, without something to cause it, by itself pertaining to being self\-caused (or possibly, without evident cause)1 In some languages one can only express ‘opened ... by itself’ in [ACT 12:10](https://ref.ly/John12:10) by ‘opened ... nobody made it open.’

[MRK 4:28](https://ref.ly/Matt4:28) · [ACT 12:10](https://ref.ly/John12:10)

1 It is difficult to determine whether αὐτόματος focuses primarily upon an event taking place because of the action of an object itself or whether the implication is that there is no evident cause.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 4:28; Acts 12:10

αὐτόπτης (G0845)

eyewitness, personal witness one who has personally seen an event and thus has personal knowledge and can be expected to attest to the occurrence of such an event

1:5](https://ref.ly/2Pet1:5)

92\.37 · Discourse Referentials · Emphatic Adjunct

\-self, \-selves (for example, myself, yourself, yourselves, ourselves, himself, herself, itself, themselves) a marker of emphasis by calling attention to the distinctiveness of the lexical item with which it occurs (used for all persons, genders, and numbers)1

1 Note the overlap between αὐτός\[b] ([92\.11](#92.11)) and αὐτός\[c] ([92\.37](#92.37)). αὐτός\[c] serves essentially as a reference to the third person, both singular and plural, and only in the nominative form does it carry emphasis. This could very well be classified as an instance of αὐτός\[c]. In the oblique cases there is no such emphasis. On the other hand, αὐτός\[c] may be an adjunct to any lexical item in an oblique case and, as such, carries emphasis.

## αὐτοῦ (id: 000843000000000)

αὐτοῦ
=====

αὐτοῦ (G0847)

adv · αὐτός

83\.3 · Spacial Positions · Here, There

here, there a position either near or far from the speaker, writer, or viewpoint character

[MAT 26:36](https://ref.ly/Matt26:36) · [LUK 9:27](https://ref.ly/Mark9:27) · [ACT 18:19](https://ref.ly/John18:19) · [ACT 21:4](https://ref.ly/John21:4)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:36; Luke 9:27; Acts 18:19; Acts 21:4


## αὐτόφωρος (id: 000844000000000)

αὐτόφωρος
=========

αὐτόφωρος (G1888a)

adj

42\.14 · Perform, Do · Do, Perform

in the act, in the very activity pertaining to the very act of doing something



## αὐτόχειρ (id: 000845000000000)

αὐτόχειρ
========

αὐτόχειρ (G0849)

adj

8\.31 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

one’s own hands of a person’s own hands But in rendering [ACT 27:19](https://ref.ly/John27:19), it may be somewhat superfluous or even misleading to speak about throwing over the ships tackle ‘with their own hands.’ The emphasis is not upon the hands so much as the activity of the people aboard the ship. Therefore, one may better translate [ACT 27:19](https://ref.ly/John27:19) as ‘they themselves threw the ships tackle overboard.’ Obviously, more than the hands were employed in this activity.

[ACT 27:19](https://ref.ly/John27:19)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 27:19


## αὐχέω (id: 000846000000000)

αὐχέω
=====

αὐχέω (G3166a)

33\.368 · Communication · Boast

to boast to express an unusually high degree of confidence in someone or something being exceptionally noteworthy1 Whether in any particular context the boasting is legitimate or not depends upon what is boasted about. In a number of languages, however, quite different terms are employed, depending upon the differing degrees of justification for such boasting.

[JAS 3:5](https://ref.ly/Heb3:5)

1 There are undoubtedly certain connotative differences in these terms for boasting, but this cannot be determined from existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** James 3:5


## αὐχμηρός (id: 000847000000000)

αὐχμηρός
========

αὐχμηρός (G0850)

adj · αὐστηρός

14\.58 · Physical Events and States · Darkness

dark and miserable pertaining to being not only dark, but also dirty and miserable In [2PE 1:19](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:19) αὐχμηρός may also suggest moral degradation, and in English the phrase ‘filthy darkness’ could suggest this type of meaning.

[2PE 1:19](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:19)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Peter 1:19


## ἀφαιρέω (id: 000848000000000)

ἀφαιρέω
=======

ἀφαιρέω (G0851)

αἱρέομαι

85\.44 · Existence in Space · Put, Place

to put away, to put out of the way, to remove to put or take something away from its normal location1 In the context of [LUK 22:50](https://ref.ly/Mark22:50) it may be far more appropriate to translate ‘and he cut off his right ear.’

[MAT 26:51](https://ref.ly/Matt26:51) · [MRK 14:47](https://ref.ly/Matt14:47) · [LUK 22:50](https://ref.ly/Mark22:50) · [REV 22:19](https://ref.ly/Jude22:19)

68\.47 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to take away (from), to cause to no longer do to cause to cease, implying that someone is no longer permitted to enjoy or participate in some state or activity

[LUK 1:25](https://ref.ly/Mark1:25) · [LUK 10:42](https://ref.ly/Mark10:42) · [LUK 16:3](https://ref.ly/Mark16:3) · [REV 22:19](https://ref.ly/Jude22:19)

13\.38 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · State

to do away with, to remove, to eliminate to cause a state to cease2

[ROM 11:27](https://ref.ly/Acts11:27) · [HEB 10:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:4)

1 It would also be possible to classify the meanings of ἀποτίθημι\[a] and ἀφαιρέω\[a] in Domain 15 Linear Movement, but in a number of contexts the focus seems not to be primarily upon the movement but upon the removal from a particular location. In other words, the focus is upon the absence of an object from a place rather than the movement involved in removal. 2 There are undoubtedly some significant differences of meaning in this series in [13\.38](#13.38), but it is not possible to determine the distinctive differences on the basis of existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:51; Mark 14:47; Luke 22:50; Revelation 22:19; Luke 1:25; Luke 10:42; Luke 16:3; Romans 11:27; Hebrews 10:4


## ἀφανής (id: 000849000000000)

ἀφανής
======

ἀφανής (G0852)

adj

28\.68 · Know · Not Able To Be Known, Secret

hidden, unknown pertaining to not being able to be seen or known, and thus to be hidden It is possible to render ‘that can be hidden from him’ as ‘that people can keep God from seeing’ or ‘that people can prevent God from knowing about.’

[HEB 4:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 4:13


## ἀφανίζω (id: 000850000000000)

ἀφανίζω
=======

ἀφανίζω (G0853) — ἀφανίζομαι

20\.46 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Destroy

to destroy, to completely ruin to destroy the value or use of something See [4\.49](#4.49).

[MAT 6:19](https://ref.ly/Matt6:19) · [MAT 6:20](https://ref.ly/Matt6:20)

79\.17 · Features of Objects · Beautiful, Ugly

to make unsightly, to disfigure, to make ugly to cause something to be unattractive or unsightly

[MAT 6:16](https://ref.ly/Matt6:16)

24\.27 · Sensory Events and States · See

to become invisible (in the passive, to disappear, to vanish) to become such as not to be seen In a number of instances one can best render ‘vanishes’ as ‘cannot be seen’ or ‘suddenly people cannot see it.’

[JAS 4:14](https://ref.ly/Heb4:14)

13\.98 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Exist

to cease to exist, to disappear, to die, disappearance to cease to exist, with the implication of no longer being evident or visible

[ACT 13:41](https://ref.ly/John13:41)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 6:19; Matthew 6:20; Matthew 6:16; James 4:14; Acts 13:41


## ἀφανισμός (id: 000851000000000)

ἀφανισμός
=========

ἀφανισμός (G0854)

noun

13\.98 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Exist

to cease to exist, to disappear, to die, disappearance to cease to exist, with the implication of no longer being evident or visible

[HEB 8:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 8:13


## ἄφαντος (id: 000852000000000)

ἄφαντος
=======

ἄφαντος (G0855)

adj

24\.28 · Sensory Events and States · See

invisible, unseen (derivative of ἀφανίζομαι\[a] ‘to become invisible,’ [24\.27](#24.27)) pertaining to that which becomes invisible

[LUK 24:31](https://ref.ly/Mark24:31)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 24:31


## ἀφεδρών (id: 000853000000000)

ἀφεδρών
=======

ἀφεδρών (G0856)

noun · ἕδρα · ἑδραῖος

7\.72 · Constructions · Miscellaneous Constructions

latrine, toilet The term ἀφεδρών occurs only in [MAT 15:17](https://ref.ly/Matt15:17) and [MRK 7:19](https://ref.ly/Matt7:19) and may be rendered in a number of languages as ‘place of defecation.’ In some languages, however, a reference to a toilet may seem inappropriate for the Scriptures, and it is possible to translate a passage such as [MAT 15:17](https://ref.ly/Matt15:17) as ‘goes into the stomach and then passes on out.’ The meaning is thus clear without a specific reference to a latrine or toilet.

[MAT 15:17](https://ref.ly/Matt15:17) · [MRK 7:19](https://ref.ly/Matt7:19)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 15:17; Mark 7:19


## ἀφειδία (id: 000854000000000)

ἀφειδία
=======

ἀφειδία (G0857)

noun

88\.90 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Self\-Control, Lack of Self\-Control

severe self\-control, harsh control over severe self\-control, suggesting an ascetic and unsparing attitude

[COL 2:23](https://ref.ly/Phil2:23)

* **Associated Passages:** Colossians 2:23


## ἀφελότης (id: 000855000000000)

ἀφελότης
========

ἀφελότης (G0858)

noun · φελής

88\.55 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Humility

humility, humbleness, simplicity humility associated with simplicity of life

[ACT 2:46](https://ref.ly/John2:46)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 2:46


## ἄφεσις (id: 000856000000000)

ἄφεσις
======

ἄφεσις (G0859)

noun · ἵημι · ἀφίημι

40\.8 · Reconciliation, Forgiveness · Forgiveness

to pardon, to forgive, forgiveness to remove the guilt resulting from wrongdoing It is extremely important to note that the focus in the meanings of ἀφίημι\[f], ἄφεσις\[a], and ἀπολύω\[e] is upon the guilt of the wrongdoer and not upon the wrongdoing itself. The event of wrongdoing is not undone, but the guilt resulting from such an event is pardoned. To forgive, therefore, means essentially to remove the guilt resulting from wrongdoing.\|Some languages make a clear distinction between guilt and sin, and terms for forgiveness are therefore related to guilt and not to the wrongdoing. Therefore, ‘to forgive sins’ is literally ‘to forgive guilt.’ Though terms for ‘forgiveness’ are often literally ‘to wipe out,’ ‘to blot out,’ or ‘to do away with,’ it is obviously not possible to blot out or to wipe out an event, but it is possible to remove or obliterate the guilt.

[MAT 26:28](https://ref.ly/Matt26:28) · [MRK 1:4](https://ref.ly/Matt1:4) · [MRK 3:29](https://ref.ly/Matt3:29) · [LUK 1:77](https://ref.ly/Mark1:77) · [LUK 3:3](https://ref.ly/Mark3:3) · [LUK 24:47](https://ref.ly/Mark24:47) · [ACT 2:38](https://ref.ly/John2:38) · [ACT 5:31](https://ref.ly/John5:31) · [ACT 10:43](https://ref.ly/John10:43) · [ACT 13:38](https://ref.ly/John13:38) · [ACT 26:18](https://ref.ly/John26:18) · [EPH 1:7](https://ref.ly/Gal1:7) · [COL 1:14](https://ref.ly/Phil1:14) · [HEB 9:22](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:22) · [HEB 10:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:18)

37\.132 · Control, Rule · Release, Set Free

release, liberty the process of setting free or liberating

[LUK 4:18](https://ref.ly/Mark4:18) · [LUK 4:18](https://ref.ly/Mark4:18)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:28; Mark 1:4; Mark 3:29; Luke 1:77; Luke 3:3; Luke 24:47; Acts 2:38; Acts 5:31; Acts 10:43; Acts 13:38; Acts 26:18; Ephesians 1:7; Colossians 1:14; Hebrews 9:22; Hebrews 10:18; Luke 4:18


## ἁφή (id: 000857000000000)

ἁφή
===

ἁφή (G0860)

noun · ἅπτω

8\.60 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

ligament, that which binds together part of the joints of the body which binds the different parts together In some languages the ligaments may be spoken of as ‘the ropes of the body’ or ‘the strings of the body.’

[EPH 4:16](https://ref.ly/Gal4:16) · [COL 2:19](https://ref.ly/Phil2:19)

## ἀφθαρσία (id: 000858000000000)

ἀφθαρσία
========

ἀφθαρσία (G0861)

noun

23\.127 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

immortal, immortality the state of not being subject to decay, leading to death It is possible to translate this clause as ‘it will be raised and will never again die.’\|In rendering ‘immortality’ it may be necessary to employ an entire clause, for example, ‘that people will not die.’ However, in [2TI 1:10](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:10) ‘life and immortality’ may be best understood as a phrase in which ‘immortality’ is a qualification of ‘life,’ and therefore one may translate ‘revealing immortal life through the gospel’ or ‘revealing by means of the good news the life that does not end.’

[ROM 2:7](https://ref.ly/Acts2:7) · [1CO 15:42](https://ref.ly/Rom15:42) · [1CO 15:50](https://ref.ly/Rom15:50) · [1CO 15:53](https://ref.ly/Rom15:53) · [1CO 15:54](https://ref.ly/Rom15:54) · [2TI 1:10](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:10)

68\.57 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

unceasing, always, eternally, undying a continuous state or process, with the implication that the state or process in question is not interrupted by death A number of scholars, however, insist that ἀφθαρσία in [EPH 6:24](https://ref.ly/Gal6:24) must be understood in the sense of ‘immortality’ or ‘eternity’ as a temporal expression, but one which can be rendered as ‘with undying love.’

[EPH 6:24](https://ref.ly/Gal6:24)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 2:7; 1 Corinthians 15:42; 1 Corinthians 15:50; 1 Corinthians 15:53; 1 Corinthians 15:54; 2 Timothy 1:10; Ephesians 6:24

[MAT 5:24](https://ref.ly/Matt5:24) · [MAT 18:12](https://ref.ly/Matt18:12) · [MAT 24:2](https://ref.ly/Matt24:2) · [MRK 13:2](https://ref.ly/Matt13:2) · [LUK 13:8](https://ref.ly/Mark13:8) · [LUK 19:44](https://ref.ly/Mark19:44) · [LUK 21:6](https://ref.ly/Mark21:6) · [JHN 14:27](https://ref.ly/Luke14:27) · [HEB 2:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:8)

34\.78 · Association · Marriage, Divorce

to divorce, to separate to dissolve the marriage bond Expressions for divorce are often based on terms meaning literally ‘to send away,’ ‘to separate from,’ or ‘to leave one another.’ However, in a number of languages idiomatic expressions are employed, for example ‘to send him off with his clothes,’ ‘to untie the knot between them,’ or ‘to throw away her hearthstones.’\|Some persons have attempted to make an important distinction between ἀφίημι in [1CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/Rom7:11) [1CO 7:13](https://ref.ly/Rom7:13) and χωρίζω in [1CO 7:15](https://ref.ly/Rom7:15) on the assumption that ἀφίημι implies legal divorce, while χωρίζω only relates to separation. Such a distinction, however, seems to be quite artificial.

[1CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/Rom7:11) · [1CO 7:12](https://ref.ly/Rom7:12) · [1CO 7:13](https://ref.ly/Rom7:13)

40\.8 · Reconciliation, Forgiveness · Forgiveness

to pardon, to forgive, forgiveness to remove the guilt resulting from wrongdoing It is extremely important to note that the focus in the meanings of ἀφίημι\[f], ἄφεσις\[a], and ἀπολύω\[e] is upon the guilt of the wrongdoer and not upon the wrongdoing itself. The event of wrongdoing is not undone, but the guilt resulting from such an event is pardoned. To forgive, therefore, means essentially to remove the guilt resulting from wrongdoing.\|Some languages make a clear distinction between guilt and sin, and terms for forgiveness are therefore related to guilt and not to the wrongdoing. Therefore, ‘to forgive sins’ is literally ‘to forgive guilt.’ Though terms for ‘forgiveness’ are often literally ‘to wipe out,’ ‘to blot out,’ or ‘to do away with,’ it is obviously not possible to blot out or to wipe out an event, but it is possible to remove or obliterate the guilt.

90\.50 · Case · Agent of a Numerable Event

to produce, to make, to give a marker of an agent relation with numerable events, with the implication of something which proceeds from an agent

[MRK 15:37](https://ref.ly/Matt15:37)

33\.137 · Communication · Interpret, Mean, Explain

**Collocation:** ἄφες τοὺς νεκροὺς θάψαι τοὺς ἑαυτῶν νεκρούς

that is not what I mean, that is not the issue, that is not the point (an idiom, possibly an adage, literally: let the dead bury their dead) the matter in question is not the real issue Some scholars, however, understand this expression as merely a figurative reference to various types of people and thus translate ‘let those who are spiritually dead take care of their own dead.’

[MAT 8:22](https://ref.ly/Matt8:22) · [LUK 9:60](https://ref.ly/Mark9:60)

23\.109 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

**Collocation:** ἀφίημι τὸ πνεῦμα

to die (an idiom, literally: to send away the spirit) interpreted by some as implying voluntarily laying down one’s life, but such an inference is not justified by normal Greek usage)

[MAT 27:50](https://ref.ly/Matt27:50)

1 It is possible that ἀπολύω\[a] and ἀφίημι\[a] differ somewhat in meaning in that ἀπολύω\[a] may suggest more permission and ἀφίημι\[a] more dismissal, but this cannot be determined from NT usage. In [ACT 15:30](https://ref.ly/John15:30), however, οἱ … ἀπολυθέντες implies clearly being sent (see ἀπολύω\[b], [15\.66](#15.66)). 2 ἀφίημι\[b] involves two principal semantic components: (1\) movement away from and (2\) resulting separation. It would appear from most contexts that the focal component of ἀφίημι\[b] is resulting separation. 3 LUK 4:39 ἀφίημι\[j] differs somewhat from the other meanings in this subdomain in that it is not an individual who ceases to be involved in a particular state, but it is the state itself which ceases. At the same time, however, one may interpret this expression in [LUK 4:39](https://ref.ly/Mark4:39) semantically as ‘she ceased to be in a state of fever.’

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:36; Matthew 4:11; Matthew 18:12; Matthew 22:22; Matthew 22:25; Matthew 24:40; Matthew 24:41; Matthew 26:44; Matthew 26:56; Mark 1:20; Mark 1:31; Mark 4:36; Mark 8:13; Mark 12:12; Mark 12:19; Mark 12:20; Mark 12:22; Mark 14:50; Luke 10:30; Luke 17:34; Luke 17:35; John 4:3; John 8:29; John 14:18; John 16:28; John 16:32; Hebrews 6:1; Matthew 4:20; Matthew 4:22; Matthew 19:27; Matthew 19:29; Matthew 23:38; Mark 1:18; Mark 10:28; Mark 10:29; Mark 13:34; Luke 5:11; Luke 13:35; Luke 18:28; Luke 18:29; John 4:28; John 10:12; Matthew 5:24; Matthew 24:2; Mark 13:2; Luke 13:8; Luke 19:44; Luke 21:6; John 14:27; Hebrews 2:8; 1 Corinthians 7:11; 1 Corinthians 7:12; 1 Corinthians 7:13; Matthew 6:12; Matthew 6:14; Matthew 6:15; Matthew 9:2; Matthew 9:5; Matthew 9:6; Matthew 12:31; Matthew 12:32; Matthew 18:21; Matthew 18:35; Mark 2:5; Mark 2:7; Mark 2:9; Mark 2:10; Mark 3:28; Mark 4:12; Mark 11:25; Luke 5:20; Luke 5:21; Luke 5:23; Luke 5:24; Luke 7:47; Luke 7:48; Luke 7:49; Luke 11:4; Luke 12:10; Luke 17:3; Luke 17:4; Luke 23:34; John 20:23; Acts 8:22; Romans 4:7; James 5:15; 1 John 1:9; 1 John 2:12; Matthew 18:27; Matthew 18:32; Matthew 23:23; Mark 7:8; Matthew 27:49; Romans 1:27; Revelation 2:4; Matthew 8:15; Luke 4:39; John 4:52; Matthew 3:15; Matthew 5:40; Matthew 7:4; Matthew 13:30; Matthew 15:14; Matthew 19:14; Matthew 23:13; Mark 1:34; Mark 5:19; Mark 5:37; Mark 7:12; Mark 7:27; Mark 10:14; Mark 11:6; Mark 11:16; Mark 14:6; Mark 15:36; Luke 6:42; Luke 8:51; Luke 12:39; Luke 18:16; John 11:44; John 11:48; John 12:7; John 18:8; Acts 5:38; Acts 14:17; Revelation 2:20; Revelation 11:9; Mark 15:37; Matthew 8:22; Luke 9:60; Matthew 27:50


## ἀφικνέομαι (id: 000862000000000)

ἀφικνέομαι
==========

ἀφικνέομαι (G0864)

ἱκνέομαι · ἱκανόω

28\.23 · Know · Known \[the content of knowledge]

to become known (a figurative extension of meaning of ἀφικνέομαι ‘to move up to a point,’ not occurring in the NT) to become known as the result of information reaching its destination

[ROM 16:19](https://ref.ly/Acts16:19)1

1 ROM 16:19 It would be possible to treat ἀφικνέομαι in [ROM 16:19](https://ref.ly/Acts16:19) as simply a matter of the movement of information. It would also be possible to handle ἀφικνέομαι in the domain of Communication (33\), involving the reception of information or messages. This is a typical instance of semantic overlapping, depending upon the degree to which one wishes to recognize figurative extensions of meaning.

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 16:19

## ἄφιξις (id: 000865000000000)

ἄφιξις
======

ἄφιξις (G0867)

noun · ἱκνέομαι · ἱκανός

15\.37 · Linear Movement · Leave, Depart, Flee, Escape, Send

to go away, to depart, to leave motion away from a reference point with emphasis upon the departure, but without implications as to any resulting state of separation or rupture1

[ACT 20:29](https://ref.ly/John20:29)

1 ἀπέρχομαι\[a] and ἄφιξις differ in meaning from πορεύομαι\[c], ἄγω\[c], ὑπάγω\[b], and μεταίρω ([15\.34](#15.34),35\) in marking the departure more specifically and overtly by the prefixial forms ἀπ\- and ἀφ\-.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 20:29

ἀφίστημι

ἵστημι

to incite to revolt, to cause to rebel to cause people to rebel against or to reject authority

[ACT 5:37](https://ref.ly/John5:37)

[LUK 2:37](https://ref.ly/Mark2:37) · [LUK 4:13](https://ref.ly/Mark4:13) · [LUK 13:27](https://ref.ly/Mark13:27) · [ACT 12:10](https://ref.ly/John12:10) · [ACT 15:38](https://ref.ly/John15:38) · [ACT 19:9](https://ref.ly/John19:9) · [ACT 22:29](https://ref.ly/John22:29) · [2CO 12:8](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:8) · [2TI 2:19](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:19)

to fall away, to forsake, to turn away to abandon a former relationship or association, or to dissociate (a type of reversal of beginning to associate)1

[LUK 8:13](https://ref.ly/Mark8:13) · [1TI 4:1](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:1) · [HEB 3:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:12)

34\.41 · Association · Limit or Avoid Association

to shun, to avoid, to keep away from, to have nothing to do with purposely to avoid association with someone2

[ACT 5:38](https://ref.ly/John5:38)

1 The meanings of παραπίπτω, ἐκπίπτω\[c], ἀποστρέφομαι\[b], and ἀφίσταμαι\[b] appear to focus upon the initial aspects of dissociation. As such, they constitute a reversal of the process of joining or beginning to associate. 2 There are probably certain differences of meaning in the terms in [34\.41](#34.41), but without more distinctive contexts, it is difficult to determine the precise areas of meaning.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 5:37; Luke 2:37; Luke 4:13; Luke 13:27; Acts 12:10; Acts 15:38; Acts 19:9; Acts 22:29; 2 Corinthians 12:8; 2 Timothy 2:19; Luke 8:13; 1 Timothy 4:1; Hebrews 3:12; Acts 5:38


## ἄφνω (id: 000867000000000)

ἄφνω
====

ἄφνω (G0869)

adv · ἐξαίφνης · ἐξάπινα

67\.113 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

suddenly, at once, immediately pertaining to an extremely short period of time between a previous state or event and a subsequent state or event (in a number of contexts there is the implication of unexpectedness; but this seems to be a derivative of the context as a whole and not a part of the meaning of the lexical items)

[ACT 2:2](https://ref.ly/John2:2) · [ACT 16:26](https://ref.ly/John16:26) · [ACT 28:6](https://ref.ly/John28:6)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 2:2; Acts 16:26; Acts 28:6


## ἀφόβως (id: 000868000000000)

ἀφόβως
======

ἀφόβως (G0870)

adv

25\.253 · Attitudes and Emotions · Fear, Terror, Alarm

fearlessly, without fear, not afraid pertaining to being without fear An adverb such as ἀφόβως often corresponds to a clause in other languages. Accordingly, ‘dare to preach the message fearlessly’ must be rendered in some languages as ‘dare to preach the message without being afraid of anyone.’

[LUK 1:74](https://ref.ly/Mark1:74) · [1CO 16:10](https://ref.ly/Rom16:10) · [PHP 1:14](https://ref.ly/Eph1:14)

88\.151 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Act Shamefully

disgracefully, shamefully pertaining to disgraceful behavior which shows no regard or fear for social sanctions For another interpretation of ἀφόβως in [JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12), see ἀφόβως\[c] [53\.60](#53.60).

[JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12)

53\.60 · Religious Activities · Worship, Reverence

without reverence, shamelessly pertaining to being without reverence or awe for God It is also possible that ἀφόβως in [JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12) should be understood as simply ‘being without fear’ or ‘boldly’ ([25\.253](#25.253)). For another interpretation of ἀφόβως in [JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12), see ἀφόβως\[b] [88\.151](#88.151).

[JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 1:74; 1 Corinthians 16:10; Philippians 1:14; Jude 1:12

ὁράω

to think about, to fix one’s attention on to keep thinking about, without having one’s attention distracted

to learn about, to find out about to acquire information, with focus presumably upon the source of such information

## ἀφορίζω (id: 000871000000000)

ἀφορίζω
=======

ἀφορίζω (G0873)

ὁρίζω

34\.36 · Association · Belong To, Be Included in the Membership of, Be Excluded From

to exclude, to separate, to get rid of to exclude or remove someone from an association It is, of course, possible to translate ἀφορίζω in [LUK 6:22](https://ref.ly/Mark6:22) as ‘not to welcome,’ but the emphasis of ἀφορίζω is to exclude from a relationship which has previously existed. One may therefore translate [LUK 6:22](https://ref.ly/Mark6:22) as ‘happy are you ... when people no longer welcome you in their company ... because of the Son of Man.’ By introducing the temporal element ‘no longer,’ the fact of exclusion is made clear.

[LUK 6:22](https://ref.ly/Mark6:22)

37\.97 · Control, Rule · Assign to a Role or Function

to appoint, to set apart for to set aside a person for a particular task or function1

[ACT 13:2](https://ref.ly/John13:2) · [ROM 1:1](https://ref.ly/Acts1:1) · [GAL 1:15](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:15)

63\.28 · Whole, Unite, Part, Divide · Separate

to separate, to set one apart from another to separate into two or more parts or groups, often by some intervening space

[MAT 13:49](https://ref.ly/Matt13:49) · [MAT 25:32](https://ref.ly/Matt25:32) · [MAT 25:32](https://ref.ly/Matt25:32) · [ACT 19:9](https://ref.ly/John19:9) · [2CO 6:17](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:17) · [GAL 2:12](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:12)

1 It is possible that ἀφορίζω\[b] is somewhat more emphatic in meaning than ὁρίζω\[b] ([37\.96](#37.96)).

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 6:22; Acts 13:2; Romans 1:1; Galatians 1:15; Matthew 13:49; Matthew 25:32; Acts 19:9; 2 Corinthians 6:17; Galatians 2:12


## ἀφορμή (id: 000872000000000)

ἀφορμή
======

ἀφορμή (G0874)

noun · ὁρμάω

22\.46 · Trouble, Hardship, Relief, Favorable Circumstances · Favorable Circumstances or State

favorable circumstances, opportunity, occasion a set of circumstances favorable for a particular activity or endeavor In a number of languages it may be necessary to translate ἀφορμή opportunity in different ways depending upon the context. For example, in [ROM 7:8](https://ref.ly/Acts7:8) ἀφορμή may be rendered as ‘a good chance to ...’ In [GAL 5:13](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:13) ἀφορμή may be rendered as ‘a good way for ...,’ and in [1TI 5:14](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:14) ἀφορμή may be rendered ‘as a good reason for ...’

[ROM 7:8](https://ref.ly/Acts7:8) · [ROM 7:11](https://ref.ly/Acts7:11) · [2CO 5:12](https://ref.ly/1Cor5:12) · [GAL 5:13](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:13) · [1TI 5:14](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:14)

89\.22 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

unwarranted reason, excuse an unwarranted reason for some event

[2CO 11:12](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:12) · [2CO 11:12](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 7:8; Romans 7:11; 2 Corinthians 5:12; Galatians 5:13; 1 Timothy 5:14; 2 Corinthians 11:12


## ἀφρίζω (id: 000873000000000)

ἀφρίζω
======

ἀφρίζω (G0875)

ἀφρός · ἐπαφρίζω

14\.28 · Physical Events and States · Events Involving Liquids and Dry Masses

to foam up, to foam at the mouth to cause or to produce the foaming up of a liquid (restricted in the NT to the foaming at the mouth of a person experiencing an epileptic seizure) Since foaming at the mouth is so characteristic of epileptic seizures, there should be no difficulty in finding a rather specific term to identify this type of event.

[MRK 9:18](https://ref.ly/Matt9:18) · [MRK 9:20](https://ref.ly/Matt9:20)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 9:18; Mark 9:20


## ἀφρός (id: 000874000000000)

ἀφρός
=====

ἀφρός (G0876)

noun · ἀφρίζω

14\.27 · Physical Events and States · Events Involving Liquids and Dry Masses

foam (derivative of ἀφρίζω ‘to cause foaming of a liquid,’ [14\.28](#14.28)) a frothy, foaming mass or substance Though ἀφρός may refer to any type of foam or frothy substance, in [LUK 9:39](https://ref.ly/Mark9:39) the reference is clearly to frothy saliva coming out of the mouth, something which is typical of persons experiencing an epileptic seizure. A number of languages have quite specific terms for this type of foaming or frothing at the mouth, expressed literally in some instances as ‘his saliva was bubbling out of his mouth’ or ‘the water of his mouth came out in bubbles.’

[LUK 9:39](https://ref.ly/Mark9:39)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 9:39


## ἀφροσύνη (id: 000875000000000)

ἀφροσύνη
========

ἀφροσύνη (G0877)

noun

32\.53 · Understand · Lack of Capacity for Understanding

to be a fool, foolishness the state of not using one’s capacity for understanding

[MRK 7:22](https://ref.ly/Matt7:22) · [2CO 11:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:1) · [2CO 11:17](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:17) · [2CO 11:21](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:21)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 7:22; 2 Corinthians 11:1; 2 Corinthians 11:17; 2 Corinthians 11:21


## ἄφρων (id: 000876000000000)

ἄφρων
=====

ἄφρων (G0878)

adj

32\.52 · Understand · Lack of Capacity for Understanding

foolish, senseless, unwise pertaining to not employing one’s understanding, particularly in practical matters It is possible that ἄφρων in [ROM 2:20](https://ref.ly/Acts2:20) (παιδευτὴν ἀφρόνων ‘instructor of the foolish’) refers to people who are simply uninstructed or ignorant, since ἀφρόνων could be parallel with νηπίων, occurring in the same verse. On the other hand, there appears to be a contrast in the meanings of ἀφρόνων and νηπίων because of the distinctiveness in meanings of παιδευτήν and διδάσκαλον. Therefore, ἄφρων in [ROM 2:20](https://ref.ly/Acts2:20) is better understood as referring to those who act foolishly because they do not use their potential for understanding.

[LUK 11:40](https://ref.ly/Mark11:40) · [LUK 12:20](https://ref.ly/Mark12:20) · [ROM 2:20](https://ref.ly/Acts2:20) · [1CO 15:36](https://ref.ly/Rom15:36) · [2CO 11:16](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:16) · [2CO 11:16](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:16) · [2CO 11:19](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:19) · [2CO 12:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:6) · [2CO 12:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:11) · [EPH 5:17](https://ref.ly/Gal5:17) · [1PE 2:15](https://ref.ly/Jas2:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 11:40; Luke 12:20; Romans 2:20; 1 Corinthians 15:36; 2 Corinthians 11:16; 2 Corinthians 11:19; 2 Corinthians 12:6; 2 Corinthians 12:11; Ephesians 5:17; 1 Peter 2:15

## ἄχρι(ς) (id: 000889000000000)

ἄχρι(ς)
=======

ἄχρι(ς) (G0891) — ἄχρι, ἄχρι \| ἄχρις, ἄχρις

prep · ἄκρον

67\.119 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Point of Time: Until, Delay, Still, From

until, to, at last, at length the continuous extent of time up to a point1 The phrase εἰ μὴ ὅταν is often translated as ‘until’ (for example, μηδενὶ ἅ εἶδον διηγήσωνταιεἰ μὴ ὅταν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐκ νεκρῶν ἀναστῇ ‘not to tell anybody what they have seen until the Son of Man has risen from death’ [MRK 9:9](https://ref.ly/Matt9:9)), but this phrase may be analyzed as meaning ‘if it is not when,’ an expression which is semantically equivalent to ‘until.’

[MAT 24:38](https://ref.ly/Matt24:38) · [LUK 1:20](https://ref.ly/Mark1:20) · [LUK 4:13](https://ref.ly/Mark4:13) · [LUK 17:27](https://ref.ly/Mark17:27) · [LUK 21:24](https://ref.ly/Mark21:24) · [ACT 1:2](https://ref.ly/John1:2) · [ACT 2:29](https://ref.ly/John2:29) · [ACT 3:21](https://ref.ly/John3:21) · [ACT 7:18](https://ref.ly/John7:18) · [ACT 13:11](https://ref.ly/John13:11) · [ACT 20:11](https://ref.ly/John20:11) · [ACT 22:22](https://ref.ly/John22:22) · [ACT 23:1](https://ref.ly/John23:1) · [ACT 26:22](https://ref.ly/John26:22) · [ROM 1:13](https://ref.ly/Acts1:13) · [ROM 5:13](https://ref.ly/Acts5:13) · [ROM 8:22](https://ref.ly/Acts8:22) · [ROM 11:25](https://ref.ly/Acts11:25) · [1CO 4:11](https://ref.ly/Rom4:11) · [1CO 11:26](https://ref.ly/Rom11:26) · [1CO 15:25](https://ref.ly/Rom15:25) · [2CO 3:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:14) · [GAL 3:19](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:19) · [GAL 4:2](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:2) · [PHP 1:5](https://ref.ly/Eph1:5) · [PHP 1:6](https://ref.ly/Eph1:6) · [HEB 3:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:13) · [HEB 6:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:11) · [REV 2:10](https://ref.ly/Jude2:10) · [REV 2:25](https://ref.ly/Jude2:25) · [REV 2:26](https://ref.ly/Jude2:26) · [REV 7:3](https://ref.ly/Jude7:3) · [REV 12:11](https://ref.ly/Jude12:11) · [REV 15:8](https://ref.ly/Jude15:8) · [REV 17:17](https://ref.ly/Jude17:17) · [REV 20:3](https://ref.ly/Jude20:3) · [REV 20:5](https://ref.ly/Jude20:5)

later, until after a point of time which is subsequent to a duration and simultaneous with another point of time

1 ἕως, ἄχρι, and μέχρι frequently occur with a postposed marker of indefinite temporal reference: οὗ, occurring with all three markers, and ὅτου with ἕως. 2 It is probable that ἕως\[c], ἄχρι\[c], and μέχρι\[b] differ slightly in meaning, but this cannot be readily determined from existing contexts.

adj

23\.98 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

lifeless, inanimate pertaining to a state of not having life In some translations it may seem strange or even meaningless to speak of ‘musical instruments’ as being ‘lifeless’ or ‘not alive,’ since everyone would obviously know such a fact. It may therefore be necessary to translate as ‘even musical instruments like the flute and the harp, which are obviously not alive, nevertheless produce a sound.’

[1CO 14:7](https://ref.ly/Rom14:7)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 14:7
